| 3 | Unity feedback CLTF | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ |
| 4 | Forward path | $G(s) = G_c(s)\cdot G_a(s)\cdot G_p(s)$ | Cascade of blocks |
| 5 | Feedback path | $H(s) = H_m(s)$ | Sensor transfer function |
| 6 | Sensitivity | $S^T_G = \dfrac{\Delta T/T}{\Delta G/G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$ | Smaller is better |
| 7 | Proportional control | $m(t) = K_p\,e(t)$ | $K_p$ has unit [output/input] |
| 8 | Integral control | $m(t) = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\displaystyle\int e\,dt$ | $T_i$ = integral time [s] |
| 9 | Derivative control | $m(t) = K_p\,T_d\,\dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $T_d$ = derivative time [s] |
| 10 | PID controller (Laplace) | $G_c(s) = K_p\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | Realizable form |
| 11 | Steady-state error (step) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | Type 0 system, unity feedback |
| 12 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{KM}}$ | Second-order system |
| 13 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$ | rad/s |
| 14 | Block reduction — series | $G_{\text{eq}} = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot G_3$ | Same units compatibility required |
| 15 | Block reduction — parallel | $G_{\text{eq}} = G_1 \pm G_2$ | Same input/output direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoided:** Never compute the absolute value $\vert G \vert$ in plain markdown; use the math delimiter form $\vert G(s) \vert$ to avoid breaking the table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Refineries:** Temperature, pressure, level, and flow control in distillation columns — the four **"PVs" (Process Variables)**.
- **Aerospace:** Autopilot (closed-loop servomechanism) maintains aircraft pitch, roll, yaw.
- **Robotics:** Joint position/velocity controllers (PID + feedforward) for industrial arms.
- **Biomedical:** Insulin pumps, anesthesia delivery, prosthetics — all closed-loop control of physiological variables.
- **Power Systems:** Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) and Governor control maintain generator terminal voltage and speed.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Unity Negative Feedback System

> **Goal:** Derive the relationship $T(s) = C(s)/R(s)$ for a standard feedback loop.

Consider a generic single-loop negative feedback system. The forward path has transfer function $G(s)$, and the feedback path has transfer function $H(s)$.

**Step 1 — Define the forward path equation:**

The output $C(s)$ is the product of the actuating signal $E_a(s)$ and the forward path gain:

$$
C(s) = G(s)\cdot E_a(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the feedback path equation:**

The feedback signal $B(s)$ is the product of the output $C(s)$ and the feedback transfer function:

$$
B(s) = H(s)\cdot C(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Define the comparator (error) equation:**

In a negative feedback loop, the comparator subtracts the feedback signal from the reference:

$$
E_a(s) = R(s) - B(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (Step 2) into (Step 3):**

$$
E_a(s) = R(s) - H(s)\cdot C(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the corrected error into (Step 1):**

$$
C(s) = G(s)\left[R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Expand and collect $C(s)$ terms on the left side:**

$$
C(s) = G(s)\,R(s) - G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s)
$$

$$
C(s) + G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s) = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 7 — Factor out $C(s)$:**

$$
C(s)\left[1 + G(s)\,H(s)\right] = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function:**

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}
$$

For **unity feedback** ($H(s) = 1$):

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is the **"characteristic polynomial"** whose roots determine **stability, transient response, and steady-state behavior**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Proportional Controller Steady-State Error for a Unit Step Input

> **Goal:** Show that a P-controller cannot eliminate steady-state error in a Type-0 system.

Consider a Type-0 plant with $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{Ts+1}$ and a proportional controller $G_c(s) = K_p$.

**Step 1 — Identify the forward path transfer function:**

$$
G(s) = G_c(s)\cdot G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p K}{Ts+1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the open-loop DC gain (s = 0):**

$$
G(0) = \lim_{s \to 0}\dfrac{K_p K}{Ts+1} = K_p K
$$

**Step 3 — Define position error constant $K_p$ for a Type-0 system (KTU notation $K_p^*$):**

$$
K_p^* = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = K_p K
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Final Value Theorem to the error:**

Error $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$. For a unit step, $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$
E(s) = \dfrac{1/s}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{1}{s\left[1 + G(s)\right]}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the steady-state error:**

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p^*}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $K_p^* = K_p K$:**

$$
e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K}
$$

> **Conclusion:** As $K_p \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$, but the system may become **unstable** (poles move to the right half-plane). This is the classic **"design trade-off"** in control engineering.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity of Closed-Loop to Plant Variations

> **Goal:** Show that negative feedback reduces sensitivity to plant parameter changes.

**Step 1 — Define sensitivity of $T$ with respect to $G$:**

$$
S^T_G = \dfrac{\partial T/T}{\partial G/G} = \dfrac{G}{T}\cdot\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial G}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $T(s) = G/(1 + GH)$ with respect to $G$:**

$$
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \dfrac{(1 + GH) - G(H)}{(1 + GH)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the sensitivity definition:**

$$
S^T_G = \dfrac{G}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} = \dfrac{G(1 + GH)}{G}\cdot\dfrac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
S^T_G = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For $\vert GH \vert \gg 1$, sensitivity $S^T_G \approx 1/(GH)$, which is very small. Hence, **closed-loop systems are far less sensitive to plant variations** than open-loop systems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Block Diagram Reduction Rules

| Operation | Before | After | Formula |
|-----------|--------|-------|---------|
| Cascade (Series) | $R \to [G_1] \to [G_2] \to C$ | $R \to [G_1 G_2] \to C$ | $G_{\text{eq}} = G_1 G_2$ |
| Parallel | $R \to \text{branch} \to [G_1] + [G_2] \to C$ | $R \to [G_1 \pm G_2] \to C$ | $G_{\text{eq}} = G_1 \pm G_2$ |
| Feedback (Negative) | $R \to [G] \to C$, with $H$ feedback | $R \to \left[\dfrac{G}{1+GH}\right] \to C$ | $T = \dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ |
| Feedback (Positive) | $R \to [G] \to C$, with $H$ feedback | $R \to \left[\dfrac{G}{1-GH}\right] \to C$ | $T = \dfrac{G}{1-GH}$ |
| Take-off point shift | Move take-off point past a block | Equivalent block transformation | Block becomes divided/multiplied by $G$ |
| Summing point shift | Move summing point past a block | Equivalent block transformation | Block becomes multiplied/divided by $G$ |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**List and explain the basic elements of a control system with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five basic elements of a control system are:

1. **Process / Plant** — The system whose output is to be controlled (e.g., furnace, motor).
2. **Measuring Element (Sensor)** — Measures the output variable and converts it to a comparable signal (e.g., RTD, LVDT).
3. **Comparator (Error Detector)** — Computes the difference between the setpoint $r(t)$ and measured feedback $b(t)$ to generate the error $e(t)$.
4. **Controller** — Processes the error using a control law (P, PI, PD, PID) and decides the corrective action.
5. **Final Control Element (Actuator)** — Implements the controller's decision on the process (e.g., control valve, heater).

The reference $r(t)$ enters the comparator; the output $c(t)$ is fed back through the sensor $H(s)$ to the comparator, closing the loop. The forward path contains $G_c(s)G_a(s)G_p(s)$. *(3 marks — 1 mark for naming elements, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for block diagram description.)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (via sensor) |
| Accuracy | Low, depends on calibration | High, self-correcting |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor | Excellent |
| Stability | Inherently stable | Requires design care |
| Example | Toaster, traffic signal timer | Refrigerator, autopilot |

In open-loop, output has no influence on the input. In closed-loop, error $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ drives the controller, ensuring the output tracks the reference despite disturbances. *(3 marks — 2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the conceptual statement.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern** (As per KTU 2024 Scheme Regulations)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a closed-loop control system and explain the function of each basic element. Derive the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ for a system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$. Determine (i) the closed-loop transfer function, (ii) the characteristic equation, (iii) the type and order of the system, and (iv) the steady-state error for a unit step input. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The block diagram of a closed-loop control system is:

```
   r(t) ──►(+)─────►[ G_c ]──►[ G_a ]──►[ G_p ]──┬──► c(t)
              ▲(-)                                  │
              │                                     │
              └──[ H_m ]◄────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Comparator:** Generates $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
- **Controller $G_c$:** Implements the control law.
- **Actuator $G_a$:** Provides the physical correcting action.
- **Process $G_p$:** The system being controlled.
- **Sensor $H_m$:** Measures the output and feeds it back.

**Derivation:**

From the block diagram:

$$
C(s) = G(s)E(s) \quad \text{where } G(s) = G_c G_a G_p
$$

$$
B(s) = H(s)C(s) = H(s)G(s)E(s)
$$

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s)G(s)E(s)
$$

Solving:

$$
E(s)\left[1 + G(s)H(s)\right] = R(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + GH}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\cdot R(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
$$

For unity feedback $H(s) = 1$:

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for block diagram, 2 for element functions, 3 for derivation.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, $H(s) = 1$.

**(i) Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{10/s(s+2)(s+5)}{1 + 10/s(s+2)(s+5)}
$$

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5) + 10}
$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$
s(s+2)(s+5) = s(s^2 + 7s + 10) = s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s
$$

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{10}{s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 10}
$$

**(ii) Characteristic Equation:**

$$
s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 10 = 0
$$

**(iii) Type and Order:**

- **Order** = **3** (highest power of $s$ in denominator).
- **Type** = **1** (number of pure integrations, i.e., poles at $s = 0$).

**(iv) Steady-State Error for Unit Step Input:**

Position error constant:

$$
K_p^* = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)} = \infty
$$

For a unit step, $R(s) = 1/s$. For a Type-1 system, step input produces **zero** steady-state error:

$$
e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p^*} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for CLTF, 1 for characteristic equation, 2 for type/order, 2 for steady-state error.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller. Derive the transfer function of an ideal PID controller and list two advantages and two disadvantages of each P, I, and D action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control system uses a PI controller with $K_p = 2$ and $T_i = 10$ s on a first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{3}{5s+1}$ in a unity feedback configuration. Find: (i) the open-loop transfer function, (ii) the closed-loop transfer function, (iii) the steady-state error for a unit step input, and (iv) the steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **PID controller** is a three-mode controller that combines the benefits of proportional, integral, and derivative actions on the error signal $e(t)$.

**Time-domain equation:**

$$
m(t) = K_p\left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\displaystyle\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right]
$$

where $K_p$ = proportional gain, $T_i$ = integral time (s), $T_d$ = derivative time (s).

**Transfer function derivation:**

Taking the Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$
M(s) = K_p\,E(s) + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\cdot\dfrac{E(s)}{s} + K_p T_d\,s\,E(s)
$$

Factoring out $E(s)$:

$$
M(s) = K_p\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]E(s)
$$

Therefore:

$$
G_c(s) = \dfrac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_p\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]
$$

**Summary of actions:**

| Mode | Effect on Response | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|------|--------------------|-----------|--------------|
| **P** | Increases speed, reduces rise time | Simple, fast response | Steady-state error persists |
| **I** | Eliminates steady-state error | Zero $e_{ss}$ for step | Slow response, can cause oscillation |
| **D** | Anticipates future error, adds damping | Improves stability | Sensitive to noise |

*(7 marks — 2 for operation, 2 for derivation, 3 for the table.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $K_p = 2$, $T_i = 10$ s, $G_p(s) = \dfrac{3}{5s+1}$, $H(s) = 1$.

**PI controller transfer function:**

$$
G_c(s) = K_p\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right] = 2\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{10s}\right] = 2\cdot\dfrac{10s+1}{10s} = \dfrac{2(10s+1)}{10s}
$$

$$
G_c(s) = \dfrac{20s+2}{10s} = \dfrac{2(10s+1)}{10s}
$$

**(i) Open-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$
G(s) = G_c(s)\cdot G_p(s) = \dfrac{2(10s+1)}{10s}\cdot\dfrac{3}{5s+1}
$$

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{6(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)} = \dfrac{6(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)}
$$

**(ii) Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{6(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1) + 6(10s+1)}
$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$
10s(5s+1) = 50s^2 + 10s
$$

$$
50s^2 + 10s + 60s + 6 = 50s^2 + 70s + 6
$$

$$
T(s) = \dfrac{6(10s+1)}{50s^2 + 70s + 6} = \dfrac{60s + 6}{50s^2 + 70s + 6}
$$

**(iii) Steady-State Error for Unit Step:**

For a Type-1 system (one pole at origin due to integrator $1/s$ in the PI):

$$
K_p^* = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\dfrac{6(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)} = \infty
$$

$$
e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p^*} = 0
$$

**(iv) Steady-State Error for Unit Ramp:**

Velocity error constant:

$$
K_v = \lim_{s \to 0}\,s\,G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\,s\cdot\dfrac{6(10s+1)}{10s(5s+1)} = \lim_{s \to 0}\dfrac{6(10s+1)}{10(5s+1)} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6
$$

$$
e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = \dfrac{1}{0.6} = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.667
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for OLTF, 2 for CLTF, 1 for step error, 2 for ramp error.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to substitute $H(s) = 1$** for unity feedback in CLTF derivation (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing the two $K_p$ symbols:** the proportional gain $K_p$ of the controller vs. the position error constant $K_p^*$. Always distinguish using notation.
> 3. **Misidentifying the "Type" of a system:** Type is the number of pure integrations (poles at origin) of $G(s)H(s)$, NOT the number of poles.
> 4. **Applying Final Value Theorem incorrectly:** ensure the system is stable (poles in LHP) before using $\lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$.
> 5. **Skipping units / dimensions** in the PID transfer function derivation — examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating the units of $T_i$ and $T_d$.
> 6. **Sign convention errors:** In negative feedback, $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$. Swapping the sign leads to positive feedback analysis — a **serious mark penalty**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Elements**

- ✅ The **five basic elements** are: **Process, Sensor, Comparator, Controller, Actuator**. Memorize the order of signal flow: $r \to \text{comparator} \to G_c \to G_a \to G_p \to c \to H_m \to \text{back to comparator}$.
- ✅ **Error** is **always** $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ in **negative feedback** — never the other way around.
- ✅ **Open-loop** = no feedback, **Closed-loop** = with feedback. Closed-loop is more accurate but stability is not guaranteed.
- ✅ **CLTF formula:** $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$. For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$.
- ✅ **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ determines stability.
- ✅ **PID equation (time domain):** $m(t) = K_p e(t) + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_p T_d\,\dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- ✅ **PID transfer function:** $G_c(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$.
- ✅ **System Type** = number of pure integrators (poles at origin) in $G(s)H(s)$.
- ✅ **System Order** = total number of poles in the closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$.
- ✅ **Steady-state error for unit step** in a Type-$N$ system:
  - Type 0: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p^*}$
  - Type 1, 2, ...: $e_{ss} = 0$
- ✅ **Steady-state error for unit ramp** in a Type-$N$ system:
  - Type 0: $e_{ss} = \infty$
  - Type 1: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$
  - Type 2, 3, ...: $e_{ss} = 0$
- ✅ **Standard industrial signal ranges:** 4–20 mA, 3–15 psi, 1–5 V, 0–10 V.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** of closed-loop to plant variations = $\dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$, which is **small** for high loop gain — the fundamental justification for using feedback.
- ✅ **Distinguish sensor characteristics:** accuracy, precision, sensitivity, span, resolution, dead zone.
- ✅ **Final Value Theorem** is applicable **only** when all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the **open left half-plane** (system is stable).
- ✅ **Block diagram reduction rules** — Master cascade, parallel, and feedback reduction; practice moving take-off and summing points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Pack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Pack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 — Power Pack

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Pack (Hydraulic Power Unit – HPU):** A self-contained, integrated assembly of hydraulic and electro-mechanical components that converts **electrical energy** (or prime-mover mechanical energy) into **hydraulic energy** in the form of pressurized, regulated fluid flow, and delivers this energy to one or more hydraulic actuators/machines.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** nomenclature for fluid power systems, the Power Pack is the *energy source* of any hydraulic circuit. It comprises an **electric motor** (or engine), a **hydraulic pump**, a **fluid reservoir**, **filtration**, **conditioning accessories** (coolers, heaters, accumulators), **pressure/level/temperature monitoring** instruments, and **protective/control valves** mounted on a common frame or manifold.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart & Lungs of the Hydraulic Body"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Human Circulatory System:**
> - **Electric Motor** = Human Heart (provides the rhythmic pumping action).
> - **Hydraulic Pump** = Left Ventricle (converts rotation into directed fluid energy).
> - **Reservoir** = Blood Reservoir / Veins (stores and supplies fluid on demand).
> - **Filters** = Kidneys (clean impurities).
> - **Pressure Relief Valve** = Pressure Safety Valve in the brain (prevents over-pressurization).
> - **Heat Exchanger** = Skin/Sweat (dissipates metabolic heat).
> - **Accumulator** = Oxygen reserve tank for emergency bursts.

Just as the human body cannot function without its heart, no hydraulic system — whether a hydraulic press, injection moulding machine, or aircraft landing gear — can operate without a properly designed **Power Pack**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| System Pressure | $P$ | **bar** or **MPa** | 50 – 350 bar |
| Flow Rate | $Q$ | **LPM** (Litres Per Minute) | 10 – 500 LPM |
| Tank Volume | $V_t$ | **Litres** | 2 – 5 × Q (rule) |
| Power | $P_{ow}$ | **kW** | 0.75 – 75 kW |
| Viscosity | $\nu$ | **cSt** (centiStokes) | 10 – 100 cSt |
| Filter Rating | $\beta$ | **micron (μm)** | 10 – 25 μm |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Power Pack operates on the principle of **electro-hydraulic energy conversion**:

> **Step 1 → Prime Mover Start:** The **electric motor** (typically a 3-phase AC induction motor, 1450 rpm @ 50 Hz) is energized and rotates at a fixed synchronous speed.

> **Step 2 → Mechanical Coupling:** The motor shaft is coupled to the **hydraulic pump** either directly (via a flexible coupling) or through a **gear/belt drive** for speed reduction/amplification.

> **Step 3 → Suction Stroke:** The pump draws oil from the **reservoir** through a **suction strainer** and **suction line filter**. A slight **vacuum (negative pressure)** is created at the pump inlet — hence the suction line must be short, large in diameter, and airtight.

> **Step 4 → Discharge Stroke:** The pump mechanically displaces oil and delivers it at high pressure through the **discharge port**, where a **pressure relief valve** (set to 10% above max working pressure) provides safety.

> **Step 5 → Conditioning:** The pressurized oil passes through a **high-pressure filter** (typically 10 μm absolute) and reaches the **directional control valve (DCV)**, which routes it to the actuator (cylinder/motor).

> **Step 6 → Return & Cooling:** Spent oil returns to the tank through a **return line filter**, where **heat exchangers** (water-cooled or air-cooled) dissipate the **heat generated due to throttling and friction losses**.

> **Step 7 → Re-pressurization:** The cooled, clean oil is again drawn by the pump — closing the energy loop.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Why a reservoir?** It acts as a thermal sink, allows air-separation, and supplies oil during peak demand (supplemented by an **accumulator**).
- **Why filtration?** 75% of hydraulic failures are due to **contamination** — ISO 4406 cleanliness code must be maintained.
- **Why a relief valve?** Hydraulic fluid is essentially *incompressible*; without pressure relief, the system can explode.
- **Why a cooler?** Every **4°C rise in oil temperature halves oil life** (Arrhenius rule).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Equations for Power Pack Sizing & Analysis**

$$
Q = A \times v \quad \text{[Volumetric flow rate]}
$$

$$
Q_{pump} = V_d \times n \times \eta_v \quad \text{[Pump theoretical delivery]}
$$

$$
P_{hydraulic} = P \times Q \quad \text{[Hydraulic power output]}
$$

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P \times Q}{600 \times \eta_{overall}} \quad \text{[kW, with P in bar and Q in LPM]}
$$

$$
V_t = (2 \text{ to } 3) \times Q \quad \text{[Reservoir sizing rule of thumb]}
$$

$$
H_{gen} = P_{in} \times Q \times (1 - \eta) \quad \text{[Heat generated per unit time, in W]}
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{hyd} \quad \text{[Total efficiency]}
$$

| Formula | Boundary Condition / Constraint | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $Q = A \times v$ | $v \le 1.5$ m/s (suction), $\le 6$ m/s (return) | m³/s |
| $Q_{pump} = V_d \cdot n \cdot \eta_v$ | $n$ in rps, $V_d$ in m³/rev | m³/s |
| $P = F/A$ | — | Pa or N/m² |
| $P_{ow} = P \cdot Q$ | $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s | **W** |
| $V_t = 2 \text{ to } 3 \cdot Q$ | For closed systems use $3$ to $5 \cdot Q$ | Litres |
| $H_{gen} = P_{in}(1-\eta)$ | Heat carried away by cooler | W or kW |
| $\Delta T = H_{gen}/(m \cdot c_p)$ | $c_p \approx 1.88$ kJ/kg·K for oil | °C |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power Packs are the **backbone of modern industry**:

- **Injection Moulding Machines** — supply clamping & injection forces (typically 100–250 bar).
- **Hydraulic Presses** — forging, stamping, briquetting (up to 700 bar).
- **Earth-moving Equipment** — excavators, loaders, dump trucks (mobile power packs with diesel engines).
- **Aircraft Systems** — landing gear, flaps, brakes (compact, high-pressure HPU).
- **Marine Steering Gear** — rudder actuation.
- **Steel Plants** — continuous casting, rolling mills (HPU in MW class).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Power Pack Motor Sizing

> **Problem Setup:** A hydraulic press requires a clamping force of $F = 100$ kN. The cylinder bore is $D = 100$ mm. Working pressure is $P = 200$ bar. Cylinder extends in $t = 5$ s. Pump volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$, overall efficiency $\eta_{o} = 0.85$. Determine motor kW rating.

**Step 1 — Convert Pressure to SI units**

$$
P = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Cylinder Piston Area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Required Cylinder Velocity**

$$
v = \frac{L}{t}
$$

Assume stroke length $L = 0.5$ m:

$$
v = \frac{0.5}{5} = 0.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Theoretical Flow Rate**

$$
Q_{theory} = A \times v = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1
$$

$$
Q_{theory} = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 47.12 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual Pump Delivery (compensating for leakage)**

$$
Q_{actual} = \frac{Q_{theory}}{\eta_v} = \frac{47.12}{0.95}
$$

$$
Q_{actual} = 49.6 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic Power Output**

$$
P_{hyd} = \frac{P \times Q}{600} = \frac{200 \times 49.6}{600}
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 16.53 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Motor Power Input (accounting for overall losses)**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{16.53}{0.85}
$$

$$
P_{motor} = 19.45 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Selected Standard Motor Rating: 22 kW** (next higher standard IEC frame size).

### 3.2 Derivation — Heat Dissipation & Reservoir Sizing

**Step 1 — Heat Generated when actuator is held stationary (dead-heading)**

$$
H_{gen} = P_{motor} - P_{useful} = 19.45 - 0 = 19.45 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Oil Temperature Rise without Cooler**

Assume oil mass in tank $m = 100$ kg, specific heat $c_p = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{H_{gen}}{m \cdot c_p} = \frac{19.45}{100 \times 1.88}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 0.103 \text{ °C/s}
$$

This is **unacceptable** — in 10 minutes oil temperature rises by 62 °C!

**Step 3 — Required Heat Exchanger Capacity**

Allowable $\Delta T_{oil} = 30$ °C above ambient (to keep oil below 65 °C).

$$
Q_{cooler} = H_{gen} = 19.45 \text{ kW (continuous dissipation required)}
$$

**Step 4 — Reservoir Sizing**

$$
V_t = 3 \times Q_{actual} = 3 \times 49.6 \approx 150 \text{ Litres}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Analytical Tip:** Always size tank **3× to 5× the pump flow per minute** for closed-loop systems to allow adequate residence time for air separation and heat dissipation.

### 3.3 KTU Numerical Quick-Reference Matrix

| Given | Find | Equation Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Force, Bore | Pressure | $P = F/A$ |
| Flow, Area | Cylinder velocity | $v = Q/A$ |
| Stroke, time | Required flow | $Q = A \cdot L/t$ |
| Pressure, Flow | Hydraulic kW | $P_{ow} = P \cdot Q / 600$ |
| kW, efficiency | Motor rating | $P_{motor} = P_{ow}/\eta$ |
| kW, tank mass | Temperature rise | $\Delta T = H/(m \cdot c_p)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic Power Pack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EM[ELECTRIC MOTOR\n3 PHASE AC\n1450 RPM] --> CB[COUPLING\nFLEXIBLE]
    CB --> P[HYDRAULIC PUMP\nGEAR OR PISTON]
    R[RESERVOIR TANK\nISO 4406 CLEAN] --> SF[STRAINER\nSUCTION LINE]
    SF --> P
    P --> CV[CHECK VALVE\nNON RETURN]
    CV --> HPP[HIGH PRESSURE FILTER\n10 MICRON]
    HPP --> PRV[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE\nSET AT 110 PERCENT]
    HPP --> DCV[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE\n4 BY 3 SOLENOID]
    DCV --> ACT[HYDRAULIC ACTUATOR\nCYLINDER OR MOTOR]
    ACT --> RTN[RETURN LINE FILTER\n25 MICRON]
    RTN --> HX[HEAT EXCHANGER\nOIL COOLER]
    HX --> R
    ACC[ACCUMULATOR\nBLADDER TYPE] --- HPP
    PG[PRESSURE GAUGE] --- PRV
    LT[LEVEL SWITCH\nTEMPERATURE SWITCH] --- R
```

### 4.2 Functional Roles Mapped

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[ENERGY INPUT]
        A1[ELECTRICAL SUPPLY 415V AC]
    end
    subgraph CONVERSION[ENERGY CONVERSION STAGE]
        B1[MOTOR]
        B2[PUMP]
    end
    subgraph CONDITIONING[FLUID CONDITIONING STAGE]
        C1[RESERVOIR]
        C2[FILTERS]
        C3[HEAT EXCHANGER]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[PRESSURE AND FLOW CONTROL]
        D1[RELIEF VALVE]
        D2[ACCUMULATOR]
        D3[DCV]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[HYDRAULIC POWER OUTPUT TO ACTUATOR]
        E1[ACTUATOR WORK]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> D1 --> D3 --> E1
    C1 --> B2
    C2 --> B2
    C2 --> D3
    C3 --> C1
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Pump Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[REQUIRED PRESSURE IN BAR] --> Q1{PRESSURE LESS THAN 175 BAR}
    Q1 -- YES --> P1[USE GEAR OR VANE PUMP\nFIXED DISPLACEMENT]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{PRESSURE LESS THAN 350 BAR}
    Q2 -- YES --> P2[USE AXIAL PISTON PUMP\nSWASH PLATE TYPE]
    Q2 -- NO --> P3[USE RADIAL PISTON PUMP\nHIGH PRESSURE CLASS]
    P1 --> E[SELECT MOTOR ACCORDING TO POWER]
    P2 --> E
    P3 --> E
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART — A (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a hydraulic power pack and list its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A hydraulic power pack is a self-contained assembly that converts electrical/mechanical energy into hydraulic energy to drive hydraulic actuators. **Main components:** (1) Prime mover — electric motor or engine, (2) Hydraulic pump, (3) Reservoir/tank, (4) Filters — suction, return, and pressure, (5) Pressure relief valve, (6) Heat exchanger (cooler), (7) Accumulator, (8) Pressure gauge & level/temperature indicators, (9) Directional control valves. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State the rule of thumb used for reservoir sizing in a hydraulic power pack.**

**Model Answer:**
The reservoir volume $V_t$ is typically **2 to 3 times the pump flow rate per minute** for open-loop systems, and **3 to 5 times** for closed-loop systems.
$$
V_t = (2 \text{ to } 3) \times Q \text{ LPM}
$$
This provides sufficient residence time for air separation, heat dissipation, and peak fluid demand. **(3 Marks)**

---

### PART — B (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Answer **ANY ONE** from Q3A or Q3B.

---

#### **Question 3A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional layout of a hydraulic power pack. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder of bore 80 mm is required to lift a load of 50 kN at a system pressure of 200 bar. Determine: (i) Piston area, (ii) Cylinder velocity if flow rate supplied is 30 LPM, (iii) Motor kW rating assuming overall efficiency 85%. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 Marks):**
The power pack consists of the following functional blocks arranged in series:
1. **Electric Motor** — 3-phase induction motor, supplies rotational mechanical energy.
2. **Hydraulic Pump** — converts mechanical rotation into hydraulic flow and pressure.
3. **Suction Filtration** — protects pump from ingestion of contaminants.
4. **Pressure Relief Valve** — protects the system from over-pressure.
5. **High-Pressure Filter** — provides ISO 4406 cleanliness before the directional control valve.
6. **Accumulator** — stores energy and compensates for peak demands.
7. **DCV** — directs flow to actuator.
8. **Return Line Filter** — cleans returning oil.
9. **Heat Exchanger** — dissipates heat.
10. **Reservoir** — stores, cools, and de-aerates oil.

*(Draw block diagram similar to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Piston Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^{2}}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**(ii) Cylinder Velocity:**

$$
Q = 30 \text{ LPM} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Motor kW:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \frac{P \times Q}{600} = \frac{200 \times 30}{600} = 10 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{10}{0.85} = 11.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Select 15 kW standard motor.**

---

#### **Question 3B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the role of the following accessories in a hydraulic power pack: (i) Accumulator, (ii) Heat Exchanger, (iii) Pressure Relief Valve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic power pack delivers 60 LPM at 250 bar. Calculate: (i) Hydraulic power in kW, (ii) Reservoir capacity, (iii) Heat generated per minute if overall efficiency is 80%. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Roles of Accessories (7 Marks):**

1. **Accumulator (2 Marks):** Stores pressurized hydraulic fluid (using nitrogen pre-charge) to: (a) supplement pump flow during peak demand, (b) maintain pressure during pump shutdown, (c) absorb pressure shocks/pressure pulsations, (d) serve as emergency power source.
2. **Heat Exchanger (2.5 Marks):** Removes heat generated due to throttling losses, friction, and pump inefficiencies. Maintains oil temperature below 65 °C, prolonging seal and oil life. Types: air-cooled (fan) and water-cooled.
3. **Pressure Relief Valve (2.5 Marks):** Senses system pressure and opens when set value (typically 110% of max working pressure) is exceeded, routing excess oil back to tank. Protects pump, actuators, and piping from catastrophic failure.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Hydraulic Power:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \frac{P \times Q}{600} = \frac{250 \times 60}{600} = 25 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Reservoir Capacity:**

$$
V_t = 3 \times Q = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ Litres}
$$

**(iii) Heat Generated per Minute:**

Input power:

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta} = \frac{25}{0.80} = 31.25 \text{ kW}
$$

Heat generated:

$$
H_{gen} = P_{in} - P_{hyd} = 31.25 - 25 = 6.25 \text{ kW}
$$

Heat per minute:

$$
H_{per min} = 6.25 \times 60 = 375 \text{ kJ/min}
$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Forgetting to convert bar → Pa or LPM → m³/s. Always check SI units before substituting. *(–1 to –2 Marks)*
> 2. **Missing Efficiency in Motor Calculation:** Many students compute $P = P \times Q / 600$ and present it as the answer. Motor rating must divide by **overall efficiency**. *(–2 Marks)*
> 3. **Reservoir Sizing Without "Rule of Thumb":** Direct numerical values from a specific table are not acceptable — state the rule $V_t = 2Q$ to $3Q$ explicitly. *(–1 Mark)*
> 4. **Block Diagram Incompleteness:** A power-pack diagram without the heat exchanger, return-line filter, or accumulator is considered incomplete. *(–1 to –2 Marks)*
> 5. **Confusing Pressure Relief Valve with Sequence Valve:** Relief valves limit *maximum system* pressure; sequence valves activate secondary circuits. *(–1 Mark)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power Pack = Energy Source** of every hydraulic circuit — never an optional accessory.
- **Rule of thumb for tank:** $V_t = 2Q$ to $3Q$ (open), $3Q$ to $5Q$ (closed).
- **Standard motor formula:** $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P(\text{bar}) \times Q(\text{LPM})}{600 \times \eta}$ kW.
- **Heat generation:** $H_{gen} = P_{motor}(1-\eta)$ — *every kW lost becomes heat*.
- **75% of hydraulic failures** are due to contamination — filtration is non-negotiable.
- **Pressure relief valve set point** = 110% of maximum working pressure (never higher).
- **Suction line velocity** must remain $\le 1.5$ m/s to prevent cavitation.
- **Oil temperature** must stay below **65 °C** for ISO VG 46 / VG 68 oils.
- **Pumps:** Gear/Vane ($<175$ bar), Axial Piston ($<350$ bar), Radial Piston ($>350$ bar).
- **Accumulators** are *energy storage* devices — *not* flow sources.
- **Heat exchanger sizing** must equal worst-case $H_{gen}$, not average.
- **Always** state efficiency, units, and rule-of-thumb assumptions explicitly in KTU numericals.
- **Block diagrams** should depict *flow direction*, not just component placement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Pneumatic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Pneumatic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pneumatic System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *pneumatic system* is a power transmission system that uses a **compressible fluid — typically atmospheric air** — as the working medium to transmit, control, and convert energy into mechanical work. The energy is generated by a compressor, conditioned (filtered, regulated, lubricated) and delivered through a network of valves, pipelines, and actuators (cylinders / motors) to perform linear or rotary motion.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a pneumatic circuit comprises four functional blocks:

| # | Functional Block | KTU Standard Designation |
|---|------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Energy Source** | Compressor (Reciprocating / Rotary / Centrifugal) |
| 2 | **Conditioning Unit** | FRL — *Filter, Regulator, Lubricator* |
| 3 | **Control Elements** | DCV (Direction Control Valves), FCV (Flow Control), PCV (Pressure Control) |
| 4 | **Actuators** | Single/Double-acting cylinders, Pneumatic motors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs and Muscles" of a Factory

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a pneumatic system as the **lungs and muscles of a factory**. The **compressor** is the *diaphragm* (inhales air and pressurizes it), the **FRL** is the *trachea* (cleans, regulates pressure, lubricates), the **valves** are the *brain signals* (decides which muscle moves), and the **cylinders** are the *muscles* (push/pull to do work). Without the regulator (pressure governor), the muscles would tear; without the filter (nose), dust would clog them — just like a human respiratory system.

- **Working fluid:** Atmospheric air (~ **78% Nitrogen, 21% Oxygen, 1% trace gases**).
- **Standard reference pressure:** **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 14.5 psi** (SI unit: Pascal).
- **Typical industrial supply pressure range:** **4 – 8 bar** (with **6 bar** as the KTU-recommended design value).
- **Sound velocity in air (20 °C):** **a ≈ 343 m/s** (relevant for surge analysis in long pipelines).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly identifies the *P-V-T behaviour of air* (Ideal Gas Law, $pV = mRT$) as foundational, because unlike hydraulics (incompressible), air's compressibility is the single biggest design factor.

---

## 1.3 Why "Basic" Pneumatic Systems?

The word **Basic** in the KTU module title indicates a focus on:
1. **Architecture** of a simple single-source pneumatic circuit.
2. **Component identification** and ISO 1219 symbology.
3. **Quantitative sizing** of compressor, receiver, and cylinder.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Operational Chain

A complete pneumatic circuit always executes energy transformation in this **strict causal order**:

1. **Intake Stage** — Atmospheric air is drawn in through a suction filter.
2. **Compression Stage** — Mechanical work → Pneumatic potential energy (compressor).
3. **Storage / Buffer Stage** — Pressurized air stored in a *receiver tank* (decouples supply from demand).
4. **Conditioning Stage** — Air is filtered (5–25 µm), pressure-regulated, and oil-mist lubricated.
5. **Distribution & Actuation Stage** — Air routed via DCVs to cylinder/motor; exhaust returned to atmosphere (through mufflers for noise reduction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Chain:** *Electrical/Mechanical Input → Compressor → Pneumatic Pressure Energy → Control Valves → Actuator → Mechanical Work Output + Heat (exhaust).*

## 2.2 Governing Physical Laws (The "Why" Behind the Math)

- **Ideal Gas Law (per kg of air):** $p \cdot v = R \cdot T$, where $R_{air} = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$.
- **Boyle's Law (Isothermal):** $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$ (used in receiver sizing).
- **Charles's Law (Isobaric):** $V_1 / T_1 = V_2 / T_2$ (used when cylinders heat air).
- **Compressibility (Isentropic work):** Air heats up on compression; this is why **after-coolers** are mandatory.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module-1 problems. Units shown in **square brackets** are SI.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Cylinder Thrust | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ [N], $P$ [Pa], $A$ [m²] | Sizing the cylinder for a given load |
| 2 | Effective Area (cylinder) | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left(D^2 - d^2 \right)$ (annulus) | $D$ = bore [m], $d$ = rod dia [m] | Double-acting cylinder net area |
| 3 | Air Consumption (per stroke) | $V_s = A \cdot L$ | $V_s$ [m³], $L$ = stroke [m] | Volumetric air per stroke |
| 4 | Free Air Delivery (FAD) | $Q_{FAD} = \dfrac{V_s \cdot N \cdot (P_w + 1)}{1}$ | $Q$ [m³/min], $P_w$ [bar(g)] | Compressor capacity sizing |
| 5 | Compressor Power (Isothermal) | $W_{iso} = p_1 V_1 \ln \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$ | $W$ [J] | Theoretical minimum work |
| 6 | Compressor Power (Adiabatic) | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} p_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | Realistic work input |
| 7 | Receiver Tank Volume | $V_{rec} = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{\left[ 1 - \dfrac{p_{min}}{p_{max}} \right]}$ | $t$ = time [min] | Buffer for peak demand |
| 8 | Pressure Drop in Pipe | $\Delta p = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\mu$ = viscosity, $v$ = velocity | Long airline design |
| 9 | Flow Velocity in Pipe | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{pipe}}$ | $A_{pipe} = \pi D^2 / 4$ | Detect choked flow ($v < 30$ m/s) |
| 10 | Efficiency of Compressor | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} \times 100$ | % | Performance evaluation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute vs. Gauge Pressure Trap:** KTU questions often switch between $p_{abs}$ and $p_{gauge}$. Remember: $p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$ and $p_{atm} = 1.01325$ bar ≈ **1 bar**. Compressor work equations demand **absolute** pressure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines** (welding clamps, robotic grippers) — pneumatic power for safety (fail-safe via spring-return).
- **Pharmaceutical packaging** — clean, oil-free air prevents contamination.
- **Railway braking systems** — fail-safe (air pressure holds brake open; loss of pressure applies brake).
- **Pneumatic tools** (drills, jackhammers) — high power-to-weight ratio.
- **Scuba & medical respirators** — regulated, filtered breathing air.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Produced by a Pneumatic Cylinder

**Starting from Newton's 2nd Law and the definition of Pressure:**

We know pressure is defined as force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Logical Step 1 — Rearranging for Force:**
Multiply both sides by $A$ to isolate $F$:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Logical Step 2 — Substituting the area of a circular piston (cylinder bore):**
For a single-acting cylinder, the piston area is a simple disc:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Logical Step 3 — Final Cylinder Thrust Equation:**

$$\boxed{F_{thrust} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$$

For a **double-acting cylinder**, the return stroke uses annular area (piston minus rod):

$$A_{annular} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left( D^2 - d^2 \right)$$

$$\boxed{F_{return} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\left( D^2 - d^2 \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $D = 0.1$ m, $P = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa → $F = 6 \times 10^5 \times (\pi/4)(0.1)^2$ ≈ **4712 N**. This matches typical industrial clamp forces, validating the formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Free Air Delivery (FAD) of a Compressor

**Why this matters:** A compressor delivers *pressurized* air, but the customer needs the equivalent volume at *atmospheric* (free) conditions to size the receiver.

**Step 1 — Compressed Volume per Stroke (at working pressure $P_w$ absolute):**

$$V_w = A_{cyl} \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Applying Boyle's Law to convert to free-air volume:**

$$P_{atm} \cdot V_{FAD} = P_w \cdot V_w$$

**Step 3 — Solving for Free Air Delivery per cycle:**

$$V_{FAD} = V_w \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_{atm}}$$

**Step 4 — Accounting for volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ (clearance losses):**

$$V_{FAD,actual} = \eta_v \cdot V_w \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_{atm}}$$

**Step 5 — For a multi-cylinder machine running at $N$ strokes/sec:**

$$\boxed{Q_{FAD} = \eta_v \cdot A_{cyl} \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \frac{P_w}{P_{atm}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 numericals, gauge pressure is typically given — remember to add **1 bar** to get absolute. Example: 6 bar(g) = 7 bar(a).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Work of Compression (Isothermal Reference)

**Why "minimum"?** Isothermal compression assumes perfect heat removal — gives the *theoretical lower bound*; real compressors lie between isothermal and adiabatic.

**Step 1 — Differential work done on a gas element:**

$$\delta W = p \, dV$$

**Step 2 — For isothermal process, $pV = C$ (Boyle's Law), so:**

$$p = \frac{C}{V} = \frac{p_1 V_1}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the work integral:**

$$W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} p \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{p_1 V_1}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Evaluating the integral:**

$$W_{iso} = p_1 V_1 \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = p_1 V_1 \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{W_{iso} = p_1 V_1 \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right) = p_1 V_1 \ln \left( \frac{p_2}{p_1} \right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Mapped Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical (Model Style):** *A double-acting cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm and rod diameter $d = 35$ mm. Supply pressure = 6 bar(g). Find the thrust and return forces, and the FAD if stroke $L = 200$ mm and 30 cycles/min.*

| Step | Calculation | Result |
|------|-------------|--------|
| Convert units | $D = 0.08$ m, $d = 0.035$ m, $P = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa | — |
| Thrust area | $A_1 = \pi/4 \times (0.08)^2$ | $5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m² |
| Thrust force | $F_1 = 6 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ | **3016 N** |
| Annular area | $A_2 = \pi/4 \times (0.08^2 - 0.035^2)$ | $4.065 \times 10^{-3}$ m² |
| Return force | $F_2 = 6 \times 10^5 \times 4.065 \times 10^{-3}$ | **2439 N** |
| Compressed vol/cycle | $V_w = (A_1 + A_2) \times 0.2$ | $1.819 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ |
| FAD (cycles/min) | $Q = 1.819 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 \times (7/1)$ | **0.382 m³/min** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Basic Pneumatic System

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below is a **block-level functional flow** (not a physical drawing), showing how each sub-system interacts. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters as required.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ATMOSPHERIC AIR INTAKE] --> A2[COMPRESSOR UNIT]
    A2 --> A3[AFTER COOLER]
    A3 --> A4[RECEIVER TANK]
    A4 --> A5[FILTER FRL SECTION]
    A5 --> A6[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    A6 --> A7[LUBRICATOR]
    A7 --> A8[DIRECTION CONTROL VALVE 5 BY 2]
    A8 --> A9[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    A9 --> A10[EXHAUST TO MUFFLER ATMOSPHERE]
    A8 --> A10
    A11[PRESSURE GAUGE] -. monitors .-> A6
    A12[SAFETY RELIEF VALVE] -. protects .-> A4
    A13[FLOW CONTROL VALVE] -. regulates speed .-> A9
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- **All node IDs** are alphanumeric with a letter prefix: `A1`, `A2`, `A3`, etc.
- **No reserved keywords** (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as node names.
- **Labels inside double-quotes/boxes** are plain UPPERCASE alphanumeric — no bold, italics, or HTML.
- **Dotted feedback lines** (using `-.` arrow notation) represent monitoring/protection paths.

## 4.3 Component Interaction Map (Textual Backup)

| From Component | To Component | Energy/Signal Type | Function |
|----------------|--------------|--------------------|----------|
| Atmosphere → Compressor | Suction side | Kinetic (low-pressure air) | Intake |
| Compressor → After-cooler | High-pressure hot air | Thermal reduction | Removes heat of compression |
| After-cooler → Receiver | Cooled high-pressure air | Storage | Decouples supply/demand |
| Receiver → FRL | Filtered air | Conditioning | Removes moisture & dust |
| Regulator → DCV | Set-pressure air | Control signal | Directs flow path |
| DCV → Cylinder | Directed air | Mechanical work | Pushes piston |
| Cylinder → Muffler | Exhaust air | Acoustic & kinetic | Noise reduction |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define a pneumatic system. List any four advantages of pneumatics over hydraulics.*

**Model Answer:**
A pneumatic system is a power transmission system that uses **compressed air** as the working medium to transmit energy and perform mechanical work via actuators.

**Four advantages over hydraulics:**
1. Air is freely available and exhaustible — no return line needed.
2. Fire and explosion-safe (no flammable fluid).
3. Lower cost of components and easy maintenance.
4. Lighter weight and higher speed capability.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *What is an FRL unit? State the function of each of its three sub-components.*

**Model Answer:**
**FRL** stands for **Filter-Regulator-Lubricator**, a conditioning unit placed immediately downstream of the receiver.

| Sub-unit | Function |
|----------|----------|
| **Filter** | Removes dust, dirt, and condensed moisture (typically down to 5–25 µm). |
| **Regulator** | Maintains downstream pressure at a constant set value irrespective of upstream fluctuations. |
| **Lubricator** | Introduces a fine oil mist into the air stream to lubricate moving valve parts. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat block diagram the functional architecture of a basic pneumatic system. Discuss the role of the receiver tank in detail.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A basic pneumatic system consists of the following sequential blocks:
**Compressor → After-cooler → Receiver → FRL → DCV → Cylinder → Exhaust.**

**Role of the Receiver Tank (5 key points):**

1. **Storage:** Stores compressed air to meet intermittent peak demand.
2. **Surge dampening:** Absorbs pulsations from reciprocating compressors.
3. **Cooling & moisture separation:** Allows hot compressed air to cool; condensate settles and is drained.
4. **Emergency reserve:** Provides a buffer if compressor trips/fails.
5. **Pressure stabilization:** Reduces the on/off cycling of the compressor (load-unload).

**Sizing formula (KTU standard):**

$$V_{rec} = \frac{Q \times t}{1 - \left( p_{min} / p_{max} \right)}$$

---

**(b)** *A single-acting cylinder has bore 100 mm, stroke 150 mm, and is supplied with 5 bar(g) air. The load to be lifted is 4000 N. Determine: (i) the thrust force, (ii) whether the cylinder can lift the load with a factor of safety of 1.5, and (iii) the air consumption per minute at 20 cycles/min.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.15$ m, $P = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa(g), $W = 4000$ N, $N = 20$ cpm, FOS = 1.5.

**(i) Thrust force:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F = P \times A = 5 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 3927 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Required force with FOS:**

$$F_{req} = 4000 \times 1.5 = 6000 \text{ N}$$

Since available thrust (3927 N) < required thrust (6000 N), the cylinder **CANNOT lift the load** with the given factor of safety.

**(iii) Air consumption per minute:**

Volume per stroke at working pressure: $V_w = A \times L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 = 1.178 \times 10^{-3}$ m³.

Free air delivery per stroke (using $P_{atm} = 1$ bar):

$$V_{FAD/cycle} = 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{(5+1)}{1} = 7.07 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Per minute: $Q = 7.07 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = \mathbf{0.1414 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**(a)** *Compare pneumatic and hydraulic systems on at least six parameters.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Pneumatic System | Hydraulic System |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Working fluid | Air (compressible) | Oil (incompressible) |
| Pressure range | 4–8 bar (typical) | 50–350 bar |
| Force capability | Low to medium | Very high |
| Speed | High (up to 1–2 m/s) | Lower (0.1–0.5 m/s) |
| Leakage effect | Not hazardous | Fire risk, contamination |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Temperature sensitivity | Sensitive (freezing, expansion) | Less sensitive |
| Component size | Smaller for same power | Larger |
| Return line | Not required | Required |
| Cleanliness | Self-cleaning (exhaust) | Needs filtration |

---

**(b)** *A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses 0.5 m³/min of free air from 1 bar to 9 bar. The compression follows the law $pV^{1.25} = C$. Calculate the work of compression per minute. Also find the isothermal efficiency if the actual power input is 8 kW.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_1 = 0.5$ m³/min, $p_1 = 1$ bar(a) = $10^5$ Pa, $p_2 = 9$ bar(a) = $9 \times 10^5$ Pa, $n = 1.25$.

**Step 1 — Polytropic work per minute:**

$$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} \, p_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

$$\frac{n}{n-1} = \frac{1.25}{0.25} = 5$$

$$\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{0.25}{1.25}} = (9)^{0.2} = 1.5518$$

$$W_{poly} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 0.5 \times (1.5518 - 1) = 5 \times 10^5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5518$$

$$W_{poly} = 1.3795 \times 10^5 \text{ J/min} = 2299 \text{ J/s} = \mathbf{2.30 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2 — Isothermal work per minute:**

$$W_{iso} = p_1 V_1 \ln(p_2/p_1) = 10^5 \times 0.5 \times \ln(9)$$

$$W_{iso} = 10^5 \times 0.5 \times 2.1972 = 1.0986 \times 10^5 \text{ J/min} = 1.831 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Isothermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{1.831}{8} \times 100 = \mathbf{22.9\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Eye):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure** in compressor work problems. KTU examiners *deliberately* give gauge values — 80% of students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing up thrust area vs. annular area** in double-acting cylinders — clearly state *which stroke* the question refers to.
> 3. **Omitting units** in final numerical answers. Always write $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$, $\text{N}$, $\text{kW}$ explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why" in theory:** KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) tests *understanding*, not recall. A 7-mark question demands a 3–4 line conceptual justification, not just a formula.
> 5. **Wrong polytropic exponent:** For air, **n = 1.25 to 1.35** (not 1.4 = adiabatic, not 1.0 = isothermal). Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Pneumatic Systems**

- ☐ **Working fluid:** Atmospheric air (compressible); $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$.
- ☐ **Standard supply pressure:** **6 bar (gauge)** unless specified otherwise.
- ☐ **Cylinder thrust:** $F = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ (extend); $F = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ (retract).
- ☐ **FRL sequence:** Filter → Regulator → Lubricator (downstream of receiver, upstream of valves).
- ☐ **Receiver function:** Storage, cooling, surge dampening, moisture separation.
- ☐ **FAD formula:** $Q = \eta_v \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (P_w / P_{atm})$.
- ☐ **Isothermal work:** $W_{iso} = p_1 V_1 \ln(p_2 / p_1)$ — theoretical minimum.
- ☐ **Polytropic work:** Use $n$ given in the problem; for air typically $1.25 \le n \le 1.35$.
- ☐ **Pressure conversion:** Absolute = Gauge + 1 bar (use in compressor equations).
- ☐ **Pipe velocity limit:** Keep below **30 m/s** to avoid excessive pressure drop and noise.
- ☐ **Advantages of pneumatics:** Clean, safe, cheap, lightweight, fast — but limited force and noisy.
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** Compressibility causes lag; air needs conditioning; loud exhaust.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** $pV = mRT$, FAD computation, receiver sizing, polytropic compression cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Pumps and Compressors: Gear, Lobe and Vane Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604 > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rotary Pumps and Compressors: Gear, Lobe and Vane Pumps ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Rotary Positive Displacement Machines — The Big Picture A **rotary pump** is a positive-displacement machine in which a rotating element (gear, lobe, vane, screw, or piston-roller) transfers a fixed quantity of fluid from the suction side to the delivery side for every revolution. Unlike centrifugal machines, the discharge is **theoretically independent of speed and pressure**, making rotary pumps ideal for high-viscosity fluids, low-to-moderate flow rates, and high-pressure applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *Rotary pumps are positive displacement pumps in which the displacement of fluid is caused by the rotary motion of one or more members. The fluid is mechanically displaced in a definite volume from the inlet to the outlet port during each revolution.* ### 1.2 Classification Snapshot | Family | Active Member | Typical Discharge Pressure | Best-Suited Fluid | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Gear Pump** | Two or three meshing gears | Up to **175 bar** | Clean lubricating oils, hydraulic fluid | | **Lobe Pump** | Two or three impeller lobes (non-contact) | Up to **15 bar** | Sanitary fluids, slurries, food & pharma | | **Vane Pump** | Sliding vanes in eccentric cam ring | Up to **210 bar** | Medium-viscosity oils, refrigerants | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade" Imagine a circular conveyor belt of small buckets travelling around a closed loop. As each bucket dips into a lower reservoir (suction), it scoops up water. By the time the bucket reaches the upper reservoir (delivery), it tips and empties. The buckets never change size — so the *total volume delivered per revolution is constant*, no matter how high the upper reservoir is. A **gear pump** works the same way: the spaces *between* gear teeth act as the buckets. A **vane pump** uses sliding partitions (vanes) sliding in and out of a rotor slot. A **lobe pump** uses impeller lobes that mesh but **do not actually touch** — instead of teeth physically carrying fluid, fluid is "pushed" between two rotating lobes as in a peristaltic squeeze. > [!NOTE] > **Why Rotary?** Rotary machines eliminate the inertia losses of reciprocating pistons, produce smoother flow (less pulsation), and are compact for the same displacement. ### 1.4 The Three Families at a Glance **(a) Gear Pump** Two identical involute gears rotate in opposite directions inside a closely-fitted casing. One is the *driver* (connected to the prime mover), the other is the *driven*. Fluid trapped between the gear teeth and the casing wall is carried from the suction port to the delivery port, where it is expelled as the teeth mesh. **(b) Lobe Pump** Two figure-8 shaped rotors (lobes) rotate in opposite directions inside a casing. Unlike gears, the lobes **do not touch each other** — a small clearance exists. Fluid is carried between the lobe profile and casing wall, displaced by the squeezing action at the delivery side. They are *non-contacting*, so they are favored for hygienic and abrasive service. **(c) Vane Pump** A slotted rotor mounted off-centre inside a circular cam ring. Rectangular vanes slide radially outward (held by centrifugal force, springs, or hydraulic pressure) to maintain contact with the cam ring. The crescent-shaped space between rotor and cam ring *expands at the suction side* (drawing fluid in) and *contracts at the delivery side* (forcing fluid out). ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 External Gear Pump — Operating Principle in Seven Steps 1. **Casing Geometry:** A pair of identical involute spur gears sits inside a precision-bored housing with minimum radial clearance. 2. **Meshing Zone:** Where the gears mesh, the teeth interlock and effectively "squeeze out" any fluid between them — this defines the **high-pressure discharge side**. 3. **Suction Side:** On the side where the teeth are *un-meshing*, a partial vacuum forms, drawing fluid in through the suction port. 4. **Carriage Phase:** Fluid trapped between adjacent teeth and the casing wall is conveyed from suction to delivery as the gears rotate. 5. **Discharge:** As teeth begin to mesh again, the trapped volume is reduced to zero (or to the *dedendum* clearance volume) and fluid is forced into the delivery line. 6. **Sealing:** Tight gear-to-casing and tooth-to-tooth contact minimises internal leakage (called *slip*). 7. **Shaft Seals:** External shaft seals (mechanical seals, lip seals, or O-rings) prevent atmospheric leakage along the drive shafts. > [!NOTE] > **Why involute teeth?** The involute profile ensures *constant velocity ratio* between meshing gears — the contact point slides along a straight line of action, giving smooth fluid delivery with minimal vibration. ### 2.2 VPI — Volume Per Indication (Per Revolution) For an external gear pump, the displacement per revolution equals the **volume of fluid carried in the tooth spaces of one gear from suction to delivery**: $$ V_{PI} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, W}{4}\,\bigl(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}\bigr) $$ Where: - $W$ = face width (axial length) of the gears, **m** - $D_o$ = outer (addendum) diameter of gear, **m** - $D_i$ = inner (dedendum / root) diameter of gear, **m** > The same formula applies to *each* gear. Two gears carry two such volumes, but the meshing region is shared, so the **net displacement per revolution** equals the volume of one gear's tooth space: $$ V_{D} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, W}{2}\,\bigl(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}\bigr) \quad \text{(some textbooks — check convention)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Most KTU-accepted form (Sachdeva / Modi & Seth):** > $$ > V_{D} \;=\; \pi \, W \,(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}) \;\;\; \text{(displacement per revolution, single gear)} > $$ > Multiply by $N$ for theoretical discharge. Always verify which gear-pair convention your textbook adopts. ### 2.3 Theoretical Discharge, Slip & Volumetric Efficiency $$ Q_{th} \;=\; V_{D}\,\cdot\,N $$ $$ \eta_{vol} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} $$ $$ Q_{slip} \;=\; Q_{th} - Q_{actual} \quad \text{(leakage from delivery to suction)} $$ Slip depends on: - **Pressure differential** $\Delta P$ across the pump - **Fluid viscosity** (high viscosity reduces slip) - **Clearances** between gears, shaft seals, and end plates ### 2.4 Lobe Pump — Theoretical Analysis Because lobes do not mesh, the *effective* displacement equals the **annular volume swept between the lobe tip and casing wall per revolution**: $$ V_{D,\text{lobe}} \;\approx\; 2\,\pi\,W\,R\,e \quad \text{(when } e \ll R \text{)} $$ where $R$ = casing (lobe-tip) radius and $e$ = eccentricity between lobe centre and casing centre. ### 2.5 Vane Pump — Theoretical Analysis A vane pump's displacement per revolution is the **crescent-shaped volume swept between the cam-ring inner surface and rotor surface**: $$ V_{D,\text{vane}} \;=\; 2\,\pi\,W\,(R + r)\,e $$ Where: - $W$ = width of vane (axial length of rotor), **m** - $R$ = inner radius of cam ring, **m** - $r$ = radius of rotor, **m** - $e$ = eccentricity between cam-ring centre and rotor centre, **m** Equivalently, since $e = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ where $D$ = cam-ring inner diameter and $d$ = rotor diameter: $$ V_{D,\text{vane}} \;=\; \pi\,W\,e\,(D + d) $$ ### 2.6 Power and Efficiency Chain For a rotary pump/compressor: $$ P_{fluid} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{actual}\,\Delta P}{1000} \quad \text{kW} \quad (\Delta P \text{ in kPa}) $$ $$ P_{shaft} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\eta_{mech}} $$ $$ \eta_{overall} \;=\; \eta_{vol}\,\cdot\,\eta_{mech} $$ ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | VPI — External Gear Pump | $V_D = \pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ | | 2 | VPI — Vane Pump | $V_D = 2\pi W (R+r)\,e$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ | | 3 | VPI — Vane Pump (alt.) | $V_D = \pi W e (D+d)$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ | | 4 | VPI — Lobe Pump | $V_D \approx 2\pi W R e$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ | | 5 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ | | 6 | Slip | $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{actual}$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ | | 7 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = Q_{actual}/Q_{th}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = P_{fluid}/P_{shaft}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{o} = \eta_{vol}\,\eta_{mech}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Fluid Power | $P_{fluid} = Q_{actual}\,\Delta P$ | **W** | | 11 | Slip Coefficient | $C_s = Q_s/Q_{th}$ | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Gear pumps dominate **hydraulic power packs** (machine tools, injection moulding, mobile hydraulics); vane pumps are extensively used in **aircraft hydraulic systems** and **automotive power-steering**; lobe pumps are the workhorse of the **dairy, brewing, and pharmaceutical industries** because they can be cleaned-in-place (CIP). ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — VPI of an External Gear Pump We will rigorously derive $V_D = \pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$. #### Step 1: Define the Geometry Consider one gear with: - Outer (addendum) radius: $R_o$ - Inner (dedendum / root) radius: $R_i$ - Axial face width: $W$ The gear has $Z$ teeth of involute profile. Between any two adjacent teeth, the *available space* to carry fluid is the volume swept by the *trapezoidal tooth-space* as the gear rotates. #### Step 2: Approximation to an Annulus For an involute gear, the *average* tooth-space area is approximately equal to the **difference between the outer-circle area and the inner-circle area, divided by 2** (because at any instant only *half* of the gear periphery is engaged in suction–delivery transfer — the other half is in the meshing zone where fluid is squeezed out): $$ A_{tooth\,\text{space,avg}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\pi R_o^{2} - \pi R_i^{2}}{2} $$ > **Why divide by 2?** At the meshing zone, the tooth spaces vanish to zero (the teeth interlock and squeeze out the fluid). So the *carrying* tooth space is approximately half the total annular area. #### Step 3: Multiply by Face Width $W$ $$ V_{carried\,\text{per\,\,half-rev}} \;=\; A_{tooth\,\text{space,avg}} \cdot W \;=\; \dfrac{\pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}{2} $$ #### Step 4: Count Per Revolution In *one full revolution*, each tooth space makes exactly one trip from suction to delivery. There are $Z$ tooth spaces, but each tooth space carries its volume only **once per revolution**. The total volume carried by *one gear* per revolution equals: $$ V_{D,\text{one gear}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})}{2} \cdot 2 \;=\; \pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}) $$ #### Step 5: Account for Both Gears For the *complete gear pair*, the meshing action at the centre squeezes out the fluid that was carried by *both* gears. The **net pump displacement per revolution** is therefore: $$ \boxed{\,V_D \;=\; \pi\,W\,(R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})\,} $$ This is exactly the volume carried by **one gear** per revolution. #### Step 6: Theoretical Discharge With $N$ revolutions per second: $$ Q_{th} \;=\; V_D \cdot N \;=\; \pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})\, N $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — VPI of a Vane Pump We will derive $V_D = 2\pi W (R + r)\,e$ rigorously. #### Step 1: Set Up the Geometry - Cam ring: circular inner surface, radius $R$, centred at $O_c$. - Rotor: circular body, radius $r$, centred at $O_r$, displaced by eccentricity $e$ from $O_c$. - Vanes of negligible thickness slide radially in rotor slots. - Width of assembly: $W$ (along the axis). #### Step 2: Area Enclosed Between Cam Ring and Rotor (at any instant) The area $A(\theta)$ of the crescent between the cam-ring inner surface and the rotor outer surface, integrated across one full rotation, gives the swept volume per revolution: $$ V_D \;=\; W \int_{0}^{2\pi} \bigl[R - r(\theta)\bigr]\,r(\theta)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta $$ For *thin, frictionless vanes* with $e$ small compared to $R$ and $r$, the integral simplifies dramatically. #### Step 3: Idealised Crescent Area per Revolution A cleaner geometric argument: imagine the rotor centre tracing a circle of radius $e$ about the cam-ring centre. The total *area swept by the rotor surface* per revolution is the **circumference of the rotor's eccentric path times the rotor diameter**, multiplied by the fraction covered by vanes. For a two-vane pump: $$ V_{D,\text{per\,vane}} \;=\; \pi W (R + r)\,e $$ For a pump with $n$ vanes, each vane sweeps the same crescent, so: $$ V_{D,\text{total}} \;=\; \dfrac{n}{2}\,\cdot\,2\pi W(R+r)\,e \quad \text{(accounting for symmetry)} $$ > **Engineering shortcut:** In standard textbook treatment, the displacement per revolution of a vane pump is taken as: $$ \boxed{\,V_D \;=\; 2\pi\,W\,(R + r)\,e\,} $$ regardless of vane number, because adding more vanes only *reduces pulsation*, not the displacement. #### Step 4: Express in Terms of Diameters Since $e = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ and $R + r = \dfrac{D + d}{2}$: $$ V_D \;=\; 2\pi\,W \cdot \dfrac{D + d}{2} \cdot \dfrac{D - d}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi W}{4}\,(D^{2} - d^{2}) $$ So the displacement can also be written compactly as: $$ V_D \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,W}{4}\,(D^{2} - d^{2}) $$ #### Step 5: Theoretical Discharge With rotational speed $N$ (rev/s): $$ Q_{th} \;=\; V_D \cdot N \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,W\,N}{4}\,(D^{2} - d^{2}) $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Slip and Volumetric Efficiency #### Step 1: Definitions - $Q_{th}$ = theoretical discharge (no leakage) - $Q_{actual}$ = discharge measured in pipeline - $Q_s$ = slip (leakage back from delivery to suction) By continuity: $$ Q_{th} \;=\; Q_{actual} + Q_s $$ #### Step 2: Solve for Slip $$ Q_s \;=\; Q_{th} - Q_{actual} $$ #### Step 3: Volumetric Efficiency $$ \eta_{vol} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_{th}} \;=\; 1 - C_s $$ where $C_s$ is the *slip coefficient*: $$ C_s \;=\; \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_{th}} $$ #### Step 4: Empirical Slip Correlation (Modi & Seth) For rotary pumps with oil as the working fluid, slip is approximately inversely proportional to fluid viscosity and proportional to pressure differential: $$ Q_s \;\propto\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu} $$ > **High-viscosity fluids ⇒ less slip ⇒ higher volumetric efficiency.** This is why gear pumps are *excellent* for heavy oils and *poor* for water or low-viscosity solvents. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > *Mermaid is used to render high-fidelity functional architecture and flow diagrams. Physical geometry is represented through labelled block schematics to convey component relationships, pressure zones, and flow paths with KTU-board clarity.* ### 4.1 External Gear Pump — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PRIME MOVER
Electric Motor] -->|Torque| B[DRIVE SHAFT] B --> C[DRIVER GEAR
Teeth = Z, R_o, R_i] C -->|Meshing| D[DRIVEN GEAR
Identical Profile] D --> E[IDLER SHAFT] C --> F[CASING HOUSING
Precision Bore] D --> F F --> G[SUCTION PORT
Low Pressure Side] F --> H[DELIVERY PORT
High Pressure Side] F --> I[SHAFT SEALS
Mechanical Lip Seal] F --> J[RELIEF VALVE
Safety Bypass] classDef mech fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000; classDef port fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000; classDef safe fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000; class A,B,C,D,E,F mech class G,H port class I,J safe ``` ### 4.2 Vane Pump — Component & Flow Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VANE_PUMP_UNIT A[DRIVE SHAFT] --> B[SLOTTED ROTOR
Radius = r] B --> C[VANES
Sliding Radial] C --> D[CAM RING
Inner Radius = R] B -.Eccentric Offset e.-> D D --> E[SUCTION SIDE
Expanding Volume] D --> F[DELIVERY SIDE
Contracting Volume] B --> G[BALANCING SLOTS
Reduce Side Load] F --> H[DELIVERY PORT] E --> I[SUCTION PORT] end classDef rot fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000; classDef vane fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,color:#000; classDef ring fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366,color:#000; classDef port fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000; class A,B,G rot class C vane class D ring class E,F,H,I port ``` ### 4.3 Lobe Pump — Working Cycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INFLOW
Suction Side] --> B[CAVITY EXPANDS
As Lobes Rotate Apart] B --> C[FLUID CARRIED
Around Casing Wall] C --> D[SQUEEZE ZONE
Lobes Approach Delivery] D --> E[OUTFLOW
Delivery Side] E --> F[CLEARANCE LEAK
Back to Suction] classDef flow fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000; classDef mech fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000; class A,B,C,E flow class D,F mech ``` ### 4.4 Pressure vs. Volume in a Vane Pump — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SV1[Zone I: 0 deg] A1[Min Volume = V_min
Volume = 0] end subgraph SV2[Zone II: 0 to 90 deg] A2[Volume INCREASES
Linear-ish Rise] end subgraph SV3[Zone III: 90 to 180 deg] A3[Volume MAX at 90 deg
Then DECREASES] end subgraph SV4[Zone IV: 180 to 360 deg] A4[Volume DECREASES
Discharge] end SV1 --> SV2 --> SV3 --> SV4 classDef min fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000; classDef rise fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000; classDef max fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,color:#000; classDef dec fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366,color:#000; class A1 min class A2 rise class A3 max class A4 dec ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *List any four differences between a centrifugal pump and a rotary (positive-displacement) pump.* **Model Answer (Key Points):** | S.No. | Centrifugal Pump | Rotary (PD) Pump | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Non-positive displacement; discharge falls with rising head | Positive displacement; discharge nearly constant with head | | 2 | Cannot handle high-viscosity fluids efficiently | Suited to high-viscosity oils, resins, syrups | | 3 | Operates at high rpm (1450–2900 rpm) | Operates at low–moderate rpm (500–1500 rpm) | | 4 | Flow has radial/axial velocity components; needs priming | Self-priming in many designs | | 5 | Suited to high flow, low–medium head | Suited to low–medium flow, high head | *(3 marks awarded for any 4 valid points with crisp phrasing.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *What is the function of balancing slots/holes in a vane pump? Why are they essential?** **Model Answer:** Balancing slots are small grooves cut in the side plates (port plates) of a vane pump that connect the high-pressure discharge region to the low-pressure region behind each vane slot. **Functions:** 1. **Hydraulic balance:** Equalises pressure on both ends of the rotor, eliminating the net side-thrust on the rotor shaft. 2. **Vane loading:** Forces vanes outward with a *uniform* pressure differential along their length, reducing wear and noise. 3. **Improved volumetric efficiency:** Reduces internal leakage through the vane-tip clearance. Without balancing slots, the rotor would experience an unbalanced axial/radial thrust, causing rapid wear of the cam ring, shaft seizure, and a fall in mechanical efficiency. *(3 marks)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** *(a)* Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of an external gear pump. Label the suction port, delivery port, driver gear, driven gear, and meshing zone. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A double-external gear pump has the following specifications: - Outer diameter of each gear: $D_o = 80 \text{ mm}$ - Inner (root) diameter of each gear: $D_i = 50 \text{ mm}$ - Axial face width: $W = 40 \text{ mm}$ - Rotational speed: $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$ - Actual discharge: $Q_{act} = 12 \text{ L/min}$ Calculate: 1. Theoretical discharge $Q_{th}$ in L/min 2. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$ 3. Slip $Q_s$ in L/min *(7 marks)* --- **Solution to Q.A:** **(a) Working Principle [7 marks]:** Provide the following explanation, supported by the block diagram in Section 4.1. The external gear pump consists of two identical involute spur gears housed in a closely fitted casing. One gear (driver) is connected to the prime mover via the drive shaft; the other (driven) meshes with the driver and rotates in the opposite direction. - The **suction port** is located on the side where the teeth are *un-meshing* (moving apart), creating a partial vacuum that draws fluid in through the suction line. - As the gears rotate, the fluid is trapped in the spaces between adjacent teeth and the casing wall, and carried around the periphery from the suction side to the delivery side. - At the **meshing zone** (top of the casing in standard orientation), the teeth of the two gears interlock, reducing the trapped volume to near-zero and expelling the fluid out through the **delivery port**. - Shaft seals prevent external leakage along the drive shafts. - An internal relief valve protects the pump from over-pressure conditions.
