The volume of fluid delivered per revolution is constant — determined by the gear geometry — making this a true *positive-displacement* machine. --- **(b) Numerical [7 marks]:** **Given:** - $D_o = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$, hence $R_o = 0.040 \text{ m}$ - $D_i = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$, hence $R_i = 0.025 \text{ m}$ - $W = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$ - $N = 1450 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{1450}{60} = 24.1667 \text{ rev/s}$ - $Q_{act} = 12 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$ **Step 1 — Displacement per revolution:** $$ V_D \;=\; \pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2}) \;=\; \pi \cdot 0.040 \cdot (0.040^{2} - 0.025^{2}) $$ Compute each term: $$ R_o^{2} \;=\; 0.040^{2} \;=\; 0.001600 \text{ m}^{2} $$ $$ R_i^{2} \;=\; 0.025^{2} \;=\; 0.000625 \text{ m}^{2} $$ $$ R_o^{2} - R_i^{2} \;=\; 0.001600 - 0.000625 \;=\; 0.000975 \text{ m}^{2} $$ $$ V_D \;=\; \pi \cdot 0.040 \cdot 0.000975 \;=\; \pi \cdot 3.9 \times 10^{-5} \;\approx\; 1.2252 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:** $$ Q_{th} \;=\; V_D \cdot N \;=\; 1.2252 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 24.1667 \;\approx\; 2.9610 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ Convert to L/min: $$ Q_{th} \;=\; 2.9610 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 60 \cdot 1000 \;=\; 177.66 \text{ L/min} $$ **Step 3 — Volumetric efficiency:** $$ \eta_{vol} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{12}{177.66} \;\approx\; 0.0676 \;\approx\; 6.76\% $$ > ⚠️ **Wait — this efficiency is unusually low.** A real gear pump at 1450 rpm typically has $\eta_{vol}$ between 70–90 %. The low value suggests either the data is illustrative or the gears are severely worn. In a *real exam*, double-check whether the question specifies an *internal* gear pump (where the formula differs) or whether slip is to be computed differently. **Step 4 — Slip:** $$ Q_s \;=\; Q_{th} - Q_{act} \;=\; 177.66 - 12 \;=\; 165.66 \text{ L/min} $$ (Equivalent to $2.761 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$.) **Final Answers:** - $Q_{th} \approx 177.66 \text{ L/min}$ - $\eta_{vol} \approx 6.76 \%$ *(suspect data — discuss with examiner if real exam)* - $Q_s \approx 165.66 \text{ L/min}$ --- #### **Question B — (14 Marks)** [INTERNAL CHOICE] **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** *(a)* Describe with a sketch the construction and working of a sliding-vane rotary pump. Explain the role of cam-ring eccentricity and balancing slots. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A sliding-vane pump has the following data: - Cam-ring inner diameter: $D = 120 \text{ mm}$ - Rotor diameter: $d = 100 \text{ mm}$ - Vane width (axial): $W = 50 \text{ mm}$ - Speed: $N = 950 \text{ rpm}$ - Pressure rise across pump: $\Delta P = 6 \text{ bar}$ Calculate: 1. Displacement per revolution $V_D$ in litres 2. Theoretical power required $P_{fluid}$ in kW *(7 marks)* --- **Solution to Q.B:** **(a) Construction & Working [7 marks]:** **Construction (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):** A sliding-vane pump has a cylindrical rotor of diameter $d$ mounted **off-centre** inside a circular cam ring of inner diameter $D$. The rotor carries a series of radial slots, each holding a rectangular vane that can slide in or out. The vanes are held against the cam-ring inner surface by: - Centrifugal force (rotational), and - Spring pressure or hydraulic fluid behind the vane (at low speeds). **Working:** - As the rotor spins, the space *between* the rotor and cam ring varies — at the suction side, it expands, drawing fluid in through the suction port; at the delivery side, it contracts, expelling fluid through the delivery port. - The vanes slide radially in and out to maintain contact with the cam-ring inner surface at all times. **Role of Cam-Ring Eccentricity ($e$):** - $e = (D - d)/2$ defines the *displacement*. A larger eccentricity → larger swept volume per revolution. - In a *fixed-displacement* pump, $e$ is set by the manufacturer. In a *variable-displacement* pump, $e$ is adjusted (by shifting the cam ring) to vary flow rate. **Role of Balancing Slots:** - Without balancing slots, the high delivery-side pressure acts on the rotor end faces, creating a huge side-thrust on the shaft. - Balancing slots connect the high-pressure region of the port plate to the *low-pressure* region behind each vane tip, equalising pressure across the rotor's side faces. - This eliminates side-thrust, reduces wear, and improves mechanical efficiency. --- **(b) Numerical [7 marks]:** **Given:** - $D = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.120 \text{ m}$ - $d = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$ - $W = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$ - $N = 950 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{950}{60} = 15.8333 \text{ rev/s}$ - $\Delta P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$ **Step 1 — Eccentricity:** $$ e \;=\; \dfrac{D - d}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.120 - 0.100}{2} \;=\; 0.010 \text{ m} \;=\; 10 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Displacement per revolution:** Using $V_D = 2\pi W (R + r)\, e$: $$ R + r \;=\; \dfrac{D + d}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.120 + 0.100}{2} \;=\; 0.110 \text{ m} $$ $$ V_D \;=\; 2\pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 0.110 \cdot 0.010 \;=\; 2\pi \cdot 5.5 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ V_D \;\approx\; 3.4558 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} \;=\; 0.3456 \text{ litres} $$ **Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:** $$ Q_{th} \;=\; V_D \cdot N \;=\; 3.4558 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 15.8333 \;\approx\; 5.4717 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Theoretical fluid power:** $$ P_{fluid} \;=\; Q_{th} \cdot \Delta P \;=\; 5.4717 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 6 \times 10^{5} $$ $$ P_{fluid} \;=\; 3283.0 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 3.283 \text{ kW} $$ **Final Answers:** - $V_D \approx 0.3456 \text{ L per revolution}$ - $P_{fluid} \approx 3.283 \text{ kW}$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s, or $\Delta P$ from bar to Pa. *Always* show the conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Formula ambiguity:** Gear pump $V_D$ is sometimes given as $\pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$ and sometimes as $\dfrac{\pi W}{2}(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2})$. Match the formula to the *convention used in your textbook* (Modi & Seth / Sachdeva). > 3. **Sign of slip:** Slip is always *positive*; students occasionally write $Q_{act} - Q_{th}$ which gives a negative number — wrong. > 4. **Vane pump — wrong radius usage:** Students sometimes use only $R$ (cam-ring radius) and forget the $+r$ term. The correct swept area depends on **both** radii. > 5. **Not drawing the figure:** Even for a "derivation" question, a quick labelled sketch (suction, delivery, meshing zone, vanes/lobes) is worth 1–2 marks easily. > 6. **Ignoring volumetric efficiency:** When the question gives $Q_{act}$, don't calculate $Q_{th}$ and stop — finish by computing $\eta_{vol}$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ Rotary pumps are **positive-displacement** machines; discharge is *theoretically* independent of pressure but *practically* falls slightly due to slip. - ✅ **External gear pump** VPI: $V_D = \pi W (R_o^{2} - R_i^{2})$. Tooth profile is **involute** for smooth meshing. - ✅ **Lobe pump** uses non-contacting rotors — suited for sanitary/abrasive service. Lobe pumps *cannot* run dry for long because they lack the close clearances of gear pumps. - ✅ **Vane pump** VPI: $V_D = 2\pi W (R + r)\, e = \dfrac{\pi W}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$. - ✅ **Eccentricity** $e$ is the heart of a vane pump — varying $e$ gives *variable displacement*. - ✅ **Balancing slots** in vane pumps eliminate side-thrust on the rotor shaft. - ✅ **Slip** = $Q_{th} - Q_{actual}$. Slip increases with pressure differential and decreases with fluid viscosity. - ✅ **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol} = 1 - C_s$. Gear pumps reach 85–95 %; vane pumps 80–95 %. - ✅ **Self-priming** capability is a hallmark of rotary PD pumps. - ✅ **Vane pumps** must NEVER run dry — vanes depend on fluid film (or pumped fluid) for lubrication and sealing. - ✅ **Gear pumps** produce slight pulsation at low vane numbers; using a *helical* or *herringbone* gear reduces noise. - ✅ **Power** $P = Q \cdot \Delta P$. Theoretical power assumes 100 % efficiency; real shaft power = theoretical power / $\eta_{mech}$. - ✅ Common KTU exam trap: confusing **gear pump VPI** with **vane pump VPI**. Memorise the radius pairings! - ✅ For 14-mark questions, *always* present: (1) labelled diagram, (2) working principle, (3) derivation or numerical, (4) efficiency calculation if data given.

topics.title: Screw, Lobe and Vane Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screw, Lobe and Vane Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Positive Displacement Rotary Compressors

Rotary compressors belong to the **positive displacement** class of machines, in which a fixed mass of gas is mechanically trapped and then compressed by reducing the volume of the trapped chamber. Unlike dynamic compressors (centrifugal, axial), where compression is achieved by imparting kinetic energy, rotary positive displacement machines confine the gas between moving and stationary members and physically squeeze it.

The three important rotary positive displacement compressors studied in this module are:
1. **Screw Compressor (Twin Screw / Rotary Screw)**
2. **Lobe Compressor (Roots Blower Type)**
3. **Vane Compressor (Sliding Vane Type)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** All three machines fall under the *rotary positive displacement* family. The examiner frequently tests the *mechanism of compression* and the *reason for absence of internal compression* in lobe compressors. Be ready with comparative tables.

---

## 1.2 Screw Compressor — Formal Definition

A **screw compressor** is a rotary positive displacement machine that compresses gas using the intermeshing of two helical rotors (a male rotor with convex lobes and a female rotor with concave grooves) housed inside a casing. The trapped volume between the rotors and casing progressively decreases as the rotors rotate, thereby raising the gas pressure.

- **Male rotor:** typically has **4 lobes** (4 tooth rotor).
- **Female rotor:** typically has **6 grooves** (6 tooth rotor).
- Standard L/D ratio (length to diameter) lies between **1.0 and 2.0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Two Squeeze Rollers:** Imagine two interlocking helical screw rods placed side by side inside a sealed tube. As the screws rotate together (one driven, one idler), the gas trapped in the grooves between the screw threads gets pushed forward, and at the same time the groove volume shrinks because the male lobe slides into the female groove. This combination of axial translation and volume reduction is exactly what compresses the gas. No valves are required because the meshing rotors themselves act as the suction and discharge valves.

---

## 1.3 Lobe Compressor — Formal Definition

A **lobe compressor** (commonly known as a **Roots blower** when operating at low pressure ratios) consists of two figure-of-eight shaped impellers rotating in opposite directions inside a casing. Gas is *trapped between the lobes and the casing*, carried around the periphery from the suction port to the discharge port, and then pushed out.

- **Critical feature:** There is **NO internal compression**. The gas is merely transferred from inlet to outlet at nearly constant volume.
- Compression occurs in the **external discharge pipe/back volume**, not inside the machine.
- Pressure ratio per stage is **typically limited to 1.5 – 2.0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Two Wringing Hands:** Picture two figure-8 shaped paddles rotating inside a sealed drum, like two hands wringing water from a cloth. The paddles trap the gas, carry it around the back of the casing, and shove it out the discharge side. The gas is *not squeezed* inside — only pushed. That is why lobe compressors are excellent **blowers and gas movers**, but poor high-pressure compressors.

---

## 1.4 Vane Compressor — Formal Definition

A **vane compressor** is a rotary positive displacement machine with an *eccentric rotor* mounted inside a cylindrical stator. Multiple spring-loaded (or centrifugal-force-loaded) **sliding vanes** slide radially in and out of slots cut in the rotor. As the rotor turns, the gas trapped between two consecutive vanes and the casing wall is progressively compressed as the eccentricity reduces the chamber volume.

- **Number of vanes:** typically **4 to 20**.
- **Rotor offset (eccentricity):** a fraction of the rotor diameter.
- Vanes are usually made of **carbon steel, phenolic resin, or Peek polymer** for low-friction sliding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Wedge Chamber:** Imagine a wheel mounted off-center inside a round drum. The gap between the wheel and the drum wall is widest at one side (suction zone) and narrowest at the opposite side (discharge zone). With thin metal plates sticking out from the wheel like window shutters, every two adjacent shutters create a small pocket. As the wheel turns, each pocket grows (suction), then shrinks (compression), then empties (discharge). That is exactly how a sliding-vane compressor works.

---

## 1.5 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Screw | Lobe | Vane |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression mechanism | Internal (volume reduction) | External (no internal compression) | Internal (eccentricity reduction) |
| Pressure ratio per stage | Up to **13:1** (oil-free) | Typically **1.5 – 2.0** | Up to **6:1** (oil-flooded) |
| Pulsation in discharge | Very low | High | Moderate |
| Oil injection | Optional (oil-free / oil-flooded) | Usually oil-free | Usually oil-flooded |
| Typical applications | Refrigeration, gas pipelines, CNG | Sewage aeration, pneumatic conveying, gas boosting | Industrial air compressors, truck brake systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants to Memorize:**
> - Atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/mol·K**
> - Specific heat ratio for air: **γ = 1.4** (assumed unless otherwise stated in KTU problems)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Screw Compressor — Operating Principle in Detail

The screw compressor goes through four distinct phases during one rotor revolution:

1. **Suction Phase:** The space between the male lobe and female groove is at maximum volume. Gas enters axially through the suction port at **suction pressure P₁**.
2. **Transfer Phase:** As the rotors mesh, the trapped gas moves axially from the suction end toward the discharge end.
3. **Compression Phase:** The male lobe progressively enters the female groove, reducing the trapped volume. Pressure rises from P₁ to discharge pressure **P₂**.
4. **Discharge Phase:** When the trapped volume equals the *discharge port volume*, the compressed gas is expelled through the discharge port at **P₂**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Built-in Volume Ratio (Vᵢ):** This is the most important parameter of a screw compressor and is the ratio of the suction volume (V₁) to the discharge volume (V₂) of the trapped chamber.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_i = \frac{V_1}{V_2}
\end{aligned}$$

If the actual discharge pressure ratio equals the *built-in* pressure ratio (which is set by the geometry), the discharge process occurs **without throttling loss** (no over-compression or under-compression). This is called **"matched pressure ratio."**

- **Under-compression:** If the actual discharge pressure is *higher* than the built-in ratio → gas re-expands before discharge (loss).
- **Over-compression:** If actual P₂/P₁ is *lower* than the built-in ratio → internal compression continues beyond the port, wasting work.

---

## 2.2 Lobe Compressor — Operating Principle in Detail

The two rotors do not touch each other or the casing directly. They are synchronized through a pair of **timing gears** external to the compression chamber.

1. **Suction:** As lobes unmesh at the suction port, a void is created and gas rushes in.
2. **Transfer:** The trapped gas is carried around the periphery of the casing.
3. **Discharge:** At the discharge port, the meshing lobes push the gas out.

Because the lobe tip traces a path very close to the casing but does **not** reduce the trapped volume, no internal compression occurs. The gas is discharged at **approximately the same pressure as the inlet**, and the system pressure is established externally.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often wrongly assume lobe compressors provide compression. The correct statement is: *"A lobe compressor (Roots blower) is essentially a gas pump — it has no internal compression; the compression takes place in the external discharge system."*

---

## 2.3 Vane Compressor — Operating Principle in Detail

For a vane compressor with rotor radius **r**, stator bore radius **R**, and eccentricity **e = R − r**:

- **Volume trapped between two vanes (θ = angle between them):**
$$\begin{aligned}
V(\theta) = \left[\pi (R^2 - r^2) - 2e(R-r)\sin\theta\right] \cdot \frac{L}{\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

- **Maximum volume** occurs at θ = 180° (between intake port and discharge port on the wide side).
- **Minimum volume** occurs at θ = 0° (closest to discharge port).

The compression process follows **polytropic law** with index **n** (typically 1.25–1.35 for oil-flooded vane compressors, or n = γ = 1.4 for adiabatic, oil-free).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** All numerical problems in KTU ESE for these compressors are solved using these formulas. Memorize the units column.

| # | Formula Description | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Built-in volume ratio of screw compressor | $V_i = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Built-in pressure ratio (matched case) | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = V_i^{\,n}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Polytropic pressure–volume relation | $P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}$ | varies |
| 4 | Indicated work per cycle (PD compressor) | $W_i = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | J |
| 5 | Indicated power (multi-cylinder / multiple cycles per sec) | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_s \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | W |
| 6 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 V_s \, N}{60 \, R \, T_1}$ | kg/s |
| 7 | Volumetric efficiency of screw compressor | $\eta_v = 1 - c \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Slip coefficient (lobe compressor) | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Power input for lobe compressor | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot (P_2 - P_1)}{\rho \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | W |
| 10 | Vane compressor trapped volume (approx.) | $V(\theta) \approx \pi \left(R^2 - r^2\right) \dfrac{L}{2\pi} - e(R-r)L \sin\theta$ | m³ |
| 11 | Theoretical displacement (vane) | $V_d = \pi \left(R^2 - r^2\right) L - e \cdot t \cdot L \cdot z$ | m³/rev |
| 12 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{IP}}{\text{BP}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Screw compressors dominate the **oil-free compressed air market** (pharmaceutical, food processing, electronics). Lobe compressors are the *workhorses of sewage treatment plants* for aeration. Vane compressors are still widely used in **automotive air brake systems** and **A/C compressors** of older vehicles.

---

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Performance

### Screw Compressor
- **Rotor profile geometry** (asymmetric vs symmetric): Asymmetric profiles give better sealing and higher efficiency.
- **Leakage paths:** Across the blow-hole (lobe tip to casing gap), between rotor flanks, and through the discharge port during the blowdown period.
- **Oil injection (oil-flooded type):** Acts as sealant, coolant, and lubricant — improves volumetric efficiency to > 95%.

### Lobe Compressor
- **Clearances** between lobe tip and casing — smaller clearance reduces back-leakage but increases sensitivity to thermal expansion.
- **Rotational speed** — higher speeds cause pulsation and noise.
- **Multi-staging** is necessary for pressure ratios > 2.

### Vane Compressor
- **Vane wear** is the primary lifetime limiter.
- **Oil flooding** is essential to seal the vane tips and dissipate heat.
- **Eccentricity ratio (e/R)** controls the displacement per revolution.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Indicated Work of a Screw Compressor (Polytropic Process)

**Statement:** The work done per unit mass during polytropic compression from state 1 (P₁, V₁) to state 2 (P₂, V₂) inside the rotor meshing chamber.

**Assumptions:**
1. Gas behaves ideally.
2. Compression follows the polytropic law: $P V^n = \text{constant}$.
3. No internal leakages (ideal case).
4. Compression and re-expansion are considered separately.

---

### Step 1 — Write the Polytropic Law

The compression process inside the trapped chamber obeys:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** This is the defining equation of a polytropic process. The exponent $n$ lies between 1 (isothermal) and $\gamma = 1.4$ (adiabatic). For oil-flooded screw compressors, $n \approx 1.3$ is typical; for oil-free and highly cooled machines, $n \approx 1.25$.

---

### Step 2 — Express Work Done for a Closed-System Compression

The work done on the gas during compression (positive when done *on* the gas) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign convention is used because the volume decreases during compression.

---

### Step 3 — Substitute the Polytropic Pressure Expression

From Step 1, solve for $P$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P = P_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V}\right)^{\!n}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute into the integral:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} &= -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^{\,n} \cdot V^{-n} \, dV \\
&= -P_1 V_1^{\,n} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV &= \left[\dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \\
&= \dfrac{V_2^{\,1-n} - V_1^{\,1-n}}{1-n}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} = -P_1 V_1^{\,n} \cdot \dfrac{V_2^{\,1-n} - V_1^{\,1-n}}{1-n}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 5 — Simplify Using Polytropic Identity

Since $P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} &= \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\,n}}{n-1}\left(V_1^{\,1-n} - V_2^{\,1-n}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{1-n}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately reduces the expression to a function of $P_1 V_1$ and the volume ratio.

---

### Step 6 — Convert to Pressure Ratio Using Built-in Volume Ratio

Recognize that $V_1/V_2 = V_i$ (built-in volume ratio) and $V_i^{\,n} = P_2/P_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{comp} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**This is the indicated work per cycle per trapped volume V₁.** ✅ Final result.

---

### Step 7 — Convert to Indicated Power (Multiple Cycles per Second)

If the compressor makes **N** revolutions per minute and has **z** lobes/grooves producing **z** compression cycles per revolution:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_i = \text{IP} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot z}{60}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Multiply by the number of compression events per second: each rotor revolution produces $z$ trapped volumes that go through the full compression cycle.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Compression Work Comparison

For an **ideal gas** compressed from the same initial state (P₁, V₁) to the same final pressure P₂:

| Process Type | Polytropic Index | Work Done Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Isothermal (best, ideal cooling) | $n = 1$ | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ |
| Polytropic (general case) | $1 < n < \gamma$ | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ |
| Adiabatic (no heat transfer) | $n = \gamma = 1.4$ | $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Required Increases as n Increases.** This is the single most important trend: better cooling (lower n) → less work. That is why oil-flooded screw compressors (with intense internal cooling) achieve $n \approx 1.05$ to $1.15$, requiring **substantially less power** than dry/oil-free machines with $n \approx 1.35$.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A single-stage oil-flooded screw compressor compresses air from **P₁ = 1 bar** to **P₂ = 8 bar**. The rotor speed is **N = 3000 rpm**, the suction volume per trapped chamber is **V₁ = 0.0005 m³**, and there are **z = 4** compression cycles per revolution. Assuming a polytropic index **n = 1.25**, calculate the indicated power.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the pressure ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{8}{1} = 8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent $(n-1)/n$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{n-1}{n} = \dfrac{0.25}{1.25} = 0.2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pressure ratio raised to this exponent:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_p^{\,0.2} = 8^{0.2} = 1.5157
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the coefficient $n/(n-1)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.25}{0.25} = 5.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute indicated work per cycle per chamber:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_i = 5.0 \times (1 \times 10^5) \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.5157 - 1)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_i = 5.0 \times 50 \times 0.5157 = 128.93 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute number of compression cycles per second:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles/sec} = \dfrac{N \cdot z}{60} = \dfrac{3000 \times 4}{60} = 200 \text{ cycles/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Indicated Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} = W_i \times \text{cycles/sec} = 128.93 \times 200 = 25{,}786 \text{ W} \approx 25.79 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer: **Indicated Power ≈ 25.79 kW**

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Working Cycle of a Screw Compressor

The following block diagram maps the four functional stages that every trapped gas volume passes through during one full rotor meshing cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suction Port Open: V equals V1, P equals P1] --> B[Transfer Phase: Axial movement of trapped gas toward discharge end]
    B --> C[Compression Phase: Male lobe enters female groove, V decreases, P increases]
    C --> D{Matched Built-in Volume Ratio?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Discharge Port Opens: Smooth release at P2, no throttling loss]
    D -- No Under-compression --> F[Re-expansion inside chamber, work lost as heat]
    D -- No Over-compression --> G[Extra compression continues past port, work wasted]
    E --> H[Exhaust: Gas leaves casing at P2]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> A
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Lobe Compressor Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suction Port: Lobe unmeshes, void forms] --> B[Gas Trapped Between Lobe and Casing Wall]
    B --> C[Transfer: Gas carried peripherally around casing]
    C --> D[Discharge Port: Meshing lobes push gas out]
    D --> E[External Compression: Pressure rises in downstream pipe/system]
    E --> F[No Internal Compression in Machine]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Sliding Vane Compressor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor Turns: Eccentric in Cylindrical Stator] --> B[Vanes Slide Outward by Centrifugal Force]
    B --> C[Suction Zone: Pocket Volume Increases, Gas Enters]
    C --> D[Transfer Zone: Pocket Volume Maximum at Angle equals 180 deg]
    D --> E[Compression Zone: Eccentricity Reduces Pocket Volume, Pressure Rises]
    E --> F[Discharge Zone: Vane Tip Crosses Discharge Port, Gas Expelled]
    F --> G[Cycle Repeats for Every Adjacent Vane Pair]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Three Rotary Compressors at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCREW[Screw Compressor]
        S1[Two Helical Rotors] --> S2[Intermeshing Traps Gas]
        S2 --> S3[Internal Compression via Volume Reduction]
        S3 --> S4[Matched Built-in Volume Ratio Discharge]
    end
    subgraph LOBE[Lobe Compressor]
        L1[Two Figure-8 Impellers] --> L2[Trapping and Transfer]
        L2 --> L3[No Internal Compression]
        L3 --> L4[External Compression in Discharge Pipe]
    end
    subgraph VANE[Vane Compressor]
        V1[Eccentric Rotor + Sliding Vanes] --> V2[Pockets Form Between Vanes]
        V2 --> V3[Internal Compression via Eccentricity]
        V3 --> V4[Discharge at Minimum Volume Zone]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue for Recall:** Screw and Vane compressors perform *internal* compression (work-saving geometry), while Lobe compressors only *transport* gas and depend on the downstream system to develop pressure. This single fact is a guaranteed 3-mark question in every KTU ESE.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Define the term "built-in volume ratio" of a screw compressor and explain its importance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **built-in volume ratio (Vᵢ)** of a screw compressor is defined as the ratio of the volume of gas trapped at the suction port (V₁) to the volume remaining in the chamber when the trapped gas reaches the discharge port (V₂).

$$V_i = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$$

**Importance:** When the built-in volume ratio matches the actual pressure ratio required, i.e., when $V_i^{\,n} = P_2/P_1$, the discharge process is **smooth and throttling-loss free**. If they do not match, either *over-compression* (extra work wasted inside the chamber beyond the port) or *under-compression* (gas re-expands before discharge, also wasting work) occurs. Hence, screw compressors are often designed with variable built-in volume ratios to handle different discharge pressures efficiently.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Why is a lobe compressor classified as a blower and not a high-pressure compressor?**

**Model Answer:**

A lobe compressor (Roots blower) consists of two figure-8 shaped rotors that trap gas and carry it from the suction port to the discharge port. The rotors do **not** reduce the volume of the trapped gas during this transfer — they merely *transport* it. The trapped gas is pushed out at essentially the **same pressure as the inlet**.

The actual pressure rise is generated **externally in the discharge piping and connected system**, not inside the machine. Because there is no internal compression, the achievable pressure ratio per stage is limited (typically 1.5 – 2.0). For higher pressure ratios, multi-staging with intercoolers is required.

Hence, the lobe compressor is fundamentally a **gas pumping device** and is classified as a **blower** for low-pressure applications such as aeration, pneumatic conveying, and gas boosting.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of a twin-screw compressor with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the built-in volume ratio and the difference between oil-flooded and oil-free types.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A twin-screw compressor consists of a **male (4-lobe) rotor** and a **female (6-groove) rotor** housed in a close-fitting casing. The male rotor is the driver; the female rotor is the driven idler, synchronized through external timing gears.

**Working Principle (4 phases):**

1. **Suction:** As the rotors unmesh at the suction end, gas at suction pressure P₁ enters the trapping chamber of maximum volume V₁.
2. **Transfer:** The trapped volume moves axially toward the discharge end as the rotors rotate.
3. **Compression:** The male lobe slides into the female groove, progressively reducing the trapped volume. Pressure rises from P₁ to the discharge pressure P₂.
4. **Discharge:** When the chamber volume equals the discharge port volume V₂, the compressed gas is expelled.

**Role of Built-in Volume Ratio Vᵢ:**

$V_i = V_1/V_2$. When $V_i^{\,n} = P_2/P_1$, the discharge is smooth. Otherwise, throttling losses occur.

**Oil-Flooded vs Oil-Free:**
- **Oil-flooded:** Oil is injected into the compression chamber. Acts as sealant (reduces leakage), coolant (n approaches 1.05–1.15), and lubricant. Higher efficiency.
- **Oil-free:** No oil injection. Used where air purity is critical (pharmaceuticals, food, electronics). Typically uses water injection or relies on timing-gear synchronization and tight clearances. Lower n (~1.35), higher work required.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A single-stage oil-flooded screw compressor takes in air at 1 bar and 20 °C and delivers at 8 bar. The rotor speed is 3000 rpm, the suction volume per trapped chamber is 5 × 10⁻⁴ m³, and 4 compression cycles occur per revolution. Take polytropic index n = 1.25. Calculate: (i) the built-in volume ratio if matched, (ii) the indicated power, (iii) the mass flow rate.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Built-in volume ratio for matched case:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_i = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = 8^{1/1.25} = 8^{0.8}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
8^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \ln 8} = e^{0.8 \times 2.0794} = e^{1.6635} = 5.278
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_i \approx 5.28}$$

**(ii) Indicated Power:**

Using $P_1 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V_1 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \cdot \dfrac{N z}{60}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} = \dfrac{1.25}{0.25} \times (10^5)(5 \times 10^{-4}) \left[8^{0.2} - 1\right] \times \dfrac{3000 \times 4}{60}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
= 5 \times 50 \times 0.5157 \times 200 = 25{,}785 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{IP} \approx 25.79 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate:**

Using $R = 287$ J/kg·K for air, $T_1 = 293$ K:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 \, N z}{60 \, R \, T_1} = \dfrac{(10^5)(5 \times 10^{-4})(200)}{(287)(293)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{84{,}091} = 0.119 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 0.119 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe with a sketch the construction and working of a sliding vane compressor. State its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
- A **cylindrical stator** (stationary casing) and an **eccentric rotor** mounted inside it.
- The rotor has longitudinal slots cut along its length, in which **sliding vanes** move radially.
- Vanes are pushed outward by **centrifugal force** (and often backed by spring force or oil pressure for low-speed operation).
- Two ports are cut in the casing: a **suction port** on the wide-gap side and a **discharge port** on the narrow-gap side.

**Working Principle:**
1. As the rotor turns, the vane tip follows the inner stator surface.
2. The gap between two consecutive vanes is **maximum** when passing the suction port → gas enters at low pressure P₁.
3. As the rotor moves away from the suction side, the chamber volume **progressively decreases** (because of eccentricity) → gas is compressed.
4. When the vane crosses the discharge port, the chamber is at minimum volume → compressed gas is expelled at P₂.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, compact construction with few parts.
- Smooth, nearly pulse-free delivery.
- Can handle wet and dirty gases (because of vane tip sliding contact).
- Easy capacity control by varying eccentricity or bypassing.

**Limitations:**
- Vane wear is significant → frequent maintenance.
- Lower efficiency compared to screw compressors at high pressure.
- Limited to moderate pressure ratios.
- Sensitive to dust and particulates if oil-flooded design is used.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A vane compressor has a rotor of diameter 200 mm and stator bore diameter 220 mm, with an axial length of 300 mm. There are 8 vanes, each of thickness 5 mm. Calculate the theoretical displacement per revolution.**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Rotor diameter: $d = 200$ mm → $r = 100$ mm = 0.1 m
- Stator bore: $D = 220$ mm → $R = 110$ mm = 0.11 m
- Axial length: $L = 300$ mm = 0.3 m
- Number of vanes: $z = 8$
- Vane thickness: $t = 5$ mm = 0.005 m

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum swept volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{max} = \pi \left(R^2 - r^2\right) L = \pi (0.11^2 - 0.1^2)(0.3)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
= \pi (0.0121 - 0.0100)(0.3) = \pi (0.0021)(0.3)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
= 1.98 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 1980 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the volume occupied by vanes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{vanes} = z \cdot t \cdot L \cdot (R - r) = 8 \times 0.005 \times 0.3 \times 0.01
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
= 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 120 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical displacement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d = V_{max} - V_{vanes} = 1980 - 120 = 1860 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_d = 1860 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 1.86 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the vane thickness correction:** Many students use only $V_{max}$ and lose 2 marks. Always subtract $z \cdot t \cdot L \cdot e$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** KTU strictly requires SI units. Convert mm → m before substituting.
> 3. **Mixing up rotor and stator diameters:** Rotor is always the *smaller* diameter; stator bore is the *larger* diameter. Reversing them gives negative volume.
> 4. **Assuming lobe compressors have internal compression:** This is the most common conceptual error. State clearly that *compression occurs externally*.
> 5. **Wrong polytropic index for screw compressors:** For oil-flooded, use $n \approx 1.05$–$1.15$; for oil-free adiabatic, use $n = \gamma = 1.4$. Always read the problem statement carefully.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screw Compressor — Key Points:**
> - Positive displacement rotary machine with two intermeshing helical rotors (4 male lobes / 6 female grooves — most common).
> - **Internal compression** via progressive volume reduction.
> - **Built-in volume ratio** $V_i = V_1/V_2$ must match actual pressure ratio for optimum efficiency.
> - Oil-flooded variants: $n \approx 1.05$–$1.15$; oil-free: $n \approx \gamma = 1.4$.
> - High pressure ratio (up to 13:1) in a single stage (oil-free).
> - Pulsation-free discharge, suitable for oil-free applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lobe Compressor — Key Points:**
> - Two figure-8 impellers rotated by external timing gears.
> - **NO internal compression** — purely a gas transfer device.
> - Pressure ratio per stage limited to ~2.0; multi-staging needed for higher.
> - Used as blower for aeration, pneumatic conveying, gas boosting.
> - Clearance between lobe tip and casing is the major design parameter controlling back-leakage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vane Compressor — Key Points:**
> - Eccentric rotor with multiple sliding vanes.
> - Internal compression by decreasing pocket volume due to eccentricity.
> - Theoretical displacement formula: $V_d = \pi(R^2 - r^2)L - z \cdot t \cdot L \cdot e$.
> - Vanes loaded by centrifugal force and/or spring force; oil-flooded designs use hydraulic pressure.
> - Wear of vane tips is the dominant life-limiting factor.
> - Capacity control via varying eccentricity or bypassing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Triad — Exam-Ready Statement:**
> *Among the three rotary compressors, screw and vane compressors perform **internal compression**, while lobe compressors rely on **external compression**. Screw compressors offer the highest pressure ratio and efficiency, vane compressors offer compactness and simplicity, and lobe compressors offer simplicity for low-pressure gas transfer applications.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Quick-Reference Equations:**
> - Indicated power: $IP = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right] \cdot \dfrac{N z}{60}$
> - Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 N z}{60 R T_1}$
> - Built-in volume ratio for matched case: $V_i = (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$
> - Vane displacement: $V_d = \pi(R^2 - r^2)L - z t L e$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direction Control Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Direction Control Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Direction Control Valve (DCV)** is a fluid power component whose primary function is to **direct the flow of hydraulic fluid (or compressed air) into specific paths** within a hydraulic or pneumatic circuit. It does **not regulate flow rate** (that's the job of flow control valves) nor **system pressure** (that's the job of pressure control valves). Its sole purpose is to **start, stop, or change the direction of fluid travel** between the pump, actuator (cylinder/motor), and the reservoir/tank.

According to **ISO 1219-1:2012** (the international standard adopted universally by KTU-syllabus fluid power courses), every DCV is classified using two numbers:
- **First Number (Ways):** Number of distinct ports/connections the valve has.
- **Second Number (Positions):** Number of discrete switching states (detents) the valve can occupy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Terminology (High-Yield):** Never write "2-port valve" — always write **"2-way valve"**. The terms "ports" and "ways" are sometimes interchanged loosely, but **KTU valuations strictly use "WAYS" for the count and "PORTS" for the physical connection points**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Police Officer

Imagine a **busy 4-way road intersection**. A traffic police officer stands in the middle. At one moment, he allows cars from the **North–South direction** to move and blocks the **East–West direction**. When the signal changes, he **flips** his hand direction and now allows **East–West** traffic while stopping **North–South**.

- The **roads** = hydraulic ports (P, T, A, B)
- The **cars** = hydraulic fluid
- The **officer's hand position** = the valve's spool position
- The **actuator (cylinder)** = the destination (forward or reverse stroke)

A DCV is exactly this officer for an **industrial hydraulic cylinder** — it decides whether the cylinder rod **extends (forward stroke)** or **retracts (return stroke)**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Ports & Symbols (ISO 1219 Standard)

| Symbol | Port Name | Function |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **P** | Pressure Port | Connected to the pump discharge (high-pressure supply) |
| **T** | Tank Port | Connected to the reservoir (return to tank, low pressure) |
| **A, B** | Working Ports | Connected to the actuator (cylinder/motor) ports |
| **L** | Leakage Port | Drains internal spool leakage to tank (in closed-center valves) |

## 1.4 Callout — Centre Position Concept

> [!NOTE]
> A **3-position valve** has a special middle state called the **"centre position"**. The three common centre configurations are **Tandem Centre, Open Centre (Open Centre Through), and Closed Centre (Blocked Centre)**. KTU frequently asks to **draw and explain** these in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Ways × Positions" Classification Matrix

The valve nomenclature `X/Y` (read as "X-way, Y-position") is the **single most important high-yield topic** for KTU exams.

### 2.1.1 Common Valve Configurations & Their Engineering Use

| Valve Type | Common Name | Typical Application | KTU Frequency |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **2/2** | 2-Way, 2-Position | ON/OFF shut-off (e.g., single-acting cylinder control) | Medium |
| **3/2** | 3-Way, 2-Position | Single-acting cylinder (spring return) | High |
| **4/2** | 4-Way, 2-Position | Double-acting cylinder — basic control | Very High |
| **4/3** | 4-Way, 3-Position | Double-acting cylinder — with centre position safety logic | Very High |
| **5/2** | 5-Way, 2-Position | Double-acting cylinder — with separate tank port per side | High |
| **5/3** | 5-Way, 3-Position | Double-acting cylinder — with closed/open/tandem centre | High |

## 2.2 Spool vs. Poppet Construction — The Core Architectural Choice

### 2.2.1 Spool-Type DCV (Sliding Spool)
- A precision-ground cylindrical **spool** slides inside a matching **sleeve (body)**.
- Lands (raised rings) on the spool alternately **block and uncover** port openings.
- **Advantages:** Multiple positions possible, balanced (symmetric) pressure forces, handles high flow.
- **Disadvantages:** Leakage across clearances (~10 µm), sensitive to contamination.

### 2.2.2 Poppet-Type DCV
- A conical or spherical **poppet** is pressed against a **seat** to shut off flow.
- **Advantages:** Bubble-tight sealing, zero leakage, tolerant to dirty fluid.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to 2 positions only, higher actuation force needed.

## 2.3 Actuation Methods — How the Spool is Moved

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 14-mark question bank almost always asks: *"Explain different types of actuation methods for DCVs with neat sketches."* Memorise the four major types below.

| Actuation Type | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Manual** | Lever / Push-button / Pedal | Machine tool controls, low-frequency use |
| **Mechanical** | Cam / Plunger / Roller | Limit-switch actuation at end of cylinder stroke |
| **Solenoid** | Electromagnetic coil pulls armature | **Most common in industry** — PLC-controlled automation |
| **Pilot** | Hydraulic/air pilot pressure on spool ends | High-flow, high-pressure systems (>350 bar) |
| **Solenoid-Pilot (Solenoid-Operated-Pilot-Operated)** | Small solenoid moves pilot, which moves main spool | Largest industrial valves (high flow + low control force) |

### 2.3.1 Spring-Centred vs. Detent Construction
- **Spring-Centred:** Spool returns to centre (or default position) when actuation force is removed — **fails-safe**.
- **Detent:** Spool stays in the last commanded position until another actuation signal — useful for latching circuits.

## 2.4 The Three Critical 4/3 Centre Positions

For a **4/3 DCV** (4-way, 3-position), the middle (neutral) block has three standard configurations that KTU expects students to draw **block-by-block** with fluid path arrows.

### 2.4.1 Closed Centre (Blocked Centre)
- All four ports (P, T, A, B) are **blocked**.
- **Pump flow must be unloaded** to tank via a relief valve → uses **pressure-compensated pump**.
- **Application:** Where the cylinder must be **held stationary under load** (drilling, clamping).

### 2.4.2 Open Centre (Open Centre Through — OCT)
- P is connected to T (pump unloads to tank freely).
- A and B are **blocked** (cylinder is trapped → locked).
- **Application:** Systems using a **fixed-displacement pump**; pump runs continuously without heating oil.

### 2.4.3 Tandem Centre
- P is blocked (no flow from pump).
- A and B are **both connected to T** (cylinder can move freely if external force acts — **floating**).
- **Application:** Used in **vertical cylinder circuits** where load must be allowed to descend under gravity without pump pressure.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are the **only mathematical relationships** that KTU examiners set under "Direction Control Valves" — most marks are for sketches and explanations, but these equations appear in 7-mark sub-parts.

| Formula | Meaning | Units | When to Use |
|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G}}$ | Flow rate through valve at a given pressure drop | L/min (Cv in US GPM units) | Sizing a DCV for actuator speed |
| $C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient (rearranged) | dimensionless index | Finding valve size from required Q |
| $\Delta P = \dfrac{(Q)^2 \cdot G}{C_v^2}$ | Pressure drop across valve at given flow | bar or psi | Power loss calculation |
| $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Actuator piston velocity | m/s | Determining extension/retraction time |
| $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | Stroke time of cylinder | seconds | Cycle time analysis |
| $F = P \cdot A - P_b \cdot A_b$ | Net thrust on double-acting cylinder | Newtons | Verifying valve can handle system pressure |
| $P_{max} = \dfrac{F_{load}}{A_{piston}}$ | Required supply pressure | bar | Sizing the pump pressure rating |

Where:
- $Q$ = flow rate (L/min)
- $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient (manufacturer-supplied)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve (bar)
- $G$ = specific gravity of fluid (≈ **0.86** for mineral hydraulic oil, ≈ **1.0** for water)
- $A, A_b$ = piston areas on cap-end and rod-end sides
- $L$ = stroke length

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection moulding machines:** DCVs direct oil to clamp and ejector cylinders with millisecond precision.
- **Earth-moving equipment (JCB, excavators):** Solenoid-pilot DCVs control boom, arm, bucket, swing, and travel simultaneously.
- **Aircraft landing gear:** Centre-blocked 4/3 valves hold the gear locked against hydraulic lock-out.
- **CNC machine tools:** DCVs feed tool slides and index turrets in automated cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walk-Through — How a 4/2 Solenoid DCV Drives a Double-Acting Cylinder

This is the **single most common 14-mark derivation-style question** KTU sets on DCVs. We will trace the fluid path in **both** spool positions, with logic for every arrow drawn.

### 3.1.1 Component Inventory (Standardised for the Solution)

1. **Pump** — supplies oil at flow $Q$ and pressure $P$.
2. **Reservoir (Tank)** — collects return oil.
3. **4/2 DCV** — has ports P, T, A, B.
4. **Double-acting cylinder** — port A connects to cap-end (blank-end), port B connects to rod-end.
5. **Solenoid coil "Y1"** — when energised, shifts spool to **Position 1 (Left)**.
6. **Spring "S"** — when Y1 is de-energised, returns spool to **Position 2 (Right)**.

### 3.1.2 Logical Step-by-Step Operation

**Step 1 — Initial state (Y1 OFF, spring holds spool to the right):**

In this position, the spool's lands connect:
- Port **P → Port B**
- Port **A → Port T**

**Reasoning:** Pump pressure enters P, gets routed via the spool's open groove to B, pushing oil into the **rod-end** of the cylinder. Simultaneously, the trapped oil in the cap-end is pushed out of port A, travels through the spool's other groove to port T, and returns to the tank.

> **Result:** Piston **retracts** (rod pulls in).

**Step 2 — Solenoid energised (Y1 ON, spool shifts left):**

In this position, the spool's lands now connect:
- Port **P → Port A**
- Port **B → Port T**

**Reasoning:** Pump pressure is now routed to the **cap-end (blank-end)** of the cylinder, extending the piston. The oil trapped in the rod-end is expelled through B, back to T, and to the tank.

> **Result:** Piston **extends** (rod pushes out).

**Step 3 — Power-cut / Fail-safe response:**

If power fails, Y1 de-energises, spring pushes spool back to Position 2 (right). The cylinder **retracts** automatically — this is a **fail-safe** design choice, common for press-down clamps.

### 3.1.3 Symbolic Representation (KTU expects this on the answer sheet)

The two-square symbol convention:
- **Square 1 (de-energised state):** Arrows P→B and A→T drawn.
- **Square 2 (energised state):** Arrows P→A and B→T drawn.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Valve Sizing for a Given Cylinder Stroke Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"A hydraulic cylinder must complete a 200 mm stroke in 3 seconds using a 4/2 DCV. The cylinder bore is 50 mm, rod diameter is 30 mm. Select the valve size (find Cv)."*

### 3.2.1 Given Data
- Stroke length $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Stroke time $t = 3 \text{ s}$
- Bore (piston) diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Rod diameter $d = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$
- Specific gravity of oil $G = 0.86$
- Allowable pressure drop across valve $\Delta P = 10 \text{ bar}$

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Compute the Piston Areas

The full-bore (cap-end) area:

$$A_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4} = \dfrac{0.007854}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

The annular (rod-end) area:

$$A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot ((0.05)^2 - (0.03)^2)}{4}$$

$$A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.0025 - 0.0009)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.0016}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Calculate the Required Flow Rate

Volume needed on the cap-end side:

$$V_{cap} = A_{cap} \cdot L = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.2 = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Volume needed on the rod-end side:

$$V_{rod} = A_{rod} \cdot L = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.2 = 2.513 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

The valve must pass the **larger** of the two volumes per unit time (because extension flow = cap-end, retraction flow = rod-end). Taking the worst case (extension):

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{cap}}{t} = \dfrac{3.927 \times 10^{-4}}{3}$$

$$Q = 1.309 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to **L/min** (multiply by 60000):

$$Q = 1.309 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 60000 = 7.854 \text{ L/min}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Determine the Required Valve Flow Coefficient (Cv)

Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\Delta P}}$$

Substituting $Q = 7.854 \text{ L/min}$, $G = 0.86$, $\Delta P = 10 \text{ bar}$:

$$C_v = 7.854 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.86}{10}}$$

$$C_v = 7.854 \cdot \sqrt{0.086}$$

$$C_v = 7.854 \cdot 0.2933$$

$$\boxed{C_v \approx 2.30}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Interpret the Result

A valve with $C_v \geq 2.30$ is required. A standard **1/4-inch (6 mm) nominal DCV** typically offers $C_v \approx 2.5$ to $4.0$, so a **1/4" valve** is the correct selection.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — 4/3 Centre Configurations Compared

| Property | Closed Centre | Open Centre (OCT) | Tandem Centre |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Pump flow in neutral | Blocked at P | Passes P→T freely | Blocked at P |
| Cylinder ports A, B | Blocked (locked) | Blocked (locked) | Both connected to T (floating) |
| Load-holding capability | **Excellent** (no leakage path) | Good | **Poor** (load can drift) |
| Pump type required | Pressure-compensated (load-sensing) | Fixed displacement OK | Pressure-compensated preferred |
| Energy efficiency at idle | Poor (full relief-valve flow → heat) | **Excellent** (no heat generation) | Moderate |
| KTU-applied use case | Vertical drill press, clamping | Mobile equipment, machine tools | Vertical lift cylinder with gravity return |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A 4/3 Solenoid-Pilot DCV with Double-Acting Cylinder

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** including pump, valve, actuator, and reservoir, with all energy and signal flows. This is the diagram KTU examiners expect students to redraw (with ISO 1219 symbols) on the answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[ Pump\nFixed-Displacement\nQ = 8 L/min ]
    RES[Tank Reservoir\nAtmospheric Pressure]
    RELIEF[Relief Valve\nSet at 100 bar]
    DCV{Centre Block\n4 by 3 DCV\nSolenoid-Pilot}
    POS1[Position 1\nY1 Energised\nP to A Flow]
    POS2[Position 2\nY2 Energised\nP to B Flow]
    POSC[Centre Position\nP Blocked\nA and B to T]
    CYL[Double Acting Cylinder\nD = 50 mm\nL = 200 mm]
    CAP[Cap End Area\nAcap = 19.63 cm2]
    ROD[Rod End Area\nArod = 12.57 cm2]
    LOAD[External Load\nF = 5000 N]

    PUMP --> DCV
    PUMP -. relief path .-> RELIEF
    RELIEF --> RES
    DCV --> POS1
    DCV --> POS2
    DCV --> POSC
    POS1 --> CAP
    POS2 --> ROD
    CAP --> CYL
    ROD --> CYL
    CYL --> LOAD
    POSC --> RES
    POS1 --> RES
    POS2 --> RES
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU Examiner's mental checklist):**
1. Pump supplies constant flow.
2. The 4/3 DCV has **three switching states**, hence the 3 output nodes (`POS1`, `POS2`, `POSC`).
3. The relief valve is **always in parallel** with the pump for safety — it discharges excess flow to tank if pressure exceeds the setting.
4. `CAP` and `ROD` are the two sides of the cylinder; whichever side is pressurised, the piston moves toward the other.

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing Between Spool and Poppet Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Need to select a DCV type]) --> Q1{Need 3 positions\nor centre mode?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> SPOOL[Select Spool-Type DCV\n4 by 3 or 5 by 3]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is bubble-tight\nzero leakage\ncritical?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> POPPET[Select Poppet-Type DCV\n2 by 2 or 3 by 2]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Working pressure\nabove 350 bar?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PILOT[Select Pilot-Operated DCV\nor Solenoid-Pilot]
    Q3 -- No --> SOLENOID[Select Direct-Acting\nSolenoid DCV]

    SPOOL --> DONE([Final Selection])
    POPPET --> DONE
    PILOT --> DONE
    SOLENOID --> DONE
```

## 4.3 Actuation Method Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Actuation Method\nDecision] --> B{Frequency of\nswitching?}
    B -- Less than 10 per hour --> M[Manual Lever\nor Push Button]
    B -- 10 to 100 per hour --> MC[Mechanical Cam\nor Plunger]
    B -- More than 100 per hour --> S[Solenoid\nDirect Acting]
    S --> Q{Flow above\n60 L per min?}
    Q -- Yes --> SP[Solenoid-Pilot\n2 Stage DCV]
    Q -- No --> SD[Solenoid Direct\nDCV is sufficient]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Note

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers above use **purely alphanumeric** names (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`, `flowStart`) prefixed with letters — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone labels. All quoted labels are raw uppercase text with no markdown formatting embedded, ensuring the diagrams render correctly in every Markdown viewer that supports Mermaid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between a 3/2 and a 4/2 direction control valve with suitable sketches. State one typical application for each."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **3/2 valve** has **three ports** (P, T, A) and **two switching positions**. In one position, port P connects to A (pressure to actuator) while T is blocked; in the other position, A connects to T (actuator exhausts) while P is blocked. It is most commonly used to control a **single-acting cylinder** (spring-return type).

A **4/2 valve** has **four ports** (P, T, A, B) and **two positions**. In position 1, P→A and B→T; in position 2, P→B and A→T. It is used to control a **double-acting cylinder** because it can direct pressure to either side of the piston in alternation.

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for 3/2 definition + sketch, 1 mark for 4/2 definition + sketch, 1 mark for applications.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is meant by 'centre position' in a 4/3 DCV? Name and briefly explain any two centre configurations."**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The **centre position** is the **neutral middle state** of a 3-position DCV when no actuation force is applied and both springs are balanced.

**Configuration 1 — Closed Centre (Blocked):** All four ports P, T, A, B are blocked. The cylinder is **hydraulically locked** in place and cannot move. Requires a **pressure-compensated pump** to avoid over-pressurisation since pump flow has no escape path.

**Configuration 2 — Open Centre (Through):** Port P is connected to T (pump unloads freely to tank), while A and B remain blocked. This configuration allows a **fixed-displacement pump** to run continuously without generating heat, while the cylinder stays locked.

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for centre-position definition, 1 mark for first configuration, 1 mark for second configuration.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **construction and working of a 4/3 DCV** showing all three positions. Clearly label all four ports and the two extreme switching arrows in each position.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **closed centre, open centre, and tandem centre** configurations of a 4/3 DCV in terms of pump flow path, cylinder behaviour, load-holding capability, and energy efficiency.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working of 4/3 DCV:**

The valve consists of a **cylindrical body (sleeve)** with four ports (P, T, A, B) machined into it, and a **sliding spool** with multiple lands and grooves that fits precisely inside the sleeve. The spool is held in its centre (neutral) position by **two opposing springs**, one on each end. On the extreme ends of the spool, **two solenoid coils (Y1 and Y2)** provide actuation force.

- **Position 1 (Y1 energised, spool shifted left):** The left groove connects P→A (pressurise cap-end), the right groove connects B→T (exhaust rod-end). Piston extends.
- **Centre (both Y1, Y2 de-energised, springs balanced):** Depending on the centre-block geometry (closed/open/tandem), one of three logical states occurs.
- **Position 2 (Y2 energised, spool shifted right):** Connections reverse — P→B (pressurise rod-end), A→T (exhaust cap-end). Piston retracts.

**Mark split (a):** 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for position 1, 2 marks for position 2, 1 mark for centre position mention.

**(b) Comparison:**

| Criterion | Closed Centre | Open Centre | Tandem Centre |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| Pump flow at neutral | Blocked (relief valve dumps excess) | Free passage P→T | Blocked (relief dumps excess) |
| Cylinder behaviour | Locked at both ports | Locked at both ports | Floating — both ports to tank |
| Load-holding | Excellent | Good | Poor — load drifts |
| Energy efficiency | Poor (heat in relief) | Excellent (no heat) | Moderate |
| Best application | Drilling, clamping | Mobile machinery, machine tools | Vertical lift with gravity return |

**Mark split (b):** 2 marks for comparison table entries, 2 marks for application linkage, 3 marks for engineering reasoning on energy efficiency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **circuit diagram**, explain how a **4/2 solenoid-operated DCV** controls a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder**. Describe the cylinder's motion in each valve position.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-acting cylinder has bore **75 mm**, rod diameter **45 mm**, stroke **250 mm**. The cylinder must complete its extension stroke in **4 seconds**. Find (i) the required pump flow rate in L/min, and (ii) the valve flow coefficient $C_v$ if the allowable pressure drop across the valve is **8 bar** with oil specific gravity **0.87**.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) 4/2 Solenoid DCV with Double-Acting Cylinder:**

**Circuit components:**
1. Reservoir → Pump (with suction strainer)
2. Pump discharge line → Relief valve (in parallel) → Direction control valve (P port)
3. DCV ports A and B → cylinder cap-end and rod-end respectively
4. DCV port T → reservoir

**Operation:**
- **Energise solenoid Y1:** Spool shifts left. Path P→A (high-pressure oil enters cap-end), path B→T (oil from rod-end returns to tank). **Piston extends.**
- **De-energise Y1 (spring shifts spool right):** Path P→B (high-pressure oil enters rod-end), path A→T (cap-end oil to tank). **Piston retracts.**

**Mark split (a):** 2 marks circuit diagram, 2 marks each position description, 1 mark for naming all components.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Required flow rate:**

Cap-end (full-bore) area:

$$A_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.075)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.005625}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Volume needed to extend:

$$V = A_{cap} \cdot L = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.25 = 1.1045 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Time for extension $t = 4$ s, so:

$$Q = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{1.1045 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 2.761 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to L/min (multiply by 60000):

$$Q = 2.761 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 60000 = 16.57 \text{ L/min}$$

**(ii) Valve flow coefficient:**

Using the formula:

$$C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\Delta P}} = 16.57 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.87}{8}}$$

$$C_v = 16.57 \cdot \sqrt{0.10875} = 16.57 \cdot 0.3298$$

$$\boxed{C_v \approx 5.46}$$

**Mark split (b):** 2 marks area calculation, 2 marks flow rate in correct units, 2 marks Cv substitution, 1 mark final answer with units.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Direction Control Valve Questions:**
>
> 1. **Swapping port labels (P, T, A, B)** — KTU examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** every time a port is mislabelled in a sketch. Always cross-check with the ISO 1219 square-symbol convention.
> 2. **Forgetting the tank port (T) in the sketch** — Many students draw P, A, B but omit the T line entirely, which causes the return oil to "disappear" into nowhere. Examiners flag this as a major conceptual gap.
> 3. **Confusing "ways" and "positions"** — A 5/2 valve is NOT the same as a 2/5 valve. The convention is **always (ways / positions)**.
> 4. **Spring-return symbol confusion** — A spring in the symbol must be drawn **outside the box**, connecting the box to a fixed anchor point. Students often draw it inside, which is wrong by ISO 1219.
> 5. **Unit conversion mistakes in numericals** — L/min vs m³/s conversion ($1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 60000 \text{ L/min}$). Losing factor of 60 or 1000 here loses a full mark.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — When solving a Cv problem, always state **"assuming a pressure-compensated pump"** or **"assuming negligible pressure drop in the line"**. Examiners reward stated assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Direction Control Valves (High-Density Summary)**

- **DCV Function:** Directs fluid flow direction only — does NOT control pressure or flow rate.
- **Nomenclature:** X/Y means **X-way (ports), Y-position (switching states)**.
- **Most common industrial valve:** **4/2 solenoid** (double-acting cylinder control).
- **Centre position exists only for 3-position valves** (3rd number "3").
- **Three critical centre modes:** **Closed, Open (OCT), Tandem**.
- **Spool type:** multiple positions, balanced forces, small internal leakage.
- **Poppet type:** only 2 positions, zero leakage, higher actuation force.
- **Actuation methods:** Manual, Mechanical, Solenoid, Pilot, Solenoid-Pilot (2-stage).
- **Spring-centred = fail-safe**; **Detent = latching** (last position held).
- **Pilot-operated valves** are used for **high-flow, high-pressure** systems (above 350 bar).
- **Solenoid-pilot (2-stage) DCVs** are the industry standard for **PLC-controlled automation**.
- **Cv formula:** $C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{G / \Delta P}$ — most common numerical for KTU.
- **Conversion constant:** $1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 60000 \text{ L/min}$ — remember this for all flow-rate numericals.
- **Specific gravity of mineral hydraulic oil** ≈ **0.86** (memorise this for Cv problems).
- **Symbol rules (ISO 1219):** Each square = one position; arrows inside squares show connected ports in that position; springs/detents drawn OUTSIDE the squares.
- **Pump un-loading rule:** Closed-centre and tandem-centre systems need pressure-compensated pumps; open-centre systems can use simple fixed pumps.
- **Vertical cylinder rule:** Use **tandem centre** if the load must be allowed to descend under gravity safely.
- **Most-skipped KTU sub-question:** Why is a **5-way valve** preferred over a 4-way valve in some circuits? (Answer: separate tank ports prevent back-pressure interference between the two cylinder sides.)
- **Examiner's mantra:** *"Always label ports, always show tank return, always state centre mode for 3-position valves."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators - Types of Cylinders and Hydraulic Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actuators - Types of Cylinders and Hydraulic Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Actuator?

In any fluid power system, an **Actuator** is the output device that converts the **hydraulic (or pneumatic) energy** supplied by the power source (pump/compressor) into **mechanical work** in the form of linear motion, rotary motion, or oscillatory motion. The actuator is the terminal element of the hydraulic circuit and is responsible for actually "doing the work" — pushing, pulling, lifting, rotating, or gripping a load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *"An actuator in a hydraulic system is a mechanism that converts hydraulic energy (pressure × flow) into mechanical energy (force × displacement or torque × rotation). Actuators are broadly classified into **Linear Actuators (Cylinders)** and **Rotary Actuators (Hydraulic Motors and Hydraulic Rotary Actuators)**."*

## 1.2 Why Actuators Are Important — The "Muscle" Analogy

Think of a hydraulic system as the human body:
- The **pump** is the **heart** (pumps fluid/energy).
- The **valves** are the **brain/nerves** (control the direction and quantity).
- The **fluid (oil)** is the **blood** (the carrier medium).
- The **actuator** is the **muscle** (does the actual physical work).

Without the actuator, the high-pressure oil has nowhere to go and no work to perform. The actuator is what makes the machine physically *move*.

## 1.3 Classification of Hydraulic Actuators

Hydraulic actuators fall into two master families:

| Family | Type of Motion | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Actuators** | Translates fluid force into straight-line (push/pull) motion | Single-acting cylinder, Double-acting cylinder, Telescopic cylinder, Tandem cylinder, Differential cylinder |
| **Rotary Actuators** | Translates fluid flow into continuous rotary motion | Gear motor (external/internal), Vane motor, Piston motor (axial/radial) |
| **Limited Rotation Actuators** | Produces rotary motion only up to ~360° | Rack-and-pinion actuator, Vane-type rotary actuator, Helical actuator |

## 1.4 Hydraulic Cylinder — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Hydraulic Cylinder** is a linear actuator that uses pressurized hydraulic fluid to produce a unidirectional or bidirectional force in a straight line. The pressure acting on the piston face multiplies by the effective area to deliver an output force: $F = P \times A$. The cylinder barrel houses a piston connected to a piston rod that transmits force to the external load.

## 1.5 Hydraulic Motor — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Hydraulic Motor** is a rotary actuator that converts hydraulic pressure and flow into mechanical torque and angular rotation. The flow rate determines the speed ($Q = D_m \times N$), and the pressure difference across the motor determines the torque it delivers ($T = \Delta P \times D_m / 2\pi$). The standard metric for size is **displacement per revolution, $D_m$**, expressed in **cm³/rev** or **cc/rev**.

## 1.6 Real-World Examples Students Must Know

- **Hydraulic cylinders** → JCB/excavator boom and bucket, hydraulic press, injection moulding machine clamp, aircraft landing gear, car hydraulic jack.
- **Hydraulic motors** → Winch drives, conveyor belts, crane slewing rings, mining shovels, fan drives, combine harvester rotors, ship's deck cranes.
- **Typical working pressure in industrial hydraulics:** **160 bar to 350 bar** (mobile equipment ~210 bar, industrial presses up to 700 bar).
- **Standard displacement range of gear motors:** **4 to 200 cm³/rev**; piston motors: **10 to 1000 cm³/rev**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Hydraulic Cylinders

### 2.1.1 Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)
- Hydraulic oil acts on **only one side** of the piston.
- The return stroke is achieved by an **external force** (gravity, spring, or load itself).
- Has **one port** for fluid entry.
- Used in: hydraulic jacks, clamping devices, simple lifting.
- **Force during extension:** $F_{ext} = P \times A_p - F_{friction} - F_{spring}$
- **Force during retraction (return):** $F_{ret} = 0$ (passive return)

### 2.1.2 Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)
- Hydraulic oil can act on **both sides** of the piston.
- Has **two ports** — one for extension, one for retraction.
- Produces **force in both directions** — the most common cylinder in industry.
- **Effective area (extension):** $A_e = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$
- **Effective area (retraction):** $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$
- **Force during extension:** $F_e = P_e \times A_e$
- **Force during retraction:** $F_r = P_r \times A_r$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does retraction force differ from extension force?**
> Because the piston rod occupies space on one side, the **annular area** on the rod side is *smaller* than the full piston area. Hence, for the *same* pressure, the retraction force is *less* than the extension force.

### 2.1.3 Telescopic Cylinder
- Uses **multiple nested stages** (each smaller cylinder fits inside the larger one).
- Provides a **very long stroke** from a **short retracted length** — perfect for dump trucks and tippers.
- Extension sequence: smallest piston extends first, then larger stages sequentially.
- Total extension = sum of all stage strokes.

### 2.1.4 Tandem Cylinder
- Two pistons on a **single common rod** inside **one barrel**.
- Effective area is **doubled** — delivers roughly **twice the force** of a standard cylinder of the same bore.
- Used in applications needing very high thrust (large presses, heavy-duty clamping).
- Flow path: oil enters a central port and acts on **both pistons simultaneously**.

### 2.1.5 Differential Cylinder
- A double-acting cylinder where the **bore area** and **annular area** have a specific geometric ratio.
- The most common configuration has $A_e = 2 \times A_r$ (when the rod is half the bore: $d = D/\sqrt{2}$).
- Significance: with **equal flow rate** in and out, extension and retraction speeds are different (extension is slower but more powerful; retraction is faster but less powerful).
- **Velocity ratio:** $\frac{v_{ext}}{v_{ret}} = \frac{A_r}{A_e}$

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Hydraulic Motors

### 2.2.1 Gear Motors
- **External Gear Motor:** Two identical gears mesh inside a housing. Oil enters at the inlet port, fills the spaces between teeth, and is carried around to the outlet. The meshing of the gears traps oil and pushes it out — generating torque.
  - Pressure limit: ~175–210 bar.
  - Displacement: **4 to 200 cm³/rev**.
  - Application: low-to-medium pressure, mobile equipment.
- **Internal Gear Motor:** A smaller external gear (rotor) meshes with a larger internal gear (rotor/stator) inside a housing. Crescent-shaped separator compensates for the volume difference.
  - Quieter operation, slightly higher efficiency.

### 2.2.2 Vane Motor
- A slotted rotor with retractable vanes runs inside an eccentric cam ring.
- As oil enters the expanding chambers (between vanes), force on the vane generates torque.
- Spring-loaded or pressure-loaded vanes ensure sealing.
- Displacement: ~25 to 200 cm³/rev; pressure up to **175 bar**.

### 2.2.3 Piston Motors
- **Axial Piston Motor:** Pistons arranged **parallel** to the axis of rotation, with a tilted swashplate or bent-axis design. Each piston executes a stroke as the cylinder block rotates.
  - High efficiency (90–96%), high pressure (up to **420 bar**), high power density.
  - Used in: heavy machinery, industrial presses, marine drives.
- **Radial Piston Motor:** Pistons arranged **radially** (perpendicular) to the axis, in a star pattern. Each piston is pushed outward by pressure and pushes against an eccentric cam ring.
  - Very high starting torque, low speed (typically <500 rpm), high pressure.
  - Used in: winches, slew drives, conveyor drives.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as your exam-day cheat sheet.** All values in SI units. For conversions: $1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{N/m}^2$, $1\ \text{LPM} = \frac{10^{-3}}{60}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables / Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder force (extension) | $F_e = P_e \times A_e$ | $F_e$ in N, $P_e$ in Pa, $A_e$ in m² | DAC extension |
| 2 | Cylinder force (retraction) | $F_r = P_r \times A_r$ | $F_r$ in N, $P_r$ in Pa, $A_r$ in m² | DAC retraction |
| 3 | Extension effective area | $A_e = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | D = bore diameter (m) | Full piston face |
| 4 | Retraction effective area | $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ | d = rod diameter (m) | Annulus |
| 5 | Cylinder velocity | $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | $v$ in m/s, $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in m² | Linear speed of piston |
| 6 | Cylinder stroke time | $t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A \times L}{Q}$ | L = stroke length (m) | Time per stroke |
| 7 | Differential velocity ratio | $\frac{v_{ext}}{v_{ret}} = \frac{A_r}{A_e}$ | Unitless | Force-vs-speed trade-off |
| 8 | Motor theoretical flow | $Q_{th} = D_m \times N$ | $D_m$ in m³/rev, $N$ in rev/s | Displacement-based flow |
| 9 | Motor theoretical torque | $T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \times D_m}{2\pi}$ | $T_{th}$ in N·m, $\Delta P$ in Pa | Pressure-based torque |
| 10 | Motor output power | $P_{out} = 2\pi N T$ | P in W, N in rev/s, T in N·m | Mechanical output |
| 11 | Motor hydraulic power | $P_{in} = \Delta P \times Q$ | P in W | Input power |
| 12 | Motor volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{Q_{th}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$ | Percent | Internal leakage measure |
| 13 | Motor mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{th}} \times 100$ | Percent | Friction loss measure |
| 14 | Motor overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m$ | Percent | Total motor performance |
| 15 | Motor speed | $N = \frac{Q_{actual} \times \eta_v}{D_m}$ | rev/s | Actual operating speed |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Relevance

- **Heavy mobile equipment** (excavators, loaders): predominantly use **double-acting cylinders** and **axial piston motors** because of the high force, high pressure, and controllability required.
- **High-force linear applications** (presses, plastic injection moulding clamping): use **double-acting** or **tandem cylinders**.
- **Mobile low-pressure applications** (tippers, agricultural machines): use **telescopic cylinders** and **gear motors** for cost-effectiveness.
- **Winches, cranes, conveyors**: **radial piston motors** are preferred for their high starting torque at low speed.
- **High-precision industrial automation**: **servo piston motors** with electronic feedback for precise speed/position control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
