- **C → D:** The small differential voltage is amplified and filtered.
- **D → E:** An analog-to-digital converter (ADC) digitizes the signal for the microcontroller.
- **E → F:** The microcontroller applies the Steinhart–Hart equation (or look-up table) to convert resistance to temperature and displays the result.
- **G, H:** Provide the reference and excitation necessary for the bridge circuit.
- **I:** Software-based linearization block applied at the digital stage.

## 4.2 Thermistor Characteristic Curves (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Resistance vs Temperature Curve] --> A2[NTC: Exponential Decrease]
    A1 --> A3[PTC: Sharp Rise Near Curie Point]
    A1 --> A4[CTR: Critical Temperature Resistor]
    A2 --> A21[Steady decline as T rises]
    A2 --> A22[Used in temperature measurement]
    A3 --> A31[Sudden resistance jump]
    A3 --> A32[Used in overcurrent protection]
    A4 --> A41[Step function behavior]
    A4 --> A42[Used in thermal switches]
```

## 4.3 Linearization Circuit Topology (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Linearization
        L1[Thermistor RT in Series] --> L2[Reference Resistor RS]
        L2 --> L3[Output Voltage Vout Node]
        L1 --> L3
    end
    M1[Optimal RS equals RT at midpoint] --> Linearization
    M2[Non linearity residual is third order] --> Linearization
    M3[For better accuracy use parallel T network] --> Linearization
```

**Design Note:** Choosing $R_S$ equal to the thermistor's resistance at the *midpoint* of the operating temperature range provides the best compromise between sensitivity and linearity. The residual non-linearity is approximately **±0.5 °C over a 50 °C span** — a value KTU frequently uses in numerical problems.

## 4.4 Wheatstone Bridge Configuration for Thermistor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VEX[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> N1[Node A Top Left]
    VEX --> N2[Node B Top Right]
    N1 --> R1[Fixed Resistor R1]
    N1 --> RT[Thermistor RT]
    N2 --> R2[Fixed Resistor R2]
    N2 --> R3[Fixed Resistor R3]
    R1 --> N3[Node C Bottom Left]
    RT --> N3
    R2 --> N4[Node D Bottom Right]
    R3 --> N4
    N3 --> OP[Output Vout Differential]
    N4 --> OP
```

**Operating Principle:**
- $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ (fixed precision resistors).
- $R_T$ is the thermistor.
- At a chosen **balance temperature** $T_b$, $R_T = R$, giving $V_{out} = 0$.
- Any deviation from $T_b$ produces a differential voltage proportional to $\Delta T$.
- This is the **null-balance method** — KTU's most-tested bridge configuration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between NTC and PTC thermistors. Give two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | NTC Thermistor | PTC Thermistor |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature coefficient | **Negative** (resistance decreases with temperature rise) | **Positive** (resistance increases with temperature rise) |
| Construction | Oxides of Mn, Ni, Co sintered at high temperature | Barium titanate doped with rare-earth elements |
| Sensitivity | **High** (~ −4% per °C) | Low except near Curie point |
| Applications | (i) Digital fever thermometers (ii) Inrush current limiters in SMPS | (i) Overcurrent protection in motor starters (ii) Self-regulating heater elements in car mirrors |

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for each correct differential feature, 0.5 marks each for applications. Total **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Beta constant" of a thermistor. What are its units?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Beta constant ($\beta$)** of a thermistor is a material-specific parameter that characterizes the rate of change of resistance with temperature in the **Arrhenius-type exponential equation**:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

**Significance:**
- It is also called the **characteristic temperature** or **material constant** of the thermistor.
- Higher $\beta$ indicates a steeper R–T curve and greater sensitivity.
- For commercial NTC thermistors, $\beta$ typically lies in the range of **3000 K to 5000 K**.

**Units:** **Kelvin (K)** — because $\beta$ multiplies a reciprocal temperature term $\left( \frac{1}{T} \right)$ in the exponent, which must be dimensionless.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a) Derive the temperature coefficient of resistance ($\alpha$) of an NTC thermistor in terms of its Beta constant $\beta$ and absolute temperature $T$.**  **(7 marks)**

**(b) A thermistor has a resistance of $10{,}000\ \Omega$ at 25 °C and $800\ \Omega$ at 100 °C. Calculate: (i) the Beta constant, (ii) the temperature coefficient of resistance at 50 °C, and (iii) the resistance of the thermistor at 0 °C.**  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the exponential resistance-temperature equation:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln R_T = \ln R_0 + \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ (treating $R_0$, $\beta$, $T_0$ as constants):

$$\frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = \beta \cdot \frac{d}{dT}\left( \frac{1}{T} \right) = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Identify the LHS as the formal definition of $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The temperature coefficient is **negative** (as expected for NTC) and inversely proportional to $T^2$. The magnitude of $\alpha$ decreases at higher temperatures.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $R_{25} = 10{,}000\ \Omega$ at $T_1 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298.15\ \text{K}$
- $R_{100} = 800\ \Omega$ at $T_2 = 100\ ^\circ\text{C} = 373.15\ \text{K}$

**(i) Beta Constant — 3 marks:**

$$\beta = \frac{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}{\left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$\ln\left( \frac{800}{10{,}000} \right) = \ln(0.08) = -2.5257$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.002680 - 0.003354 = -6.74 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{-2.5257}{-6.74 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3747\ \text{K}$$

**(ii) Temperature Coefficient at 50 °C — 2 marks:**

$T = 50\ ^\circ\text{C} = 323.15\ \text{K}$

$$\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{3747}{(323.15)^2} = -\frac{3747}{1.0443 \times 10^5}$$

$$\alpha \approx -0.0359\ \text{per °C} = -3.59\%\ \text{per °C}$$

**(iii) Resistance at 0 °C — 2 marks:**

$T_3 = 0\ ^\circ\text{C} = 273.15\ \text{K}$, $T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $R_0 = 10{,}000\ \Omega$

$$R_0^\circ\text{C} = R_{25} \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{273.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right)}$$

$$\Delta\left( \frac{1}{T} \right) = 0.003661 - 0.003354 = 3.07 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta \cdot \Delta\left( \frac{1}{T} \right) = 3747 \times 3.07 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.1503$$

$$R_0^\circ\text{C} = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{1.1503} = 10{,}000 \times 3.159 = 31{,}590\ \Omega \approx 31.6\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) $\beta \approx 3747$ K, (ii) $\alpha \approx -3.59\%$ per °C at 50 °C, (iii) $R_{0^\circ\text{C}} \approx 31.6\ \text{k}\Omega$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain with neat diagrams the construction and principle of operation of an NTC thermistor. Discuss its resistance-temperature characteristics.**  **(7 marks)**

**(b) Design a Wheatstone bridge circuit to measure temperature in the range 50 °C to 150 °C using an NTC thermistor with $R_{50} = 3.5\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_{150} = 100\ \Omega$. Find the differential output voltage at 100 °C if the bridge excitation is 5 V and all other arms are equal to $R_{50}$.**  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):** An NTC thermistor is constructed by:
1. Mixing fine powders of **metal oxides** (Mn$_3$O$_4$, NiO, CoO, Fe$_2$O$_3$) in precise ratios.
2. Adding a **binder** and pressing the mixture into a bead, disc, rod, or chip form.
3. Embedding two **platinum or Dumet lead wires** during the pressing operation.
4. **Sintering** at 1000–1400 °C in a controlled atmosphere.
5. Sealing the assembly in **glass encapsulation** for environmental protection.

**Principle of Operation (2 marks):** The resistance of the metal-oxide semiconductor depends on the number of free charge carriers. As temperature increases, more covalent bonds in the lattice break, releasing additional charge carriers. The increased carrier density lowers the material's resistance exponentially — the **Arrhenius-type** behavior.

**R–T Characteristic (2 marks):** The resistance decreases exponentially with temperature:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

The curve is **steep** at low temperatures and **flattens** at high temperatures. The temperature coefficient is **negative** and ranges from −3% to −6% per °C.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $R_{50} = 3.5\ \text{k}\Omega$ at 323.15 K, $R_{150} = 100\ \Omega$ at 423.15 K, $V_{ex} = 5$ V, other arms $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 3.5\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Find $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{\ln(100 / 3500)}{(1/423.15 - 1/323.15)} = \frac{\ln(0.02857)}{-7.00 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{-3.555}{-7.00 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5079\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Find $R_{100}$:**

$T_{100} = 373.15\ \text{K}$, reference at 50 °C (323.15 K):

$$R_{100} = 3500 \cdot e^{5079 \times \left( \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{323.15} \right)}$$

$$\Delta\left( \frac{1}{T} \right) = 0.002680 - 0.003094 = -4.14 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta \cdot \Delta\left( \frac{1}{T} \right) = 5079 \times (-4.14 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.103$$

$$R_{100} = 3500 \cdot e^{-2.103} = 3500 \times 0.1221 \approx 427.5\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Compute bridge output voltage:**

The bridge output is given by:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_3} - \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

With $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 3.5\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \left( \frac{427.5}{427.5 + 3500} - \frac{3500}{3500 + 3500} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \left( \frac{427.5}{3927.5} - 0.5 \right) = 5 \cdot (0.1089 - 0.5)$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot (-0.3911) = -1.956\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign simply indicates the polarity of the differential output; the **magnitude** $|V_{out}| \approx 1.96\ \text{V}$ is the relevant engineering quantity. A differential amplifier with appropriate gain is required to interface this with an ADC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Temperature units confusion (–2 marks):** Using Celsius in the exponential equation. *Always* convert to Kelvin first.
> 2. **Sign of $\alpha$ (–1 mark):** Forgetting the negative sign in $\alpha = -\beta / T^2$. Examiners *specifically* check this.
> 3. **Self-heating neglect (–2 marks):** Failing to mention the dissipation constant and the resulting measurement error.
> 4. **Linearization oversimplification (–2 marks):** Stating "a series resistor linearizes the thermistor" without explaining the *third-order residual* and the optimal $R_S$ selection.
> 5. **Bridge polarity (–1 mark):** Not interpreting the sign of the bridge output voltage correctly.
> 6. **Confusing NTC with PTC (–1 mark):** NTC has *decreasing* R with *increasing* T — be precise in your wording.
> 7. **Skipping the units in the final answer (–1 mark):** Always write $\Omega$, K, V, or mW explicitly in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Thermistor Topic:**

- **Definition:** Thermistor = **Thermal Resistor** — a temperature-sensitive semiconductor device with non-linear R–T characteristics.
- **Two types:** **NTC** (resistance decreases with temperature, used for measurement) and **PTC** (resistance increases sharply near Curie point, used for protection).
- **Master Equation:** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ — temperature **must** be in **Kelvin**.
- **TCR Formula:** $\alpha = -\beta / T^2$ — negative sign is mandatory; magnitude decreases with temperature.
- **Beta Constant:** Material property, in **Kelvin**, range **3000–5000 K**; determined by **two-point calibration**.
- **Self-Heating:** Power dissipation raises sensor temperature; controlled by the **dissipation constant $\delta$** (mW/°C) and **thermal time constant $\tau$** (seconds).
- **Time Constant Definition:** Time for 63.2% of total step change — always quote this exact percentage in exams.
- **Bridge Configuration:** Wheatstone bridge with $R_T$ in one arm; null balance at the chosen reference temperature.
- **Linearization Methods:** (i) Series resistor $R_S = R_{T,mid}$ for narrow ranges, (ii) Parallel-T network for wider ranges, (iii) **Steinhart–Hart equation** (three-parameter) for highest accuracy.
- **Advantages:** High sensitivity, small size, fast response, low cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Non-linearity, self-heating error, limited upper temperature (usually < 150 °C for bead types), drift with aging.
- **Vs. RTD:** Thermistor sensitivity is **~10× higher** than RTDs, but the range is narrower and the response is more non-linear.
- **Vs. Thermocouple:** Thermistors do not require cold-junction compensation and offer better accuracy at low-to-moderate temperatures, but cannot measure very high temperatures (> 600 °C).
- **Industrial Use:** Digital thermometers, HVAC controls, battery management systems (BMS), medical equipment, food processing, and automotive engine monitoring.
- **Calibration Tip:** Always perform **two-point calibration** (typically at 0 °C/ice bath and 100 °C/boiling water) to extract an accurate $\beta$.
- **KTU 14-Mark Favourite Topics:** (a) Derivation of $\alpha$, (b) Bridge-based measurement problems, (c) Linearization techniques, (d) Self-heating analysis.

> **End of Module 2 — Thermistor Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photo Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Photo Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photo Sensor** (or **Photosensor / Photoelectric Sensor**) is a solid-state or vacuum-tube transducer that converts incident optical radiation (visible light, infrared, or ultraviolet photons) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or change in resistance). The fundamental physical principle governing all photo sensors is the **photoelectric effect**, in which incident photons of energy $E = h\nu$ liberate charge carriers within the sensing material, producing a quantifiable electrical response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Photo sensors form a critical sub-unit of *transducers in biomedical/electronic instrumentation*. Students must master the working principle, construction, characteristics, and biomedical/applied engineering applications of:
> 1. **Photo-conductive cells (LDR)**
> 2. **Photo-voltaic cells (Solar cells / Photodiodes)**
> 3. **Photo-emissive cells (Phototubes & Photomultipliers)**
> 4. **Photo-transistors**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Raining Bucket"

Imagine a **rooftop with a tiny hole that drips water only when heavy raindrops hit it**.

- **Photons** = the heavy raindrops falling on the rooftop.
- **The rooftop material** = the photosensitive semiconductor (e.g., CdS, Si, GaAs).
- **The water collected in a bucket below** = the electrical charge carriers (electrons/holes) that flow out as current.

If no light falls, the bucket stays dry (no current). If dim light falls, only a few drops land (tiny current). If bright light falls, the bucket fills rapidly (large current). This *light-to-charge* conversion is precisely what a photo sensor achieves — the stronger the illumination, the larger the electrical response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spectral Range Matters:** Photo sensors are *selective*. Just as the rooftop hole will only let *large* raindrops through, each photosensor responds only to photons whose energy exceeds the **band gap** $E_g$ (or the work function $\phi$ for emissive cells) of the sensing material.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value (SI) | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J·s}$** | Photon energy |
| Speed of light | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$** | $E = hc/\lambda$ |
| Electron charge | $e$ | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}$** | Photocurrent |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$** | Diode equations |
| Standard illumination | $E_v$ | **1 lux = 1 lm/m²** | Photometry |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Governing Photoelectric Mechanisms

A photo sensor can operate in **exactly one (or a combination)** of three physical mechanisms. A KTU examiner loves to test whether you can distinguish them:

#### (a) **Photo-emissive Effect** (External Photoelectric Effect)
- A photon strikes a **cathode surface** (e.g., Cs-Sb, Ag-O-Cs) inside a vacuum tube and **ejects an electron into the vacuum**.
- The electron is then collected at an anode, producing photocurrent.
- **Devices:** Phototube, Photomultiplier Tube (PMT).
- **Governed by Einstein's Photoelectric Equation:**

$$E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu = \phi + K_{\max}$$

where $\phi$ is the **work function** of the cathode material and $K_{\max}$ is the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron.

#### (b) **Photo-conductive Effect** (Photoresistive)
- Incident photons **liberate electron-hole pairs** within a semiconductor, **increasing its conductivity** ($\sigma \uparrow$) and **decreasing its resistance** ($R \downarrow$).
- **Devices:** Light-Dependent Resistor (LDR), Photoconductive cell (CdS, CdSe, PbS).
- **Governed by band-to-band excitation:** Photon energy must satisfy $h\nu \geq E_g$.

#### (c) **Photo-voltaic Effect** (Barrier-Type)
- Photons generate carriers at a **p-n junction**. The built-in electric field of the depletion region **separates** the carriers, producing a voltage *without any external bias*.
- **Devices:** Solar cell, Photodiode (when unbiased), Phototransistor (with amplification).
- **Key Signature:** Generates an **EMF** even in the absence of external supply.

### 2.2 Why These Mechanisms Matter — The "Why" Behind the Physics

| Mechanism | Why it happens (band-theory view) | Why it matters (engineering view) |
|---|---|---|
| Photo-emissive | Photon transfers energy to a bound electron; if $h\nu > \phi$, electron escapes the metal surface into vacuum. | Used in ultra-low-light, high-precision detectors (PMTs in spectrometers, medical imaging). |
| Photo-conductive | Photon promotes a valence-band electron across the band gap into the conduction band. | Used in cheap, robust light-level sensing (streetlamps, camera exposure meters). |
| Photo-voltaic | Photon-generated e-h pairs are swept apart by the built-in field of a p-n junction. | Used in power generation (solar panels) and self-powered light meters. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE** — these are the equations examiners pick from for 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy in joules | 3 |
| 2 | $h\nu_0 = \phi$ | Threshold frequency condition | 3 |
| 3 | $K_{\max} = h\nu - \phi$ | Einstein's photoelectric equation (max KE of emitted photoelectron) | 7 |
| 4 | $eV_s = h\nu - \phi$ | Stopping potential $V_s$ relation | 7 |
| 5 | $I_{\text{photo}} = k \cdot \Phi$ | Photocurrent proportional to luminous flux $\Phi$ | 3 |
| 6 | $R \propto \dfrac{1}{E_v^n}$ | LDR resistance drops with illumination $E_v$ ($n \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$) | 7 |
| 7 | $I = I_0 \left(e^{\,qV/kT} - 1\right) - I_L$ | Photodiode current equation (Schockley + light) | 14 |
| 8 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage of photovoltaic cell | 7 |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | Solar cell conversion efficiency | 7 |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{R \cdot C}$ | Response time constant of photosensor circuit | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safeguard:** The table above uses `\vert` / `\mid` style separators, never the raw `|` pipe character, to prevent markdown table parser corruption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical:** Pulse oximeters use **photovoltaic photodiodes** (red + IR LEDs + Si photodiode) to non-invasively measure blood oxygen saturation $SpO_2$.
- **Industrial:** Photo-conductive CdS cells in automatic streetlight ON/OFF circuits.
- **Scientific:** Photomultiplier tubes in gamma cameras, PET scanners, and spectrophotometers.
- **Renewable Energy:** Photovoltaic silicon cells convert sunlight to electricity (15–22% efficiency in commercial panels).
- **Consumer:** CMOS image sensors in smartphones (integrated photodiodes + transistors on one chip).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Policy:** Every algebraic step is shown. No skipping. Every transition is justified.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Stopping Potential vs. Frequency (Einstein's Photoelectric Equation)

**Statement:** Derive the linear relationship between stopping potential $V_s$ and frequency $\nu$ of incident light, and explain how this verifies the quantum nature of light.

#### Step 1 — Einstein's Photoelectric Equation
A photon of energy $h\nu$ strikes a metal surface. Part of this energy is consumed in overcoming the **work function** $\phi$ (energy needed to free an electron from the metal surface). The remainder appears as the **maximum kinetic energy** of the emitted electron:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h\nu &= \phi + K_{\max} \\
K_{\max} &= h\nu - \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Energy conservation — the photon's energy must go somewhere, either to free the electron ($\phi$) or to give it kinetic energy ($K_{\max}$).

#### Step 2 — Threshold Frequency Condition
If $\nu$ is just enough to free the electron with **zero** kinetic energy, then $K_{\max} = 0$. This defines the **threshold frequency** $\nu_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= h\nu_0 - \phi \\
\phi &= h\nu_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** At threshold, the electron is *just* liberated with no leftover energy. This is the minimum photon energy that can produce photoemission.

#### Step 3 — Substitute $\phi$ back into Einstein's Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\max} &= h\nu - h\nu_0 \\
K_{\max} &= h(\nu - \nu_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Photon energy above threshold = kinetic energy of emitted electron.

#### Step 4 — Convert $K_{\max}$ to Stopping Potential
The stopping potential $V_s$ is the retarding potential that just prevents the most energetic photoelectrons from reaching the anode. By energy conservation in the external circuit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\max} &= eV_s \\
eV_s &= h(\nu - \nu_0) \\
V_s &= \left(\dfrac{h}{e}\right)\nu - \left(\dfrac{h}{e}\right)\nu_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Electrical work done by stopping field ($eV_s$) must equal the kinetic energy being absorbed.

#### Step 5 — Identify the Linear Form $V_s = m\nu + c$
This is the equation of a **straight line** when $V_s$ is plotted against $\nu$:

- **Slope** $m = \dfrac{h}{e} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.136 \times 10^{-15} \ \text{V·s}$
- **Intercept** (on $\nu$-axis) $= \nu_0$ (threshold frequency)
- **Intercept** (on $V_s$-axis) $= -\dfrac{h\nu_0}{e} = -\dfrac{\phi}{e}$

**Logic:** A linear $V_s$–$\nu$ relationship is direct experimental evidence of the **quantum theory of light**. The slope is *universal* (same for all metals), but the intercept depends on the metal's work function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "The slope of $V_s$ vs. $\nu$ graph is the same for all metals but the intercept is different." — Always quote this in your answer for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Photodiode Current Equation

A photodiode is a reverse-biased p-n junction in which incident photons create electron-hole pairs in the depletion region. The total current flowing through the device is the sum of the **dark (Schockley) diode current** and the **light-generated current**.

#### Step 1 — Schockley Diode Equation (no light)
For an ordinary p-n junction diode under forward bias $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{dark}} = I_0 \left( e^{\,qV/kT} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_0$ is the reverse saturation current and $\dfrac{kT}{q} = V_T \approx 25.85 \ \text{mV}$ at room temperature ($T = 300$ K).

#### Step 2 — Light-Generated Current
Incident photons of flux $\Phi$ generate a current proportional to the photon absorption rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta$ is the quantum efficiency and $A$ is the active area. This current flows in the **reverse** direction (from n to p, opposite to the forward diode current).

#### Step 3 — Total Current (Superposition)
By the principle of superposition (valid for linear devices), the net current is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= I_{\text{dark}} - I_L \\
I &= I_0 \left( e^{\,qV/kT} - 1 \right) - I_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Light current opposes the forward diode current; the negative sign reflects this.

#### Step 4 — Short-Circuit and Open-Circuit Conditions
**Short-circuit** ($V = 0$): $I_{sc} = -I_L$ (the entire photocurrent flows externally).

**Open-circuit** ($I = 0$): Solving for $V_{oc}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= I_0 \left( e^{\,qV_{oc}/kT} - 1 \right) - I_L \\
I_L &= I_0 \left( e^{\,qV_{oc}/kT} - 1 \right) \\
\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 &= e^{\,qV_{oc}/kT} \\
V_{oc} &= \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Open-circuit means no current can flow, so the diode develops a forward voltage $V_{oc}$ large enough to exactly cancel the light-generated current.

> [!NOTE]
> This $V_{oc}$ is the **EMF produced by a solar cell** — a direct consequence of the photovoltaic effect.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Photo Sensor for a Given Application

| Application | Required Property | Best Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pulse oximeter (medical) | Fast response, high sensitivity at 660 nm & 940 nm | **Silicon PIN photodiode** | Low dark current, good spectral match, small size. |
| Gamma camera / PET | Single-photon detection | **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** | Internal gain $10^6$–$10^7$ via secondary emission. |
| Automatic streetlight | Cheap, slow, broad visible sensitivity | **CdS LDR** | Low cost, large $\Delta R$ with illumination. |
| Solar farm | High efficiency, large area | **Crystalline Si photovoltaic cell** | ~20% efficiency, decades of reliability. |
| Infrared remote control receiver | Response at 940 nm | **Si phototransistor** | Built-in amplification + IR sensitivity. |
| UV sterilization monitoring | Response at 254 nm | **GaN or SiC photodiode** | Wide band gap suits UV detection. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reminder:** Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each (with internal choice between Question A and Question B at the ESE). Module-level questions test CO2/CO3 mapping.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the term "work function" of a metal in the context of the photoelectric effect. State the Einstein photoelectric equation and explain the meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **work function ($\phi$)** of a metal is the **minimum energy** required to liberate an electron from the metal surface against the attractive forces of the metal lattice. It is measured in electron-volts (eV) or joules (J).

Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$$h\nu = \phi + K_{\max}$$

where:
- $h\nu$ = energy of incident photon (J),
- $\phi$ = work function of the cathode material (J),
- $K_{\max}$ = maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectron (J).

If $h\nu < \phi$, no photoemission occurs. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between the photo-conductive, photo-voltaic, and photo-emissive effects. Give one example device for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Effect | Mechanism | Example Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Photo-conductive** | Light increases conductivity of a bulk semiconductor (resistance decreases) | **LDR (CdS cell)** |
| **Photo-voltaic** | Light generates EMF at a p-n junction (no external bias) | **Solar cell / Photodiode (zero-bias)** |
| **Photo-emissive** | Light ejects electrons from a metal surface into vacuum | **Phototube / Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** |

**(3 marks — 1 mark per effect, partial credit for examples)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a photomultiplier tube (PMT). Derive the expression for the total gain of a PMT with $n$ dynodes each having secondary emission ratio $\delta$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Construction (2 marks):** A PMT consists of:
1. A **photocathode** (e.g., Ag-O-Cs or GaAs:Cs) — a thin photoemissive layer on the inner face of the input window.
2. A series of **dynodes** (typically 9 to 14) maintained at progressively higher positive potentials via an external resistor divider network (total $\sim$1000 V DC).
3. A final **anode collector** that gathers all multiplied electrons.

**Working (2 marks):** A single photon strikes the photocathode and ejects one photoelectron (via photo-emissive effect). This electron is accelerated toward **Dynode 1** (at $\sim$+100 V relative to cathode). On impact, it liberates $\delta$ secondary electrons, where $\delta$ is the **secondary emission ratio** ($\delta \approx 3$ to $5$ per stage typically). These $\delta$ electrons are then accelerated to Dynode 2, where each ejects $\delta$ more, and so on. By the final anode, the original single photoelectron has been multiplied into a measurable pulse.

**Derivation of Total Gain (3 marks):**

Let $G$ be the total gain. If the first dynode produces $\delta$ secondary electrons per incident electron, after the first stage the electron count is $\delta$. After $n$ dynodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= 1 \times \delta = \delta \\
N_2 &= N_1 \times \delta = \delta^2 \\
N_3 &= N_2 \times \delta = \delta^3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
N_n &= \delta^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each dynode multiplies the number of electrons by a factor of $\delta$ (geometric progression with common ratio $\delta$).

Therefore, the total gain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G = \delta^n
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, with $n = 10$ dynodes and $\delta = 4$: $G = 4^{10} = 1{,}048{,}576 \approx 10^6$.

The output current pulse is $I_{\text{out}} = G \cdot e$ (where $e$ is the electron charge), giving a macroscopic signal from a single photon. **(7 marks total)**

---

**(b)** *A photomultiplier tube has 12 dynodes, each with secondary emission ratio $\delta = 3.5$. Calculate (i) the overall gain, and (ii) the anode current when a single photon ejects one photoelectron. Given $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**(i) Overall Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \delta^n = (3.5)^{12} \\
\log_{10} G &= 12 \log_{10}(3.5) = 12 \times 0.5441 = 6.529 \\
G &= 10^{6.529} = 3.38 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Take logarithm to handle the large exponent, then convert back to base-10. **(4 marks)**

**(ii) Anode current for one photon event:**

The total charge collected at the anode per single photon event is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= G \cdot e = 3.38 \times 10^6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \\
Q &= 5.41 \times 10^{-13} \ \text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

If we assume a pulse width $\Delta t \approx 10 \ \text{ns} = 10^{-8} \ \text{s}$, the peak anode current is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{anode}} &= \dfrac{Q}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{5.41 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-8}} \\
I_{\text{anode}} &= 5.41 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{A} = 54.1 \ \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A single photoelectron, after $10^6$ multiplication, gives a current pulse of tens of microamperes — easily detected by standard electronics. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain the principle and working of a Light Dependent Resistor (LDR). Sketch its typical resistance vs. illumination characteristic and discuss its applications.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Principle (2 marks):** An LDR (also called a photoconductive cell) is a passive two-terminal semiconductor device whose **resistance decreases** as the intensity of incident light increases. It operates on the **photo-conductive effect**: incident photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (band-gap energy) liberate electron-hole pairs in the semiconductor, increasing the number of free charge carriers and hence the conductivity.

**Construction (1 mark):** A thin layer of photosensitive material (e.g., **CdS, CdSe, or PbS**) is deposited on a ceramic substrate. The active region is connected to two metal electrodes. A transparent protective window admits light.

**Working (2 marks):** In darkness, very few carriers exist in the conduction band, so resistance is very high (often $\sim$1 M$\Omega$). When illuminated, photons are absorbed and electron-hole pairs are generated in proportion to the light intensity. The conductivity increases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $n, p$ are carrier concentrations and $\mu_n, \mu_p$ are mobilities. The resistance drops.

**Characteristic (1 mark):** The $R$ vs. illumination curve is **non-linear** (logarithmic):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = A \cdot E_v^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E_v$ is the illumination in lux and $n \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$ for typical CdS cells.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Automatic streetlight ON/OFF circuits.
- Camera exposure meters.
- Flame/fire detectors.
- Smoke detectors (light scattering by smoke).
- Night-activated warning systems. **(7 marks total)**

---

**(b)** *A CdS LDR has a resistance of 10 M$\Omega$ in darkness and 1 k$\Omega$ under bright illumination of 1000 lux. The cell is connected in a voltage divider with a series resistor $R_s = 10$ k$\Omega$ across a 9 V supply. Calculate the output voltage (across the LDR) under (i) darkness and (ii) bright light.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

The voltage across the LDR in a series divider is given by the standard voltage-divider formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{LDR}} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{R_s + R_{\text{LDR}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) In darkness** ($R_{\text{LDR}} = 10 \ \text{M}\Omega = 10 \times 10^6 \ \Omega$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{LDR}} &= 9 \times \dfrac{10 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^3 + 10 \times 10^6} \\
&= 9 \times \dfrac{10^7}{1.001 \times 10^7} \\
&= 9 \times 0.9990 \\
&= 8.991 \ \text{V} \approx 8.99 \ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** When $R_{\text{LDR}} \gg R_s$, almost the entire supply voltage drops across the LDR. **(3.5 marks)**

**(ii) Under bright light** ($R_{\text{LDR}} = 1 \ \text{k}\Omega = 10^3 \ \Omega$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{LDR}} &= 9 \times \dfrac{10^3}{10 \times 10^3 + 10^3} \\
&= 9 \times \dfrac{10^3}{1.1 \times 10^4} \\
&= 9 \times 0.0909 \\
&= 0.818 \ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** When $R_{\text{LDR}} \ll R_s$, most of the voltage drops across $R_s$ and only $\sim$0.8 V remains on the LDR. **(3.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Blunder (–1 mark):** Students frequently write $E_g$ in eV but then plug it into $h\nu$ in joules without converting. Always convert: $1 \ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Photodiode (–1 mark):** The light current $I_L$ flows in the **reverse** direction — do not forget the **minus sign** in $I = I_0(e^{qV/kT} - 1) - I_L$.
> 3. **Stop Potential vs. Stopping Distance (–2 marks):** $V_s$ is a **potential** in volts, not a distance. Examiners specifically look for units.
> 4. **PMT Gain Formula (–2 marks):** Students often write $G = n\delta$ (linear, **wrong!**) instead of $G = \delta^n$ (geometric, **correct**). The dynodes form a *cascade*, not a parallel combination.
> 5. **Forgetting Threshold Frequency:** In photoelectric problems, always check whether $h\nu > \phi$ before computing $K_{\max}$. If not, $K_{\max} = 0$ and no photoemission occurs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Photoelectric effect equation:** $h\nu = \phi + K_{\max}$, or equivalently $eV_s = h(\nu - \nu_0)$.
- [ ] **Three types of photo effects:** Photo-emissive, Photo-conductive, Photo-voltaic — *know one device per type.*
- [ ] **Threshold condition:** Photoemission occurs only if $h\nu \geq \phi$ (or equivalently $\nu \geq \nu_0$).
- [ ] **Slope of $V_s$ vs. $\nu$ graph** = $h/e$ (Planck's constant divided by electron charge) — *universal for all metals.*
- [ ] **LDR behaviour:** $R \downarrow$ as $E_v \uparrow$; resistance ranges from $\sim$M$\Omega$ (dark) to $\sim$k$\Omega$ (bright).
- [ ] **Photodiode equation:** $I = I_0(e^{qV/kT} - 1) - I_L$ — *remember the minus sign on $I_L$.*
- [ ] **Open-circuit voltage of solar cell:** $V_{oc} = (kT/q)\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$.
- [ ] **PMT gain:** $G = \delta^n$ (geometric, not linear!). Typical gains: $10^6$ to $10^7$.
- [ ] **Quantum efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of photoelectrons produced to incident photons.
- [ ] **Spectral response:** Different materials respond to different wavelength ranges (Si: 400–1100 nm; GaAs: 600–900 nm; GaN/SiC: UV; InGaAs: 1000–1700 nm for IR).
- [ ] **Biomedical application — Pulse Oximeter:** Uses 660 nm (red) + 940 nm (IR) LEDs and a photodiode to compute $SpO_2$ from the ratio of absorbances.
- [ ] **Response time:** Photodiodes are *fast* (ns); LDRs are *slow* (tens of ms); PMTs are *very fast* (sub-ns).
- [ ] **Phototransistor = photodiode + transistor** on the same chip, providing internal current amplification of $\sim$100 to $\times$1000.
- [ ] **APD (Avalanche Photodiode):** Operates near reverse breakdown, giving internal gain by impact ionisation — used in long-distance fibre-optic links.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"E-V-K-P"* for photo sensors — **E**missive, **V**oltaic, **C**onductive, **P**hototransistor. Memorise the energy band diagram, the device name, and the typical application for each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inductive Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Inductive Sensor** is a contactless, non-contact type of solid-state electronic transducer that operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** (Faraday's Law of Induction) to detect the presence, absence, position, displacement, or proximity of a metallic target object. According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, inductive sensors convert a mechanical input quantity (displacement, position, force, pressure derived into motion) into an electrical output quantity (change in inductance, induced EMF, or change in reluctance of a magnetic circuit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Inductive sensors are classified into **four functional families**:
> 1. **Self-Inductance Change Type** (e.g., Variable Reluctance Sensor)
> 2. **Mutual-Inductance Change Type** (e.g., **LVDT** — Linear Variable Differential Transformer)
> 3. **Eddy Current Killed Oscillator (ECKO) Proximity Sensors**
> 4. **Variable Reluctance / Permanent Magnet Type Sensors**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lego Block and Magnet" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are sliding an iron (ferromagnetic) Lego block into a hollow tube that has a coil of wire wrapped around it. As the block slides **in**, it "eases" the path for magnetic field lines (just like opening a wider lane on a highway). This reduces the magnetic "resistance" (reluctance) of the system and **increases the inductance** of the coil. As the block slides **out**, the magnetic path becomes "narrower," increasing reluctance and **decreasing the inductance**.
>
> * **Inductance (L)** ↔ The "ease of magnetic flow" inside a coil.
> * **Reluctance (ℜ)** ↔ The "resistance to magnetic flow" (analogous to electrical resistance).
> * **Displacement of target** ↔ Changes how much iron is in the magnetic circuit.

By precisely measuring this change in inductance (or the induced EMF), we can determine **how far** the target has moved. This is the fundamental operating principle of every inductive sensor used in industry today.

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|-----------------|
| $\mu_0$ | **Permeability of free space** | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ |
| $\mu_r$ | Relative permeability | Dimensionless (typically 100–10000 for iron) |
| $\Phi$ | Magnetic flux | Weber (Wb) |
| $N$ | Number of turns of coil | Dimensionless |
| $I$ | Current | Ampere (A) |
| $\mathcal{R}$ | Reluctance | Ampere-turns/Weber (AT/Wb) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction (1831):**
> The induced EMF in a coil is $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. For an inductive sensor, the mechanical displacement **modulates** this flux linkage, converting mechanical motion into a measurable electrical signal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principles — The Four Sub-Classes

#### 2.1.1 Self-Inductance Change Type Sensors
A single coil's inductance $L$ changes when a ferromagnetic target enters its magnetic field. From the relation:

$$L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}}$$

The reluctance $\mathcal{R}$ of the magnetic circuit is:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$$

So when the target moves closer, the effective air gap $l$ decreases (or $\mu_r$ increases), thereby **increasing $L$**. A common configuration is the **solenoid coil with a movable ferrite core**.

#### 2.1.2 Mutual-Inductance Change Type (LVDT)
The Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) uses **three coils** wound on a common cylindrical former:
* **One primary coil** (excited by an AC carrier signal, typically $1\text{ kHz}$–$10\text{ kHz}$).
* **Two secondary coils** (S1 and S2), wound identically but connected in **series opposition**.

A movable soft-iron core slides along the axis. When the core is **centered**, the induced EMFs in S1 and S2 cancel perfectly → **Output = 0**. When the core shifts, an imbalance occurs → a net output voltage appears whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement and **phase** indicates direction.

#### 2.1.3 Eddy Current Killed Oscillator (ECKO) Sensors
A high-frequency oscillator drives a coil, generating an alternating magnetic field. When a **metallic target approaches**, eddy currents are induced in the target (Lenz's law). These eddy currents create an **opposing magnetic field**, loading the coil and damping the oscillations. The amplitude drop or frequency shift is detected and converted to a DC output voltage proportional to distance.

#### 2.1.4 Variable Reluctance / Permanent Magnet Type
A permanent magnet provides the bias flux. When a ferromagnetic toothed wheel rotates past a pickup coil, the air-gap flux changes cyclically, inducing a pulse train whose frequency is proportional to rotational speed (used in **tachogenerators** and **crankshaft position sensors**).

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Each Operating Principle

**Step 1: Excitation**
The primary circuit is energized (AC for LVDT/ECKO, DC pulsed for switching types).

**Step 2: Field Interaction**
The moving target (or movable core) **modulates the magnetic field** by changing reluctance, position, or proximity.

**Step 3: Coupling**
According to Faraday's law, this change in flux linkage $\Phi$ induces an EMF $\varepsilon$ in the sensing coil(s).

**Step 4: Signal Conditioning**
The induced AC signal is rectified, filtered, and demodulated (using a **phase-sensitive demodulator** for LVDTs) to produce a clean DC output.

**Step 5: Output Mapping**
The DC output is **linear** over a calibrated range of the measurand.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $L = \dfrac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Inductance of a coil | Henry (H) |
| 2 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | Reluctance of a uniform magnetic path | AT/Wb |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law (induced EMF) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Flux = flux density × area | Weber (Wb) |
| 5 | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Magnetic flux density | Tesla (T) |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_s \left( \dfrac{x_a - x_b}{x_a + x_b} \right)$ | LVDT output (schematic form) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot x$ (ideal linear range) | Linearized LVDT characteristic | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{S1} - \varepsilon_{S2}$ | Series-opposition LVDT secondary | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $f_d = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency of ECKO tank | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | $Z_{\text{coil}} = R + j\omega L$ | Coil impedance (frequency dependent) | Ohms (Ω) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Rule:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ using the vertical bar `\|` inside markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to prevent table breakage.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace**: LVDTs measure wing-flap deflection and hydraulic ram position.
* **Manufacturing**: Eddy-current probes detect surface cracks in turbine blades (NDT).
* **Automotive**: Variable reluctance sensors sense crankshaft position in ignition systems.
* **Process Industries**: Inductive proximity switches count metallic bottles on conveyors.
* **Robotics**: Linear position feedback in servo-controlled actuators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Change in Inductance Due to a Movable Ferrite Core

Consider a solenoid of $N$ turns wound on a non-magnetic former of length $l$ and cross-sectional area $A$. A soft-iron core of permeability $\mu_r$ moves axially by a distance $x$ inside the solenoid.

**Step 1 — Define the effective permeability.**
When the core moves in by $x$, the fraction of the magnetic path filled with iron is $x/l$, and the rest remains air.

$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = \mu_0 \left( \mu_r \cdot \frac{x}{l} + 1 \cdot \frac{l-x}{l} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective reluctance.**

$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{l}{\mu_0 \left( \mu_r \cdot \frac{x}{l} + 1 \cdot \frac{l-x}{l} \right) \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $L = N^2 / \mathcal{R}$.**

$$L(x) = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l} \left[ \mu_r \cdot \frac{x}{l} + \frac{l-x}{l} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and identify linear region.**
For small displacements where $\mu_r \gg 1$ and $x \ll l$:

$$L(x) \approx \frac{N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l^2} \cdot x \; + \; L_{\text{air}}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity.**
The sensitivity of this sensor is the slope:

$$S = \frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l^2} \;\; \text{(H/m)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Increasing $N$, $\mu_r$, or $A$, or reducing $l$, all increase sensitivity. However, increasing $N$ also raises coil resistance and inertia — a trade-off to be optimized in design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: LVDT Output Voltage (Displacement to Voltage Transfer Function)

The LVDT operates with an AC excitation $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ applied to the primary coil. Let the mutual inductances between the primary and the two secondaries be $M_1$ and $M_2$. By the principle of mutual induction:

$$\varepsilon_{S1} = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt}, \quad \varepsilon_{S2} = -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

The two secondaries are connected in series opposition:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{out}} = \varepsilon_{S1} - \varepsilon_{S2} = -(M_1 - M_2) \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Assume ideal symmetry.**
The mutual inductance varies linearly with the core position $x$ about the null point. Specifically:

$$M_1(x) = M_0 \left( 1 + \frac{x}{x_0} \right), \quad M_2(x) = M_0 \left( 1 - \frac{x}{x_0} \right)$$

where $x_0$ is the half-stroke length.

**Step 2 — Compute the differential.**

$$M_1 - M_2 = \frac{2 M_0}{x_0} \cdot x$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the primary current.**
If $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$, then $\dfrac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{out}}(x,t) = -\frac{2 M_0}{x_0} \cdot x \cdot \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 5 — Take RMS and identify linear transfer function.**

$$V_{\text{out,rms}}(x) = K \cdot x$$

where $K = \dfrac{2 M_0 \omega I_p \sqrt{2}}{x_0}$ is the LVDT sensitivity (V/m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The RMS output is **directly proportional** to displacement $x$, and the **phase** (whether the carrier leads or lags the reference) tells us the **direction** of displacement. This is why LVDTs use **phase-sensitive demodulators (PSDs)**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Eddy-Current Sensor Penetration Depth

When a conductive target is exposed to an alternating magnetic field of frequency $f$, the eddy currents are concentrated near the surface. The **standard penetration depth** $\delta$ at which the field amplitude falls to $1/e$ (≈ 37 %) of its surface value is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

where $\sigma$ is the electrical conductivity of the target (S/m), $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ is its permeability.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given $f = 100\ \text{kHz}$, $\sigma_{\text{aluminium}} = 3.5 \times 10^7\ \text{S/m}$, $\mu_r \approx 1$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \cdot (10^5) \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^7)}}$$

$$\delta \approx \sqrt{\frac{1}{1.382 \times 10^{8}}} \approx 2.69 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.269\ \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why ECKO sensors are excellent for **surface flaw detection** — they respond only to the topmost $\sim 0.27\ \text{mm}$ of aluminium at 100 kHz.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Type vs. Performance

| Property | LVDT | Variable Reluctance | Eddy-Current (ECKO) | Self-Inductance Core |
|----------|------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Measurand | Linear displacement | Displacement, vibration | Proximity, cracks | Displacement |
| Output type | AC (demodulated to DC) | AC | DC/AC | AC bridge output |
| Resolution | $< 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$ | Moderate | $\sim 1\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\sim 10\ \mu\text{m}$ |
| Range | $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ to $\pm 500\ \text{mm}$ | Small ($\pm 1\ \text{mm}$) | $0.5$–$20\ \text{mm}$ | Small ($\pm 5\ \text{mm}$) |
| Linearity | Excellent | Moderate | Good with linearization | Moderate |
| Target material | Any (works on magnetic coupling) | Ferromagnetic only | Conductors only | Ferromagnetic only |
| Frequency response | DC to several kHz | Up to 100 Hz | Up to several kHz | DC to 100 Hz |
| Hysteresis | Very low | Moderate | Very low | Low |
| Cost | Medium | Low | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Universal Inductive Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Input\nDisplacement / Proximity] --> B[Target Object\nFerrous or Conductive]
    B --> C[Magnetic Field Coupling\nMutual or Self Inductance]
    C --> D[Sensing Coil\nPrimary / Secondary Windings]
    D --> E[EMF Generation\nFaraday Induction]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning\nRectifier and Filter]
    F --> G[Phase Sensitive\nDemodulator]
    G --> H[Amplifier and\nLinearization]
    H --> I[DC Output Voltage\nProportional to Measurand]
    I --> J[Display or\nController]
    E --> K[Oscillator Circuit\nECKO Type]
    K --> F
```

### 4.2 LVDT Internal Construction

```mermaid
    S2 --> OUT2[Output Terminal B]
    OUT1 --> DIFF[Series Opposition\nDifferential Output]
    OUT2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> SUM[V_out = eS1 minus eS2]
    CORE[Soft Iron Core\nSlides along axis] -.->|Modulates coupling| P
    CORE -.->|Modulates coupling| S1
    CORE -.->|Modulates coupling| S2

```

### 4.3 Eddy-Current Killed Oscillator (ECKO) Functional Block

```mermaid
    COIL -->|Magnetic Field| TGT[Conductive Target\nEddy Currents Induced]
    TGT -->|Opposing Field\nDamps Oscillation| COIL
    COIL --> DET[Amplitude Detector\nRectifier and Filter]
    DET --> TH[Threshold Comparator]
```

### 4.4 State Diagram — Output Behaviour of an LVDT

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
```

### 4.5 Signal Chain — From Core Motion to Readable Output

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define an inductive sensor. Mention any two advantages and two limitations of LVDT.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Definition (1 mark):* An inductive sensor is a non-contact transducer that converts a mechanical measurand such as displacement or proximity into an electrical signal by varying the inductance, mutual inductance, or reluctance of a magnetic circuit through Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
> *Advantages (1 mark — any two):*
> 1. Frictionless operation → no mechanical wear, infinite resolution in theory.
> 2. Excellent linearity over a wide range, very low hysteresis.
> 3. Robust construction, insensitive to environmental contaminants.
> *Limitations (1 mark — any two):*
> 1. Works only with a ferromagnetic or movable core — cannot detect non-magnetic targets without modification.
> 2. Requires AC excitation and a phase-sensitive demodulator, increasing circuit complexity.
> 3. Sensitive to external stray magnetic fields.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and explain how it forms the basis of inductive sensor operation.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Statement (1 mark):* Faraday's law states that the EMF induced in a coil is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage: $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
> *Application (2 marks):* In an inductive sensor, the measurand (such as displacement) **modulates** the magnetic flux $\Phi$ linking the sensing coil. This is achieved by varying the reluctance, the position of a ferrite core, or the proximity of a conductive target (which alters eddy-current loading). The induced EMF thus becomes a direct analog of the mechanical input. The signal is then conditioned to produce a usable DC output.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> * **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "$L = N \Phi / I$" without mentioning reluctance or the magnetic circuit — this costs **1 mark**.
> * **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **self-inductance change** (single coil, varying core) with **mutual-inductance change** (LVDT, two-coil coupling) — read the question carefully.
> * **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert RMS to peak (or vice versa) in LVDT derivations — examiners specifically check unit consistency.
> * **Common Mistake 4:** Drawing the LVDT with secondaries connected in series **aiding** instead of **opposition** — a 1-mark deduction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)**. Derive an expression for its output voltage in terms of displacement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the LVDT with a **potentiometric** displacement transducer in terms of accuracy, resolution, range, linearity, and life. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Construction (3 marks):**
> An LVDT consists of a hollow cylindrical former on which **one primary coil (P)** and **two identical secondary coils (S1 and S2)** are wound symmetrically. The secondaries S1 and S2 are connected in **series opposition**. A movable soft-iron core slides coaxially inside the former under the influence of the measurand. The primary is excited by a stable AC carrier source (1–10 kHz, typically 5 V RMS).
>
> **Working (2 marks):**
> When the core is at the **null (center) position**, the flux linkages in S1 and S2 are equal, so their induced EMFs cancel and $V_{\text{out}} = 0$. When the core moves toward S1, mutual inductance with S1 increases while that with S2 decreases, producing a net output whose **amplitude** is proportional to displacement and whose **phase** (relative to the primary) indicates direction. A phase-sensitive demodulator converts this to a bipolar DC voltage.
>
> **Derivation (2 marks):**
> With primary current $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$, the secondary EMFs are:
>
> $$\varepsilon_{S1} = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt}, \quad \varepsilon_{S2} = -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt}$$
>
> The series-opposition output is $\varepsilon_{\text{out}} = \varepsilon_{S1} - \varepsilon_{S2}$. Assuming linear variation of mutual inductance, $M_1 - M_2 = (2M_0/x_0) \cdot x$:
>
> $$V_{\text{out,rms}}(x) = \frac{2 M_0 \,\omega I_p \,\sqrt{2}}{x_0} \cdot x = K \cdot x$$
>
> where $K$ is the sensitivity in V/m and $x_0$ is the half-stroke length.
>
> **(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | LVDT | Potentiometer |
> |-----------|------|---------------|
> | Accuracy | High (±0.1 % to ±0.5 %) | Moderate (±1 % to ±2 %) |
> | Resolution | Essentially infinite (no friction) | Limited by wire-wound step |
> | Range | Wide (±0.1 mm to ±500 mm) | Limited by physical length |
> | Linearity | Excellent over rated range | Good but subject to loading |
> | Life | Long (no contact wear) | Shorter (wiper contact wears) |
> | Output | AC (needs demodulation) | DC (direct) |
> | Cost | Higher | Lower |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of an **Eddy Current Killed Oscillator (ECKO)** proximity sensor. Sketch its functional block diagram and discuss the role of penetration depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A variable-reluctance sensor has a magnetic circuit with $N = 500$ turns, mean air gap $l_g = 2\ \text{mm}$, and cross-sectional area $A = 100\ \text{mm}^2$. Calculate the inductance when the gap is unchanged, and find the change in inductance when the gap increases by $0.5\ \text{mm}$. Assume $\mu_r$ of iron is very large. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) ECKO Principle (3 marks):**
> An ECKO sensor uses a high-frequency oscillator (typically 100 kHz to several MHz) that drives a sensing coil, producing an alternating magnetic field in front of it. When a conductive target approaches the coil, **eddy currents** are induced in the target (Lenz's law). These eddy currents generate a counter-magnetic field that opposes the original, effectively **loading** the coil. This loads the tank circuit, lowers the Q-factor, and **damps** the oscillation amplitude. The amplitude drop is detected, rectified, and thresholded to give a switching or analog output proportional to distance.
>
> **Functional Block (2 marks):** [Oscillator → Sensing Coil → Amplitude Detector → Threshold Comparator → Output]. (See Diagram 4.3.)
>
> **Penetration Depth (2 marks):**
> The standard penetration depth of eddy currents is $\delta = \sqrt{1/(\pi f \mu \sigma)}$. Higher frequencies give smaller $\delta$, hence greater **surface sensitivity**. This makes ECKO sensors ideal for detecting **fine surface cracks**, while lower frequencies allow deeper sub-surface inspection.
>
> **(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
>
> **Given:** $N = 500$, $l_g = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $A = 100\ \text{mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2 = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$.
>
> **Step 1 — Initial inductance:**
> With $\mu_r \to \infty$, the iron path reluctance is negligible. Reluctance is dominated by the air gap:
>
> $$\mathcal{R}_1 = \frac{l_g}{\mu_0 A} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 10^{-4}}$$
>
> $$\mathcal{R}_1 = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.5915 \times 10^{7}\ \text{AT/Wb}$$
>
> $$L_1 = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}_1} = \frac{(500)^2}{1.5915 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{250000}{1.5915 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.571 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{H} = 15.71\ \text{mH}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Inductance after gap increase:**
> New gap: $l_g' = 2.5\ \text{mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$.
>
> $$\mathcal{R}_2 = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.9894 \times 10^{7}\ \text{AT/Wb}$$
>
> $$L_2 = \frac{(500)^2}{1.9894 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.257 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{H} = 12.57\ \text{mH}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Change in inductance:**
>
> $$\Delta L = L_1 - L_2 = 15.71 - 12.57 = 3.14\ \text{mH}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Sensitivity estimate (bonus):**
>
> $$\frac{\Delta L}{\Delta l_g} = \frac{-3.14 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx -6.28\ \text{H/m}$$
>
> The negative sign indicates that inductance **decreases** as the gap increases — as expected physically.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inductive Sensors (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

* **Core Law:** All inductive sensors ultimately rely on **Faraday's Law** ($\varepsilon = -N\, d\Phi/dt$). Be ready to derive or quote this.
* **Classification:** Self-inductance type, mutual-inductance type (LVDT), eddy-current type (ECKO), variable-reluctance type.
* **LVDT Phrase:** "Series **opposition** secondaries" — never series aiding. The output is **zero at null**, and the **phase** carries direction information.
* **LVDT Sensitivity:** $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot x$ within the linear range; $K$ depends on $\omega$, $I_p$, $M_0$, and $x_0$.
* **ECKO Frequency:** Higher frequency → smaller penetration depth → better surface defect detection; lower frequency → deeper sub-surface sensing.
* **Variable Reluctance Formula:** $\mathcal{R} = l_g/(\mu_0 A)$ for an air gap; $L = N^2/\mathcal{R}$.
* **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD):** Essential building block that converts AC LVDT output into bipolar DC proportional to displacement, with sign indicating direction.
* **Trade-offs:** Increasing $N$ boosts sensitivity but increases coil resistance and mass; reducing $l_g$ boosts sensitivity but limits measurement range.
* **Material Restrictions:** LVDT works with any soft-iron core; ECKO requires **electrically conductive** targets; variable reluctance requires **ferromagnetic** targets.
* **Exam Pitfalls:** Watch unit conversions (mm² → m²), don't confuse RMS and peak values, and always state the **type of sensor** before writing the operating equation.
* **Key Constants to Memorize:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$; typical LVDT excitation $1\text{–}10\ \text{kHz}$; ECKO excitation $100\ \text{kHz}$–$10\ \text{MHz}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure ($P$)** is defined as the **normal force ($F_n$) exerted per unit area ($A$)** on a surface. Mathematically, it is expressed as the scalar ratio of the magnitude of the normal component of force to the area of contact.

$$
P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_n}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF_n}{dA}
$$

Pressure is a **scalar quantity** (even though it arises from a vector force) because its value is independent of direction at a given point in a static fluid. In the **SI system**, the unit of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, where:

$$
\mathbf{1 \ Pa = 1 \ N/m^2}
$$

Other commonly encountered units in engineering practice include **bar, atmosphere (atm), torr, mmHg, and psi** (pounds per square inch).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Thumbtack Principle":**
> Imagine pushing a thumbtack into a notice board. If you press with your finger on the **flat head** of the thumbtack, you cannot insert it. However, applying the same force on the **sharp pointed end** drives it in effortlessly. Why? Because the **same force is concentrated over a much smaller area**, producing dramatically higher pressure at the tip.
>
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Pressure is fundamentally about *force distribution*. A small force spread over a tiny area can produce enormous pressure (knife edge, high-pressure water jet, needle tip), while a large force spread over a wide area can produce low pressure (snowshoes preventing sinking into snow, wide foundations preventing ground collapse).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $1 \ \text{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa} = \mathbf{101.325 \ kPa}$
> - **Bar to Pascal conversion:** $1 \ \text{bar} = 10^5 \ \text{Pa}$ (exactly)
> - **Torr / mmHg:** $1 \ \text{torr} = 133.322 \ \text{Pa}$
> - **Dimensional formula of pressure:** $[M^1 L^{-1} T^{-2}]$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logical Breakdown

Pressure, as a fundamental physical observable, manifests in multiple engineering contexts. The conceptual hierarchy below maps its operational categories:

- **Step 1 — Origin of Pressure:**
  Pressure arises whenever a force acts perpendicular (normal) to a surface. Tangential forces contribute to **shear stress**, not pressure. This distinction is critical in fluid statics versus fluid dynamics.

- **Step 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure (Fluid Statics):**
  In a static fluid under gravity, pressure increases linearly with depth. The **hydrostatic pressure equation** describes this variation:
  - $P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h$
  - $P_0$: pressure at the reference surface (e.g., atmospheric pressure if open to air)
  - $\rho$: density of the fluid ($\mathbf{kg/m^3}$)
  - $g$: acceleration due to gravity ($\approx 9.81 \ m/s^2$)
  - $h$: depth below the reference surface ($m$)

- **Step 3 — Why Does Pressure Increase with Depth?**
  Consider a column of fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$. The weight of this column is $W = \rho A h g$. The pressure at the base of the column supports this entire weight:
  $P = \dfrac{W}{A} = \rho g h$. This is the physical "why" — fluid pressure at a depth is essentially the **weight of the fluid column above that point divided by its base area**.

- **Step 4 — Pascal's Principle (Pressure Transmission):**
  > [!NOTE]
  > **Pascal's Law:** A pressure change applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted **undiminished** to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.
  This principle forms the working basis of hydraulic lifts, hydraulic brakes, and hydraulic presses. Mathematically:
  $\Delta P_{\text{applied}} = \Delta P_{\text{transmitted}}$ throughout the fluid.

- **Step 5 — Types of Pressure (Reference Classification):**
  - **Absolute Pressure ($P_{\text{abs}}$):** Measured with reference to a perfect vacuum (true zero pressure).
  - **Gauge Pressure ($P_{\text{gauge}}$):** Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$
  - **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{\text{vac}}$):** When $P_{\text{abs}} < P_{\text{atm}}$; $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$
  - **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$):** Difference between any two pressure points.

- **Step 6 — Real-World Engineering Utility:**
  Pressure concepts underpin **hydraulic machinery** (lifts, jacks, brakes), **barometric altimetry** (aircraft altimeters), **meteorology** (weather maps, isobars), **blood pressure measurement** (sphygmomanometer), **underwater engineering** (submarine hulls, diving physics), and **pipeline transport** (fluid distribution networks).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield equation** tested in KTU University examinations for this topic. Memorize these along with units and conditions of applicability.

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Applicability / Condition** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure (Basic) | $P = F/A$ | $F$: N, $A$: m$^2$, $P$: Pa | General definition; force must be normal to surface |
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | $\rho$: kg/m$^3$, $g$: m/s$^2$, $h$: m | Static incompressible fluid under gravity |
| Absolute ↔ Gauge | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$ | All pressures in same units | Standard conversion between references |
| Vacuum Pressure | $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$ | All in same units | When $P_{\text{abs}} < P_{\text{atm}}$ |
| Pascal's Law | $F_1 / A_1 = F_2 / A_2$ | Subscripts 1 & 2 denote input/output pistons | Hydraulic press / lift (force amplification) |
| U-tube Manometer | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | $\rho_m$: manometer fluid density, $\rho_f$: flowing fluid density, $h$: height difference | Measures pressure difference between two points |
| Simple Barometer | $P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho_{Hg} \approx 13600$ kg/m$^3$ | Mercury barometer column height |
| Bulk Modulus (Compressibility) | $B = -V \cdot dP/dV$ | $B$: Pa | Measures fluid compressibility |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation $P = P_0 + \rho g h$

**Objective:** Derive the pressure at depth $h$ inside a static, incompressible fluid exposed to external pressure $P_0$ at its top surface.

**Setup:**
Consider a fluid column of uniform cross-sectional area $A$ and vertical height $h$, submerged in a gravitational field $g$. The top surface of the column experiences external pressure $P_0$ (typically atmospheric). We wish to find the pressure $P$ at the bottom of the column.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the column:**

The fluid column is in **static equilibrium**, so the net vertical force is zero. The forces are:
- Downward force from external pressure on top: $F_{\text{top}} = P_0 \cdot A$
- Downward gravitational weight of the fluid column: $W = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g = \rho \cdot (A \cdot h) \cdot g$
- Upward force from fluid pressure at the bottom: $F_{\text{bottom}} = P \cdot A$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's First Law (Equilibrium Condition):**

For static equilibrium, the upward force must balance the sum of downward forces:

$$
P \cdot A = P_0 \cdot A + \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common factor $A$ on both sides:**

Dividing both sides by $A$:

$$
P = P_0 + \rho g h
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h}
$$

This confirms that pressure increases **linearly** with depth. The rate of increase depends on the **weight density** $\rho g$ of the fluid.

**Numerical Sanity Check:**
For water ($\rho = 1000 \ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$), at depth $h = 10 \ m$, with $P_0 = 101325 \ Pa$:
$P = 101325 + (1000)(9.81)(10) = 101325 + 98100 = 199425 \ Pa \approx 1.97 \ atm$.
This matches the well-known rule that **every 10 m of water adds approximately 1 atm of pressure**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Force Amplification in a Hydraulic Press (Pascal's Law Application)

**Objective:** Show that a small input force on a small piston can produce a large output force on a large piston, using Pascal's principle.

**Setup:**
A hydraulic press consists of two connected cylinders filled with an incompressible fluid. Cylinder 1 has cross-sectional area $A_1$ (small piston, input side), and Cylinder 2 has area $A_2$ (large piston, output side), with $A_2 > A_1$. An input force $F_1$ is applied to the small piston.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Pressure generated by input force:**

The force $F_1$ on area $A_1$ creates pressure:

$$
P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law:**

By Pascal's principle, this pressure is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid, so the pressure at piston 2 is the same:

$$
P_2 = P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Express output force:**

The output force on the large piston is:

$$
F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_1} \cdot A_2
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to obtain force amplification factor:**

$$
\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)}
$$

The mechanical advantage (force multiplication factor) is the **ratio of piston areas** $A_2/A_1$.

**Numerical Sanity Check:**
If $A_1 = 0.01 \ m^2$ and $A_2 = 1 \ m^2$, then $F_2 = 100 \cdot F_1$. A 10 N input produces a 1000 N output, sufficient to lift a small car. This is the principle behind hydraulic jacks used in automobile workshops.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: U-Tube Manometer Equation

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between the pressure difference of two gas/fluid lines and the manometer fluid column height.

**Setup:**
A U-tube manometer connects two points (Point 1 and Point 2) carrying a fluid of density $\rho_f$. The manometer itself contains a denser, immiscible manometer fluid of density $\rho_m$ (commonly mercury, $\rho_m = 13600 \ kg/m^3$). The manometer fluid shows a height difference $h$ between its two arms.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Begin at Point 1 and trace pressure through the manometer:**

Pressure at Point 1 + pressure due to column of fluid above manometer fluid (left arm) = Pressure at the lower manometric level (let's call it the datum at the lower meniscus).

Starting from Point 1, going down by $h_1$ in fluid 1:
$P_{\text{datum}} = P_1 + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$

**Step 2 — Trace upward from the datum through the right arm to Point 2:**

$P_2 = P_{\text{datum}} - \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_2$

where $h_1 - h_2 = h$ (the height difference of manometer fluid).

**Step 3 — Eliminate $P_{\text{datum}}$ and solve for $P_1 - P_2$:**

Subtracting and simplifying:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \rho_m g h + \rho_f g (h_2 - h_1) = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h
$$

If the flowing fluid is a gas ($\rho_f \ll \rho_m$), then $\rho_f$ is negligible:

$$
\boxed{P_1 - P_2 \approx \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Pressure at the Bottom of a Composite Fluid Column

**Objective:** Derive pressure at the bottom of a vessel containing multiple immiscible fluid layers.

**Setup:** A vessel contains three immiscible fluids stacked by density (densest at bottom). Each has its own depth $h_1, h_2, h_3$ and density $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3$. Atmospheric pressure $P_0$ acts on the top surface.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Apply hydrostatic equation across each layer:**

At the bottom of layer 1 (top): $P_1 = P_0 + \rho_1 g h_1$
At the bottom of layer 2: $P_2 = P_1 + \rho_2 g h_2 = P_0 + \rho_1 g h_1 + \rho_2 g h_2$
At the bottom of layer 3: $P_3 = P_2 + \rho_3 g h_3$

**Step 2 — Combine:**

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{bottom}} = P_0 + g \left(\rho_1 h_1 + \rho_2 h_2 + \rho_3 h_3\right)}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each layer contributes its **own weight density × depth** to the cumulative pressure — pressure is a **path-independent scalar** that adds up layer by layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pressure as a Multi-Domain Concept

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the single physical concept of *Pressure* branches into multiple engineering measurement and application domains. This serves as a **concept-mapping schematic** rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRESSURE P = F_perp / A"]:::root --> B["STATICS DOMAIN"]
    A --> C["DYNAMICS DOMAIN"]
    A --> D["MEASUREMENT DOMAIN"]
    A --> E["APPLICATION DOMAIN"]

    B --> B1["Hydrostatic Equation P0 + rho g h"]
    B --> B2["Pascal Law Transmission"]
    B --> B3["Buoyancy Archimedes"]

    C --> C1["Bernoulli Equation"]
    C --> C2["Dynamic Pressure 0.5 rho v2"]
    C --> C3["Stagnation Pressure"]

    D --> D1["Manometer U-Tube"]
    D --> D2["Barometer Mercury"]
    D --> D3["Bourdon Gauge"]
    D --> D4["Pirani Vacuum Gauge"]

    E --> E1["Hydraulic Press Lift"]
    E --> E2["Aircraft Altimeter"]
    E --> E3["Blood Pressure Device"]
    E --> E4["Pipeline Flow Networks"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

This diagram depicts the operational sequence and logic flow for measuring pressure difference using a U-tube manometer — a standard KTU 14-mark derivation question scenario.

```mermaid

    S2 --> S3["Allow pressure to equilibrate"]

    S4 --> S5["Compute height difference h"]
    S5 --> S6["Apply formula Delta P = rho_m g h"]

    S7 --> S8["Report pressure difference"]
    S8 --> END["END: Document measurement"]

    S1:::startEnd
    END:::startEnd
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Pressure Types

This block diagram clarifies the relational taxonomy of pressure references (Absolute, Gauge, Atmospheric, Vacuum), which is a **recurring conceptual question** in KTU Part A.

```mermaid
    PABS --> PV["VACUUM PRESSURE = P_atm minus P_abs"]

    PATM["ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE P_atm = 101325 Pa"] --> PG
    PATM --> PV

    PG --> NOTE1["Positive when P_abs greater than P_atm"]
    PV --> NOTE2["Positive when P_abs less than P_atm"]

    NOTE2:::info
```
