---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define pressure. State its SI unit and dimensional formula.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Pressure is the normal force exerted per unit area on a surface. Mathematically, $P = F_{\perp} / A$.
>
> **SI Unit (1 Mark):** The SI unit of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, defined as $1 \ Pa = 1 \ N/m^2$.
>
> **Dimensional Formula (1 Mark):** $[M^1 L^{-1} T^{-2}]$
> Derivation: $P = F/A = [MLT^{-2}] / [L^2] = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Distinguish between **absolute pressure** and **gauge pressure** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute Pressure (1.5 Marks):** Pressure measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (true zero). $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$.
>
> **Gauge Pressure (1.5 Marks):** Pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. A tire pressure gauge reading of "30 psi" means the air inside is 30 psi **above** atmospheric; the absolute pressure is $30 + 14.7 = 44.7 \ psi$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **hydrostatic pressure** at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a liquid of density $\rho$ exposed to atmospheric pressure $P_0$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A U-tube manometer containing mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \ kg/m^3$) is connected to two gas pipelines. The mercury level in the left arm is $12 \ cm$ higher than in the right arm. If atmospheric pressure is $101.3 \ kPa$, find the pressure difference between the two pipelines. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Hydrostatic Pressure Equation

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Consider a fluid column of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ inside the static fluid, with the top face exposed to atmospheric pressure $P_0$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Identify the forces acting on this fluid column:
- Downward force from atmosphere on top: $F_{\text{top}} = P_0 \cdot A$
- Downward weight of the fluid column: $W = m g = \rho (A h) g$
- Upward force from fluid below on the bottom face: $F_{\text{bottom}} = P \cdot A$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply static equilibrium ($\sum F = 0$):

$$
P \cdot A = P_0 \cdot A + \rho A h g
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Divide by $A$ and simplify to obtain:

$$
\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}
$$

This is the **fundamental equation of fluid statics**, showing that pressure increases linearly with depth.

#### Part (b) — U-Tube Manometer Numerical

**Given:**
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \ kg/m^3$
- Height difference $h = 12 \ cm = 0.12 \ m$
- $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$
- Both pipes carry gas (negligible density compared to mercury)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Apply the U-tube manometer formula for gas:

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Substitute values:

$$
\Delta P = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.12
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute:

$$
\Delta P = 13600 \times 9.81 = 133416 \ Pa/m
$$

$$
\Delta P = 133416 \times 0.12 = 16009.92 \ Pa \approx 16.01 \ kPa
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Final Answer with units:

$$
\boxed{\Delta P \approx 16.01 \ kPa \ \text{or} \ 16010 \ Pa}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** State and explain **Pascal's Law**. Using this principle, derive the force amplification equation for a **hydraulic press**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $5 \ cm$ and a large piston of diameter $50 \ cm$. A force of $200 \ N$ is applied on the small piston. Calculate: (i) the pressure transmitted through the fluid, and (ii) the force exerted on the large piston. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Pascal's Law and Hydraulic Press Derivation

**Statement (2 Marks):**
> **Pascal's Law:** A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted **undiminished** to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.

**Explanation (1 Mark):** This law holds because fluids (liquids) are nearly incompressible and cannot store shear stress in static conditions — they redistribute pressure uniformly.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Consider a hydraulic press with two pistons of areas $A_1$ (small) and $A_2$ (large).

**Step 1:** Force $F_1$ on small piston produces pressure:

$$
P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}
$$

**Step 2:** By Pascal's Law, pressure is transmitted unchanged:

$$
P_2 = P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}
$$

**Step 3:** Output force on large piston:

$$
F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1 \cdot A_2}{A_1}
$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)}
$$

The factor $A_2/A_1$ is the **mechanical advantage**.

#### Part (b) — Hydraulic Press Numerical

**Given:**
- Diameter of small piston $d_1 = 5 \ cm = 0.05 \ m$, so $r_1 = 0.025 \ m$
- Diameter of large piston $d_2 = 50 \ cm = 0.5 \ m$, so $r_2 = 0.25 \ m$
- Input force $F_1 = 200 \ N$

**(i) Pressure transmitted (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Area of small piston:

$$
A_1 = \pi r_1^2 = \pi (0.025)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2
$$

**Step 2:** Pressure transmitted:

$$
P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 101859 \ Pa \approx 101.86 \ kPa
$$

**(ii) Force on large piston (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Area of large piston:

$$
A_2 = \pi r_2^2 = \pi (0.25)^2 = 0.19635 \ m^2
$$

**Step 2:** Output force:

$$
F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 101859 \times 0.19635 = 20000 \ N = 20 \ kN
$$

**Step 3:** Verification using area ratio method:

$$
\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{50}{5}\right)^2 = 100
$$

$$
F_2 = F_1 \times 100 = 200 \times 100 = 20000 \ N
$$

$$
\boxed{F_2 = 20 \ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm to m:** Students frequently plug centimetres directly into the area formula $\pi r^2$ without unit conversion, producing answers off by factors of 100 or 10000. **Always convert all lengths to SI units (metres) before computing.**
>
> 2. **Confusing absolute and gauge pressure:** If a numerical problem states "a tyre has pressure 200 kPa," failing to clarify whether this is gauge or absolute will lead to incorrect conversion to $P_{\text{abs}}$. **In KTU papers, the convention is: if not specified, it is usually gauge pressure.**
>
> 3. **Using the wrong radius in piston problems:** Diameter is given in most KTU problems, not radius. Dividing by 2 (or multiplying by 2) inconsistently costs 2–3 marks easily. **Write $r = d/2$ explicitly.**
>
> 4. **Missing the atmospheric pressure term in hydrostatic problems:** Some students write $P = \rho g h$ instead of $P = P_0 + \rho g h$, forgetting the surface pressure $P_0$. This loses 1–2 marks per derivation.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in manometer equations:** When applying $P_1 - P_2 = \rho g h$, students often reverse the sign based on which arm is higher. **Always trace the pressure from one point to the other step-by-step** rather than memorizing signs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Pressure**

- **Core Formula:** $P = F_\perp / A$; SI unit is **Pascal (Pa)** $= N/m^2$; dimensional formula $[M^1 L^{-1} T^{-2}]$.
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. Pressure increases linearly with depth.
- **Pressure is a scalar**, even though it originates from a vector force. Only the normal component of force contributes.
- **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. Foundation of hydraulic systems.
- **Hydraulic Press Force:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$. Force amplification = **area ratio**.
- **Pressure Types:**
  - $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$
  - $P_{\text{vacuum}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$
  - $1 \ atm = 101325 \ Pa = 1.01325 \ bar = 760 \ mmHg$
- **Manometer Equation:** $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ for fluid-fluid; $\Delta P \approx \rho_m g h$ for gas-mercury.
- **Barometer Equation:** $P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ — mercury column height is inversely proportional to atmospheric pressure.
- **Bulk Modulus:** $B = -V \cdot (dP/dV)$ — measures fluid's resistance to compression; high for liquids, low for gases.
- **Numerical shortcuts to memorize:**
  - Water: every **10.3 m depth adds ~1 atm**.
  - Mercury: **760 mm column = 1 atm** at standard gravity.
  - Density ratios: $\rho_{Hg}/\rho_{\text{water}} \approx 13.6$.
- **Engineering applications** to mention in theory questions: hydraulic brakes, hydraulic lifts, barometric altimeters, sphygmomanometers, Bourdon gauges, Pitot-static tubes (aircraft), dam wall thickness design (pressure increases with reservoir depth).
- **Common exam traps:** Forgetting $P_0$, mixing up diameters and radii, ignoring atmospheric pressure in absolute-pressure contexts, and sign errors in manometer readings.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain and Piezoelectric Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain and Piezoelectric Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strain Gauges — The Resistor That Feels

### Formal Definition
A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a change in electrical resistance. The fundamental relationship is expressed through the **gauge factor (GF)**, a dimensionless constant that quantifies the sensitivity of the device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain (ε)** is defined as the ratio of change in length to original length:  
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$
> It is a dimensionless quantity, often expressed in *microstrain* (μɛ) where 1 μɛ = 10⁻⁶.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Ruler"
Imagine a thin copper wire stretched across a metal ruler like a guitar string. When you bend the ruler, the wire either stretches (gets longer and thinner) or compresses (gets shorter and thicker). Since electrical resistance depends on both length and cross-sectional area, the wire's resistance changes predictably with the bend. A strain gauge works on this exact principle, except the "ruler" is typically a tiny foil pattern etched onto a flexible backing, and the change in resistance is measured with extreme precision (often in the milliohm range).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Strain gauges do **not** measure force or pressure directly. They measure *deformation*. To obtain force/pressure, the gauge must be bonded to a calibrated elastic element (a diaphragm, beam, or load cell).

## 1.2 Piezoelectric Sensors — The Crystal That Speaks

### Formal Definition
**Piezoelectric sensors** are active transducers that exploit the **direct piezoelectric effect** — the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals to generate an electric charge in response to applied mechanical stress. The governing constant is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient (d)** with units of Coulomb/Newton (C/N) or meters/Volt (m/V).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Electric Matchstick
Take a quartz crystal (the same material used in watches). Squeeze it between your fingers with sudden force, and a tiny voltage spike appears across its faces — like striking a match. This is the **direct piezoelectric effect**. Conversely, if you apply a voltage across the crystal, it deforms slightly — the **converse piezoelectric effect** (used in speakers and inkjet printers). The sensor uses the *direct* effect: stress in, charge out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curie's Discovery (1880):** Jacques and Pierre Curie discovered that applying mechanical stress to crystals like quartz, Rochelle salt, and tourmaline generated a measurable surface charge. This is the foundational principle of modern piezoelectric sensing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Piezoelectric Materials:**
> - **Quartz (SiO₂)** — Natural crystal, excellent stability, low d coefficient (~2.3 pC/N)
> - **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** — Synthetic ceramic, very high d coefficient (~250–600 pC/N), most widely used
> - **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** — Polymer film, flexible, used in dynamic pressure and vibration sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strain Gauge — Operational Logic

A strain gauge operates on three coupled physical effects when strained:

1. **Geometric Effect:** Length changes → resistance changes
2. **Piezoresistive Effect:** Material resistivity changes under stress (dominant in semiconductors)
3. **Cross-sectional Effect:** Area changes (Poisson contraction) → resistance changes

### Step-by-Step Resistance Change Logic

**Step 1 — Start with the resistance equation:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where ρ is resistivity, L is length, A is cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Apply a small perturbation** (differential change). Take the logarithmic differential:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Poisson relationship.** For a circular conductor, if the radius changes by dr under axial strain dL/L, then:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

where ν is Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.3 for metals).

**Step 4 — Combine the three effects:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Gauge Factor:**

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **metallic foil gauges**, the piezoresistive term is negligible, so **GF ≈ 1 + 2ν ≈ 2.0** (for constantan/nichrome alloys).  
> For **semiconductor gauges**, the piezoresistive term dominates, yielding GF values of **50 to 200**.

## 2.2 Wheatstone Bridge — The Measurement Engine

Strain-induced resistance changes are tiny (typically ΔR/R ≈ 0.001 to 0.01). A **Wheatstone bridge** converts these minute changes into measurable voltage.

### Bridge Output Equation (General Form)

For four arms R₁, R₂, R₃, R₄ with excitation voltage V_ex:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

### Common Configurations

**Configuration 1 — Quarter Bridge (Single Active Gauge):**
Only R₁ is the active strain gauge; R₂, R₃, R₄ are fixed precision resistors.

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Configuration 2 — Half Bridge (Two Active Gauges):**
Two adjacent or opposite arms are active. Opposite-arm configuration gives:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Configuration 3 — Full Bridge (Four Active Gauges):**
All four arms are active (common in load cells). Maximum sensitivity and **automatic temperature compensation** when configured properly.

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

## 2.3 Piezoelectric Sensor — The Charge Generation Logic

### The Direct Piezoelectric Effect

When a force F is applied to a piezoelectric crystal along its poling axis, charge Q is generated on the electrodes:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where **d** is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N).

### Voltage Output (Open-Circuit / Voltage Mode)

If the crystal has electrode area A, thickness t, and dielectric constant ε = ε₀εᵣ, the capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

The open-circuit voltage is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{d \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = g \cdot \frac{F \cdot t}{A}$$

where **g = d / (ε₀εᵣ)** is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** (V·m/N).

### Equivalent Circuit Model

A piezoelectric sensor is modeled as either:
- **Charge source** in parallel with capacitance C_p and resistance R_p (insulation/leakage resistance)
- **Voltage source** in series with capacitance C_p

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The low-frequency response of piezoelectric sensors is poor because the charge leaks away through R_p. They are ideal for **dynamic measurements** (vibration, acceleration, dynamic pressure) but unsuitable for **static measurements** unless a charge amplifier with very high input impedance is used.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Strain & Piezoelectric Sensors

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless, often in μɛ |
| Resistance of conductor | $R = \rho L / A$ | Ohm's law for geometry |
| Gauge Factor | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Sensitivity, dimensionless |
| Gauge Factor (metal) | $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu$ | Poisson-dominated regime |
| Gauge Factor (semi) | $GF = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive term added |
| Bridge output (general) | $V_{out} = V_{ex}[R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_4/(R_3+R_4)]$ | Wheatstone bridge equation |
| Quarter bridge | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / 4R$ | One active gauge |
| Half bridge | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / 2R$ | Two active gauges |
| Full bridge | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ | Four active gauges |
| Piezo charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Direct effect, C/N |
| Piezo voltage | $V = g \cdot F \cdot t / A$ | Open-circuit voltage |
| g-coefficient relation | $g = d / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)$ | Material constant |
| Resonant frequency | $f_r = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ | Mechanical resonance, Hz |
| Charge amplifier output | $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$ | Feedback capacitor C_f |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote units for the gauge factor (dimensionless) and piezoelectric coefficients (pC/N for d, mV·m/N for g). Numerical problems often award 1 mark for correct unit usage alone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gauge Factor (Exhaustive)

### Starting Premise
Consider a cylindrical metallic wire of original length L, cross-sectional area A, and resistivity ρ. Its electrical resistance is:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

### Step 1 — Apply a Differential Perturbation
When the wire is subjected to an axial mechanical force, the length, area, and resistivity all change by small amounts dL, dA, and dρ respectively. Taking the total differential of R:

$$dR = \frac{\partial R}{\partial \rho} d\rho + \frac{\partial R}{\partial L} dL + \frac{\partial R}{\partial A} dA$$

Computing each partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial R}{\partial \rho} = \frac{L}{A}, \quad \frac{\partial R}{\partial L} = \frac{\rho}{A}, \quad \frac{\partial R}{\partial A} = -\frac{\rho L}{A^2}$$

### Step 2 — Assemble the Differential Expression

$$dR = \frac{L}{A} d\rho + \frac{\rho}{A} dL - \frac{\rho L}{A^2} dA$$

### Step 3 — Normalize by R
Divide both sides by R = ρL/A:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{L}{A} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{\rho L / A} \cdot \frac{1}{1} + \frac{\rho}{A} \cdot \frac{dL}{\rho L / A} \cdot \frac{1}{1} - \frac{\rho L}{A^2} \cdot \frac{dA}{\rho L / A} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

After cancellation (verified algebraically):

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Poisson's Ratio
For a circular wire of radius r, area A = πr². Differentiating:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r}$$

By definition of Poisson's ratio, the lateral strain is related to axial strain as:

$$\frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the Normalized Equation

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

### Step 6 — Define the Gauge Factor

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The term (1 + 2ν) arises purely from geometry (length + area change). The term (Δρ/ρ)/ε is the **piezoresistive term**, which is near zero for metals but dominant in semiconductors like silicon and germanium.

## 3.2 Derivation of Piezoelectric Sensor Output Voltage

### Setup
A piezoelectric disk of thickness t and electrode area A is subjected to a uniform compressive force F along its poling axis (z-axis).

### Step 1 — Charge Generated (Direct Effect)

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

where d₃₃ is the longitudinal piezoelectric charge coefficient (the subscripts "33" denote the z-axis for both applied stress and induced polarization).

### Step 2 — Equivalent Capacitance of the Crystal

Treating the crystal as a parallel-plate capacitor with the electrode area as plates:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

### Step 3 — Open-Circuit Output Voltage
Since V = Q/C for a capacitor:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot A}$$

### Step 4 — Express Stress and Pressure
The applied stress is σ = F/A (force per unit area). Therefore:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \cdot \sigma = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma$$

where the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** is:

$$g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$

### Step 5 — Charge Amplifier Output
When interfacing with a charge amplifier (feedback capacitor C_f, op-amp), the output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}$$

This is independent of cable capacitance (a major advantage of the charge-mode amplifier over voltage-mode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The charge amplifier's output depends *only* on the feedback capacitor and the input charge, **not** on the cable capacitance between sensor and amplifier. This is why charge amplifiers are preferred in industrial piezoelectric measurements.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A strain gauge with gauge factor 2.1 and nominal resistance 120 Ω is bonded to a steel beam. Under load, its resistance changes by 0.24 Ω. The gauge is connected in a quarter-bridge configuration with a 5 V excitation. Calculate:
(i) The strain experienced by the beam
(ii) The bridge output voltage

### Solution

**Given:** GF = 2.1, R = 120 Ω, ΔR = 0.24 Ω, V_ex = 5 V

**(i) Strain calculation:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{GF} = \frac{0.24 / 120}{2.1} = \frac{0.002}{2.1} = 9.524 \times 10^{-4}$$

Converting to microstrain:

$$\varepsilon = 952.4 \ \mu\varepsilon$$

**(ii) Bridge output voltage (quarter bridge):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \cdot 0.002 = 0.0025 \ \text{V} = 2.5 \ \text{mV}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Strain Gauge Measurement System

The following functional flow shows the complete signal chain from mechanical deformation to a digital readout:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Load applied to Beam] --> B[Strain Gauge bonded to Beam Surface]
    B --> C[Change in Resistance deltaR]
    C --> D[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    E --> F[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Signal Processing]
    G --> H[Display or Data Logger]
    I[Excitation Voltage Source Vex] --> D
    J[Bridge Completion Resistors] --> D
    E --> K[Gain and Offset Calibration Block]
    K --> F
```

### Component Description

- **Strain Gauge:** Converts strain (ε) → resistance change (ΔR)
- **Wheatstone Bridge:** Converts ΔR → differential voltage (mV range)
- **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Boosts mV-level signal to 0–5 V range with high CMRR (>100 dB)
- **ADC + Microcontroller:** Digitizes, linearizes, and applies temperature compensation
- **Excitation Source:** Stable DC voltage (typically 5 V or 10 V) or AC carrier (for noise immunity)

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Mechanical Stress on Piezo Crystal] --> P2[Charge Q generated on electrodes]
    P2 --> P3{Pickoff Mode}
    P3 -->|Voltage Mode| P4[High Impedance Voltage Follower]
    P3 -->|Charge Mode| P5[Charge Amplifier Op-Amp Circuit]
    P4 --> P6[AC Coupled Amplifier]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Bandpass Filter 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz]
    P7 --> P8[ADC Acquisition]
    P8 --> P9[Microcontroller FFT and Analysis]
    P9 --> P10[Display Vibration Amplitude and Frequency]
    P2 -.-> P11[Crystal Capacitance Cp parallel]
    P2 -.-> P12[Insulation Resistance Rp shunt]
```

### Mode Comparison Table (Embedded in the Architecture)

| Parameter | Voltage Mode | Charge Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit | Voltage follower | Op-amp with C_f feedback |
| Cable effect | Sensitive to cable C | Independent of cable C |
| Frequency response | Limited by cable C | Wide, set by op-amp |
| Best for | Short cables, lab use | Industrial, long cables |
| Lower cutoff | Set by R_p × C_p | Set by feedback R_f × C_f |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The choice between voltage mode and charge mode is a frequently asked concept. Always cite the **cable capacitance sensitivity** as the deciding factor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the gauge factor of a strain gauge. A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of 350 Ω and a gauge factor of 2.2. When subjected to a stress, its resistance changes by 0.7 Ω. Determine the strain experienced by the gauge.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The gauge factor (GF) of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (strain). It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates the sensitivity of the strain gauge.

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: R = 350 Ω, ΔR = 0.7 Ω, GF = 2.2

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{GF} = \frac{0.7 / 350}{2.2} = \frac{0.002}{2.2} = 9.09 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Strain ≈ 909 microstrain (μɛ)**

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical answer with units)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the direct piezoelectric effect. List any two piezoelectric materials with their typical d₃₃ values.**

**Model Answer:**

**Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** It is the phenomenon in which certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., quartz, PZT) generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress. The generated charge is proportional to the applied force:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where d is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (in C/N).

**Two Piezoelectric Materials:**

1. **Quartz (SiO₂):** d₃₃ ≈ 2.3 pC/N — high stability, low sensitivity
2. **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate):** d₃₃ ≈ 250–600 pC/N — high sensitivity, widely used

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + equation, 1 mark each for the two materials)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge from the fundamental resistance equation. Discuss the role of Poisson's ratio in the derived expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Wheatstone bridge used for strain measurement. Derive the expression for the output voltage when a single active strain gauge is used in one arm of the bridge. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Gauge Factor:**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

Starting from $R = \rho L / A$, taking the differential, normalizing, and applying the Poisson relationship:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Role of Poisson's Ratio (ν):**
- For metals, ν ≈ 0.3, so the geometric term (1 + 2ν) ≈ 1.6
- The piezoresistive term is negligible, giving GF ≈ 1.6 to 2.1 for typical alloys
- In semiconductors, the piezoresistive term dominates, making GF as high as 100–200
- Higher Poisson's ratio leads to greater resistance change for the same axial strain

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for Poisson's role, 2 for numerical example or comparison with semiconductor gauges)*

---

**(b) Wheatstone Bridge — Single Active Gauge:**

**Circuit Description:**
A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms (R₁, R₂, R₃, R₄) arranged in a diamond configuration. A DC excitation voltage V_ex is applied across one diagonal, and the output voltage V_out is measured across the other diagonal. In the single active gauge (quarter-bridge) configuration, R₁ is the active strain gauge while R₂, R₃, R₄ are fixed precision resistors of equal value R.

**Working:**
When R₁ changes by ΔR due to strain, the bridge becomes unbalanced. The two voltage dividers no longer produce equal potentials, and a differential voltage V_out appears.

**Output Voltage Derivation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

Substituting R₁ = R + ΔR, and R₂ = R₃ = R₄ = R:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

For small ΔR (ΔR ≪ R), the denominator simplifies to 4R:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

*(7 marks: 2 for circuit description, 3 for derivation, 1 for approximation, 1 for final simplified form)*

---

### Option B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with suitable diagrams. List four important piezoelectric materials and their typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric pressure sensor has a charge sensitivity of 20 pC/kPa. It is connected to a charge amplifier with a feedback capacitor of 1 nF. The sensor experiences a pressure change of 50 kPa. Calculate the output voltage of the charge amplifier. Also discuss why a charge amplifier is preferred over a voltage amplifier for piezoelectric sensors. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Direct and Converse Piezoelectric Effects:**

**Direct Effect:** When mechanical stress is applied to a piezoelectric crystal, electric charge appears on its opposite faces. The polarity of the charge reverses when the stress changes from compressive to tensile. This is described by $Q = d \cdot F$.

**Converse Effect:** When an electric field is applied across a piezoelectric crystal, the crystal undergoes mechanical deformation. The strain produced is proportional to the applied electric field: $\varepsilon = d \cdot E$ (using the same coefficient d). This effect is used in actuators, speakers, and ultrasonic transducers.

**Four Piezoelectric Materials:**

| Material | d₃₃ (pC/N) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Quartz (SiO₂) | 2.3 | High-stability accelerometers, oscillators |
| PZT (ceramic) | 300 | Force sensors, ultrasonic transducers |
| PVDF (polymer) | 30 | Flexible pressure sensors, medical |
| Rochelle Salt | 350 | Early phonograph pickups, low-frequency microphones |

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for direct + converse effects, 1 mark for the table of materials with 4 entries)*

---

**(b) Charge Amplifier Calculation and Discussion:**

**Given:**
- Charge sensitivity: d = 20 pC/kPa
- Feedback capacitor: C_f = 1 nF = 10⁻⁹ F
- Pressure change: ΔP = 50 kPa

**Step 1 — Charge generated by the sensor:**

$$Q = d \cdot \Delta P = 20 \ \text{pC/kPa} \times 50 \ \text{kPa} = 1000 \ \text{pC} = 1 \ \text{nC}$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage of charge amplifier:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{1 \ \text{nC}}{1 \ \text{nF}} = -1 \ \text{V}$$

**Output voltage ≈ −1 V** (the negative sign indicates phase inversion by the op-amp)

**Why Charge Amplifier is Preferred:**

1. **Cable Capacitance Independence:** The output voltage depends only on C_f, not on the cable capacitance between sensor and amplifier. This allows long cables without recalibration.
2. **Low-Frequency Response:** Voltage amplifiers suffer from low-frequency roll-off due to the time constant R_p · (C_p + C_cable). Charge amplifiers extend the low-frequency response.
3. **High Input Impedance:** The op-amp's MOSFET or JFET input stage presents near-infinite impedance, preventing charge leakage.
4. **Linearity:** With a stable C_f, the output is linearly proportional to input charge over a wide dynamic range.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for calculation, 3 marks for the four-point discussion, 2 marks for conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in Bridge Equation:** Students often forget the negative sign convention. The bridge output is **V_out = V_ex [R₁/(R₁+R₂) − R₄/(R₃+R₄)]**, not just the magnitude.
> 
> 2. **Unit Confusion in Piezoelectric Calculations:** Mixing pC, nC, μC, or using V instead of V/m/N for g-coefficient leads to catastrophic calculation errors. Always convert to SI units (C, F, V) before computing.
> 
> 3. **Static vs Dynamic Error:** A very common KTU question asks *why piezoelectric sensors cannot measure static pressure*. The answer is **charge leakage through insulation resistance R_p**, not a deficiency in the crystal itself. Charge amplifiers only partially solve this.
> 
> 4. **Gauge Factor Dimensionless Forgot:** Writing GF with units (e.g., "GF = 2.1 Ω/Ω") is a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction. Always state "GF is dimensionless."
> 
> 5. **Full-Bridge vs Half-Bridge Confusion:** A full-bridge with all four gauges active gives **4× the sensitivity** of a quarter-bridge, not 2×. The factor is the **number of active gauges**, not just doubled.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Strain Gauges
- Strain gauge converts **strain (ε) → resistance change (ΔR)**
- **Gauge Factor (GF)** is dimensionless: $GF = (\Delta R/R) / \varepsilon$
- For metals: GF ≈ 1 + 2ν ≈ 2.0
- For semiconductors: GF can be 50–200
- **Wheatstone bridge** converts ΔR → voltage
  - Quarter bridge: $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / 4R$
  - Half bridge: $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / 2R$
  - Full bridge: $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$
- **Temperature compensation** achieved by using dummy gauge in adjacent arm
- Applications: load cells, pressure transducers, torque sensors, structural health monitoring

### Piezoelectric Sensors
- **Direct effect:** stress → charge: $Q = d \cdot F$
- **Converse effect:** voltage → strain: $S = d \cdot E$
- **d-coefficient (charge):** C/N or pC/N
- **g-coefficient (voltage):** V·m/N, related by $g = d / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)$
- **Voltage mode:** $V_{oc} = g \cdot F \cdot t / A$ — sensitive to cable capacitance
- **Charge mode:** $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$ — independent of cable capacitance
- **Cannot measure static signals** (charge leaks through R_p)
- **Best for dynamic measurements:** vibration, acceleration, dynamic pressure
- **Common materials:** Quartz (stable), PZT (high sensitivity), PVDF (flexible)

### Key Differences at a Glance

| Feature | Strain Gauge | Piezoelectric Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Piezoresistive | Direct piezoelectric effect |
| Output type | Resistance change | Charge generation |
| Power needed | External excitation (V_ex) | Self-generating (active) |
| Static measurement | Yes (excellent) | No (poor) |
| Dynamic measurement | Good | Excellent (high bandwidth) |
| Sensitivity (typical) | GF ≈ 2, ΔR/R ≈ 0.001 | 20–500 pC/N |
| Signal conditioning | Wheatstone bridge | Charge amplifier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mantra:** "Strain gauge measures deformation continuously; piezoelectric sensor measures dynamic stress in bursts. Bridge for resistance, amplifier for charge."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - Microprocessors and Microcontrollers in Power Electronics
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Microprocessor** is a single-chip, VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) based central processing unit (CPU) that contains the arithmetic logic unit (ALU), control unit (CU), register array, decoder, timing and control circuitry, and bus interface logic on a single integrated circuit. It functions as the programmable "brain" of any microcomputer system, executing binary instructions fetched from external memory to perform arithmetic, logical, data-transfer, and control operations.

In the context of **PBPET604 (Power Electronics Applications)**, the **8086 Microprocessor** is studied as the standard 16-bit control engine used for digital firing-angle generation, PWM modulation, protection sequencing, and real-time monitoring of power electronic converters (e.g., rectifiers, choppers, inverters, and cyclo-converters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PBPET604 focuses on the **8086 Internal Architecture, Register Organisation, Memory Segmentation, Addressing Modes, and the Instruction Set of the 8086 microprocessor**. The mathematical/control relevance lies in using the 8086 to compute firing angles (α) for thyristor bridges in real-time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **post office sorting room**:
- The **clerk (ALU)** is the only person who can open, weigh, stamp, or count letters.
- The **supervisor (Control Unit)** reads the instruction slip attached to each letter and tells the clerk what to do.
- The **in-trays (Registers)** are small, fast-access desks where the clerk keeps letters currently being worked on.
- The **mail bags (External Memory)** contain thousands of letters waiting their turn.
- The **conveyor belts (Address, Data, Control Buses)** are the paths along which letters travel.

The microprocessor is *exactly* this post office, shrunk onto a fingernail-sized silicon chip using VLSI fabrication. For a power electronics engineer, this "post office" is what computes the precise moment to fire a thyristor in an HVDC link or a cyclo-converter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term – Bus:** A *bus* is a shared set of parallel conductors that act as the "conveyor belt" carrying address, data, or control signals. The 8086 has a **16-bit data bus**, a **20-bit address bus**, and a **16-bit control bus**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Clock frequency (8086):** **5 MHz, 8 MHz, or 10 MHz** (depending on variant: 8086, 8086-2, 8086-1).
- **Data bus width:** **16 bits** (D0–D15).
- **Address bus width:** **20 bits** (A0–A19).
- **Maximum addressable memory:** **2^20 = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB**.
- **Register size:** **16 bits** (most general-purpose registers).
- **Pin count (DIP package):** **40 pins**.
- **Technology:** **HMOS** (High-performance Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) — Intel's 3 µm process for the original 8086.
- **Operating voltage:** **+5 V DC** (single supply).
- **Address calculation time (memory access):** **4 T-states minimum** (1 T-state = 1 internal clock cycle = 2 oscillator periods → 200 ns at 10 MHz).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the 8086 (Block-Level Decomposition)

The 8086 microprocessor is internally divided into **two independent processing units** that operate in parallel through a **16-bit internal bus (the Bus Interface Unit–Execution Unit highway)**:

### 2.1.1 Bus Interface Unit (BIU)
The BIU handles *all* data, address, and instruction transfers with external buses and memory.

**Components of the BIU:**

1. **Instruction Queue (6-byte prefetch FIFO):**
   - Holds up to **6 pre-fetched instruction bytes**.
   - Implements **instruction pipelining** — while the EU is decoding/executing the *n*-th instruction, the BIU is fetching the *(n+1)*-th.
   - This is the origin of the famous 8086 efficiency claim: it executes instructions in **~2 T-states average** versus **~12 T-states** for the non-pipelined 8085.

2. **Segment Registers (×4) — each 16 bits:**
   - `CS` (Code Segment)
   - `DS` (Data Segment)
   - `SS` (Stack Segment)
   - `ES` (Extra Segment)

3. **Instruction Pointer (IP):** 16-bit offset into the Code Segment; points to the next instruction to be fetched (analogous to the 8085 PC).

4. **Address Generation Adder (20-bit):** Performs the canonical physical address calculation:
   $$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Segment Register} \times 16 + \text{Effective Address (Offset)}$$
   Multiplying by 16 is a **4-bit logical left-shift** (equivalent to appending `0000₂`).

5. **Bus Control Logic:** Manages RD, WR, M/IO, BHE, DEN, DT/R signals.

### 2.1.2 Execution Unit (EU)
The EU decodes and executes instructions, performing all arithmetic and logical operations.

**Components of the EU:**

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** 16-bit adder, logic unit, and shifter.
2. **Flag Register (16 bits, of which 9 are active):** Status and control flags.
3. **General-Purpose Registers (×8) — each 16 bits:**
   - Data registers: `AX`, `BX`, `CX`, `DX` (each split into two 8-bit halves: H = high byte, L = low byte → e.g., `AH`, `AL`).
   - Pointer registers: `SP` (Stack Pointer), `BP` (Base Pointer).
   - Index registers: `SI` (Source Index), `DI` (Destination Index).
4. **Decoder & Control Logic:** Converts machine opcodes into micro-operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why segment registers exist:** Since registers are 16 bits, they can address only 2^16 = 64 KB linearly. To reach the full **1 MB** address space, Intel introduced *segmentation* — the segment register is shifted left by 4 bits and added to a 16-bit offset, producing a 20-bit physical address.

## 2.2 Flag Register (Bit-by-Bit Map)

The 8086 **FLAGS** register is a 16-bit register, but only **9 bits are functional**:

| Bit | Flag | Type | Meaning |
|-----|------|------|---------|
| 0 | CF (Carry) | Status | Set if an unsigned arithmetic operation produces a carry/borrow. |
| 2 | PF (Parity) | Status | Set if the low 8 bits of the result contain an even number of 1s. |
| 4 | AF (Auxiliary Carry) | Status | Set on carry/borrow from bit 3 → used in BCD arithmetic. |
| 6 | ZF (Zero) | Status | Set if the result is zero. |
| 7 | SF (Sign) | Status | Mirror of the MSB of the result (1 = negative in two's complement). |
| 8 | TF (Trap) | Control | Single-step mode (used by debuggers). |
| 9 | IF (Interrupt Enable) | Control | Enables maskable hardware interrupts (INTR) when set. |
| 10 | DF (Direction) | Control | Direction for string instructions (0 = auto-increment, 1 = auto-decrement). |
| 11 | OF (Overflow) | Status | Set on signed arithmetic overflow. |

> Bits 1, 3, 5, 12, 13, 14, 15 are **reserved (undefined)** and read as `0` on a clean reset.

## 2.3 Memory Segmentation — The 20-bit Addressing Trick

### 2.3.1 Logical Address (the programmer's view)
A logical address on the 8086 is the 2-tuple:
$$\text{Logical Address} = (\text{Segment Selector}, \text{Effective Address})$$
For example, the instruction pointer always references `CS : IP`.

### 2.3.2 Physical Address (the hardware's view)
The 8086's internal 20-bit address adder computes:
$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment} \times 10_H) + \text{Effective Address (Offset)}$$

This is equivalent to: shift the segment register left by 4 binary positions (multiply by 2⁴ = 16), then add the 16-bit offset.

### 2.3.3 Default Segment Selection Rules

| Memory Reference Type | Default Segment | Default Offset |
|-----------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Instruction fetch | `CS` | `IP` |
| Stack operation (PUSH/POP/CALL/RET) | `SS` | `SP` |
| Data access (except BP-relative) | `DS` | Effective Address (EA) |
| Data access when EA is `[BP]` or `[BP+disp]` | `SS` | EA |
| String source (`MOVS`, `LODS`) | `DS` | `SI` |
| String destination (`MOVS`, `STOS`, `CMPS`) | `ES` | `DI` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Power Electronics Context:** A real-time firing-angle control program for a 3-phase thyristor bridge stores its lookup table for sin(α) in the **Data Segment (DS)**, while the 8086's interrupt service routine (ISR) for the zero-crossing detector is automatically pushed onto the **Stack Segment (SS)** when hardware interrupt INT0 fires.

## 2.4 Addressing Modes of the 8086

The 8086 has **8 primary addressing modes**. These determine *how* the Effective Address (EA) is calculated.

### Mode 1 — Register (Immediate-Register)
The operand is a register itself.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, BX` → `AX ← BX`
- No memory access; no EA calculation; execution in **2 clock cycles**.

### Mode 2 — Immediate
The operand is a constant encoded directly in the instruction stream.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, 25A3H` → `AX ← 25A3H` immediately
- The immediate value is part of the instruction's machine code.

### Mode 3 — Direct
The EA is given directly as a 16-bit displacement in the instruction.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, [2000H]` → EA = `2000H`; `AX ← DS:2000H`
- **Displacement = 16 bits** (no base/index register contribution).

### Mode 4 — Register Indirect
The EA is the *contents* of a base or index register.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, [BX]` → EA = `(BX)`; `AX ← DS:(BX)`
- Allowed registers: `BX`, `SI`, `DI`, `BP` (only these four).
- Used heavily in **table-walking and array access** in power-electronics lookup routines.

### Mode 5 — Based Relative
EA = Base register + 8-bit or 16-bit displacement.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, [BX + 4]` or `MOV AX, [BP + 6]`
- Used for accessing **structured data records** (e.g., a thyristor firing pulse descriptor with α, β, gate-pulse width, dead-time).

### Mode 6 — Indexed Relative
EA = Index register (SI or DI) + 8-bit or 16-bit displacement.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, [SI + 100H]`
- Ideal for **sequential table scanning** of a sine-PWM modulation table.

### Mode 7 — Based Indexed
EA = Base register + Index register.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, [BX + SI]` or `MOV AX, [BX + DI]`
- Useful for **2D arrays** (e.g., 3-phase firing matrix where rows = phase, columns = half-cycle).

### Mode 8 — Relative Based Indexed
EA = Base + Index + 8-bit or 16-bit displacement.
- Syntax: `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 4]`
- The most flexible mode; used for **complex data structures** like the PWM comparator descriptor.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Value |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Physical Address (PA) | `PA = Segment × 16 + Offset` | Decimal/hex address |
| 2 | Segment–Offset equivalence | `PA = (Segment << 4) + Offset` | Logical left-shift by 4 bits |
| 3 | Memory capacity | `2^20 = 1,048,576 = 1 MB` | Bytes |
| 4 | Single segment capacity | `2^16 = 65,536 = 64 KB` | Bytes |
| 5 | T-state duration | `T = 1 / f_CPU` (oscillator period is **2T**) | Seconds |
| 6 | Cycle duration (1 machine cycle) | 4 T-states minimum | Clock periods |
| 7 | 8086 instruction sizes | 1 to 6 bytes | Bytes |
| 8 | Stack growth | Decrementing (top moves to **lower** addresses) | — |
| 9 | Parity rule | Even parity of low 8 bits sets PF = 1 | — |
| 10 | Pipelined avg. execution | ~2 T-states/instruction | vs. 8085's 12 T-states |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-tip:** In KTU valuation, when asked to compute a physical address, write the equation `PA = Segment × 10H + Offset` *first*, then substitute values. Examiners reward the *method* over the *number*.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8086 architecture underpins the design of **digital controllers for power electronic converters**:

1. **Thyristor Firing Schemes:** The 8086 reads a zero-crossing detector through an interrupt line, computes α from a user-set potentiometer (via ADC), waits the calculated delay via a software loop, then outputs a gate pulse to the SCR through an I/O port.
2. **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM):** Modern SVPWM for three-phase VSI uses the 8086 to compute sector identification, dwell times T₁, T₂, and T₀, and switch vector sequences — all in real-time (sub-100 µs for 1 kHz fundamental).
3. **Digital Protection Relays:** Over-current, earth-fault, and differential protections are coded in 8086 assembly with sub-µs response times.
4. **HVDC and FACTS:** The firing-angle references for line-commutated converters in HVDC stations are computed digitally using 8086-class processors (or modern descendants in the x86 family).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Physical Address Calculation

We will derive the 8086 physical address from the *logical address* given as `1234 : 5678 H`.

### Step 1: State the canonical formula
The 8086 BIU computes the 20-bit physical address by **left-shifting the segment register by 4 bit positions** and then **adding the 16-bit effective address (offset)**.

$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 2^4) + \text{Effective Address}$$

### Step 2: Convert hexadecimal to binary
Write both numbers in binary to make the left-shift visible.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment} &= 1234_H = 0001\,0010\,0011\,0100_2 \\
\text{Offset}  &= 5678_H = 0101\,0110\,0111\,1000_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Perform the logical left-shift by 4 bits
Append four zero bits to the right of the segment value (this is *exactly* multiplication by 2⁴ = 16).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment} \ll 4 &= 0001\,0010\,0011\,0100\,\underbrace{0000}_{4\text{ zeros}} \\
                     &= 0001\,0010\,0011\,0100\,0000_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert the shifted segment back to hexadecimal
Group the binary into 4-bit nibbles from the right:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0001\,0010\,0011\,0100\,0000_2 &= 1\,2\,3\,4\,0_H \\
&= 12340_H
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** This shows that multiplying the segment by 16 is the same as appending one hex `0` to the right of the segment value. So `1234H × 10H = 12340H`.

### Step 5: Add the offset in hexadecimal
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= 12340_H + 5678_H \\
&= 179B8_H
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Cross-check by decimal arithmetic
$$
\begin{aligned}
1234_H &= 1 \times 16^3 + 2 \times 16^2 + 3 \times 16^1 + 4 \times 16^0 \\
       &= 4096 + 512 + 48 + 4 = 4660_{10} \\
5678_H &= 5 \times 16^3 + 6 \times 16^2 + 7 \times 16^1 + 8 \times 16^0 \\
       &= 20480 + 1536 + 112 + 8 = 22136_{10} \\
4660 \times 16 &= 74560_{10} \\
74560 + 22136 &= 96696_{10} \\
96696_{10} &\stackrel{?}{=} 179B8_H \\
1 \times 16^4 &= 65536 \\
7 \times 16^3 &= 28672 \\
9 \times 16^2 &= 2304 \\
B \times 16^1 &= 11 \times 16 = 176 \\
8 \times 16^0 &= 8 \\
\text{Sum} &= 65536 + 28672 + 2304 + 176 + 8 = 96696_{10} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Both methods yield 179B8 H = 96696 decimal.** The result is a 20-bit address (`1 0111 1001 1011 1000₂`), which lies in the range `[00000 H, FFFFF H]` — exactly the **1 MB** boundary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address (EA) for Each Addressing Mode

The 8086 BIU uses the following table to compute EA. Each mode corresponds to a specific **mod–r/m byte** in the machine instruction.

| Mode | Effective Address (EA) Formula |
|------|-------------------------------|
| Direct | `EA = disp16` |
| Register Indirect (BX) | `EA = (BX)` |
| Register Indirect (BP) | `EA = (BP)` |
| Register Indirect (SI) | `EA = (SI)` |
| Register Indirect (DI) | `EA = (DI)` |
| Based Relative | `EA = (BX) + disp8/disp16` or `EA = (BP) + disp8/disp16` |
| Indexed Relative | `EA = (SI) + disp8/disp16` or `EA = (DI) + disp8/disp16` |
| Based Indexed | `EA = (BX) + (SI)` or `EA = (BX) + (DI)` or `EA = (BP) + (SI)` or `EA = (BP) + (DI)` |
| Relative Based Indexed | `EA = (BX) + (SI) + disp8/disp16` or any permutation |

### Worked Example: EA Calculation for a PWM Duty-Cycle Lookup

Suppose we store a 256-byte sine table starting at logical address `2000 : 1000 H`. To read the table at index `i` (held in `SI`):

1. The programmer writes: `MOV AL, [SI + 1000H]`
2. The assembler chooses DS as default segment since `[SI]` is used (no BP).
3. Effective Address = `(SI) + 1000 H`
4. Physical Address = `DS × 10 H + (SI) + 1000 H`

For a 256-point sine table with `i = 50` (0x32) and `DS = 2000 H`, `SI = 0032 H`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= 20000_H + 0032_H + 1000_H \\
         &= 21032_H
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Power-Electronics Insight:** The 8086's indexed addressing mode is the *exact* hardware mechanism needed for **direct digital synthesis of SPWM** — a pointer (`SI`) walks the sine table at a variable rate (proportional to the fundamental frequency), and the lookup value becomes the modulating wave.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 8086 vs 8085 (KTU Favourite Comparison)

| Parameter | 8085 | 8086 |
|-----------|------|------|
| Data bus | 8-bit | 16-bit |
| Address bus | 16-bit | 20-bit |
| Addressable memory | 64 KB | 1 MB |
| Register size | 8-bit (A, B, C, D, E, H, L) | 16-bit (AX, BX, CX, DX, SP, BP, SI, DI) |
| Segment registers | None | 4 (CS, DS, SS, ES) |
| Pipelining | None | 6-byte prefetch queue |
| Avg. T-states/instruction | 10–12 | 4–5 |
| Operating clock | 3 MHz typical | 5–10 MHz typical |
| Instruction size | 1, 2, or 3 bytes | 1 to 6 bytes |
| Pin count | 40 | 40 (DIP) |
| Flag register | 8-bit (5 flags) | 16-bit (9 active flags) |
| Multimode operation | No | Minimum/Maximum mode via MN/MX pin |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: T-state, Machine Cycle, and Instruction Cycle Relationship

### Definitions
- **Clock cycle (oscillator period):** Period of the external crystal, e.g., 200 ns for a 5 MHz crystal.
- **T-state:** Internal time period; one T-state = **½ oscillator period** = 100 ns at 5 MHz.
- **Machine cycle (MC):** Basic bus operation (memory read, memory write, I/O read, I/O write, etc.) consuming **4 T-states minimum**.
- **Instruction cycle (IC):** Total time to fetch, decode, execute, and store results for one instruction. Composed of 1 to many machine cycles.

### Relationship
$$\text{Instruction Cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Machine Cycle}_i$$
where each Machine Cycle = **4 T-states × 1/(2·f_oscillator)**.

### Worked Example
The instruction `MOV AX, [2000H]` (a 3-byte, 2-machine-cycle instruction) at 5 MHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T-state duration} &= \frac{1}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6} = 100 \text{ ns} \\
\text{MC duration} &= 4 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 400 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Instruction duration} &= 2 \text{ MC} = 800 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU valuation tip:** When a question asks "find the time taken by an instruction", always start by writing the T-state duration equation before any numbers. The KTU model answer scheme deducts 1 mark if units are missing.

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given the logical address `CS : IP = 2000 H : 1020 H`, find the physical address of the next instruction to be fetched. State the bus cycle timing if the 8086 runs at 8 MHz.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= (\text{CS} \times 10_H) + \text{IP} \\
         &= (2000 \times 10)_H + 1020_H \\
         &= 20000_H + 1020_H \\
         &= 21020_H
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a 20-bit address: `0010 0001 0000 0010 0000₂`.

For the bus timing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oscillator period} &= \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6} = 125 \text{ ns} \\
\text{T-state} &= \frac{125}{2} = 62.5 \text{ ns} \\
\text{One machine cycle (4 T)} &= 4 \times 62.5 = 250 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since instruction prefetch is pipelined, the effective *latency* of any single instruction fetch is just **1 machine cycle = 250 ns**, even though the absolute instruction cycle may span several machine cycles.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the 8086 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIU["BUS INTERFACE UNIT (BIU)"]
        direction TB
        nodeQueue["6-Byte Prefetch Queue (FIFO)"]
        nodeSeg["Segment Registers: CS, DS, SS, ES"]
        nodeIP["Instruction Pointer (IP)"]
        nodeAdder["20-bit Address Adder"]
        nodeBusCtrl["Bus Control Logic: RD, WR, M/IO, BHE, DEN, DT/R"]
        nodeSeg --> nodeAdder
        nodeIP --> nodeAdder
        nodeAdder --> nodeBusCtrl
    end

    subgraph IBUS["INTERNAL 16-BIT DATA BUS (BIU-EU HIGHWAY)"]
        nodeIBUS["Instruction and Operand Stream"]
    end

    subgraph EU["EXECUTION UNIT (EU)"]
        direction TB
        nodeALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) 16-bit"]
        nodeFlags["Flag Register (FLAGS) - 9 active bits"]
        nodeGPR["General Purpose Registers: AX, BX, CX, DX"]
        nodePtr["Pointer Registers: SP, BP"]
        nodeIdx["Index Registers: SI, DI"]
        nodeDecode["Instruction Decoder and Timing Logic"]
        nodeDecode --> nodeALU
        nodeGPR --> nodeALU
        nodeALU --> nodeFlags
    end

    nodeQueue -- "Instruction bytes" --> IBUS
    IBUS --> nodeDecode
    nodeBusCtrl <--> nodeAdder
    nodeFlags -. "Status feedback" .-> nodeDecode
```

## 4.2 Physical Address Generation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[16-bit Segment Register: e.g. CS] --> B["Logical Left Shift by 4 bits"]
    B --> C["Append 0000 to LSBs"]
    C --> D["20-bit Seg x 16 Value"]
    E[16-bit Effective Address Offset: e.g. IP] --> F["Sign Extended to 20 bits"]
    F --> G["Add at BIU Adder"]
    D --> G
    G --> H["20-bit Physical Address Bus A0-A19"]
    H --> I["External Memory or I/O Device Selected"]
```

## 4.3 8086 Memory Segmentation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["8086 1 MB Address Space (00000 H to FFFFF H)"]
        direction TB
        segA["Segment 1: 0000 H - F000 H range"]
        segB["Segment 2: 0000 H - F000 H range"]
        segC["Segment 3: 0000 H - F000 H range"]
        segD["Segment 4: 0000 H - F000 H range"]
        ovA["Overlap zone between adjacent segments"]
        ovB["Each 64 KB block can be entered through 16 different Segment:Offset pairs"]
    end
    segA ~~~ segB
    segB ~~~ segC
    segC ~~~ segD
    segD ~~~ ovA
    ovA ~~~ ovB
```

> **Key Insight from the diagram:** The same physical address can be reached by **4096 = 2¹² different segment–offset pairs** in the worst case (since 2⁴ × 2¹² = 2¹⁶ = 65 536, but each segment has 16 valid starting positions to address any given byte via the 4-bit shift). This redundancy is intentional — it allows the 8086 assembler to keep code, data, and stack in **relocatable 64 KB windows** without re-linking.

## 4.4 Power-Electronics Control: 8086 Interfaced to a 3-Phase Thyristor Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["8086 Microprocessor Core"]
    ZCD["Zero-Crossing Detector Circuit"]
    ADC["ADC 0808 (8-bit ADC)"]
    POT["Potentiometer - Sets Alpha"]
    ISO["Optocoupler Isolator MOC3021"]
    GATE["Thyristor Gate Pulses T1 to T6"]
    BRIDGE["3-Phase Thyristor Bridge Rectifier"]

    ZCD -->|"Square wave at line zero crossings"| CPU
    POT -->|"Reference voltage 0-5V"| ADC
    ADC -->|"8-bit digital value"| CPU
    CPU -->|"Calculated firing delay alpha"| ISO
    ISO -->|"Isolated gate pulses"| GATE
    GATE --> BRIDGE
    BRIDGE -->|"DC output to load"| LOAD["DC Load Motor or Resistive Load"]
```

> **Functional Walkthrough:**
> 1. The 8086's **INT0** line receives a hardware interrupt from the ZCD at every zero crossing of the AC mains.
> 2. Inside the ISR, the 8086 reads the ADC value (α command) via an `IN` instruction.
> 3. A software delay loop is executed for the time equivalent to α (e.g., 1° ≈ 55.5 µs at 50 Hz).
> 4. After the delay, a `OUT` instruction fires the corresponding SCR gate through the optocoupler.
> 5. The DC output voltage is `V_dc = (3√2·V_LL/π)·cos(α)`.
