# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**Define the term *microprocessor* and list the four segment registers of the 8086.**

**Model Answer:**
A microprocessor is a single-chip VLSI device that integrates the arithmetic logic unit, control unit, registers, decoder, and bus interface onto one IC, acting as the CPU of a microcomputer system.
The four segment registers of the 8086 are: **CS** (Code Segment), **DS** (Data Segment), **SS** (Stack Segment), and **ES** (Extra Segment). Each is 16 bits wide and is used in conjunction with a 16-bit offset to generate the 20-bit physical address.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat diagram, explain the function of the instruction queue in the 8086.**

**Model Answer:**
The 8086 contains a **6-byte FIFO instruction queue** inside its Bus Interface Unit. While the Execution Unit is decoding and executing the current instruction, the BIU pre-fetches the next instruction bytes from the memory pointed to by `CS:IP` and stores them in the queue. This **instruction pipelining** overlaps fetch and execute phases, reducing the average instruction execution time from ~12 T-states (as in the 8085) to ~4 T-states. The queue also automatically refills itself whenever one byte is consumed and 2 or more empty slots exist.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of the 8086 microprocessor with a neat block diagram. Highlight the roles of the BIU and EU. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the memory segmentation scheme of the 8086. Given the logical address `3000 H : 1234 H`, compute the physical address. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

The 8086 is internally partitioned into two units operating in parallel:

**1. Bus Interface Unit (BIU):** Responsible for all bus activities — fetching instructions, reading/writing data from/to memory or I/O ports, and computing physical addresses. Major blocks: 6-byte prefetch queue, four 16-bit segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES), 16-bit instruction pointer (IP), 20-bit address adder, and bus control logic (RD, WR, M/IO, BHE, DEN, DT/R).

**2. Execution Unit (EU):** Decodes the opcodes fetched by the BIU and executes them using the 16-bit ALU. It contains the 16-bit flag register, the eight general-purpose registers (AX, BX, CX, DX, SP, BP, SI, DI), and the instruction decoder.

**Function-flow:** A 6-byte queue decouples fetch from execute, implementing a *primitive pipeline*. The EU pulls instructions from the queue via a 16-bit internal data bus, eliminating the need to wait for external memory during decode/execute.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Segmentation Concept:** Because registers are only 16 bits (max 64 KB linear addressing), the 8086 extends addressing to 1 MB using *segmentation*. Every memory reference is composed of:

- A **segment base** (16-bit segment register, multiplied by 16).
- A 16-bit **effective address (offset)** within that segment.

The BIU computes the 20-bit physical address as:
$$\text{PA} = (\text{Segment} \times 10_H) + \text{Offset}$$

**Default segment rules:** Instructions → `CS:IP`; Stack → `SS:SP`; Data → `DS:EA`; Strings source/destination → `DS:SI` / `ES:DI`.

**Numerical Computation for `3000 H : 1234 H`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= 30000_H + 1234_H \\
         &= 31234_H
\end{aligned}
$$

In binary, this is `0011 0001 0010 0011 0100₂` (a valid 20-bit address).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. *Forgetting to multiply by 10H* — many students write 3000H + 1234H = 4234H, which is wrong and loses 2 marks.
> 2. *Confusing the segment register* — for a code reference, students often use DS instead of CS, losing 1 mark.
> 3. *Not stating the default segment rules* — full 7 marks requires both the rule *and* the numerical answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** List and explain the addressing modes of the 8086 microprocessor with one example each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe how an 8086 microprocessor is interfaced to a 3-phase thyristor bridge rectifier for phase-angle control. State the relevant firing-angle equation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

The 8086 supports **8 addressing modes**:

1. **Register:** Operand is a register. Example: `MOV AX, BX` → `AX ← BX`.
2. **Immediate:** Constant in the instruction. Example: `MOV CX, 0A5FH`.
3. **Direct:** Memory address given directly. Example: `MOV AX, [2000H]`.
4. **Register Indirect:** Address in a base/index register. Example: `MOV AX, [BX]`.
5. **Based Relative:** Base + displacement. Example: `MOV AX, [BP + 4]`.
6. **Indexed Relative:** Index + displacement. Example: `MOV AX, [SI + 100H]`.
7. **Based Indexed:** Base + Index. Example: `MOV AX, [BX + SI]`.
8. **Relative Based Indexed:** Base + Index + displacement. Example: `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 4]`.

The default segment for modes 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 (when not using BP) is **DS**; for BP-relative addressing it is **SS**; for string source it is **DS**; for string destination it is **ES**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.4 of these notes for the full mermaid schematic.) The 8086 reads:
- A **zero-crossing detector** through INT0 (phase reference).
- A **potentiometer (via ADC0808)** through I/O port for the α command.

**Algorithm:**
1. On INT0 (zero crossing), enter the ISR.
2. Read α command from the ADC.
3. Convert α to a delay: `T_delay = (α / 180°) × (T_mains / 2)`.
   - For 50 Hz: `T_mains/2 = 10 ms`, so `1° ≈ 55.56 µs`.
4. Execute a software delay (e.g., inner/outer loop counter).
5. Output gate pulse to the appropriate SCR through a port pin driving an MOC3021 optocoupler.
6. Repeat for all six SCRs with 60° phase spacing for a 3-phase bridge.

**Firing-angle equation** for a fully controlled 3-phase bridge:
$$
V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\,V_{LL}\cos(\alpha) \quad \text{for } 0^\circ \le \alpha \le 90^\circ
$$
where `V_LL` is the RMS line-to-line AC voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Skipping the isolation stage* — connecting the 8086 directly to SCR gates will fail the isolation criterion. Always mention the **opto-coupler (MOC3021/MOC3051)** for safety.
> 2. *Not computing the delay correctly* — for a 3-phase bridge the zero-crossing-to-firing interval is **α**, not `180° − α`. Many students invert this.
> 3. *Forgetting the 60° phase shift* between thyristor pairs — the full firing sequence T1→T2→T3→T4→T5→T6 occurs every 60°.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 8086 Microprocessor Module 2**

- [x] **8086 = 16-bit data bus, 20-bit address bus, 1 MB addressable memory.**
- [x] **Two units:** BIU (fetch + addressing) and EU (decode + execute). They communicate over a 16-bit internal bus.
- [x] **6-byte prefetch queue** is the heart of 8086 pipelining — reduces effective T-states per instruction.
- [x] **Segment registers:** CS (code), DS (data), SS (stack), ES (extra). Each 16 bits, base of a 64 KB window.
- [x] **Physical Address Formula:** `PA = (Segment × 10H) + Offset`. Equivalent to left-shifting the segment by 4 bits and adding the offset.
- [x] **Default segment rules:** CS:IP for instruction fetch; SS:SP for stack; DS:EA for data; DS:SI and ES:DI for string operations; SS:BP for BP-relative.
- [x] **8 Addressing Modes:** Register, Immediate, Direct, Register Indirect, Based Relative, Indexed Relative, Based Indexed, Relative Based Indexed.
- [x] **Flag Register (16 bits):** 9 active — CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF (status); TF, IF, DF (control).
- [x] **T-state duration** = 1 / (2 × f_oscillator). One machine cycle = minimum 4 T-states.
- [x] **Instruction sizes** range from **1 to 6 bytes**.
- [x] **Power-electronics application:** 8086 can compute real-time firing angle α for thyristor bridges, generate SPWM, and execute SVPWM algorithms for VSI.
- [x] **KTU answer-presentation rule:** Always state the *formula* before substituting numerical values; examiners give **method marks** even if the final number is wrong.
- [x] **Memory capacity constants to memorize:** 2^20 = 1 MB; 2^16 = 64 KB; 2^4 = 16 (segment shift).
- [x] **For sine-PWM lookup tables** in power electronics: prefer *Indexed Relative* mode (`[SI + disp]`) because `SI` auto-increments per sample.
- [x] **Reset vector of 8086:** `FFFF0 H` (16 bytes below the top of the 1 MB address space) — the first instruction after reset is fetched from this address.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question gives a logical address and asks for the physical address, *always* write the formula `PA = Segment × 10H + Offset` *first*, then the substitution, then the hex addition, and finally the binary verification (optional but shows depth and earns full marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microcontroller (μC / MCU):** A *single-chip, self-contained, programmable digital computing device* that integrates a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **program memory (ROM/Flash)**, **data memory (RAM)**, **input/output (I/O) ports**, **timers/counters**, and **communication peripherals** onto a single integrated circuit (IC) optimized for embedded control applications.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, a microcontroller is treated as the **heart of any embedded system** — a "computer-on-a-chip" designed to perform a *specific, dedicated task* rather than general-purpose computing.

### Key Terminology Distilled

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|------|------------------------|
| **Embedded System** | A computer system that is part of a larger device, dedicated to a specific function (e.g., washing machine, ECU in a car). |
| **System on Chip (SoC)** | A more advanced descendant of the MCU, integrating RF, GPU, and high-speed peripherals. |
| **Firmware** | The low-level program (machine code) permanently stored in the MCU's non-volatile memory. |
| **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** | The set of native machine-level commands the CPU understands (e.g., 8051 ISA, ARM Cortex-M ISA). |
| **RISC / CISC** | Reduced / Complex Instruction Set Computer — two competing MCU design philosophies. |

---

## 1.2 Microcontroller vs. Microprocessor (Conceptual Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife vs. The Carpenter's Toolkit:**
> - A **microprocessor (MPU)** is like a *fully equipped carpenter's toolkit*: a powerful CPU with no built-in tools. You must buy and connect RAM, ROM, timers, ADC, and I/O separately on a motherboard (e.g., desktop PC using Intel i7).
> - A **microcontroller (MCU)** is like a *Swiss Army knife*: CPU + memory + I/O + timers are all *built into a single, compact, low-power device*, ready to be embedded into any appliance (e.g., a microwave oven using an 8051).

This is precisely why MCUs dominate **embedded applications** while MPUs dominate **general-purpose computing**.

### 1.2.1 Tabular Comparison

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Core blocks on chip** | Only CPU | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O + Timers + ADC |
| **External components needed** | RAM, ROM, I/O controller, bus drivers | Usually *none* (self-contained) |
| **Cost** | High (system-level) | Very low (chip-level) |
| **Power consumption** | High (watts to tens of watts) | Low (milliwatts to microwatts in sleep) |
| **Clock speed** | 1 GHz – 5 GHz+ | 1 MHz – 200 MHz (typical) |
| **Bit-width** | 32 / 64 bit | 8 / 16 / 32 bit |
| **Primary use** | PCs, laptops, servers | Washing machines, cars, IoT, robotics |
| **Example** | Intel Core i7, AMD Ryzen | 8051, PIC16F877, ARM Cortex-M0 |
| **KTU-relevant example** | — | **Intel 8051** (most-tested) |

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold for Recall)

- **Operating Voltage (V\_CC)**: typically **+5 V DC** (classic 8051) or **+3.3 V DC** (modern ARM MCUs).
- **Logic HIGH level**: **V\_IH ≥ 2.0 V** (for 5 V CMOS logic).
- **Logic LOW level**: **V\_IL ≤ 0.8 V** (for 5 V CMOS logic).
- **Standard crystal frequencies**: **11.0592 MHz**, **12 MHz**, **16 MHz**, **24 MHz** (the value **11.0592 MHz** is intentionally chosen to generate *exact* standard UART baud rates such as 9600 bps).
- **Machine Cycle frequency** (8051): f\_{machine} = f\_{osc} / **12**.
- **Standard baud rates used in KTU problems**: **9600 bps**, **4800 bps**, **1200 bps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always quote the value **11.0592 MHz** when solving UART/baud-rate numericals — it is a "favourite number" of 8051 problems in KTU question papers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Classifications (Why & How)

### 2.1.1 Von Neumann Architecture (Princeton)

- A **single, common bus** is used to fetch *both* instructions and data.
- The CPU alternates between *instruction fetch* and *data fetch* → called the **Von Neumann Bottleneck**.
- **Why used:** Simpler hardware, cheaper to design.
- **Example MCU:** Most 8-bit PIC microcontrollers (modified Harvard).

### 2.1.2 Harvard Architecture

- Uses **two physically separate bus systems**:
  1. **Program bus** → reads instructions from code memory.
  2. **Data bus** → reads/writes data memory.
- Both can be accessed *simultaneously* → **faster execution** (no bottleneck).
- **Why used:** Deterministic timing (crucial for real-time embedded control).
- **Example MCU:** **Intel 8051** (uses *modified Harvard* — code memory is read-only, data memory is read/write, but the bus is unified through a special path).

> [!NOTE]
> **Modified Harvard (8051-specific):** Code and data are in *physically separate address spaces* (4 KB internal ROM + 64 KB external code memory, 128 B internal RAM + 64 KB external data memory), but the CPU can read code and read/write data in the *same* machine cycle through a dedicated internal path.

---

## 2.2 Block Diagram of a Generic Microcontroller (The 8051 Model)

A typical 8-bit MCU (8051 family) contains the following on-chip peripherals:

1. **8-bit CPU (ALU + Accumulator + Registers + Decoder)**
2. **Program Memory (ROM/Flash)** — 4 KB on-chip (8051)
3. **Data Memory (RAM)** — 128 B internal (8051)
4. **Four 8-bit I/O Ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3 (32 I/O lines total)
5. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — T0 and T1
6. **Full-Duplex UART** (Serial Port) for asynchronous communication
7. **Interrupt Controller** — 5 interrupt sources (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial)
8. **Oscillator & Clock Circuit** — connects to external crystal
9. **Bus Control Unit** — for external memory interfacing
10. **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — memory-mapped control of peripherals

---

## 2.3 Addressing Modes of the 8051 (KTU High-Yield)

The 8051 supports **five distinct addressing modes** (and 1 implicit). Every KTU question paper has at least one addressing-mode question.

| # | Mode | Syntax Example (8051) | Purpose |
|---|------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `MOV A, #25H` | Loads a *constant* into the accumulator. |
| 2 | **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | Operates on one of the 8 working registers (R0–R7). |
| 3 | **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | Accesses any of the 128 internal RAM bytes (00H–7FH) or SFRs (80H–FFH). |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | Uses R0/R1 (or DPTR) as a *pointer* to data. |
| 5 | **Indexed** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Used for *table lookups* (PC or DPTR as base). |
| — | **Implicit** | `CPL A` (complement A) | Operates on the accumulator *only* — operand is implied. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse **Register Indirect** (`@R0` — *the contents of R0 is the address*) with **Direct** (`30H` — *30H is itself the address*). This is one of the highest-weighted items in KTU Part A.

---

## 2.4 Memory Organization of the 8051 (A KTU Favourite)

The 8051 has a **segregated, three-address-space** memory model:

### (a) Internal RAM (128 B, addresses 00H–7FH)
- **00H–1FH** → Four register banks (Bank 0 to Bank 3), each 8 bytes (R0–R7).
- **20H–2FH** → 16 bytes *bit-addressable* RAM (128 individually addressable bits).
- **30H–7FH** → 80 bytes of *general-purpose* scratchpad RAM.

### (b) Special Function Registers (SFRs, addresses 80H–FFH)
- **Accumulator (A)**: E0H, **B register**: F0H
- **Stack Pointer (SP)**: 81H (resets to **07H** on power-up)
- **Data Pointer (DPTR)**: 82H (DPL) + 83H (DPH)
- **Program Counter (PC)**: 16-bit, *not* SFR-mapped (auto-increments).
- **Port latches**: P0 (80H), P1 (90H), P2 (A0H), P3 (B0H).
- **Timer registers**: TL0, TH0, TL1, TH1, TMOD, TCON.
- **Serial port**: SCON, SBUF.
- **Interrupt**: IE, IP.
- **Power control**: PCON.

### (c) Program Memory (64 KB max, addresses 0000H–FFFFH)
- **0000H** → Reset vector (CPU jumps here on power-up).
- **0003H** → External Interrupt 0 (INT0) vector.
- **000BH** → Timer 0 overflow vector.
- **0013H** → External Interrupt 1 (INT1) vector.
- **001BH** → Timer 1 overflow vector.
- **0023H** → Serial Port Interrupt vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If asked "How many bytes of on-chip data memory does 8051 have?" — answer is **128 B internal RAM + 128 B SFR space = 256 B total** (the SFR space is accessed only through direct addressing and is *not* general-purpose RAM).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Typical Values |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Machine cycle period** (8051) | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | f\_{osc} in Hz |
| 2 | **Instruction cycle count** | varies: 1 / 2 / 4 machine cycles per instruction | MC | 8051 instructions take 1, 2 or 4 MC |
| 3 | **Timer clock frequency** | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH0)}$ (8-bit auto-reload Mode 2) | Hz | When 65536 is *not* involved |
| 4 | **Timer delay (Mode 1, 16-bit)** | $t = (65536 - N) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | N = Timer initial value |
| 5 | **Timer delay (Mode 2, 8-bit auto-reload)** | $t = (256 - N) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | Auto-reloads from TH0 |
| 6 | **Baud rate generation (Mode 1, Timer 1, auto-reload)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps | SMOD = 0 or 1 (PCON.7) |
| 7 | **Standard crystal (8051)** | $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ | Hz | Gives *exact* 9600 baud |
| 8 | **Effective CPU clock** | $f_{CPU} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Hz | For 8051 only |
| 9 | **Program memory addressing range** | $0$ to $2^{n} - 1$ where n = address bus width | bytes | n = 16 → 64 KB |
| 10 | **Data memory addressing range** | $0$ to $2^{n} - 1$ | bytes | 8051 internal = 128 B (n = 7) |
| 11 | **External memory addressing (8051)** | Uses P0 (low addr + data multiplexed) + P2 (high addr) | — | Latch 74HC573 used for ALE |
| 12 | **Power-down current (80C51)** | $I_{PD} \approx 50 \text{ μA}$ | amps | Idle/Power-down modes |
| 13 | **Stack growth direction** | Stack grows **upward** (towards higher addresses) | — | PUSH increments SP |
| 14 | **PUSH/POP effect** | SP → SP+1 on PUSH; SP → SP-1 on POP | bytes | Stack pointer at 07H on reset |

> [!TIP]
> **Why is 11.0592 MHz special?**
> For SMOD = 0 and TH1 = 253 (−3 in unsigned, i.e., 0xFD):
> $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{1}{32} \times \dfrac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 3} = 9600 \text{ bps}$ — *exact integer*, no rounding error. This is why **all 8051 serial communication labs use 11.0592 MHz**.

---

## 2.6 Interrupt System of the 8051

The 8051 has **5 interrupt sources**, each with a fixed vector address:

| Source | Flag | Vector Address | Default Priority |
|--------|------|----------------|------------------|
| Reset | — | 0000H | Highest |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 | 0003H | 1 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | 000BH | 2 |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 | 0013H | 3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | 001BH | 4 |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI | 0023H | 5 (lowest) |

### Key SFRs:
- **IE (Interrupt Enable, A8H)** — master bit EA + individual enables.
- **TCON (Timer/Counter Control, 88H)** — IT0/IT1 select edge-triggered (ITx = 1) or level-triggered (ITx = 0).
- **IP (Interrupt Priority, B8H)** — PT0, PX0, PT1, PX1, PS bits to set high/low priority.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Without setting **EA = 1** in the IE register, *no* interrupt will be serviced, even if individual bits are set. This is a classic KTU Part A trap question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle Period from Crystal Frequency

We derive the fundamental timing unit of the 8051.

**Given:**
- Crystal (external oscillator) frequency: $f_{osc}$ (in Hz).
- The 8051 internally divides this frequency by **12** to derive the *one machine cycle*.

**Step 1** — Express one oscillator period:

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} \quad \text{(seconds per oscillator clock pulse)}
$$

**Step 2** — The 8051's internal timing logic counts **12 oscillator pulses** to produce *one machine cycle*:

$$
T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc}
$$

**Step 3** — Substitute $T_{osc} = 1 / f_{osc}$:

$$
T_{MC} = 12 \times \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

**Step 4** — Final compact form (boxed for the answer sheet):

$$
\boxed{\,T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}\ \text{seconds}\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical (KTU Standard)

**Q:** An 8051 system uses a 12 MHz crystal. Calculate the time taken to execute **one** instruction that requires **2 machine cycles**.

**Step 1** — Machine cycle period:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2** — Time for 2 machine cycles:

$$
T_{inst} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times 1\ \mu s = 2\ \mu s
$$

**Answer:** **2 μs** per instruction. (Note: with a 12 MHz crystal, the convention "1 μs per machine cycle" is a useful sanity check.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Timer Delay in Mode 1 (16-bit)

**Given:**
- Crystal frequency $f_{osc}$.
- Timer initial value = $N$ (a 16-bit value loaded into TH|TL).
- Mode 1: 16-bit timer that overflows from FFFFH → 0000H.

**Step 1** — Number of timer clock counts needed to reach overflow:

$$
\text{Counts to overflow} = 65536 - N
$$

(For example, if $N = 0$ (i.e., TH = TL = 0), the timer counts all the way from 0 to 65535, then overflows at 65536 counts.)

**Step 2** — Time per *single timer count* (timer is clocked at $f_{osc}/12$):

$$
T_{count} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \quad \text{(this is one machine cycle)}
$$

**Step 3** — Total time delay before overflow:

$$
t_{delay} = \text{Counts} \times T_{count}
$$

$$
t_{delay} = (65536 - N) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

**Step 4** — Boxed result:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{delay} = (65536 - N) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}\ \text{seconds}\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical

**Q:** Calculate the value to be loaded into Timer 0 (Mode 1) to generate a delay of **50 ms** with an 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Step 1** — Identify $T_{count}$:

$$
T_{count} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\ \mu s \approx 1.085 \times 10^{-6}\ s
$$

**Step 2** — Required counts:

$$
\text{Counts} = \frac{t_{delay}}{T_{count}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080
$$

**Step 3** — Timer initial value:

$$
N = 65536 - 46080 = 19456
$$

**Step 4** — Split into TH0 and TL0 (in hex):

$$
19456 \div 256 = 76 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 19456 = 4C00H
$$

**Step 5** — Final values:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{TH0} = 4C_{H},\quad \text{TL0} = 00_{H}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: UART Baud Rate (Mode 1, Timer 1, Auto-Reload)

**Given:**
- Crystal $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.
- Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload).
- SMOD = 0 (default) or 1 (set in PCON.7).

**Step 1** — Timer 1 overflow rate (overflows every $(256 - TH1)$ timer clocks):

$$
f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2** — UART Mode 1 divides the overflow rate by **32** internally, and the SMOD bit *doubles* it if set:

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times f_{overflow}
$$

**Step 3** — Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}\ \text{bps}\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical

**Q:** Find TH1 to generate **9600 baud** with $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz and SMOD = 0.

**Step 1** — Substitute:

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2** — Multiply both sides by $32 \times 12$:

$$
9600 \times 32 \times 12 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{256 - TH1}
$$

$$
3\,686\,400 = \frac{11\,059\,200}{256 - TH1}
$$

**Step 3** — Solve for $(256 - TH1)$:

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11\,059\,200}{3\,686\,400} = 3
$$

**Step 4** — Therefore:

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Answer:** **TH1 = 0xFD = 253**. (This is the canonical 8051 value for 9600 baud, and is memorisable for KTU.)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pin Functions of 8051 (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Pin (DIP-40) | Name | Type | Alternate Function |
|--------------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1–8 | P1.0–P1.7 | I/O | T2 (52-pin version), or general I/O |
| 9 | RST | Input | Reset (active HIGH, must be held for ≥ 2 MC) |
| 10–17 | P3.0–P3.7 | I/O | RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD |
| 18, 19 | X1, X2 | Crystal | Connect to 11.0592 / 12 MHz crystal |
| 20 | GND | Power | 0 V |
| 21–28 | P2.0–P2.7 | I/O | High-byte address bus (A8–A15) for external memory |
| 29 | PSEN | Output | Program Store Enable — read strobe for external code memory |
| 30 | ALE | Output | Address Latch Enable — demultiplexes P0 (low byte + data) |
| 31 | EA | Input | External Access — 1 = internal ROM; 0 = external ROM |
| 32–39 | P0.0–P0.7 | I/O | Multiplexed low-byte address (A0–A7) + data (D0–D7) |
| 40 | V\_CC | Power | **+5 V DC** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Recall:** P0 is *open-drain* (requires external pull-up resistors of typically **10 kΩ**), while P1, P2, P3 are *quasi-bidirectional* (internal pull-ups). Forgetting the pull-up on P0 during external-memory interfacing is a guaranteed circuit-failure in KTU practical exams.

---

## 3.5 Pin Function P3.0–P3.7 (Alternate Functions)

| Pin | Primary | Alternate | Alternate Function Detail |
|-----|---------|-----------|----------------------------|
| P3.0 | I/O | **RXD** | Serial input (receive) |
| P3.1 | I/O | **TXD** | Serial output (transmit) |
| P3.2 | I/O | **INT0** | External interrupt 0 (active low) |
| P3.3 | I/O | **INT1** | External interrupt 1 (active low) |
| P3.4 | I/O | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | I/O | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | I/O | **WR** | External data memory *write* strobe |
| P3.7 | I/O | **RD** | External data memory *read* strobe |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of 8051 (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CPU
        ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
        ACC[Accumulator A]
        BREG[B Register]
        TMP1[Temp Registers TMP1 TMP2]
        PSW[Program Status Word PSW]
        PC[Program Counter PC 16 bit]
        DPTR[Data Pointer DPTR 16 bit]
        SP[Stack Pointer SP]
    end

    subgraph CodeMemory
        ROM[Internal ROM 4 KB Flash / Mask ROM]
        EPC[External Code Memory up to 64 KB]
    end

    subgraph DataMemory
        IRAM[Internal RAM 128 Bytes]
        SFR[SFR Block 128 Bytes]
        ERAM[External Data Memory up to 64 KB]
    end

    subgraph Peripherals
        P0[Port 0 8 bit]
        P1[Port 1 8 bit]
        P2[Port 2 8 bit]
        P3[Port 3 8 bit]
        T0[Timer 0 16 bit]
        T1[Timer 1 16 bit]
        UART[Serial Port UART]
        INT[Interrupt Controller]
    end

    OSC[Crystal Oscillator X1 X2]
    CTL[Bus and Timing Control Unit]
    RST[Reset Circuit]
    ALE[ALE Pin]
    PSEN[PSEN Pin]

    OSC --> CTL
    RST --> CTL
    CTL --> ALU
    CTL --> PC
    ALE --> P0
    PSEN --> ROM
    PSEN --> EPC
    ALU --> ACC
    ALU --> BREG
    ALU --> TMP1
    ALU --> PSW
    PC --> ROM
    PC --> EPC
    DPTR --> ERAM
    DPTR --> SFR
    SP --> IRAM
    INT --> P3
    T0 --> P3
    T1 --> P3
    UART --> P3
```

---

## 4.2 Program Execution Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON or RST pulse] --> B[PC = 0000H]
    B --> C[Fetch opcode from 0000H]
    C --> D[Decode instruction in ALU]
    D --> E{Is it a Control Transfer LJMP SJMP AJMP CJNE JZ etc}
    E -->|No| F[Execute ALU or Data Transfer]
    E -->|Yes| G[Update PC with target address]
    F --> H[PC = PC + instruction length]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Interrupt pending and EA = 1}
    I -->|Yes| J[Push PC on stack SP increments]
    J --> K[Load PC with vector address 0003H etc]
    K --> L[Execute ISR]
    L --> M[RETI pops PC and returns]
    I -->|No| C
    M --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Map of the 8051 (Mermaid Block Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PROG[Program Memory Space 64 KB]
        P0000[0000H Reset Vector]
        P0003[0003H INT0 ISR]
        P000B[000BH Timer 0 ISR]
        P0013[0013H INT1 ISR]
        P001B[001BH Timer 1 ISR]
        P0023[0023H Serial ISR]
        PCODE[Rest of Program Code]
    end

    subgraph DATAMEM[Internal Data Memory 128 B]
        RB0[00H to 07H Register Bank 0]
        RB1[08H to 0FH Register Bank 1]
        RB2[10H to 17H Register Bank 2]
        RB3[18H to 1FH Register Bank 3]
        BITS[20H to 2FH Bit Addressable 128 bits]
        GPR[30H to 7FH General Purpose 80 bytes]
    end

    subgraph SFRM[SFR Space 80H to FFH selected registers]
        A_SFR[A at E0H]
        B_SFR[B at F0H]
        SP_SFR[SP at 81H]
        P0_SFR[P0 at 80H]
        P1_SFR[P1 at 90H]
        P2_SFR[P2 at A0H]
        P3_SFR[P3 at B0H]
        TCON_SFR[TCON at 88H]
        TMOD_SFR[TMOD at 89H]
        SCON_SFR[SCON at 98H]
        IE_SFR[IE at A8H]
        IP_SFR[IP at B8H]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block-Level Architecture (System-on-Board view)

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCU[8051 MCU Chip]
    XTAL[11.0592 MHz Crystal]
    RSTBTN[Reset Push Button and Capacitor]
    LATCH[74HC573 Address Latch for P0 demultiplexing]
    EPROM[External EPROM 27C256 32 KB]
        XRAM[External SRAM 6264 8 KB]
    LEDS[LEDs on P1 for output]
    SW[Switches on P3 for input]
    LCD[16x2 LCD on P0 and P2]
    UART[PC COM Port via MAX232]
    SENS[Analog Sensor LM35]
    ADC[ADC0804 Parallel ADC]
    BUZZ[Buzzer on P1.5]

    XTAL --> MCU
    RSTBTN --> MCU
    MCU -->|P0 low addr data| LATCH
    LATCH -->|A0 to A7| EPROM
    LATCH -->|A0 to A7| XRAM
    MCU -->|P2 A8 to A15| EPROM
    MCU -->|P2 A8 to A15| XRAM
    MCU -->|PSEN| EPROM
    MCU -->|RD WR| XRAM
    MCU -->|P1| LEDS
    MCU -->|P3| SW
    MCU -->|P0 P2| LCD
    MCU -->|TXD RXD| UART
    SENS --> ADC
    ADC -->|P0| MCU
    MCU --> BUZZ
```

---

## 4.5 Timer Operation Mode Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Mode0 : 13 bit timer
    Mode0 --> Mode1 : TMOD set M1=0 M0=1
    Mode1 : 16 bit timer TH and TL both 8 bit
    Mode1 --> Mode2 : 8 bit auto reload
    Mode2 : TL counts TH holds reload value
    Mode2 --> Mode3 : Split Timer 0 only
    Mode3 : TL0 and TH0 as two 8 bit timers
    Mode3 --> [*] : TR0 cleared
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architecture. Which one does the 8051 microcontroller use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard (Modified) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Bus structure | Single common bus for code and data | Separate code and data buses |
| Speed | Slower (von Neumann bottleneck) | Faster (simultaneous fetch) |
| Memory | Single address space | Separate code and data spaces |
| Used in | General-purpose computers, x86 | DSPs, 8051, ARM Cortex-M |

**Conclusion:** The **8051** uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture** — code and data are in physically separate memory spaces, but a single bus path internally allows unified instruction flow. **(1 mark for conclusion)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: List any three Special Function Registers (SFRs) of the 8051 and state their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per SFR):**

1. **ACC (Accumulator, address E0H):** The primary 8-bit working register of the ALU; almost every arithmetic/logic operation uses it as a source or destination. **(1)**
2. **SP (Stack Pointer, address 81H):** An 8-bit register that points to the top of the stack in internal RAM; it is initialized to **07H** on reset and is incremented before a PUSH. **(1)**
3. **TMOD (Timer Mode register, address 89H):** Selects the *mode* (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) and the *operation* (timer vs. counter) of Timer 0 and Timer 1. **(1)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write **TCON** when the question asks for *mode* selection — **TCON** is the *control* register (TR0, TR1, TF0, TF1, etc.), not the *mode* selector. Watch the wording!

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a) Draw and explain the internal block diagram of the Intel 8051 microcontroller. List its salient features.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Block diagram** — Refer to the mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for the standard KTU diagram. Students should reproduce the following major blocks and label interconnections:

- CPU (ALU, ACC, B, PSW, PC, DPTR, SP) — **1 mark**
- Internal ROM (4 KB) and Internal RAM (128 B) — **1 mark**
- Four 8-bit I/O ports (P0–P3) — **1 mark**
- Two 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0, T1) — **0.5 mark**
- Serial Port (UART) — **0.5 mark**
- Interrupt Controller (5 sources) — **0.5 mark**
- Oscillator and Timing Circuit — **0.5 mark**

**(b) Salient features of 8051 (7 marks → listing with one-line each):**

1. **8-bit CPU** with optimized CISC instruction set.
2. **4 KB on-chip program memory** (ROM/EPROM/Flash depending on variant).
3. **128 B on-chip data memory** (RAM).
4. **Four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports** (P0–P3, 32 lines).
5. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1), each with 4 operating modes.
6. **Full-duplex enhanced UART** (serial port).
7. **5 interrupt sources** with 2 priority levels.
8. **On-chip oscillator** accepts external crystal up to 12–24 MHz.
9. **64 KB external code memory** + **64 KB external data memory** addressable.
10. **Boolean processor** — single-bit operations on bit-addressable memory.
11. **Low-power Idle and Power-down modes** (in CMOS 80C51).
12. **Harvard architecture** (modified) for fast, deterministic execution.

**(a) Block diagram** — Refer to the mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Students should reproduce the following major blocks and label interconnections.

---

**(b) Discuss the I/O port structure of the 8051. Why does Port 0 require external pull-up resistors while Port 3 does not?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Each of the four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) consists of:
- A **D-type latch** (one per pin) that holds the output value.
- An **output driver** (FET pair) that drives the pin.
- An **input buffer** that reads the pin.
- A **read-latch** vs **read-pin** select logic controlled by the internal "Read" signal.

**Port structure (4 marks):**

| Port | Output Type | Drive Strength | Notes |
|------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| P0 | **Open-drain** (no internal pull-up) | Strong pull-down; needs external 10 kΩ pull-ups | Multiplexed A/D bus |
| P1 | Quasi-bidirectional (internal pull-up) | ~20–30 kΩ weak internal pull-up | General I/O only |
| P2 | Quasi-bidirectional (internal pull-up) | Similar to P1 | High-byte address |
| P3 | Quasi-bidirectional (internal pull-up) | Similar to P1 | Has alt. functions (RXD, TXD, INT0/1, T0/1, WR, RD) |

**Why P0 needs external pull-ups (3 marks):**

1. P0's output transistors are of the **open-drain** type — they can *only pull the pin LOW actively*. To make the pin HIGH, the circuit must rely on an **external pull-up resistor** to V\_CC.
2. When P0 is used as the **multiplexed low-byte address/data bus** (in external-memory mode), it must drive both the address (8 bits) and the data (8 bits). During data-read cycles, P0 is configured as an input — open-drain topology is essential to avoid *bus contention* (two outputs fighting each other).
3. P1, P2, and P3 have **internal pull-up FETs** that weakly pull the pin HIGH, so they don't need external resistors for plain I/O operation.
4. If P0 is used only for general-purpose output *and* not for external memory, **10 kΩ pull-up resistors to +5 V** are still mandatory — forgetting this is the most common reason an 8051 prototype "doesn't work" in KTU lab exams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss:** Students frequently draw a *tri-state* output stage for P0, but technically it is *open-drain + weak pull-up only during external-memory mode*. Writing "tri-state" loses 1 mark; the correct term is "open-drain with optional internal pull-up during address transmission."

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the memory organization of the 8051 microcontroller. Differentiate between code memory, internal RAM, and SFRs.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 has a **three-address-space** memory model (Harvard, modified):

1. **Code Memory (Program Memory) — addresses 0000H to FFFFH (64 KB):**
   - Stores the executable machine instructions.
   - **0000H** → Reset vector.
   - **0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H** → Interrupt vectors.
   - On-chip ROM = **4 KB** (in classic 8051).
   - Read-only to the CPU (unless a Flash variant is being programmed).
   - Accessed via the `MOVC` instruction.
   - **EA pin** selects internal (EA = 1) or external (EA = 0) code memory. **(2 marks)**

2. **Internal Data RAM — addresses 00H to 7FH (128 B):**
   - **00H–1FH** → Four register banks (each 8 bytes). PSW bits RS0, RS1 select the active bank.
   - **20H–2FH** → 16 bytes *bit-addressable* RAM (128 individually addressable bits, 00H–7FH of bit-space).
   - **30H–7FH** → 80 bytes general-purpose scratchpad RAM.
   - Accessed via direct, indirect, and register addressing.
   - Volatile — contents lost on power-off. **(2.5 marks)**

3. **Special Function Registers (SFRs) — addresses 80H to FFH (128 B):**
   - Memory-mapped *control and status registers* for the on-chip peripherals.
   - Examples: **ACC (E0H), B (F0H), SP (81H), DPTR (82H/83H), P0–P3 (80/90/A0/B0H), TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP, PCON**.
   - Accessed only via **direct addressing** (not by indirect).
   - Only the addresses that fall on an *even* multiple of 8 (80H, 88H, 90H, 98H, A0H, A8H, B0H, B8H, …) are bit-addressable (16 such SFRs, giving 128 bit-addressable SFR bits). **(2.5 marks)**

---

**(b) With a crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz, find the value to be loaded into TH1 to generate a baud rate of 4800 bps in UART Mode 1 using Timer 1 in auto-reload mode (SMOD = 0). Also, calculate the actual baud rate obtained.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(b) Baud rate formula** (Mode 1, Timer 1 auto-reload):

$$
\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 1** — Substitute given values: $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, SMOD = 0, Baud = 4800.

$$
4800 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2** — Solve for $(256 - TH1)$:

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 4800}
$$

**Step 3** — Denominator:

$$
32 \times 12 \times 4800 = 1\,843\,200
$$

**Step 4** — Division:

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11\,059\,200}{1\,843\,200} = 6
$$

**Step 5** — Solve for TH1:

$$
TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 = 0xFA
$$

**Step 6** — Verify the actual baud rate (substitute back):

$$
\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11\,059\,200}{12 \times 6} = \frac{11\,059\,200}{2304} = 4800 \text{ bps} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,TH1 = FA_{H} = 250_{dec}\,,\quad \text{Actual Baud Rate} = 4800 \text{ bps (exact)}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often use the **12 MHz** crystal in UART problems and get a *non-integer* TH1 — KTU then deducts 1 mark for "use of an unsuitable crystal for serial communication." Always *prefer* **11.0592 MHz** in UART problems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in Microcontroller Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing addressing modes** — saying "Register Indirect" when the instruction uses "Direct" mode. *Worth 1–2 marks per error.*
> 2. **Forgetting to set EA = 1** before describing an interrupt system. *Worth 1 mark.*
> 3. **Using 12 MHz crystal in baud-rate problems** — yields non-integer TH1, which examiners explicitly mark down.
> 4. **Drawing an incorrect block diagram** — missing the *oscillator* or *interrupt controller*. *Up to 2 marks lost.*
> 5. **Writing "Port 0 is tri-state"** instead of "open-drain with optional weak pull-up". *1 mark lost.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must memorise before the exam:**

- ☐ **8051 = 8-bit, CISC, Modified Harvard, 40-pin DIP**, +5 V.
- ☐ **f\_{osc} = 11.0592 MHz** (for UART), **12 MHz** (for general timing).
- ☐ **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** (T\_{MC} = 12 / f\_{osc}).
- ☐ **4 KB internal ROM**, **128 B internal RAM**, **128 B SFR space**, **64 KB external code + 64 KB external data**.
- ☐ **Four ports (P0–P3), 32 I/O lines**; **P0 is open-drain** (needs 10 kΩ pull-up); P1/P2/P3 quasi-bidirectional.
- ☐ **Five addressing modes:** Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed.
- ☐ **Stack Pointer (SP) initialises to 07H**, stack grows *upward* (PUSH increments).
- ☐ **Five interrupt sources** (INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial) with vectors at 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H; **EA must be 1** to enable.
- ☐ **Two 16-bit Timers** in 4 modes; Mode 1 (16-bit) and Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) are most-used.
- ☐ **Baud rate formula** (Mode 1, Timer 1, auto-reload): $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12(256 - TH1)}$.
- ☐ **Key SFR addresses:** A=E0H, B=F0H, SP=81H, P0=80H, P1=90H, P2=A0H, P3=B0H, TCON=88H, TMOD=89H, SCON=98H, IE=A8H, IP=B8H.
- ☐ **Bit-addressable regions:** 20H–2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits) and selected SFRs on multiples of 8.
- ☐ **ALE** demultiplexes P0 (low-byte address + data) using an external **74HC573** latch.
- ☐ **PSEN** reads external code memory; **RD/WR** read/write external data memory.
- ☐ **EA = 1** → internal ROM enabled; **EA = 0** → external ROM only (used for programmers).
- ☐ **Reset circuit** = 10 μF capacitor + 10 kΩ resistor + push-button on pin 9 (RST, active HIGH).
- ☐ **P3 alternate functions** memorise: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
- ☐ **Boolean processor** — 8051 can do single-bit SETB, CLR, ANL, ORL on 256 bit-addressable locations.
- ☐ **Power-save modes:** Idle (CPU halted, peripherals run) and Power-down (oscillator halted, lowest I\_{CC}).

---

**End of Notes — Module 2 — Microcontrollers**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Course Outcomes CO2 / CO3 mapping.*
