- 5G NR supports **multiple numerologies** ($\mu = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$) with sub-carrier spacings of $15 \times 2^{\mu}$ kHz = **15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz**.
- Higher numerologies (e.g., 120 kHz for mmWave at 28 GHz) are **resilient against phase noise** at high carrier frequencies.
- **Mini-slot** transmission of 2, 4, or 7 OFDM symbols (vs LTE's 7-symbol slot) reduces latency to **1 ms URLLC** target.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Wireless Networks Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the path-loss exponent** in cellular reuse numericals — never default to $n=4$ without stating the assumption.
> 2. **Mixing CSMA/CA and CSMA/CD** — the KTU examiner will immediately deduct 1 mark if you claim "wireless uses collision detection".
> 3. **Confusing the HLR (permanent) with the VLR (temporary)** in GSM register descriptions — list the *contents* (IMSI, MSISDN, current location) for each.
> 4. **Wrong order in IFS hierarchy** — SIFS $<$ PIFS $<$ DIFS, never any other sequence.
> 5. **Omitting the CSMA/CA backoff randomness** — collision avoidance relies on the random backoff, not on channel sensing alone.
> 6. **In LTE questions, omitting the all-IP nature of EPC** — explicitly mention "no circuit-switched fallback in pure LTE" for full marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spectrum scarcity** drives the design of multiple access techniques (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, OFDMA).
- **Friis path loss formula** in dB: $P_{L} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ — must memorize.
- **Co-channel reuse ratio:** $q = \sqrt{3N}$; SIR $= q^{n}/j_{0}$ (for $j_{0}=6$ interferers).
- **Cellular architecture (LTE):** UE $\rightarrow$ eNodeB $\rightarrow$ S-GW $\rightarrow$ P-GW; control plane via MME + HSS.
- **GSM Registers:** **HLR** (home), **VLR** (visitor), **AUC** (auth), **EIR** (equipment black-list).
- **IEEE 802.11:** BSS = AP + STAs; ESS = multiple BSS via DS; IBSS = ad-hoc; uses CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS.
- **IFS hierarchy:** **SIFS** $<$ **PIFS** $<$ **DIFS** — guarantees ACK/CTS priority.
- **OFDM** divides a wideband channel into many orthogonal narrowband sub-carriers (15 kHz in LTE).
- **OFDMA** (used in 4G/5G) = OFDM + multi-user scheduling in time-frequency Resource Blocks.
- **Hidden node problem** $\Rightarrow$ why CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS is mandatory in WLAN.
- **5G NR numerology:** $\Delta f = 15 \times 2^{\mu}$ kHz with $\mu \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ — flexible for diverse use cases (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC).
- **Typical frequencies:** Wi-Fi 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz; Cellular 700 MHz – 28 GHz (5G mmWave); Bluetooth 2.4 GHz ISM band.
- **Ad-hoc networks (MANET):** no infrastructure, multi-hop routing (AODV, DSR, OLSR protocols).
- **Mobility management:** Cellular uses Handover (hard/soft); Mobile IP uses Home Agent / Foreign Agent tunneling at Layer 3.
- **Fading types:** Rayleigh (no LOS), Rician (strong LOS component), Nakagami (generalized empirical fit).
- **5G network slicing** creates virtual dedicated networks on a shared physical substrate — key differentiator from 4G.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of engineering systems and embedded architectures, **Hardware** refers to the tangible, physical components and electronic circuits that constitute a computing or processing system. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hardware encompasses the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory subsystems** (both volatile and non-volatile), **Input/Output (I/O) peripherals**, **interconnecting buses**, **clock and timing circuits**, **power management units**, and **supporting integrated circuits (ICs)** that collectively execute the instructions defined by software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Hardware is the collective term for all physical, electronic, and electromechanical elements of a computing system, as opposed to the software (instructions and data) that runs on it. It operates on binary logic using Boolean algebra, with voltage thresholds standardized (e.g., **TTL: 0V–0.8V = LOW, 2.0V–5.0V = HIGH**).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **modern restaurant kitchen** as a complete computer system:

| Kitchen Element | Computer Hardware Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Head Chef | **CPU (Processor)** | Reads recipes (instructions) and cooks (processes) |
| Recipe Bookshelf | **ROM / Hard Disk (Storage)** | Holds recipes permanently (non-volatile) |
| Kitchen Counter | **RAM (Main Memory)** | Temporary workspace for active cooking (volatile) |
| Waiter taking orders | **Input Devices (Keyboard, Sensors)** | Brings raw materials/orders to chef |
| Serving window | **Output Devices (Monitor, Actuators)** | Delivers finished dishes to customer |
| Intercom / Pass window | **System Bus (Data, Address, Control)** | Communication channel between sections |
| Power supply / Generator | **SMPS / Voltage Regulators** | Provides stable electrical energy |

Just as a kitchen cannot function without physical equipment (knives, stoves, pans), software (recipes) is useless without hardware (the kitchen itself). The **CPU** is the "brain" that fetches instructions, decodes them, and executes operations using data stored in memory and routed through buses.

## 1.3 Standard Hardware Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Hardware Parameters (KTU 2024 Expectation):**
> - **Clock Frequency ($f$):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** — cycles per second. Modern CPUs operate at **GHz** range ($1 \text{ GHz} = 10^9 \text{ Hz}$).
> - **Word Length:** Number of bits processed in one cycle (**8, 16, 32, or 64 bits**).
> - **Bus Width:** Number of parallel data lines (e.g., **64-bit data bus**).
> - **Logic Voltage Levels (TTL Standard):** $V_{IL} \le 0.8V$, $V_{IH} \ge 2.0V$.
> - **CMOS Standard (3.3V):** $V_{IL} \le 0.8V$, $V_{IH} \ge 2.0V$.

## 1.4 Classification of Computer Hardware

Hardware is broadly classified into **four functional categories**:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — Control Unit (CU), Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), Registers.
2. **Memory** — Primary (RAM, ROM, Cache) and Secondary (HDD, SSD).
3. **Input/Output Devices** — Keyboards, mice, displays, sensors, actuators.
4. **Interconnection** — System buses, interfaces, controllers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Von Neumann Architecture (Foundation Model)

The **Von Neumann Architecture** (1945) is the foundational model underlying nearly all modern computers. Its defining feature is the **stored-program concept**: both instructions and data reside in the **same memory** and share the **same bus**.

### 2.1.1 Operational Steps (Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle)

The CPU continuously performs the **Instruction Cycle** consisting of these phases:

- **Step 1 — Fetch:** The Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction. The address is placed on the **address bus**, a `READ` signal is asserted on the **control bus**, and the instruction is loaded from memory into the **Instruction Register (IR)** via the data bus. The PC is then incremented.

- **Step 2 — Decode:** The **Control Unit (CU)** decodes the instruction in the IR, identifies the opcode and operands, and generates the necessary control signals for execution.

- **Step 3 — Execute:** The **ALU** performs the operation (arithmetic, logic, or data transfer). Results may be stored in registers or written back to memory.

- **Step 4 — Store:** The result is written to the destination register or memory location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Von Neumann Bottleneck:** Since instructions and data share a single bus, the CPU spends time **idle** waiting for memory access. This limitation is called the **Von Neumann Bottleneck** and motivates the use of **cache memory** and **Harvard Architecture** in modern designs.

## 2.2 Harvard Architecture

In **Harvard Architecture**, instruction memory and data memory are **physically separate**, with **dedicated buses** for each. This allows **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access, eliminating the bottleneck.

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single unified memory | Separate instruction & data memory |
| Bus | Shared single bus | Separate instruction & data buses |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster (parallel access) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Used in | General-purpose PCs, Laptops | DSPs, Microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, PIC) |

## 2.3 CPU Subsystems — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Control Unit (CU)
The CU orchestrates all operations. It can be implemented as:
- **Hardwired CU:** Uses combinational logic circuits (fast, inflexible).
- **Microprogrammed CU:** Uses a microcode ROM (flexible, slightly slower).

### 2.3.2 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
Performs arithmetic ($+$, $-$, $\times$, $\div$), logical (AND, OR, XOR, NOT), and shift/rotate operations. The core adder is a **ripple-carry adder** or faster variants like **carry-lookahead adder**.

### 2.3.3 Registers
High-speed internal CPU storage. Types include:
- **Program Counter (PC):** Holds next instruction address.
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds currently fetched instruction.
- **Accumulator (ACC):** Default ALU operand & result holder.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** Points to top of stack.
- **General Purpose Registers (GPRs):** For temporary data storage.

## 2.4 Memory Hierarchy

Memory is organized in a **hierarchy** based on **speed**, **cost**, and **capacity**:

$$\text{Registers} \rightarrow \text{Cache (L1, L2, L3)} \rightarrow \text{Main Memory (RAM)} \rightarrow \text{Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)} \rightarrow \text{Tertiary (Optical/Tape)}$$

As we move **down** the hierarchy: **capacity increases**, **cost per bit decreases**, but **access time increases**.

### 2.4.1 Cache Memory Principles
Cache exploits the **Principle of Locality**:
- **Temporal Locality:** Recently accessed items tend to be accessed again soon.
- **Spatial Locality:** Items near recently accessed items tend to be accessed soon.

**Mapping Techniques:**
- **Direct Mapped Cache**
- **Fully Associative Cache**
- **Set-Associative Cache** (most common — N-way set associative)

## 2.5 System Buses

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of multiple signal lines. Three primary buses:

| Bus Type | Function | Direction | Width (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus** | Carries actual data | Bidirectional | 8, 16, 32, 64 bits |
| **Address Bus** | Carries memory/I/O addresses | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | 16, 20, 24, 32, 64 bits |
| **Control Bus** | Carries control signals (RD, WR, INT, CLK) | Mixed | Varies |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the EXAM GOLDEN REFERENCE for Hardware topics in KTU 2024.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{clock}}$ | Total time for program execution | Seconds |
| Cycles Per Instruction (CPI) | $CPI = \frac{\text{Total Cycles}}{\text{Instruction Count}}$ | Average cycles per instruction | Dimensionless |
| MIPS Rating | $MIPS = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| Throughput | $Throughput = \frac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{T_{total}}$ | Work done per unit time | Tasks/s |
| Addressable Memory | $M = 2^n$ | where $n$ = address bus width | Bytes |
| Memory Bandwidth | $BW = \frac{\text{Data Transferred}}{T_{transfer}}$ | Data rate of bus | Bytes/s |
| Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = speedup factor | Dimensionless |
| Hit Ratio | $H = \frac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Accesses}}$ | Cache effectiveness | $0 \le H \le 1$ |
| Average Access Time | $T_{avg} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{main}$ | With cache hierarchy | Seconds |
| Clock Period | $T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}}$ | Time of one clock cycle | Seconds |

**CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Absolute value expressions such as $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert f - 1 \vert$ use the LaTeX `\vert` command to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hardware principles drive critical engineering domains:

- **Embedded Systems Design:** Microcontroller selection (8051, ARM Cortex) relies on Harvard vs. Von Neumann trade-offs.
- **VLSI Chip Design:** Bus width and clock frequency directly determine silicon area, power dissipation, and performance.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Hardware-software co-design optimizes power, latency, and cost.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Multi-core CPUs, GPUs, and NUMA architectures overcome memory bottlenecks.
- **Cybersecurity:** Hardware roots of trust (TPM, secure enclaves) defend against firmware attacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Memory Addressability from Address Bus Width

A CPU with an **$n$-bit address bus** can uniquely address $2^n$ memory locations. We prove this:

**Starting Point:** Each address line can carry one of two states (binary 0 or 1). With $n$ independent lines, the total number of unique patterns is found by the multiplication principle of counting.

$$\text{Number of unique addresses} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}}$$

This is a direct application of the product rule of combinatorics:

$$M = 2^n$$

**Numerical Evaluation:** For a CPU with a 20-bit address bus (e.g., Intel 8086):

$$M = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ locations} = 1 \text{ MB}$$

**Real-world mapping:** If each location holds 1 byte:

$$\text{Total Addressable Memory} = 2^{20} \times 1 \text{ byte} = 1 \text{ MB}$$

**Generalized to word-addressable memory (with $w$-bit words):**

$$\text{Total Memory (bits)} = 2^n \times w$$

**Worked Example:** A 32-bit CPU with 32-bit address bus and 32-bit word size:

$$M = 2^{32} \text{ words} = 4 \text{ GB of addressable memory}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with Cache

Let the cache access time be $T_c$ and the main memory access time be $T_m$. The hit ratio is $H$. The **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the two cases:**
- **Case A (Cache Hit):** Probability $= H$, Cost $= T_c$
- **Case B (Cache Miss):** Probability $= (1 - H)$, Cost $= T_c + T_m$ (cache check time + main memory access)

**Step 2 — Write expected value equation:**

$$T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot (T_c + T_m)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the expression:**

$$T_{avg} = H \cdot T_c + T_c + T_m - H \cdot T_c - H \cdot T_m$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling $H \cdot T_c$ terms:**

$$T_{avg} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$T_{avg} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_m$$

**Numerical Example:** Given $T_c = 5 \text{ ns}$, $T_m = 100 \text{ ns}$, $H = 0.9$:

$$T_{avg} = 5 + (1 - 0.9) \cdot 100 = 5 + 0.1 \cdot 100 = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ ns}$$

This shows a **6.67× speedup** over main memory access (100 ns → 15 ns).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CPU Performance using Amdahl's Law

**Amdahl's Law** quantifies the theoretical speedup when only a portion of a system is improved.

**Step 1 — Define variables:**
- $f$ = fraction of execution time that can be improved (parallelized).
- $(1 - f)$ = fraction that remains unchanged.
- $S$ = speedup factor of the improved portion.

**Step 2 — New execution time equation:**

$$T_{new} = (1 - f) \cdot T_{old} + \frac{f \cdot T_{old}}{S}$$

**Step 3 — Compute overall speedup $\text{Speedup}_{overall}$:**

$$\text{Speedup}_{overall} = \frac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{new}$:**

$$\text{Speedup}_{overall} = \frac{T_{old}}{(1 - f) \cdot T_{old} + \frac{f \cdot T_{old}}{S}}$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $T_{old}$:**

$$\text{Speedup}_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{S}}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting case as $S \to \infty$:**

$$\text{Speedup}_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose 40% of a program can be parallelized ($f = 0.4$), and we use 4 processors ($S = 4$):

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.4) + \frac{0.4}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.6 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.7} \approx 1.43$$

**Insight:** Even with infinite processors, the maximum speedup is bounded:

$$\text{Speedup}_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.4} = \frac{1}{0.6} \approx 1.67$$

This demonstrates the **diminishing returns** of hardware improvements.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study Table (KTU 2024)

| Hardware Component | Function | Speed (typical) | Volatile? | Capacity (typical) | Technology Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Register** | Fastest CPU storage | $\sim 1$ ns | Yes | 8–256 bytes | Flip-flops (SRAM cells) |
| **L1 Cache** | Instruction/Data cache | 1–3 ns | Yes | 16–64 KB | SRAM (6T cell) |
| **L2 Cache** | Secondary cache | 3–10 ns | Yes | 256 KB – 8 MB | SRAM |
| **L3 Cache** | Shared cache | 10–20 ns | Yes | 4–64 MB | SRAM |
| **Main Memory (RAM)** | Active programs/data | 50–100 ns | Yes | 4–64 GB | DRAM (1T + 1C cell) |
| **SSD** | Persistent storage | 50–150 $\mu$s | No | 256 GB – 8 TB | NAND Flash |
| **HDD** | Bulk persistent storage | 5–15 ms | No | 500 GB – 20 TB | Magnetic platters |
| **ROM** | Firmware/BIOS | 10–50 ns | No (mostly) | 4 KB – 32 MB | Mask ROM, EEPROM, Flash |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Memory Interleaving and Effective Access Time

To overcome memory bottleneck, **interleaved memory** is used. Memory is divided into $n$ banks, each with its own address buffer. Consecutive addresses are spread across banks.

**Effective access time with $n$-way interleaving:**

$$T_{eff} = T_{access} + \frac{(n-1) \cdot T_{cycle}}{n}$$

**Example:** With 4-way interleaving, $T_{access} = 200$ ns, $T_{cycle} = 50$ ns:

$$T_{eff} = 200 + \frac{3 \cdot 50}{4} = 200 + 37.5 = 237.5 \text{ ns}$$

This shows a **bottleneck reduction** by distributing sequential access load.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Von Neumann Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[CPU Central Processing Unit] --> node2[Memory Unit RAM and ROM]
    node2 --> node1
    node1 --> node3[Input Devices]
    node1 --> node4[Output Devices]
    node1 --> node5[System Bus]
    node2 --> node5
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
```

**Description:** The CPU, Memory, and I/O devices all share a **common system bus**. Data and instructions flow bidirectionally over this bus, illustrating the Von Neumann model.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Harvard Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[CPU] --> nodeB[Instruction Memory ROM]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Data Memory RAM]
    nodeB --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeD[I/O Devices]
    nodeD --> nodeA
```

**Description:** Instruction memory and data memory are **separate** with **dedicated paths** to the CPU, allowing simultaneous fetch and data access.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Internal CPU Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    reg1[Program Counter PC] --> reg2[Instruction Register IR]
    reg2 --> cu[Control Unit CU]
    cu --> alu[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
    cu --> reg3[General Purpose Registers GPR]
    reg3 --> alu
    alu --> acc[Accumulator ACC]
    reg1 --> bus[Address Bus]
    bus --> mem[External Memory]
    mem --> bus2[Data Bus]
    bus2 --> reg2
```

**Description:** This flowchart shows the **internal data path** of a simple CPU. The PC drives the address bus, memory returns the instruction via the data bus into the IR, the CU decodes and controls the ALU using values from registers, and results accumulate in the ACC.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Memory Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lvl1[Level 1 Registers] --> lvl2[Level 2 L1 Cache]
    lvl2 --> lvl3[Level 3 L2 Cache]
    lvl3 --> lvl4[Level 4 L3 Cache]
    lvl4 --> lvl5[Level 5 Main Memory RAM]
    lvl5 --> lvl6[Level 6 SSD]
    lvl6 --> lvl7[Level 7 HDD]
    lvl7 --> lvl8[Level 8 Tertiary Tape or Cloud]
```

**Description:** Visualizes the **pyramid of memory** — faster, smaller, costlier storage at the top; slower, larger, cheaper storage at the bottom.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — System Bus Organization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu[CPU] --> adbus[Address Bus Unidirectional]
    cpu --> ctlbus[Control Bus RD WR INT]
    cpu -.Bidirectional.-> databus[Data Bus]
    adbus --> memunit[Memory]
    adbus --> ioctrl[I/O Controller]
    databus --> memunit
    databus --> ioctrl
    ctlbus --> memunit
    ctlbus --> ioctrl
```

**Description:** Shows the **three-bus organization** of a typical computer. The address bus is one-way (CPU to peripherals), the data bus is bidirectional, and the control bus carries timing and command signals.

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Instruction Execution Cycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state1[Fetch Instruction from Memory] --> state2[Decode Instruction in CU]
    state2 --> state3[Fetch Operands from Registers or Memory]
    state3 --> state4[Execute Operation in ALU]
    state4 --> state5[Store Result in Register or Memory]
    state5 --> state1
```

**Description:** A **state machine representation** of the fetch-decode-execute cycle, the heartbeat of every CPU.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the following terms with respect to computer hardware: *(a) ALU, (b) Control Unit, (c) Register.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** The ALU is the computational core of the CPU. It performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division), logical operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shift, rotate), and comparison operations on binary data. The ALU receives operands from registers, processes them based on control signals from the CU, and outputs the result to a destination register (often the Accumulator).

**(b) Control Unit (CU):** The CU acts as the "traffic controller" of the CPU. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and generates the appropriate control signals to coordinate the ALU, registers, memory, and I/O devices. The CU does not process data itself; it manages the data flow and timing of all other units.

**(c) Register:** A register is a small, high-speed storage element inside the CPU built from flip-flops. Registers temporarily hold data, instructions, or memory addresses during processing. Common registers include the Program Counter (PC), Instruction Register (IR), Accumulator (ACC), Stack Pointer (SP), and General Purpose Registers (GPRs). Registers are the fastest form of memory in a computer, with access times of approximately **1 nanosecond**.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between **Von Neumann** and **Harvard** architectures with suitable diagrams.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Von Neumann Architecture | Harvard Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single shared memory for instructions and data | Separate memory for instructions and data |
| **Bus** | Common data + address bus for both | Separate instruction and data buses |
| **Speed** | Slower due to bus contention | Faster due to parallel access |
| **Hardware Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Bottleneck** | Suffers from Von Neumann bottleneck | No such bottleneck |
| **Applications** | General-purpose PCs, laptops, servers | Microcontrollers (8051, PIC, ARM), DSPs |
| **Flexibility** | Programs can self-modify | Programs cannot self-modify easily |

**Key Insight:** Modern processors (e.g., ARM Cortex) use a **modified Harvard architecture** internally with separate L1 caches for instructions and data, but expose a unified memory model to the programmer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Von Neumann architecture** of a computer system. List its main components and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle** in detail. How does the Program Counter contribute to instruction sequencing? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Von Neumann Architecture

The Von Neumann architecture, proposed by **John von Neumann in 1945**, is the foundational model for most modern computers. Its defining innovation is the **stored-program concept**: both program instructions and data reside in the same memory and are accessed via the same bus.

**Main Components:**

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU):** Contains the **Control Unit (CU)**, **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, and **Registers**. The CPU executes instructions sequentially.

2. **Memory Unit:** Stores both instructions and data. Typically uses **RAM** (volatile) and **ROM** (non-volatile). The memory is addressed linearly using the address bus.

3. **Input Devices:** Provide data and instructions to the system (e.g., keyboard, mouse, scanner, sensors).

4. **Output Devices:** Display or transmit processed results (e.g., monitor, printer, actuators).

5. **System Bus:** A shared communication channel comprising:
   - **Address Bus:** Unidirectional, carries memory addresses from CPU to memory/I/O.
   - **Data Bus:** Bidirectional, carries actual data.
   - **Control Bus:** Carries control signals like `RD`, `WR`, `INT`, `CLK`.

**Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to the Von Neumann diagram in Section 4.1.

**Key Limitation:** The **Von Neumann Bottleneck** — since the single bus is shared, the CPU must wait while memory is being accessed, limiting performance.

#### Part (b) — Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle

The CPU continuously performs the following three-step cycle for every instruction:

**Step 1 — FETCH:**
- The **Program Counter (PC)** contains the address of the next instruction to be executed.
- The address is placed on the **address bus**.
- The CU asserts a `MEM_READ` signal on the **control bus**.
- The instruction at that address is loaded from memory into the **Instruction Register (IR)** via the data bus.
- The **PC is incremented** by 1 (or by the instruction length) to point to the next instruction.

**Step 2 — DECODE:**
- The instruction in the IR is decoded by the **Control Unit**.
- The opcode is identified (e.g., ADD, LOAD, JMP).
- The CU generates the necessary control signals to coordinate the ALU, registers, and memory.

**Step 3 — EXECUTE:**
- For arithmetic/logical instructions, the **ALU** performs the operation on operands fetched from registers.
- For memory instructions, the address bus is used to access memory.
- For control instructions (e.g., JMP), the PC is updated to the target address.
- The result is stored in a register (e.g., the Accumulator) or written back to memory.

**Role of the Program Counter (PC):**
- The PC is a special register that **tracks the address of the next instruction** to be fetched.
- After each fetch, it auto-increments, ensuring **sequential execution**.
- For branch/jump instructions, the PC is **overwritten** with the target address, enabling non-sequential execution.
- On reset, the PC is initialized to a fixed value (e.g., `0x0000` in most microcontrollers, or the BIOS entry point in PCs).

**Cycle Timing:** Each cycle takes a fixed number of clock cycles, and the CPU repeats this process billions of times per second (e.g., a **3 GHz** CPU completes **3 billion cycles/second**).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** What is **memory hierarchy**? Explain each level in detail with access time, capacity, and cost trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has an **L1 cache access time of 5 ns** and **main memory access time of 100 ns**. The **cache hit ratio is 90%**. Calculate the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**. If we add an L2 cache with access time 20 ns and L2 hit ratio 95% (after L1 miss), recalculate the AMAT and comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Memory Hierarchy

Memory hierarchy is the arrangement of different memory types in a computer system, organized to balance **speed**, **cost**, and **capacity** efficiently. The principle is: **smaller, faster, costlier memory** at the top (closest to CPU), and **larger, slower, cheaper memory** at the bottom.

**Levels of Memory Hierarchy (from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest):**

1. **Registers:** Inside the CPU. Fastest (~1 ns), smallest (bytes), highest cost/bit.

2. **L1 Cache (Level 1):** On-chip SRAM. Access time **1–3 ns**, capacity **16–64 KB**. Split into L1i (instruction) and L1d (data) in modern CPUs.

3. **L2 Cache (Level 2):** On-chip or off-chip SRAM. Access time **3–10 ns**, capacity **256 KB – 8 MB**.

4. **L3 Cache (Level 3):** Shared across cores. Access time **10–20 ns**, capacity **4–64 MB**.

5. **Main Memory (RAM):** DRAM-based volatile memory. Access time **50–100 ns**, capacity **4–64 GB**.

6. **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD):** Non-volatile persistent storage. SSD: **50–150 μs**, HDD: **5–15 ms**. Capacity: **256 GB – 20 TB**.

7. **Tertiary Storage (Optical, Tape, Cloud):** Slowest, used for archival and backup.

**Trade-off Principle:** As we move **down** the hierarchy: capacity ↑, cost per bit ↓, access time ↑.

**Principle of Locality** justifies this design — most programs access a small subset of memory frequently, so keeping that subset in fast memory yields significant speedup.

#### Part (b) — AMAT Calculation

**Step 1: Given data**
- $T_{L1} = 5$ ns (L1 cache access time)
- $T_{RAM} = 100$ ns (main memory access time)
- $H_{L1} = 0.90$ (L1 hit ratio)
- $T_{L2} = 20$ ns (L2 cache access time, after L1 miss)
- $H_{L2} = 0.95$ (L2 hit ratio, given L1 missed)

**Step 2: AMAT for L1 + RAM only**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$T_{avg} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot T_{RAM}$$

$$T_{avg} = 5 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 100 = 5 + 0.10 \cdot 100 = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: AMAT with L1 + L2 + RAM**

For two-level cache:

$$T_{avg} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot [T_{L2} + (1 - H_{L2}) \cdot T_{RAM}]$$

Substituting values:

$$T_{avg} = 5 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot [20 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 100]$$

$$T_{avg} = 5 + 0.10 \cdot [20 + 0.05 \cdot 100]$$

$$T_{avg} = 5 + 0.10 \cdot [20 + 5]$$

$$T_{avg} = 5 + 0.10 \cdot 25$$

$$T_{avg} = 5 + 2.5 = 7.5 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4: Comparison and Comment**

| Configuration | AMAT |
|---|---|
| L1 + RAM only | 15 ns |
| L1 + L2 + RAM | 7.5 ns |

**Comment:** Adding an L2 cache **halved** the average memory access time (from 15 ns to 7.5 ns), demonstrating the effectiveness of multi-level caching. The improvement is **2× speedup** at minimal additional hardware cost. This is the foundation of modern multi-level cache architectures in CPUs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN HARDWARE QUESTIONS:**
> 1. **Confusing data bus direction:** Students often incorrectly state the data bus as unidirectional. The data bus is **bidirectional**, while the address bus is **unidirectional**.
> 2. **Mixing up CPI and MIPS:** CPI is cycles per instruction; MIPS is millions of instructions per second. They are inversely related but not the same.
> 3. **Forgetting to increment PC:** In the fetch–decode–execute cycle, students often forget to mention that the PC must be incremented **after** the fetch and **before** the execute phase, otherwise the same instruction repeats infinitely.
> 4. **Cache AMAT formula mistakes:** For multi-level cache, students apply the formula incorrectly by omitting the nested brackets. Always use the **recursive** form: $T_{avg} = T_{Li} + (1 - H_{Li}) \cdot T_{L(i+1)}$.
> 5. **Address bus width vs. memory size:** Addressable memory is $2^n$ **bytes** (for byte-addressable systems). Students often forget the unit conversion.
> 6. **Block diagram accuracy:** In Von Neumann diagram, **do not** draw separate instruction and data memories — it should be a single unified memory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — HARDWARE (MODULE 3):**
> - **CPU Components:** CU (controller), ALU (processor), Registers (storage). Registers include PC, IR, ACC, SP, GPRs.
> - **Von Neumann vs. Harvard:** Single memory/bus vs. Separate memory/buses. Trade-off: cost vs. speed.
> - **Von Neumann Bottleneck:** Shared bus limits performance. Solved using cache.
> - **Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle:** Three-step process repeated continuously. PC drives the instruction sequence.
> - **Memory Hierarchy:** Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → SSD → HDD → Tape. **Speed ↓, Capacity ↑, Cost/bit ↓** as we go down.
> - **Locality Principles:** Temporal (recent items reused) and Spatial (nearby items accessed).
> - **Bus Types:** Address (unidirectional), Data (bidirectional), Control (mixed).
> - **AMAT Formula:** $T_{avg} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{main}$. For multi-level, apply recursively.
> - **Addressable Memory:** $M = 2^n$ where $n$ = address bus width (in bytes for byte-addressable systems).
> - **Amdahl's Law:** $\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{S}}$. Demonstrates diminishing returns of hardware improvement.
> - **Standard Voltages:** TTL uses 5V; CMOS uses 3.3V or lower. Logic LOW and HIGH thresholds must be memorized.
> - **CPU Time Formula:** $T_{CPU} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$.
> - **CPI Definition:** Average cycles per instruction, dimensionless, between 1 and several for complex CPUs.
> - **MIPS Rating:** $MIPS = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ — measure of CPU throughput.
> - **Cache Mapping:** Direct, Fully Associative, Set-Associative. Each has different complexity vs. hit rate trade-offs.
> - **Interleaved Memory:** Distributes sequential addresses across multiple banks to reduce access bottleneck.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Processing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Event Processing

# 📘 Module 3 — Event Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Event Processing** is a computing paradigm that executes operations on events — discrete, time-stamped records of something that has happened — to detect meaningful patterns, derive higher-level insights, and trigger real-time responses. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus under PBPET604, event processing is treated as a foundational pillar of *event-driven architectures (EDA)* and forms the backbone of modern streaming analytics platforms.

An **Event** is formally defined as a tuple:

$$E = \langle id, type, timestamp, payload, source \rangle$$

where each component carries semantic significance in distributed event-driven systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Event Processing in PBPET604 covers **Event Stream Processing (ESP)**, **Complex Event Processing (CEP)**, **publish/subscribe semantics**, **windowing operators**, and **event-time vs processing-time semantics** — all critical for building reactive, low-latency distributed systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Event Processing** like a **newspaper newsroom with smoke detectors and surveillance cameras** wired to a central control room:

- 📸 **Events** = each camera snapshot or sensor reading (a moment captured in time).
- 🎛️ **Event Stream** = the continuous live feed of all those snapshots flowing into the control room.
- 🧠 **Event Processing Engine** = the analyst watching multiple screens, looking for *patterns* (e.g., "smoke detected on floor 3 AND motion in stairwell = possible fire, sound alarm").
- ⚡ **Reaction** = the automatic fire alarm, door unlock, or emergency call triggered the instant a pattern is recognized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In plain English: **Event Processing = the discipline of making sense of streams of "things that happened" so the system can react intelligently within milliseconds.**

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Event** | A record that "something happened" at a given time | A credit card swipe at 14:32 |
| **Event Source / Producer** | Component that emits events | POS terminal, IoT sensor |
| **Event Consumer / Sink** | Component that reacts to events | Fraud detection engine |
| **Event Channel / Broker** | Middleware transport (queue/topic) | Kafka topic, RabbitMQ exchange |
| **Event Processing Agent (EPA)** | Atomic logic unit operating on events | Filter, Aggregator, Pattern matcher |
| **Event Processing Network (EPN)** | Directed graph of EPAs | Pipeline of filters + correlators |
| **Stream** | Ordered (often unbounded) sequence of events | Clickstream, telemetry feed |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Major Branches of Event Processing

KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly distinguishes between two complementary paradigms:

#### 2.1.1 Event Stream Processing (ESP)

ESP focuses on **stateless or minimally-stateful transformations** over continuous, unbounded flows of events. It is heavily inspired by relational query processing applied to streams rather than tables.

- Operates on **streams as first-class citizens**.
- Uses **streaming SQL** dialects (e.g., **Kafka Streams KSQL**, **Flink SQL**).
- Emphasizes **throughput**, **latency**, and **horizontal scalability**.

#### 2.1.2 Complex Event Processing (CEP)

CEP focuses on **detecting complex, often temporal patterns** across many primitive events to infer higher-level business situations (a "complex event").

- Operates on **event clouds** rather than ordered streams.
- Uses **rule engines** with pattern languages (e.g., **EPL — Event Processing Language**, used by Esper, Drools Fusion).
- Emphasizes **pattern matching**, **temporal logic**, and **causal inference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Distinction (frequently tested):**
> - **ESP** = continuous transformation over ordered streams (think pipelines/ETL on data-in-motion).
> - **CEP** = pattern detection over event clouds (think rule-based reasoning over event clouds).

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — How an Event Processing Engine Works

The end-to-end pipeline of an event processing system can be broken into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Ingestion / Acquisition**
   - Events are received from external producers via adapters (HTTP, MQTT, Kafka, Kinesis).
   - Each event is validated against its schema (Avro, JSON Schema, Protobuf).

2. **Pre-processing / Normalization**
   - Timestamps are parsed into a canonical format (typically **Unix epoch in milliseconds**).
   - Out-of-order events are buffered for reordering.

3. **Windowing**
   - The unbounded stream is sliced into finite chunks called **windows** to make aggregation tractable.
   - Window types: **Tumbling**, **Sliding**, **Session**, **Global**.

4. **Transformation / Aggregation**
   - Stateless operators: `map`, `filter`, `project`.
   - Stateful operators: `reduce`, `aggregate`, `join`.

5. **Pattern Detection (CEP only)**
   - Continuous queries check for temporal sequences (e.g., `A followed by B within 5 seconds`).

6. **Reaction / Sink**
   - The processed result (or detected pattern) is forwarded to downstream sinks: databases, dashboards, webhooks, message brokers, or actuators.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Is Implemented |
|------|----------------|------------------------|
| Ingestion | Decouples producers from consumers | Async I/O, back-pressure, batching |
| Normalization | Ensures time semantics correctness | Watermarks, idempotent timestamps |
| Windowing | Bounded memory + finite computation | Time/count/session windows |
| Transformation | Extracts business value | Map-reduce-like operators |
| Pattern Detection | Surfaces rare but critical events | CEP rules, finite automata |
| Reaction | Closes the loop with the real world | Push notifications, DB writes, API calls |

### 2.4 Windowing — The Heart of Stateful Stream Processing

Windowing is **the single most important concept** in ESP and is heavily tested in KTU exams. Below are the four canonical window types:

#### 2.4.1 Tumbling Window
- Fixed-size, **non-overlapping** intervals.
- Each event belongs to **exactly one** window.
- Equation for window index: $W_i = \lfloor \frac{t - t_0}{\Delta} \rfloor$

#### 2.4.2 Sliding Window
- Fixed-size windows that **overlap** by a configurable slide interval.
- An event can belong to **multiple** windows.

#### 2.4.3 Session Window
- Dynamic-size windows based on **activity gaps**.
- A session closes when no event arrives for a configurable **gap timeout** $g$.

#### 2.4.4 Global Window
- A single window that spans **the entire stream** (used with triggers).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the canonical reference students must memorize for ESE preparation.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Event tuple | $E = \langle id, type, ts, payload, source \rangle$ | $ts$ typically in **ms** since epoch |
| Tumbling window index | $W_i = \left\lfloor \dfrac{t - t_0}{\Delta} \right\rfloor$ | $\Delta$ = window size (s) |
| Window overlap (Sliding) | $overlap = \Delta - slide$ | $0 < slide \le \Delta$ |
| Throughput | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{events}}{T_{window}}$ | events/sec |
| End-to-end latency | $L = L_{ingest} + L_{window} + L_{process} + L_{sink}$ | milliseconds |
| Out-of-orderness bound | $OO = ts_{max} - ts_{min}$ | bounded vs unbounded |
| Watermark advance rule | $W(t) = \max(ts) - maxOutOfOrderness$ | **ms** since epoch |
| CEP pattern probability | $P(AB) = P(A) \cdot P(B \vert A)$ | conditional independence assumed |
| Memory per window (approx) | $M \approx \lambda \cdot \Delta \cdot S_{event}$ | bytes |
| Back-pressure signal | $B = \dfrac{q_{in} - q_{out}}{C}$ | $0 \le B \le 1$ (saturated) |

> **Note:** All vertical bars in the table above are rendered using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Event processing is the operational substrate behind:

- 🏦 **Banking**: real-time fraud detection (Visa processes ~5,000 txn/s using CEP).
- 🌐 **Telecom**: network anomaly detection and dynamic load balancing.
- 🛒 **E-commerce**: personalized recommendation engines reacting to clickstreams.
- 🏭 **Industry 4.0**: predictive maintenance on IoT sensor telemetry.
- 🚗 **Connected Vehicles**: V2X (vehicle-to-everything) event correlation for ADAS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tumbling Window Index

We want a deterministic function that, given an event timestamp $t$, returns the index of the tumbling window to which this event belongs.

**Step 1 — Define the origin and window size.**
Let $t_0$ be the epoch origin (window 0 starts at $t_0$) and $\Delta$ be the constant window size in seconds.

**Step 2 — Compute elapsed time since origin.**
The elapsed time from the origin is simply:

$$\Delta t = t - t_0$$

**Step 3 — Divide elapsed time by window size.**
The number of full windows elapsed is:

$$n = \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta} = \frac{t - t_0}{\Delta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the floor function.**
Because we want the *index* of the current (potentially partial) window, we take the integer part:

$$W_i = \left\lfloor \frac{t - t_0}{\Delta} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 5 — Validate with a numeric example.**
Suppose $t_0 = 0$, $\Delta = 60$ s. For $t = 145$ s:

$$W_i = \left\lfloor \frac{145 - 0}{60} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2.4166 \right\rfloor = 2$$

The event falls into window $[120, 180)$, which is correct. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation: Sliding Window Count

A sliding window of size $\Delta$ and slide $s$ will produce a stream of overlapping windows. For an event arriving at time $t$, the number of active windows containing it is:

**Step 1 — Determine which windows include the event.**
A window starting at time $w_s$ includes $t$ if $w_s \le t < w_s + \Delta$.

**Step 2 — Express $w_s$ in terms of $s$.**
$w_s = t_0 + k \cdot s$ for integer $k \ge 0$.

**Step 3 — Solve for the range of valid $k$.**
From $w_s \le t$:

$$t_0 + k s \le t \implies k \le \frac{t - t_0}{s}$$

From $w_s + \Delta > t$:

$$t_0 + k s + \Delta > t \implies k > \frac{t - t_0 - \Delta}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the count.**
The number of valid integers $k$ is approximately:

$$N_{windows}(t) \approx \left\lceil \frac{\Delta}{s} \right\rceil$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
A 60-second sliding window with a 10-second slide produces **6 active windows** at any instant — explaining why sliding windows are memory-heavier than tumbling windows.

### 3.3 Derivation: CEP Pattern Probability

For a CEP rule "A followed by B within time $\tau$", we want the probability that the complex event fires.

**Step 1 — Define events.**
Let $A$ be the triggering primitive event and $B$ be the confirming event.

**Step 2 — Apply conditional probability.**
Assuming $A$ has occurred at $t_A$, the probability that $B$ occurs within $\tau$:

$$P(complex) = P(A) \cdot P(t_B - t_A \le \tau \;\vert\; A)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute a Poisson model.**
If $B$ follows a Poisson process with rate $\lambda_B$:

$$P(t_B - t_A \le \tau) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_B \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Final closed form.**

$$P(complex) = P(A) \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_B \tau} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**
If $P(A) = 0.2$, $\lambda_B = 0.5$/s, $\tau = 4$ s:

$$P(complex) = 0.2 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.5 \times 4}) = 0.2 \cdot (1 - e^{-2}) = 0.2 \cdot 0.8647 \approx 0.173$$

So ~17.3% of triggering $A$ events will be confirmed by $B$ within 4 s.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CEP Pattern Operators

| Operator | Symbol | Semantics | Use Case |
|----------|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Sequence** | `->` | A then B (any time) | Order tracking |
| **Bounded Sequence** | `-[\tau]->` | A then B within $\tau$ | SLA breaches |
| **Conjunction** | `and` | A and B (any order, within window) | Multi-signal fusion |
| **Disjunction** | `or` | A or B | Fallback trigger |
| **Negation** | `not` | A without B in window | Absence detection |
| **Repetition** | `A{n,m}` | n..m occurrences of A | Burst detection |
| **Window** | `win:time(\tau)` | All events inside a time window | Local aggregation |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Event Processing Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagrams a **complete event-driven pipeline** from producers through the CEP/ESP engine to downstream sinks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[IoT Sensor A] -->|Event| BusA[(Kafka Topic A)]
    P2[Web Clickstream] -->|Event| BusB[(Kafka Topic B)]
    P3[POS Terminal] -->|Event| BusC[(Kafka Topic C)]

    BusA --> Ingest1[Ingestion Adapter]
    BusB --> Ingest2[Ingestion Adapter]
    BusC --> Ingest3[Ingestion Adapter]

    Ingest1 --> Normalize[Timestamp Normalizer]
    Ingest2 --> Normalize
    Ingest3 --> Normalize

    Normalize --> WindowOp[Windowing Operator]

    WindowOp --> FilterA[Filter: type==transaction]
    WindowOp --> FilterB[Filter: amount greater 10000]
    FilterA --> Agg[Aggregator: sum per user]
    FilterB --> Agg

    Agg --> CEPEngine[CEP Engine Pattern Matcher]
    CEPEngine -->|Match| AlertSvc[Alert Service]
    CEPEngine -->|No Match| DashStore[(Dashboard DB)]

    AlertSvc --> Notify[Push Notification]
    AlertSvc --> Audit[(Audit Log)]
```

### 4.2 Event Processing Network (EPN) Internal View

The following block diagram details how multiple Event Processing Agents (EPAs) compose into an Event Processing Network.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Producers
        SRC1[Source 1]
        SRC2[Source 2]
        SRC3[Source 3]
    end

    SRC1 --> E1[Filter EPA]
    SRC2 --> E2[Filter EPA]
    SRC3 --> E3[Filter EPA]

    E1 --> E4[Join EPA]
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E5[Pattern Detector EPA]
    E4 --> E5

    E5 --> E6[Aggregator EPA]
    E6 --> E7[Sink EPA]

    E7 --> DB[(Database)]
    E7 --> MQ[(Message Queue)]
    E7 --> API[REST API]
```

### 4.3 Windowing Comparison — Visual Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tumbling
        T1[Window 0 to 60]:::t
        T2[Window 60 to 120]:::t
        T3[Window 120 to 180]:::t
    end

    subgraph Sliding
        S1[Window 0 to 60]:::s
        S2[Window 30 to 90]:::s
        S3[Window 60 to 120]:::s
        S4[Window 90 to 150]:::s
    end

    classDef t fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#0366d6
    classDef s fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#d97706
```

### 4.4 CEP Pattern Matching — State Machine View

The following diagram models a "Fraud Alert" CEP rule as a finite state machine.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> SawSwipe: Event Login
    SawSwipe --> SawLargeTxn: Event Txn greater 50000
    SawSwipe --> Idle: Timer 5m expires
    SawLargeTxn --> Alert: Event LocationChange
    SawLargeTxn --> Idle: Timer 10s expires
    Alert --> [*]: Notify Sink
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an "Event" in the context of Event Processing. List any four mandatory attributes an event tuple must contain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Event** is an immutable, time-stamped record that represents a discrete occurrence of interest within a system. It is the fundamental unit of information in event-driven architectures and event processing engines.

**Four mandatory attributes of an event tuple:**

1. **Event ID** — A unique identifier for de-duplication and tracking (e.g., UUID).
2. **Event Type** — A categorical label (e.g., `LOGIN`, `TRANSACTION`, `TEMPERATURE_READING`).
3. **Timestamp** — The moment the event occurred, typically in **Unix epoch milliseconds**.
4. **Payload** — The business data carried by the event (e.g., `{userId, amount, location}`).

*(Optional 5th attribute for full credit: **Source/Origin** — which producer emitted the event.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Event Stream Processing (ESP) and Complex Event Processing (CEP).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **ESP** | **CEP** |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Primary Goal** | Continuous transformation of data-in-motion | Detection of complex patterns across events |
| **Data Model** | Ordered unbounded streams | Event clouds (partially ordered sets) |
| **Query Style** | Streaming SQL (windowed aggregations) | Pattern rules (EPL — Event Processing Language) |
| **State** | Mostly window-bounded state | Often long-lived correlator state |
| **Example Tool** | Apache Flink, Kafka Streams | Esper, Drools Fusion |
| **Use Case** | Rolling 5-minute average price | "Login from new device AND >$10k txn within 60s → fraud" |

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an Event Processing system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the four major windowing strategies used in Event Stream Processing. Compare their memory footprints using suitable formulas. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture of an Event Processing System (7 marks)**

A typical event processing system is organized into **five logical layers**, each with a distinct responsibility:

1. **Event Source Layer**
   - Producers (IoT devices, web apps, microservices, databases via CDC).
   - Emits events asynchronously using protocols like HTTP, MQTT, AMQP, or Kafka.

2. **Ingestion / Transport Layer**
   - Message brokers (Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS Kinesis) provide durable, ordered, replayable buffers.
   - Decouples producers from consumers; handles **back-pressure** and **load spikes**.

3. **Processing Layer (The Engine)**
   - The core ESP/CEP engine (Flink, Spark Streaming, Esper).
   - Performs filtering, transformation, aggregation, and pattern detection.
   - Maintains **windowed state** using RocksDB or in-memory stores.

4. **Storage / Sink Layer**
   - Results are persisted to OLTP DBs, data warehouses, data lakes, or cache stores.
   - May also trigger webhooks, push notifications, or actuators.

5. **Monitoring & Orchestration Layer**
   - Observability via metrics (Prometheus), logs (ELK), and tracing (Jaeger).
   - Tools like Kubernetes provide auto-scaling and fault tolerance.

*(Diagram: refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**(b) Four Windowing Strategies (7 marks)**

1. **Tumbling Window** — fixed-size, non-overlapping. Memory per window: $M_T = \lambda \cdot \Delta \cdot S_{event}$.

2. **Sliding Window** — fixed-size, overlapping by slide $s$. Number of concurrent windows: $\lceil \Delta/s \rceil$. Total memory: $M_S = M_T \cdot \lceil \Delta/s \rceil$.

3. **Session Window** — dynamic, gap-based. Memory grows with session length $L_{sess}$: $M_{Se} = \lambda \cdot L_{sess} \cdot S_{event}$.

4. **Global Window** — single window over entire stream. Memory: $M_G = \lambda \cdot T_{total} \cdot S_{event}$ (potentially unbounded).

**Comparative Table:**

| Window Type | Memory Cost | Use Case |
|-------------|-------------|----------|
| Tumbling | $M_T$ | Periodic reports |
| Sliding | $M_T \cdot \lceil \Delta/s \rceil$ | Moving averages |
| Session | $\lambda \cdot L_{sess} \cdot S_{event}$ | User activity sessions |
| Global | $\lambda \cdot T_{total} \cdot S_{event}$ | Whole-stream correlation |

---

#### **🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is Complex Event Processing (CEP)? Explain any three CEP pattern operators with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A banking system receives 200 transactions per second on average. Each transaction event has an average size of 512 bytes. Compute the memory required for (i) a 60-second tumbling window, (ii) a 60-second sliding window with 10-second slide, and (iii) a session window where the average session lasts 8 minutes. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) CEP and Three Pattern Operators (7 marks)**

**Complex Event Processing (CEP)** is a paradigm that analyzes and correlates large volumes of primitive events to detect higher-level, business-meaningful occurrences (complex events) in real time. It uses rule-based pattern languages to express temporal, causal, and logical relationships.

**Three pattern operators:**

1. **Sequence Operator (`->`)** — Fires when A is followed by B (regardless of intervening events). Example: `Login -> Logout` (track user sessions).

2. **Bounded Sequence (`-[τ]->`)** — A followed by B within time τ. Example: `CardSwipe -[60s]-> LargePurchase` (potential fraud within 1 minute).

3. **Negation (`not`)** — Detects the *absence* of an expected event. Example: `OrderPlaced -> not PaymentReceived within 300s` (abandoned carts).

*(Other operators: conjunction `and`, disjunction `or`, repetition `A{n,m}`, all valid for full credit.)*

**(b) Memory Computations (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\lambda = 200$ events/s, $S_{event} = 512$ bytes, $\Delta = 60$ s, $s = 10$ s, $L_{sess} = 8 \text{ min} = 480$ s.

**(i) Tumbling Window (60 s):**
$M_T = \lambda \cdot \Delta \cdot S_{event} = 200 \times 60 \times 512$
$M_T = 6,144,000 \text{ bytes} \approx 6.14 \text{ MB}$

**(ii) Sliding Window (60 s, slide 10 s):**
Number of concurrent windows $= \lceil \Delta/s \rceil = \lceil 60/10 \rceil = 6$
$M_S = M_T \times 6 = 6.14 \times 6 = 36.86 \text{ MB}$

**(iii) Session Window (avg 480 s):**
$M_{Se} = \lambda \cdot L_{sess} \cdot S_{event} = 200 \times 480 \times 512$
$M_{Se} = 49,152,000 \text{ bytes} \approx 49.15 \text{ MB}$

**Conclusion:** Sliding windows consume ~6× more memory than tumbling, and session windows can consume orders of magnitude more depending on session length — a key architectural trade-off.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (from KTU 2019–2024 valuation reports):**
> 1. **Confusing ESP with CEP** — Examiners report ~30% of students lose 2–3 marks by treating them as synonyms. Always state *what data model* each operates on.
> 2. **Forgetting the timestamp** — An "event" without a timestamp is merely a message. The timestamp is *what makes it an event*.
> 3. **Wrong units in windowing math** — Mixing seconds and milliseconds yields 1000× errors. Always declare units.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams** — Even in 7-mark sub-parts, a labeled block diagram earns 1–2 easy marks.
> 5. **No "real-world use case"** — KTU 2024 Scheme rewards application-level thinking (per Bloom's Level 3–4). Always anchor with a use case.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Event Processing**

- ✅ **Event** = `<id, type, timestamp, payload, source>` — memorize the tuple structure.
- ✅ **ESP vs CEP** — ESP = stream transformations; CEP = pattern detection. *Never use interchangeably.*
- ✅ **Four window types** — Tumbling (no overlap), Sliding (overlap), Session (gap-based), Global (single).
- ✅ **Window index formula** — $W_i = \lfloor (t - t_0)/\Delta \rfloor$
- ✅ **Sliding window count** — $\lceil \Delta / s \rceil$ concurrent windows.
- ✅ **Memory formula** — $M = \lambda \cdot \Delta \cdot S_{event}$ (tumbling baseline).
- ✅ **Watermarks** — $W(t) = \max(ts) - maxOutOfOrderness$ for handling late events.
- ✅ **CEP operators** — Sequence (`->`), Bounded (`-[τ]->`), Negation (`not`), Conjunction (`and`), Repetition (`A{n,m}`).
- ✅ **Poisson CEP probability** — $P(complex) = P(A) \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda_B \tau})$.
- ✅ **Architecture layers** — Source → Ingestion → Processing → Storage → Monitoring.
- ✅ **Tools to remember** — Apache Kafka (broker), Apache Flink (ESP), Esper (CEP), Drools Fusion (CEP), KSQL (streaming SQL).
- ✅ **Latency formula** — $L = L_{ingest} + L_{window} + L_{process} + L_{sink}$.
- ✅ **Bloom's cognitive focus** — KTU asks L2 (Understand) for definitions, L3 (Apply) for windowing problems, L4 (Analyze) for CEP rules.

---

*End of Notes — Event Processing (Module 3, PBPET604, KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organisation of IoT System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Organisation of IoT System

# 🛰️ Organisation of IoT System — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard)**
> The *Organisation of an IoT System* refers to the **structured, hierarchical arrangement of hardware, software, communication protocols, data processing pipelines, and management layers** that enable seamless interaction between physical sensing devices, network infrastructure, cloud/edge computing platforms, and end-user applications. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), it is formally described across four reference architecture models: the **three-layer, four-layer, five-layer, and cloud-fog-edge (CFE) models**, together with the ITU-T Y.2060 reference model.

Mathematically, the functional capacity of an organized IoT system can be represented as a tuple:

$$ \mathcal{S}_{IoT} = \langle D, N, P, A, U \rangle $$

where:
- $D$ = **Device Layer** (sensors, actuators, RFID tags)
- $N$ = **Network Layer** (gateways, routers, communication protocols)
- $P$ = **Processing Layer** (edge/fog/cloud compute nodes)
- $A$ = **Application Layer** (vertical-specific services & dashboards)
- $U$ = **User / Business Layer** (analytics, decision support)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Imagine an IoT system as a **modern smart hospital**:
> - **Patients (Things/Sensors)** continuously emit health data (temperature, heart rate, BP).
> - **Ward nurses (Edge Gateways)** collect and pre-screen this data locally.
> - **Specialist doctors (Fog Nodes)** analyse critical patterns locally.
> - **Hospital Central Records (Cloud Server)** archive, analyse, and trigger AI models.
> - **The Hospital Admin Dashboard (Application Layer)** shows insights to management.
> - **Patients' families (End Users)** consume meaningful alerts and notifications.

Each layer *does a specific job*, has its **own latency tolerance**, **computational power**, and **security policy** — exactly mirroring how an IoT system is *organised*.

### 1.3 Why Organisation Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a **well-defined organisation**, an IoT deployment collapses into a "data swamp" — unmanageable, insecure, and unscalable. Proper organisation ensures:
> - **Interoperability** across heterogeneous devices
> - **Deterministic latency** for time-critical actuation
> - **Energy efficiency** at the device tier
> - **Scalability** to millions of nodes
> - **Security by design** (per-layer policy enforcement)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical IoT Organisation Models

#### 🔹 Model 1 — Three-Layer Architecture (Classical View)

This is the **simplest and most frequently tested** model in KTU board exams.

| Layer | Functional Role | Typical Components | KTU High-Yield Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical sensing & actuation | Sensors, RFID, GPS, Actuators | "Sense the World" |
| **Network Layer** | Data transport | Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, Gateways | "Connect & Transmit" |
| **Application Layer** | Service delivery to user | Smart Home, Health Apps, Dashboards | "Deliver Value" |

#### 🔹 Model 2 — Four-Layer Architecture (Middleware-Enabled)

The three-layer model **fails** when raw sensor data must be normalised, filtered, and contextualised. A **Middleware / Support Layer** is inserted between Network and Application layers:

$$ \text{4-Layer Model} = \text{Perception} \rightarrow \text{Network} \rightarrow \text{Middleware (Processing)} \rightarrow \text{Application} $$

#### 🔹 Model 3 — Five-Layer Architecture (Cloud-Integrated, KTU Favourite)

The **most important model for KTU 14-mark questions**:

| Layer | Function | Sub-Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception / Sensors** | Physical world capture | Sensors, Actuators, Edge devices |
| **Transport / Network** | Bidirectional routing | Gateways, Cellular, LPWAN, Wi-Fi |
| **Processing / Edge-Cloud** | Storage, analytics, decision | Edge, Fog, Cloud servers |
| **Application / Service** | Domain-specific delivery | Smart grid, Healthcare, Industry 4.0 |
| **Business / Management** | Strategic orchestration, SLA, billing | KPI dashboards, ROI models, Governance |

#### 🔹 Model 4 — Cloud–Fog–Edge (CFE) Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CFE model** is mandated by KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus because it reflects *where computation happens*, addressing the **3Vs of latency-critical IoT** (Volume, Velocity, Veracity).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edge Tier} &: \text{Millisecond latency, sensor-adjacent, low power} \\
\text{Fog Tier} &: \text{Sub-second latency, LAN-wide aggregation} \\
\text{Cloud Tier} &: \text{Multi-second latency, global analytics, archival storage}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2 Core Components of an Organised IoT System

A **complete IoT organisation** is built from **six functional building blocks**:

1. **Things / Devices (DT)** — uniquely identifiable physical/virtual entities with embedded sensors/actuators.
2. **Gateways (GW)** — protocol translators (e.g., ZigBee → IP).
3. **Network & Communication Fabric (NCF)** — wired/wireless infrastructure.
4. **Cloud / Edge / Fog Compute (CEFC)** — processing continuum.
5. **Application Services (AS)** — vertical-domain applications.
6. **Management, Security & Analytics Plane (MSAP)** — cross-cutting plane.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the equations below. They appear in **both Part A and Part B** of KTU ESE papers.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon channel capacity | $C$ in bps, $B$ in Hz, SNR dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_s + T_{\text{net}} + T_p + T_a$ | End-to-end latency budget | All in ms |
| 3 | $D_{\text{rate}} = N_s \cdot f_s \cdot b$ | Aggregate data rate | $D_{\text{rate}}$ in bps |
| 4 | $E_{\text{batt}} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{op}}$ | Battery energy budget | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $N_{\max} = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_{\text{per\_node}}}$ | Max nodes per gateway | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \dfrac{U_{\text{data}}}{T_{\text{data}}}$ | Useful throughput efficiency | bps / bps |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i}$ | Cluster-level reliability | Hours |
| 8 | $P_{\text{proc}} \propto f_{\text{clk}} \cdot V_{dd}^{2}$ | CMOS dynamic power model | Watts |
| 9 | $Q_{\text{queue}} = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}$ for $\rho < 1$ | Queueing delay (M/M/1) | Erlangs |
| 10 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{service}} - C_{\text{total}})}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Business-layer KPI | Percentage (%) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Five-layer model enables predictive maintenance at the Edge layer while inventory forecasting runs on Cloud.
- **Precision Agriculture:** LoRa-PER (Perception) + Cellular-NET + Fog-PROCESS + Web/Mobile-APP + BUSINESS dashboards.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Edge processing preserves patient privacy (HIPAA/GDPR compliance).
- **Smart Cities:** Multi-tenant organisational structures support traffic, lighting, and pollution subsystems on a **shared infrastructure**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **foundational equations** that govern IoT system organisation. Each derivation is exhaustive, follows KTU board valuation expectations, and is suitable for full 7-mark sub-questions.

### 3.1 Derivation I — End-to-End Latency Composition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** "Explain with derivation how end-to-end latency is composed in an organised IoT system." (Expected: 7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start from the architectural decomposition.**

An organised IoT pipeline traverses four sequential stages: sensing, transmission, processing, and actuation/display.

$$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{app}} $$

**Step 2 — Expand each sub-term to its physical constituents.**

The sensing latency is dominated by ADC conversion time and sensor settling time:

$$ T_{\text{sense}} = T_{\text{settle}} + T_{\text{ADC}} + T_{\text{digital\_filt}} $$

The transmission latency includes propagation, queuing, and serialization:

$$ T_{\text{transmit}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{serial}} $$

**Step 3 — Quantify the transmission sub-terms.**

Propagation latency for a distance $d$ over a medium with propagation speed $v_p$:

$$ T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_p} $$

Serialization latency for a packet of length $L$ bits over link rate $R$:

$$ T_{\text{serial}} = \frac{L}{R} $$

**Step 4 — Introduce the M/M/1 queueing model for network buffering.**

For an M/M/1 queue with arrival rate $\lambda$ and service rate $\mu$, the utilisation factor is $\rho = \lambda / \mu$. The mean queueing delay:

$$ T_{\text{queue}} = \frac{\rho}{\mu (1 - \rho)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} $$

This holds strictly under the **stability condition $\rho < 1$**, i.e., $\lambda < \mu$.

**Step 5 — Compose the final consolidated form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= \left( T_{\text{settle}} + T_{\text{ADC}} + T_{\text{digital\_filt}} \right) \\
&\quad + \left( \frac{d}{v_p} + \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} + \frac{L}{R} \right) \\
&\quad + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{app}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineer the equation for real-time guarantees.**

For a hard real-time IoT control loop, the deadline $D_{\text{max}}$ must satisfy:

$$ D_{\text{max}} \geq T_{\text{total}} + T_{\text{jitter}} + T_{\text{safety\_margin}} $$

This is the **principal KTU organisational principle** for time-critical systems (e.g., autonomous vehicles, industrial robotic arms).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Shannon Capacity & Maximum Node Density

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** "Derive the maximum number of IoT devices a gateway can support." (Expected: 7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem.**

For an AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR}$:

$$ C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad [\text{bits/s}] $$

**Step 2 — Express SNR in terms of received power and noise floor.**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_r}{P_n} \quad ; \quad P_n = k_B T_0 B \cdot F
$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is the **Boltzmann constant**, $T_0$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $F$ is the receiver noise figure (dimensionless).

**Step 3 — Substitute to obtain the capacity in terms of physical layer parameters.**

$$ C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P_r}{k_B T_0 B F}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Define the per-node bandwidth requirement.**

Each IoT node requires a minimum data rate $R_{\text{node}}$ for its sampled data:

$$ R_{\text{node}} = N_s \cdot f_s \cdot b $$

with $N_s$ sensors, sampling frequency $f_s$ in Hz, and $b$ bits per sample.

**Step 5 — Derive the maximum node density $N_{\max}$.**

If the gateway must serve $N_{\max}$ nodes fairly, with a fairness factor $\alpha \in (0,1]$:

$$ N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\alpha \cdot C}{R_{\text{node}}} \right\rfloor $$

**Step 6 — Substitute Shannon's formula explicitly.**

$$ N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\alpha \cdot B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})}{N_s f_s b} \right\rfloor $$

This is the **canonical KTU equation for gateway dimensioning**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Battery Lifetime for Battery-Operated IoT Nodes

**Step 1 — Define battery energy.**

A battery of voltage $V$ supplying current $I$ for time $t$ delivers:

$$ E_{\text{batt}} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{op}} $$

**Step 2 — Express operational current as the sum of subsystem currents.**

$$ I = I_{\text{sleep}} + I_{\text{active}} \cdot D_c + I_{\text{tx}} \cdot D_t $$

where $D_c$ is the duty cycle (fraction of time active) and $D_t$ is the transmission duty cycle.

**Step 3 — Express lifetime as energy divided by average power.**

$$ t_{\text{op}} = \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} $$

with $Q_{\text{mAh}}$ as battery capacity and $I_{\text{avg}}$ as average current draw.

**Step 4 — Solve for operational lifetime.**

$$
t_{\text{op}} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{sleep}} + I_{\text{active}} D_c + I_{\text{tx}} D_t}
$$

This equation is central to **Energy-Harvesting IoT organisation**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Layer-by-Layer Functional Mapping

| Architectural Layer | Primary KPI | Governing Equation | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perception (DT)** | Sensor accuracy, energy | $E_{\text{batt}} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{op}}$ | Battery drain |
| **Transport (NCF)** | Bandwidth efficiency | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Channel saturation |
| **Middleware/PROC** | Processing delay | $T_q = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Queue overflow |
| **Application (AS)** | Service availability | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$ | SLA breach |
| **Business (MSAP)** | ROI & compliance | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{V_s - C_t}{C_t} \times 100$ | Negative ROI |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Master Five-Layer IoT Organisational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BUSINESS["BUSINESS AND MANAGEMENT LAYER"]
        B1[KPI DASHBOARDS]
        B2[BILLING AND SLA GOVERNANCE]
        B3[ROI AND COMPLIANCE]
    end

    subgraph APPLICATION["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1[SMART HEALTH APP]
        A2[INDUSTRY 4 DASHBOARD]
        A3[SMART CITY PORTAL]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["PROCESSING LAYER EDGE FOG CLOUD"]
        P1[EDGE NODE MCU]
        P2[FOG NODE LAN SERVER]
        P3[CLOUD DATA CENTRE]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK["NETWORK LAYER"]
        N1[WIFI 6 GATEWAY]
        N2[ZIGBEE MESH]
        N3[5G NR SMALL CELL]
        N4[LORA BASESTATION]
    end

    subgraph PERCEPTION["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        PE1[TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
        PE2[RFID TAG]
        PE3[ACTUATOR MOTOR]
        PE4[CAMERA MODULE]
    end

    PE1 --> N1
    PE2 --> N2
    PE3 --> N3
    PE4 --> N4

    N1 --> P1
    N2 --> P1
    N3 --> P2
    N4 --> P2

    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3

    P3 --> A1
    P3 --> A2
    P3 --> A3

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Guide:** Follow the arrows from **bottom (physical world)** to **top (business value)**. Each tier adds **abstraction, latency tolerance, and computational capacity** while reducing **time-criticality**.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Cloud-Fog-Edge Compute Continuum (CFE)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEVICE["DEVICE TIER"]
        D1[SENSOR NODE A]
        D2[SENSOR NODE B]
        D3[ACTUATOR NODE]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE TIER LATENCY 1 MS"]
        E1[EDGE GATEWAY 1]
        E2[EDGE GATEWAY 2]
    end

    subgraph FOG["FOG TIER LATENCY 10 MS"]
        F1[FOG AGGREGATOR LAN]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD TIER LATENCY 200 MS"]
        C1[REGIONAL CLOUD]
        C2[GLOBAL CLOUD]
    end

    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E2

    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how latency grows **exponentially** as we move from Edge → Fog → Cloud. This is the engineering rationale for placing **time-critical AI inference at the Edge**.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cross-Cutting Management, Security & Analytics Plane (MSAP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MSAP["CROSS CUTTING MSAP PLANE"]
        M1[IDENTITY AND ACCESS MANAGEMENT]
        M2[DEVICE PROVISIONING]
        M3[OTA FIRMWARE UPDATE]
        M4[ANOMALY DETECTION AI]
        M5[ENCRYPTION KEY MANAGEMENT]
    end

    subgraph SYSTEM["ORGANISED IOT STACK"]
        S1[PERCEPTION]
        S2[NETWORK]
        S3[PROCESSING]
        S4[APPLICATION]
        S5[BUSINESS]
    end

    M1 -.-> S1
    M1 -.-> S2
    M2 -.-> S1
    M3 -.-> S1
    M4 -.-> S3
    M5 -.-> S2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The MSAP is a *cross-cutting* plane — it **does not appear sequentially in the data path** but **touches every layer** to enforce consistent security, observability, and lifecycle management.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Functional Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DEV[DEVICES AND THINGS DT] --> GW[GATEWAYS GW]
    GW --> NCF[NETWORK AND COMM FABRIC NCF]
    NCF --> CEFC[CLOUD EDGE FOG COMPUTE CEFC]
    CEFC --> AS[APPLICATION SERVICES AS]
    AS --> MSAP[MANAGEMENT SECURITY ANALYTICS MSAP]
    MSAP -.-> DEV
    MSAP -.-> GW
    MSAP -.-> NCF
    MSAP -.-> CEFC
    MSAP -.-> AS
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** Define the *five-layer architecture* of an IoT system and list its layers.

**Model Answer:**
The five-layer architecture is the most widely accepted organisational model for IoT systems. It comprises:
1. **Perception Layer** — sensors, actuators, RFID, GPS that capture physical data.
2. **Network / Transport Layer** — Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, gateways that route data.
3. **Processing Layer** — edge, fog, and cloud servers that analyse data.
4. **Application Layer** — domain-specific services like smart healthcare, smart grid.
5. **Business Layer** — manages KPIs, ROI, SLAs, billing, and governance.

It improves upon the three-layer model by adding middleware/processing and business management tiers, enabling scalability, security, and analytics. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Distinguish between the **Edge**, **Fog**, and **Cloud** tiers in the CFE organisational model.

**Model Answer:**

| Tier | Location | Latency | Compute | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge** | Sensor-adjacent | $\sim$1 ms | Microcontroller / SoC | Threshold alerts |
| **Fog** | LAN-level | $\sim$10 ms | Local server / gateway | Cluster analytics |
| **Cloud** | Global data-centre | $\sim$200 ms | Hyperscale cluster | AI training, archival |

The organisational choice depends on the **latency-budget**, **bandwidth-availability**, and **privacy-constraints** of the application. *(3 marks)*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Internal Choice Format*

---

#### **Question B (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture** of an organised IoT system. Describe the function of each layer with **two real-world components** per layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Shannon channel capacity** equation and use it to compute the maximum number of IoT nodes $N_{\max}$ a LoRa gateway can serve. Given: $B = 125$ kHz, $\text{SNR} = 12$ dB, $N_s = 2$ sensors per node, $f_s = 1$ Hz, $b = 16$ bits, $\alpha = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram drawn must resemble **Diagram 4.1** above, with clear labelling of all five layers and minimum two components per layer.

| Layer | Function | Two Real-World Components |
|---|---|---|
| Perception | Sense physical phenomena | DHT22 temperature sensor, RFID tag |
| Network | Transmit data reliably | LoRa SX1276, Wi-Fi 6 AP |
| Processing | Analyse, filter, store | Raspberry Pi (Edge), AWS IoT Core (Cloud) |
| Application | Deliver vertical service | Smart Home App, Industrial SCADA |
| Business | Govern, monetise | SLA Dashboard, Billing Engine |

Each layer must be explained in **2–3 sentences** with its role and one example for full marks. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85 $$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's theorem.**

$$ C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) $$

$$ C = 125 \times 10^{3} \cdot \log_2(1 + 15.85) $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$ \log_2(16.85) = \dfrac{\ln(16.85)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2.824}{0.6931} \approx 4.075 $$

**Step 4 — Compute capacity.**

$$ C \approx 125{,}000 \times 4.075 \approx 509{,}375 \text{ bps} $$

**Step 5 — Compute per-node data rate.**

$$ R_{\text{node}} = N_s \cdot f_s \cdot b = 2 \cdot 1 \cdot 16 = 32 \text{ bps} $$

**Step 6 — Apply fairness factor and round down.**

$$ N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\alpha \cdot C}{R_{\text{node}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{0.5 \times 509{,}375}{32} \right\rfloor $$

$$ N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{254{,}687.5}{32} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 7{,}959 \rfloor $$
