**Final Answer:** The LoRa gateway can support up to **$N_{\max} = 7{,}959$ nodes**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **cloud-fog-edge (CFE) organisational model** with a labelled block diagram. Compare it with the classical three-layer model in terms of latency, scalability, and privacy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A battery-operated IoT sensor node has the following specifications: $V = 3.3$ V, $Q_{\text{mAh}} = 2400$ mAh, $I_{\text{sleep}} = 10 \ \mu$A, $I_{\text{active}} = 25$ mA, $I_{\text{tx}} = 120$ mA, $D_c = 0.01$, $D_t = 0.001$. Calculate the **operational lifetime in days**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The CFE model **decomposes the Processing Layer** into three sub-tiers:

- **Edge** — co-located with sensors, millisecond response, micro-power devices.
- **Fog** — LAN-aggregation, sub-second response, intermediate storage.
- **Cloud** — global compute, multi-second response, archival and ML training.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Three-Layer Model | CFE Model |
|---|---|---|
| Latency control | Single network tier | Per-tier optimisation |
| Scalability | Limited (cloud bottleneck) | High (compute offload) |
| Privacy | Low (all data to cloud) | High (edge filtering) |
| Energy | High (always-on) | Low (sleep + edge bursts) |

*(7 marks — include labelled diagram resembling Diagram 4.2)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute average current consumption.**

$$ I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{sleep}} + I_{\text{active}} \cdot D_c + I_{\text{tx}} \cdot D_t $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$ I_{\text{avg}} = 10 \times 10^{-6} + 25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 + 120 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.001 $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate term-by-term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{sleep}} &= 0.000010 \text{ A} \\
I_{\text{active}} \cdot D_c &= 0.025 \times 0.01 = 0.000250 \text{ A} \\
I_{\text{tx}} \cdot D_t &= 0.120 \times 0.001 = 0.000120 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions.**

$$ I_{\text{avg}} = 0.000010 + 0.000250 + 0.000120 = 0.000380 \text{ A} = 380 \ \mu\text{A} $$

**Step 5 — Compute operational lifetime in hours.**

$$ t_{\text{op}} = \frac{Q_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{avg,mA}}} = \frac{2400}{0.380} \approx 6315.8 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 6 — Convert to days.**

$$ t_{\text{days}} = \frac{6315.8}{24} \approx 263.2 \text{ days} \approx 8.6 \text{ months} $$

**Final Answer:** The IoT node operates for approximately **263 days** (~8.6 months) on a single charge. *(7 marks)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Layer" with "Tier":** A *layer* is functional (e.g., Application Layer), while a *tier* is architectural (Edge Tier). Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Missing unit conversion for SNR:** SNR in dB **must** be converted to linear (e.g., 12 dB → 15.85) before plugging into Shannon's formula. Skipping this costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring fairness factor $\alpha$:** In gateway dimensioning problems, $\alpha$ reflects MAC-layer overhead. Omitting it leads to **over-engineering** the system — examiner marks this as incorrect.
> 4. **Skipping stability condition $\rho < 1$** in queueing derivations.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without labels:** Unlabelled arrows or boxes receive **zero credit** per KTU valuation guidelines.
> 6. **Conflating Fog and Edge:** Fog is **LAN-level**, Edge is **device-level**. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **IoT Organisation** = structured arrangement of **DT + GW + NCF + CEFC + AS + MSAP**.
- ✅ **Five-layer model** is the **most-tested** architecture in KTU exams.
- ✅ **CFE model** is **mandatory** under KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus.
- ✅ **Shannon's equation** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ is the **gateway dimensioning backbone**.
- ✅ **End-to-end latency** $T_{\text{total}}$ composes from sensing + transmission + processing + application delays.
- ✅ **MSAP** is a **cross-cutting plane**, not a sequential layer.
- ✅ Always convert **dB → linear** before any Shannon/SNR substitution.
- ✅ Always quote **$\rho < 1$** when using M/M/1 queueing model.
- ✅ Battery lifetime formula: $t_{\text{op}} = Q / I_{\text{avg}}$ — convert mAh to mA consistently.
- ✅ Distinguish **layer (functional)** vs **tier (architectural)** — KTU rewards precision.
- ✅ Draw **labelled Mermaid/block diagrams** — they fetch **2–3 easy marks** in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ ROI, MTTF, and SLA are **business-layer KPIs** — do not place them in technical layers.

---

> **🎯 End of Module 3 Notes — Organisation of IoT System**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 | Outcome-Based Education (OBE)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subscriber Publish Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Subscriber Publish Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) Model** is an asynchronous, event-driven messaging paradigm extensively used in parallel and distributed computing, in which the sender of a message (the **Publisher**) does not address the message directly to a specific receiver. Instead, the publisher categorizes the message into a logical class or **Topic**, and any number of independent consumers (**Subscribers**) that have expressed prior interest in that class receive the message automatically through an intermediary routing infrastructure known as the **Event Broker** (or Message Broker, Event Bus, Dispatcher).

Mathematically, the model can be expressed as a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{M}_{pub/sub} = \langle P, S, T, B, \sigma, \delta \rangle
$$

Where:
- $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ is the set of publishers
- $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_m\}$ is the set of subscribers
- $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k\}$ is the set of topics (logical channels)
- $B$ is the event broker / dispatching service
- $\sigma : S \rightarrow 2^T$ is the subscription function (mapping each subscriber to a set of topics)
- $\delta : P \times T \rightarrow M$ is the publish function (mapping publisher-topic pair to message)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The Pub/Sub model is a cornerstone of **decoupled distributed architectures** and is frequently tested under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework. Students must understand the differences between topic-based, content-based, and channel-based variants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a popular **YouTube Channel**:
- A content creator (the **Publisher**) uploads a new video. They do not personally know or message every viewer.
- The creator simply tags the video with a category (e.g., "Programming", "Music") — this is the **Topic**.
- A viewer (the **Subscriber**) clicks the "Subscribe" button and selects the categories of interest. The platform (the **Event Broker**) takes care of pushing notifications to interested viewers.

The key insight: the creator and viewer **never communicate directly**. The notification infrastructure handles the matching, delivery, and routing.

| Real-World Analogy Component | Pub/Sub Model Equivalent |
|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| YouTube channel | Publisher |
| Viewer with subscription | Subscriber |
| Video category/playlist | Topic |
| YouTube notification engine | Event Broker |
| Push notification | Delivered Message |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The decoupling property means publishers and subscribers can operate at different rates, fail independently, scale horizontally, and evolve without coordination — a critical property for **cloud-native**, **microservices**, and **IoT** systems.

## 1.3 Foundational Properties of the Model

1. **Space Decoupling** — Publisher and Subscriber need not know each other (no direct address knowledge).
2. **Time Decoupling** — Publisher and Subscriber need not be active simultaneously; the broker can persist messages.
3. **Synchronization Decoupling** — Blocking operations are not required; the system is fundamentally non-blocking.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mnemonic for exam:** *"The Three Decouplings"* — Space, Time, Synchronization. Memorize this trio for 3-mark definitional questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Components (Structured Logic)

The Pub/Sub system decomposes into **five** primary logical entities:

1. **Publisher (Producer):** The entity that emits messages tagged with a topic. The publisher's API call is typically `publish(topic, payload)`.
2. **Subscriber (Consumer):** The entity that registers interest via `subscribe(topic, callback)`. It may also include **filters** to refine matching.
3. **Event Broker / Message Broker:** The middleware that maintains topic registries, performs message routing, and may persist messages.
4. **Topic (Channel / Subject):** A logical label or routing key. In some implementations (e.g., Kafka), this is called a *partitioned log*.
5. **Subscription Filter:** Optional predicate logic that allows subscribers to receive only a subset of messages under a topic.

## 2.2 Classification of Pub/Sub Models

The taxonomy is essential for KTU 14-mark essay-style questions:

### 2.2.1 Topic-Based (Subject-Based) Pub/Sub
- Subscribers subscribe to named topics.
- Each message is delivered to all subscribers of that topic.
- Example: **Apache Kafka**, **RabbitMQ topic exchange**, **Google Cloud Pub/Sub**.

### 2.2.2 Content-Based Pub/Sub
- Subscribers register interest using **predicates over message attributes**.
- The broker evaluates each message against all predicates and delivers selectively.
- Example: **Gryphon** (IBM), **Siena**, **CEDRUS**.

### 2.2.3 Type-Based Pub/Sub
- Uses object-oriented **class hierarchies** as the subscription key.
- Subscribers express interest in a class or its subclasses.
- Example: **CORBA Event Service**, **Java Message Service (JMS)** topic destinations.

### 2.2.4 Channel-Based (Subject Addressing) Pub/Sub
- Each subscriber gets its own queue (channel); publishers send to a channel.
- Stronger isolation; less common in modern cloud systems.

## 2.3 The Core "Why" and "How"

- **Why is it asynchronous?** Because the publisher does not wait for subscriber acknowledgment at the call site. The broker absorbs the latency.
- **How does scaling work?** Through **fan-out** — one published message can be replicated to $N$ subscribers in parallel.
- **How is ordering preserved?** Within a single partition/topic, total ordering is maintained; across topics, only causal ordering is feasible.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | Notes for KTU |
|------------------|---------|---------------|----------------|
| $T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = T_{pub} + T_{route} + T_{deliver}$ | Total message latency | milliseconds (ms) | Sum of publishing, broker routing, and delivery times |
| $F = \vert S \vert = N_{subscribers}$ | Fan-out factor (count) | dimensionless | Number of subscribers reached per publish |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{msg}}{T_{window}}$ | Throughput | messages per second (msg/s) | $T_{window}$ is the observation interval |
| $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | Little's Law (queueing) | entities in system | $L$ = avg entities, $W$ = avg time in system |
| $C_{total} = C_{broker} + F \cdot C_{net}$ | Communication cost per publish | bytes / network hops | $C_{net}$ is per-subscriber network cost |
| $P_{delivery} = 1 - (1-p)^{F}$ | Probabilistic delivery success | probability | $p$ = per-subscriber delivery probability |
| $T_{scale} = O(\log_k N)$ | Hierarchical broker scale | time | $k$ = branching factor, $N$ = nodes |
| $R_{effective} = \dfrac{R_{broker}}{F}$ | Effective per-subscriber rate | events / sec | Throughput divided by fan-out |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe character rule:** In the table above, absolute value symbols use the LaTeX `\vert` command (rendered as $F = \vert S \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax, in compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 formatting safeguards.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pub/Sub model is foundational to:
- **Microservices Communication** (e.g., service meshes, event-driven APIs)
- **Real-Time Stream Processing** (Apache Kafka, Apache Pulsar, AWS Kinesis)
- **IoT Telemetry Ingestion** (millions of sensor publishers → analytics subscribers)
- **Notification Systems** (push, email, SMS fan-out)
- **Database Change Data Capture (CDC)** (Debezium → Kafka → multiple sinks)
- **Financial Market Data Dissemination** (stock tickers to thousands of algorithmic traders)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Decomposition

The total time from publisher emission to subscriber reception decomposes into three additive stages.

**Step 1: Publishing Latency**
The publisher serializes the message payload and transmits it to the broker. For a payload of size $S$ bytes over a network of bandwidth $B_{net}$ bytes/sec:

$$
T_{pub} = \frac{S}{B_{net}} + T_{serial}
$$

where $T_{serial}$ is the CPU time spent encoding the payload (e.g., JSON, Protocol Buffers, Avro).

**Step 2: Broker Routing Latency**
The broker must perform topic matching, filtering, and dispatch decisions. This includes:

- **Hash-table topic lookup:** $O(1)$ average
- **Predicate evaluation** (for content-based): $O(N_{pred})$
- **Queueing delay** at the broker: $W_q$ from queueing theory

$$
T_{route} = T_{lookup} + T_{filter} + W_q
$$

**Step 3: Delivery Latency**
The broker fans out to all $F$ matching subscribers. The slowest subscriber determines the completion:

$$
T_{deliver} = \max_{i \in \text{matched}}(T_{net,i} + T_{sub,i})
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate End-to-End Latency**

$$
T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = \frac{S}{B_{net}} + T_{serial} + T_{lookup} + T_{filter} + W_q + \max_{i}(T_{net,i} + T_{sub,i})
$$

This additive model shows that the **bottleneck is typically the slowest subscriber** in fan-out delivery, motivating the use of **parallel delivery workers** in production brokers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Little's Law Applied to the Broker

**Step 1: Statement of Little's Law**
For a system in steady state, the long-term average number of entities $L$ equals the arrival rate $\lambda$ multiplied by the average time $W$ an entity spends in the system:

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

**Step 2: Application to the Broker**
Treat the broker as the system under observation. Each in-flight message is an "entity."

- $\lambda$ = publish rate (messages per second)
- $W$ = average residence time of a message in the broker
- $L$ = average number of messages resident in broker buffers

**Step 3: Solving for Maximum Throughput**
The broker has finite buffer capacity $L_{max}$. The maximum sustainable throughput is:

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{L_{max}}{W}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Illustration**
Suppose a Kafka broker holds at most $L_{max} = 100{,}000$ in-flight messages, and the average message residence time is $W = 50$ ms. Then:

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{100{,}000}{0.050} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ messages/sec}
$$

$$
\lambda_{max} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ msg/s}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Probabilistic Delivery in Unreliable Networks

**Step 1: Per-Subscriber Delivery Probability**
Let $p$ be the probability that a single message successfully reaches a given subscriber (accounting for packet loss, retransmissions, broker crashes).

**Step 2: Failure Probability for a Single Subscriber**

$$
P_{fail,1} = 1 - p
$$

**Step 3: Failure Probability for All $F$ Subscribers**

$$
P_{fail,all} = (1 - p)^{F}
$$

**Step 4: Probability That At Least One Subscriber Receives the Message**

$$
P_{delivery} = 1 - (1 - p)^{F}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example**
Suppose $p = 0.99$ and $F = 10$ subscribers.

$$
P_{delivery} = 1 - (0.01)^{10} = 1 - 10^{-20} \approx 1.000
$$

This shows that even with moderate per-subscriber reliability, the **system-level delivery becomes nearly certain** because failure events are statistically independent.

## 3.4 Derivation: Communication Cost and Fan-Out

**Step 1: Cost Definition**
Let $C_{broker}$ be the fixed cost of the broker accepting one message. Let $C_{net}$ be the cost of network transfer to one subscriber.

**Step 2: Linear Cost Model**

$$
C_{total} = C_{broker} + F \cdot C_{net}
$$

**Step 3: Effective Cost per Subscriber**

$$
C_{effective} = \frac{C_{total}}{F} = \frac{C_{broker}}{F} + C_{net}
$$

As $F \to \infty$, the broker cost amortizes to zero per subscriber — this is the **economy of scale** that makes Pub/Sub efficient for high fan-out workloads.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Pub/Sub Variants

| Property | Topic-Based | Content-Based | Type-Based | Channel-Based |
|----------|-------------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| Subscription key | Topic name | Predicate on attributes | Class type | Unique channel ID |
| Broker complexity | Low | High | Medium | Low |
| Filtering cost | $O(1)$ | $O(N_{pred})$ | $O(\text{class hierarchy})$ | $O(1)$ |
| Selectivity | Coarse | Fine | Medium | Per-receiver |
| Real-world example | Kafka topics | Gryphon, Siena | CORBA Event Service | Unique queues |
| Expressiveness | Low | Very High | Medium | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Content-based systems offer the highest expressiveness but suffer from the **predicate evaluation bottleneck** — a critical KTU discussion point. Topic-based systems are the most widely deployed because their $O(1)$ matching scales to millions of subscribers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The diagram below illustrates the canonical Publish/Subscribe architecture with multiple publishers, a topic-based event broker, and fan-out delivery to heterogeneous subscribers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef publisher fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B07A00,color:#000000
    classDef broker fill:#A0D8F0,stroke:#005A8C,color:#000000
    classDef subscriber fill:#C8E6A0,stroke:#3E7A00,color:#000000
    classDef topic fill:#F0A0A0,stroke:#8C0000,color:#000000

    pubA["Publisher A\nSensor Data Source"]:::publisher
    pubB["Publisher B\nApplication Logs"]:::publisher
    pubC["Publisher C\nUser Event Stream"]:::publisher

    broker["Event Broker\nMiddleware Service"]:::broker
    subMgr["Subscription\nManager"]:::broker
    filterEng["Filter\nEngine"]:::broker
    persistLayer["Persistence\nLog Storage"]:::broker

    topicX["Topic X\nTelemetry Data"]:::topic
    topicY["Topic Y\nError Logs"]:::topic
    topicZ["Topic Z\nClickstream"]:::topic

    subA["Subscriber A\nReal-Time Dashboard"]:::subscriber
    subB["Subscriber B\nData Lake Archiver"]:::subscriber
    subC["Subscriber C\nAlerting Engine"]:::subscriber
    subD["Subscriber D\nML Inference Worker"]:::subscriber

    pubA --> topicX
    pubB --> topicY
    pubC --> topicZ

    topicX --> broker
    topicY --> broker
    topicZ --> broker

    broker --> subMgr
    broker --> filterEng
    broker --> persistLayer

    subMgr --> subA
    subMgr --> subB
    subMgr --> subC
    subMgr --> subD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** shows three independent publishers (no awareness of subscribers).
- The **middle column** shows the broker's internal components: subscription manager, filter engine, and persistence log.
- The **right column** shows four heterogeneous subscribers each consuming a different subset of messages.
- Decoupling is preserved along **all three axes** (space, time, synchronization).

## 4.2 Message Lifecycle Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Pub as Publisher
    participant Broker as Event Broker
    participant SubA as Subscriber A
    participant SubB as Subscriber B
    participant SubC as Subscriber C

    Pub->>Broker: publish(topic="orders", payload={id:42, amt:99})
    Note over Broker: Topic match lookup O(1)
    Broker->>Broker: Evaluate subscription filters
    Broker->>Broker: Persist to log storage
    par Parallel fan-out delivery
        Broker->>SubA: deliver(message)
        Broker->>SubB: deliver(message)
        Broker->>SubC: deliver(message) [filtered out by predicate]
    end
    SubA-->>Broker: ACK
    SubB-->>Broker: ACK
    Note over Pub,SubC: Publisher does not wait for ACKs
    Note over Broker: Time-decoupled, async
```

**Interpretation:**
- Step 5 shows **asynchronous fan-out** in parallel lanes using the `par` keyword in Mermaid.
- Step 6 illustrates that **not all subscribers receive the message** — content-based filtering is active.
- The dashed ACK arrows confirm the subscriber-broker acknowledgment loop runs **out of band** from the publisher's original call.

## 4.3 Hierarchical Broker Topology (Scalability Pattern)

For very large deployments, a single broker becomes a bottleneck. The hierarchical pattern distributes load:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef root fill:#F0A0A0,stroke:#8C0000,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B07A00,color:#000000
    classDef leaf fill:#A0D8F0,stroke:#005A8C,color:#000000

    rB["Root Broker\nGlobal Dispatcher"]:::root
    mB1["Regional Broker\nNorth America"]:::mid
    mB2["Regional Broker\nEurope"]:::mid
    mB3["Regional Broker\nAsia Pacific"]:::mid
    lN1["Edge Broker\nNA-East-1"]:::leaf
    lN2["Edge Broker\nNA-West-1"]:::leaf
    lE1["Edge Broker\nEU-Central"]:::leaf
    lE2["Edge Broker\nAP-South-1"]:::leaf

    rB --- mB1
    rB --- mB2
    rB --- mB3
    mB1 --- lN1
    mB1 --- lN2
    mB2 --- lE1
    mB3 --- lE2
```

**Key insight:** Routing latency scales as $O(\log_k N)$ where $k$ is the branching factor (typically 3–8) and $N$ is the number of edge brokers. This is the same scaling law as B-trees and skip lists.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Publish/Subscribe messaging model. List any four distinguishing characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
The Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) model is an asynchronous messaging pattern in which publishers send messages to logical channels called *topics* without knowing the identities of subscribers. Subscribers register interest in topics through an event broker, which performs the routing and delivery.

Four distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Space Decoupling** — Publisher and Subscriber do not know each other's identity or address.
2. **Time Decoupling** — Publisher and Subscriber need not be active concurrently; messages can be persisted.
3. **Synchronization Decoupling** — Publisher does not block waiting for subscriber acknowledgments.
4. **One-to-Many Fan-Out** — A single message can be delivered to multiple subscribers in parallel.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between topic-based and content-based publish/subscribe systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Topic-Based | Content-Based |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Subscription key | Named topic | Predicate over message attributes |
| Filter location | N/A | Broker evaluates predicates |
| Expressiveness | Coarse-grained | Fine-grained |
| Broker overhead | Low (hash lookup $O(1)$) | High (predicate evaluation $O(N_{pred})$) |
| Example | Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ | Gryphon, Siena |

In topic-based systems, the broker simply looks up the topic in a hash table. In content-based systems, the broker must evaluate each incoming message against all active subscription predicates, making it more flexible but computationally expensive.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the Publish/Subscribe model. Describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture consists of **four primary components** (see SECTION 4.1 diagram):

1. **Publishers** — Generate messages and invoke `publish(topic, payload)`. They are unaware of subscriber identities.
2. **Event Broker** — The central middleware. It contains:
   - *Subscription Manager* — Maintains the registry of active subscriptions
   - *Filter Engine* — Evaluates incoming messages against subscription predicates
   - *Persistence Layer* — Stores messages for at-least-once or exactly-once delivery guarantees
3. **Subscribers** — Register interest via `subscribe(topic, callback)`. Receive messages asynchronously and send ACKs back to the broker.
4. **Topics / Channels** — Logical routing keys that decouple publisher intent from subscriber identity.

**Operational Flow:**
- Publisher $\rightarrow$ Broker (with topic label)
- Broker evaluates topic and filters
- Broker $\rightarrow$ All matching subscribers (parallel fan-out)
- Subscribers $\rightarrow$ Broker (ACK)

**(b)** Discuss the different types of Publish/Subscribe systems. Compare topic-based, content-based, and type-based variants with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three major variants are:

1. **Topic-Based:** Subscriptions are pure topic names. Simple, scalable, widely deployed. **Example:** Apache Kafka, where a *topic* is an append-only log partitioned across brokers.

2. **Content-Based:** Subscriptions are Boolean predicates over message headers or payload attributes. More expressive but computationally expensive. **Example:** **Siena** (University of Colorado) — supports hierarchical and covering-based subscriptions.

3. **Type-Based:** Used in object-oriented systems. Subscriptions are class types, and the system uses subclass relationships. **Example:** **CORBA Event Service**, **Java Message Service (JMS)** topic destinations.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Topic-Based | Content-Based | Type-Based |
|---------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| Expressiveness | Low | Very High | Medium |
| Scalability | Very High | Medium | High |
| Broker complexity | Low | High | Medium |
| Real-world use | Most cloud systems | Niche research/enterprise | Legacy O-O systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by (a) failing to label the broker's internal sub-components (Subscription Manager, Filter Engine, Persistence), and (b) not mentioning the **three decoupling properties** explicitly. Always include a dedicated "Properties" subsection in 7-mark architecture questions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the Publish/Subscribe model with the **Point-to-Point (Message Queue)** messaging model. Highlight at least five distinguishing parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Publish/Subscribe | Point-to-Point (Message Queue) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------------------|
| Number of receivers | Many (one-to-many) | Exactly one (one-to-one) |
| Coupling | Loosely coupled, anonymous | Tightly coupled, named receiver |
| Message destination | Topic / channel | Specific queue |
| Message consumption | Broadcast to all subscribers | Removed after one consumer reads |
| Use case | Event notification, fan-out | Work distribution, task queues |
| Persistence | Optional (depends on broker) | Usually persistent until consumed |
| Example | Kafka topic | RabbitMQ default queue, JMS Queue |

**Key Distinction:** In Pub/Sub, a message can be delivered to **multiple** subscribers. In Point-to-Point, a message is delivered to **exactly one** consumer and is then removed from the queue.

**(b)** Explain the major **advantages and challenges** of implementing Publish/Subscribe systems in distributed environments. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**

1. **Scalability** — Horizontal scaling via broker federation; supports $O(\log_k N)$ hierarchical routing.
2. **Loose Coupling** — Publishers and subscribers evolve independently; new subscribers can be added without modifying publishers.
3. **Asynchronous Throughput** — High message rates possible since the publisher does not block.
4. **Fault Isolation** — A failing subscriber does not affect other subscribers or the publisher.
5. **Heterogeneous Integration** — Different subscriber types (mobile, web, batch processors) can coexist.

**Challenges:**

1. **Message Ordering** — Across topics, only causal ordering is feasible; strict total ordering requires expensive consensus.
2. **Delivery Guarantees** — Achieving *exactly-once* delivery in the presence of network partitions is non-trivial (CAP theorem trade-offs).
3. **Broker as Single Point of Failure** — Requires replication, leader election (e.g., Raft, Paxos).
4. **Content-Based Filtering Cost** — Predicate evaluation at the broker can become a bottleneck.
5. **Security and Authorization** — Fine-grained topic-level access control is operationally complex.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** The most common mistake in comparison questions is **listing features without tabular contrast**. KTU examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for a structured table. The second most common error is omitting **CAP theorem implications** in distributed challenges — this is a high-value discussion point for 2024 scheme papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Pub/Sub = Publish + Subscribe + Broker** (three entities, never forget the broker)
- [ ] **Three Decouplings:** Space, Time, Synchronization
- [ ] **Variants:** Topic-based, Content-based, Type-based, Channel-based
- [ ] **Fan-out:** $F = \vert S \vert$ subscribers reached per publish
- [ ] **Latency equation:** $T_{total} = T_{pub} + T_{route} + T_{deliver}$
- [ ] **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — apply to broker for throughput sizing
- [ ] **Delivery probability:** $P_{delivery} = 1 - (1-p)^{F}$
- [ ] **Cost amortizes:** $C_{effective} = C_{broker}/F + C_{net}$
- [ ] **Hierarchical scaling:** $O(\log_k N)$ using tree of brokers
- [ ] **Real-world tools:** Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, Google Pub/Sub, AWS SNS, Apache Pulsar
- [ ] **Key contrast:** Pub/Sub = one-to-many broadcast; Point-to-Point = one-to-one queue consumption
- [ ] **CAP Theorem** governs distributed broker consistency vs availability trade-offs
- [ ] **Exactly-once delivery** is the hardest guarantee — almost all systems offer at-least-once
- [ ] **Common exam trap:** Confusing *topic* (a logical label) with *queue* (a physical buffer)
- [ ] **Mnemonic for 3 properties:** **S**pace, **T**ime, **S**ync — *"STS"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** Always draw the **block diagram with explicit broker internals** (Subscription Manager, Filter Engine, Persistence) for full marks in 7-mark architecture questions. Always cite a **real-world example** (Kafka, RabbitMQ, etc.) — KTU 2024 scheme rewards applied knowledge per NEP 2020 outcome-based education guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604 > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Protocol # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Protocol**, in the context of embedded systems, industrial communication, and networked engineering systems, is a **formally defined set of rules, conventions, and data structures** that govern the exchange of information between two or more communicating entities. According to the ISO (International Organization for Standardization) and IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers) standards, a communication protocol specifies the **syntax (format)**, **semantics (meaning)**, **timing (synchronization)**, and **error handling** procedures required for reliable data transmission across a physical or logical medium. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework, a protocol is treated as a **standardized communication contract** that ensures **interoperability**, **deterministic behavior**, and **robustness** between heterogeneous hardware/software systems. > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A protocol is NOT merely a piece of software or hardware. It is a *specification* — a binding agreement between a transmitter and a receiver that defines *what* to send, *when* to send, *how* to frame it, and *how* to recover from errors. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two people speaking different languages trying to communicate over a noisy telephone line: - **Without a protocol** → Garbled, ambiguous, lost messages. - **With a protocol** → They agree on (a) a common language, (b) a sentence structure, (c) a signal to indicate the start and end of speech, and (d) a method to ask for repetition if words are lost. A communication protocol does the same thing for machines. Consider a **microcontroller (MCU)** talking to a **temperature sensor**: - The MCU must speak in a *specific voltage level* (e.g., **3.3 V** or **5 V**). - The bits must be arranged in a *specific frame* (start bit, data bits, parity, stop bit). - The MCU must *clock* the data in or out at an agreed **baud rate** (e.g., **9600 bps**). - Both must agree on *who speaks first* (master/slave relationship). > [!IMPORTANT] > A protocol encapsulates **five engineering realities**: > 1. **Hardware compatibility** (voltage, current, impedance) > 2. **Timing precision** (clock edges, latency budgets) > 3. **Data integrity** (parity, CRC, checksums) > 4. **Access control** (master/slave, token passing, CSMA/CD) > 5. **Error recovery** (ACK/NACK, retransmission, timeout) ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | Context | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Standard UART voltage | **3.3 V / 5 V** | TTL-level logic | | RS-232 voltage swing | **±3 V to ±15 V** | Legacy serial port | | I²C bus clock (Standard Mode) | **100 kHz** | Philips/NXP spec | | I²C bus clock (Fast Mode) | **400 kHz** | High-speed I²C | | SPI bus clock | up to **> 50 MHz** | Application-dependent | | CAN bus bit rate | up to **1 Mbps** | ISO 11898 | | Standard baud rates | **9600, 115200 bps** | UART/Serial | | CAN frame max payload | **8 bytes** | Classical CAN | | CAN FD payload | up to **64 bytes** | CAN with Flexible Data-rate | > [!TIP] > Always memorize the **baud rate divisor formula** for UART: `Baud Rate = Clock Frequency / (16 × (UBRR + 1))` where UBRR is the USART Baud Rate Register value. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of a Protocol A communication protocol is best understood as a **layered system**. We analyze it across four orthogonal dimensions: ### 2.1.1 Physical Layer (Layer 1) — The "Wire" Defines the **electrical, mechanical, and procedural** aspects of bit transmission. - Voltage levels (TTL, RS-232, RS-485, LVDS) - Cable type (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber) - Connector pinout (DB-9, RJ-45) - Maximum cable length (e.g., **RS-485 up to 1200 m**) ### 2.1.2 Data Link Layer (Layer 2) — The "Frame" Defines how bits are grouped into **frames**, with start/stop delimiters and error detection. - Frame format (start bit, data, parity, stop bit) - Addressing (device ID, slave address) - Error detection (parity bit, CRC-8, CRC-16) - Flow control (RTS/CTS, XON/XOFF) ### 2.1.3 Network/Application Layer (Layer 3+) — The "Meaning" Defines the **semantics** — what the bytes actually *mean*. - Command-response structure - Data encoding (ASCII, binary, JSON, protobuf) - Session management - Encryption/authentication (in modern protocols) ### 2.1.4 Timing & Synchronization Defines **when** data may be sent. - Synchronous (clock line shared, e.g., SPI, I²C) - Asynchronous (no shared clock, e.g., UART) - Isochronous (real-time periodic, e.g., USB isochronous endpoints) ## 2.2 Taxonomy of Communication Protocols | Category | Examples | Key Characteristic | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Asynchronous Serial** | UART, RS-232, RS-485 | No clock line; uses start/stop bits | | **Synchronous Serial** | SPI, I²C, Microwire | Shared clock line | | **Fieldbus / Industrial** | CAN, Modbus, Profibus, DeviceNet | Robust, deterministic, multi-drop | | **Wireless** | BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, Wi-Fi, 6LoWPAN | RF-based, no physical wires | | **Automotive** | CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet | EMI-hardened, real-time | | **High-speed / Embedded** | USB, Ethernet, PCIe | Host-peripheral, packet-based | | **Sensor-specific** | 1-Wire, SPMI | Minimal pin count, low bandwidth | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Constraints | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{baud}}$ | Time duration of one bit | $T_{bit}$ in s, $f_{baud}$ in bps | | 2 | $N_{UBRR} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot f_{baud}} - 1$ | UART baud rate divisor | Dimensionless integer | | 3 | $f_{SCL} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{16 + 2 \cdot TWBR \cdot Prescaler}$ | I²C SCL frequency | $f_{SCL}$ up to 400 kHz (Fast Mode) | | 4 | $f_{SPI} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{2 \cdot SPR}$ | SPI clock rate | $SPR$ = SPI Prescaler Register | | 5 | $e_{BER} = \dfrac{N_{err}}{N_{total}}$ | Bit Error Rate | Dimensionless ratio | | 6 | $L_{max} = \dfrac{v_{prop} \cdot t_{bit}}{2}$ | Max cable length (RS-485) | $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | | 7 | $T_{frame} = N_{bits} \cdot T_{bit}$ | Total frame transmission time | seconds | | 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{frame}}$ | Protocol efficiency / throughput | $0 < \eta \le 1$ | | 9 | $CRC = X^{n} \cdot M(x) \mod G(x)$ | Cyclic Redundancy Check | $G(x)$ is generator polynomial | | 10 | $T_{timeout} = k \cdot T_{frame}$ | ACK timeout period | $k \ge 2$ typical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Point:** When using `|x|` (absolute value) in any formula inside a markdown table, ALWAYS write it as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Automation:** Modbus RTU over RS-485 connects PLCs to field instruments over **kilometers** of cable. - **Automotive:** CAN bus carries up to **1000+ messages per second** between ECUs in a modern car. - **IoT Edge Devices:** MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) over TCP/IP for low-bandwidth telemetry. - **Power Electronics:** SPI links a microcontroller to gate drivers and ADC chips (e.g., 16-bit ADCs in motor control). - **Medical Devices:** SPI/I²C link microcontrollers to sensors (ECG, SpO₂) in patient monitors. - **Aerospace:** MIL-STD-1553B is a redundant, deterministic protocol for avionics buses. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: UART Baud Rate Divisor The **Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter (UART)** is the most fundamental asynchronous protocol. The baud rate is generated by dividing the system clock by a programmable integer. ### Step 1 — Identify the source oscillator Most microcontrollers (e.g., ATmega328P used in Arduino Uno) derive the UART clock from the system crystal oscillator: $$f_{osc} = 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = \text{16 MHz}$$ > The "16×" comes from the fact that the UART hardware oversamples each bit 16 times to improve noise immunity. ### Step 2 — Define the desired baud rate For USB-to-serial communication on modern PCs, the standard is: $$f_{baud} = 9600 \text{ bps}$$ ### Step 3 — Set up the divisor equation Let **UBRR** be the unsigned 16-bit register holding the integer divisor. The hardware clocks 16 times per bit, so: $$f_{baud} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$$ We need to **solve for UBRR**. ### Step 4 — Algebraic rearrangement $$16 \times f_{baud} \times (UBRR + 1) = f_{osc}$$ $$UBRR + 1 = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \times f_{baud}}$$ $$UBRR = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \times f_{baud}} - 1$$ ### Step 5 — Plug in the numbers $$ \begin{aligned} UBRR &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 9600} - 1 \\ &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{153600} - 1 \\ &= 104.166\overline{6} - 1 \\ &= 103.166\overline{6} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Round to nearest integer Since UBRR must be an integer, we **round to the nearest value**: $$UBRR = 103$$ ### Step 7 — Compute the actual achieved baud rate $$ \begin{aligned} f_{baud,actual} &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times (103 + 1)} \\ &= \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 104} \\ &= \dfrac{10^{6}}{104} \\ &= 9615.38 \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Compute the error percentage $$ \begin{aligned} e_\% &= \dfrac{\vert f_{actual} - f_{target} \vert}{f_{target}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{\vert 9615.38 - 9600 \vert}{9600} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{15.38}{9600} \times 100 \\ &\approx 0.16\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > A baud rate error of **< 2%** is generally acceptable for UART. The 0.16% error for UBRR=103 at 16 MHz is excellent. ## 3.2 Derivation: Protocol Efficiency (Frame Overhead) Consider a standard UART frame: **1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 parity bit + 1 stop bit** = 11 bits per frame. The **efficiency** $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of useful data bits to total transmitted bits: $$ \eta = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{frame}} $$ For the 8N1P1 configuration: $$ \eta = \dfrac{8}{1 + 8 + 1 + 1} = \dfrac{8}{11} \approx 0.727 \text{ or } 72.7\% $$ This means **27.3% of every transmission is overhead** (start, parity, stop bits). In high-speed, low-power IoT applications, this drives the choice toward protocols with smaller overheads (e.g., SPI with no framing bits per byte). ## 3.3 Derivation: I²C SCL Frequency (ATmega example) The TWI (Two-Wire Interface) clock in Atmel MCUs is generated by: $$f_{SCL} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{16 + 2 \cdot TWBR \cdot Prescaler}$$ To achieve $f_{SCL} = 100$ kHz with $f_{CPU} = 8$ MHz and Prescaler = 1: $$ \begin{aligned} TWBR &= \dfrac{f_{CPU}/f_{SCL} - 16}{2 \cdot Prescaler} \\ &= \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6} / 10^{5} - 16}{2 \cdot 1} \\ &= \dfrac{80 - 16}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{64}{2} \\ &= 32 \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > Verification: $f_{SCL} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}}{16 + 2 \times 32 \times 1} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}}{80} = 100$ kHz ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Protocol | Wires | Speed | Max Nodes | Master/Slave | Overhead | Application | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **UART** | 2 (TX,RX) | up to 1 Mbps | 2 (point-to-point) | None (peer) | 27–36% | Debug, GPS modules | | **SPI** | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS) | 10–50 MHz | Limited by CS lines | Yes (1 Master, N Slaves) | ~0% | SD cards, ADC, displays | | **I²C** | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz | 127 (7-bit) | Yes (multi-master) | 10–20% | Sensors, EEPROMs, RTCs | | **CAN** | 2 (CANH, CANL) | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | 2¹¹ (11-bit ID) | Peer (message-based) | 30–45% | Automotive, industrial | | **Modbus RTU** | 2 (RS-485) | 9.6 – 115.2 kbps | 247 (1 byte addr) | Master/Slave | ~10% | PLCs, SCADA | | **USB 2.0** | 4 (D+, D-, VBUS, GND) | 480 Mbps | 127 hubs | Host/Peripheral | Token + packet | PCs, peripherals | | **Ethernet** | 2/4/8 | 10M–10G bps | Many (MAC-based) | CSMA/CD | ~5% | LAN, IoT gateways | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Layered Architecture of a Communication Protocol ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Layer
User Software, Firmware, SCADA]:::app B[Transport Layer
TCP, UDP, Message Segmentation]:::trans C[Network Layer
IP Addressing, Routing, CAN ID]:::net D[Data Link Layer
Framing, CRC, ACK NACK, Flow Control]:::link E[Physical Layer
Voltage Levels, Connectors, Bit Timing]:::phy F[Physical Medium
Copper Wire, Fiber, RF Channel]:::medium A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F classDef app fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef trans fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef net fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef link fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phy fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef medium fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Each layer adds its own **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)** as a header (and sometimes trailer). At the receiver, the headers are stripped in reverse order, like peeling an onion. This is the **OSI encapsulation model**. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: UART Frame Structure (8N1 with Parity) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IDLE
Line HIGH]:::idle --> B[START BIT
0 LOW]:::start B --> C1[D0]:::data C1 --> C2[D1]:::data C2 --> C3[D2]:::data C3 --> C4[D3]:::data C4 --> C5[D4]:::data C5 --> C6[D5]:::data C6 --> C7[D6]:::data C7 --> C8[D7]:::data C8 --> D[PARITY BIT
P]:::parity D --> E[STOP BIT
1 HIGH]:::stop E --> F[IDLE
Line HIGH]:::idle2 classDef idle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef idle2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef start fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF classDef data fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef parity fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000 classDef stop fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: I²C Transaction Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant M as Master MCU participant S as Slave Sensor Note over M,S: Bus Idle, SDA and SCL HIGH M->>M: Generate START condition (SDA falls while SCL HIGH) M->>S: Send 7-bit slave address + R/W bit S-->>M: ACK (SDA pulled LOW on 9th clock) alt WRITE operation M->>S: Send data byte S-->>M: ACK M->>M: Generate STOP condition (SDA rises while SCL HIGH) else READ operation S->>M: Send data byte M-->>S: ACK M->>M: Generate STOP condition end ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Master-Slave SPI Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Master
Microcontroller]:::master S1[SPI Slave 1
ADC]:::slave S2[SPI Slave 2
Flash Memory]:::slave S3[SPI Slave 3
Display]:::slave M -- SCK ----> S1 M -- MOSI ---> S1 S1 -- MISO --> M M -- CS1 -----> S1 M -- SCK ----> S2 M -- MOSI ---> S2 S2 -- MISO --> M M -- CS2 -----> S2 M -- SCK ----> S3 M -- MOSI ---> S3 S3 -- MISO --> M M -- CS3 -----> S3 classDef master fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000,stroke-width:3px classDef slave fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** In SPI, the **Chip Select (CS)** line is active-low. The master pulls CS LOW to "select" a slave. Only one CS line should be LOW at any time to avoid **bus contention** (a guaranteed way to damage output drivers if the slaves have push-pull outputs without tri-state). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define a communication protocol. List any four essential elements that a communication protocol must specify.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A communication protocol is a **formal set of rules and conventions** that govern the exchange of data between two or more entities in a communication system. It defines the **syntax, semantics, timing, and error-handling** procedures for reliable transmission. Four essential elements: 1. **Syntax** — Structure or format of data (e.g., frame format with start, data, parity, stop bits). 2. **Semantics** — Meaning of each piece of data (e.g., which byte is the temperature value). 3. **Timing** — When data should be sent and at what speed (baud rate, clock edge). 4. **Error handling** — Procedures to detect and correct errors (parity, CRC, ACK/NACK). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous serial communication protocols. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Clock line** | Shared clock between sender and receiver | No shared clock | | **Framing bits** | None required; data sent in streams | Start, stop, parity bits per byte | | **Speed** | Typically very high (tens of MHz) | Limited (typically up to 1 Mbps) | | **Overhead** | Low | High (~20–30%) | | **Example** | SPI, I²C | UART, RS-232 | *Example:* **SPI** is synchronous (uses SCK line); **UART** is asynchronous (no clock line, uses start/stop bits). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a standard UART frame (8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit). Define the term **baud rate** and explain how it differs from **bit rate**. (7 marks) **(b)** A microcontroller operates at a clock frequency of **20 MHz**. Compute the value of the **UBRR register** required to achieve a baud rate of **115200 bps** in asynchronous normal mode. Also calculate the percentage error in the achieved baud rate. (7 marks) --- ### **Question A — Model Solution** #### **Part (a) — UART Frame Structure and Baud Rate (7 marks)** A standard UART frame in the **8N1** configuration consists of: 1. **Start bit** (1 bit, always **LOW** / logic 0) 2. **Data bits** (8 bits, transmitted **LSB first**) 3. **No parity bit** 4. **Stop bit** (1 bit, always **HIGH** / logic 1) 5. **Idle state** — line held HIGH between frames **Baud Rate vs Bit Rate:** - **Baud rate** = number of *signal changes* (symbols) per second on the line. - **Bit rate** = number of *information bits* transmitted per second. - For UART, one bit = one symbol, so **bit rate = baud rate**. **Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.2 for the Mermaid rendering).** For 8N1, total bits per frame = **10 bits**. (Start=1 + Data=8 + Stop=1) Frame transmission time at 9600 bps: $$T_{frame} = \dfrac{10}{9600} = 1.0417 \text{ ms}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — UBRR Calculation (7 marks)** Given: $f_{osc} = 20 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $f_{baud} = 115200$ bps. **Step 1:** Use the UBRR formula: $$UBRR = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot f_{baud}} - 1$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$ \begin{aligned} UBRR &= \dfrac{20 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 115200} - 1 \\ &= \dfrac{20 \times 10^{6}}{1843200} - 1 \\ &= 10.8507 - 1 \\ &= 9.8507 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Round to nearest integer: $$UBRR = 10$$ **Step 4:** Compute actual achieved baud rate: $$ \begin{aligned} f_{baud,actual} &= \dfrac{20 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times (10 + 1)} \\ &= \dfrac{20 \times 10^{6}}{176} \\ &= 113636.36 \text{ bps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Compute the percentage error: $$ \begin{aligned} e_\% &= \dfrac{\vert 113636.36 - 115200 \vert}{115200} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{1563.64}{115200} \times 100 \\ &\approx 1.36\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The error of **1.36%** is within the acceptable **2%** limit for reliable UART communication. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]** **(a)** Compare the **SPI** and **I²C** protocols in terms of wiring, speed, addressing, and master-slave configuration. Mention two advantages of each. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the working of the **I²C bus** with a suitable timing diagram. Describe the **START** and **STOP** conditions. In a system with a master running at **8 MHz** and I²C prescaler of 1, calculate the **TWBR** value required to generate an SCL frequency of **400 kHz** (Fast Mode). (7 marks) --- ### **Question B — Model Solution** #### **Part (a) — SPI vs I²C Comparison (7 marks)** | Parameter | SPI | I²C | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Wires required** | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | | **Speed** | Up to 50 MHz (or more) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz | | **Addressing** | Hardware (separate CS line per slave) | Software (7-bit or 10-bit slave address) | | **Master/Slave** | Single master only | Multi-master capable | | **Directionality** | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | | **Open-drain** | Push-pull (not open-drain) | Open-drain (requires pull-up resistors) | | **Max devices** | Limited by GPIO pins for CS | 127 (7-bit address) | | **Power consumption** | Higher (4 lines switching) | Lower (2 lines, can be slowed) | **Advantages of SPI (2 marks):** 1. Higher speed than I²C (tens of MHz vs 400 kHz Fast Mode). 2. Full-duplex communication (simultaneous TX and RX). **Advantages of I²C (2 marks):** 1. Only 2 wires regardless of number of slaves → GPIO savings. 2. Built-in slave addressing → no need for extra CS lines; supports multi-master. --- #### **Part (b) — I²C Working and TWBR Calculation (7 marks)** **Working of I²C Bus:** I²C uses **two open-drain lines** (SDA — data, SCL — clock) pulled HIGH by external resistors (typically **4.7 kΩ** to Vcc). **START condition:** SDA transitions from **HIGH to LOW** while **SCL is HIGH**. Initiated only by the master. **STOP condition:** SDA transitions from **LOW to HIGH** while **SCL is HIGH**. Indicates the bus is released. **Data validity:** Data on SDA must remain **stable** while SCL is HIGH; data may change only when SCL is LOW. **Byte transmission:** 8 bits are sent MSB first, followed by a 9th clock pulse during which the receiver pulls SDA LOW to send **ACK**. (Refer to **SECTION 4.3 Mermaid sequence diagram** for the transaction flow.) **TWBR Calculation:** Given: $f_{CPU} = 8 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $f_{SCL} = 400 \times 10^{3}$ Hz, Prescaler = 1. **Step 1:** Use the I²C SCL frequency formula: $$f_{SCL} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{16 + 2 \cdot TWBR \cdot Prescaler}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for TWBR: $$2 \cdot TWBR \cdot Prescaler = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{f_{SCL}} - 16$$ $$TWBR = \dfrac{\dfrac{f_{CPU}}{f_{SCL}} - 16}{2 \cdot Prescaler}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} TWBR &= \dfrac{\dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}}{400 \times 10^{3}} - 16}{2 \cdot 1} \\ &= \dfrac{20 - 16}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{4}{2} \\ &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Verification: $$f_{SCL} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}}{16 + 2 \times 2 \times 1} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}}{20} = 400 \text{ kHz} \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Note: $f_{CPU}/f_{SCL} = 20$ must be **≥ 16** for the formula to be physically realizable. If the ratio drops below 16, you must increase the prescaler (to 4, 16, or 64). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make — and how to lose marks:** > 1. **Baud rate ≠ Bit rate confusion** — They are equal for UART but differ in modem schemes (e.g., QAM-16 has 4 bits per symbol, so baud rate is ¼ of bit rate). Examiners WILL deduct marks for treating them as universally identical. > 2. **Forgetting the "+1"** in the UBRR formula. The divisor is `(UBRR + 1)`, not `UBRR`. This single missing +1 will give you a wrong baud rate and a wrong error percentage — expect 2–3 mark deduction. > 3. **Wrong MSB/LSB order.** UART transmits **LSB first**; I²C transmits **MSB first**. Confusing these will mark your answer wrong. > 4. **Not specifying open-drain** when describing I²C. Many students forget that I²C lines *cannot* be push-pulled — this is a fundamental property. > 5. **UBRR not rounded to integer.** The UBRR register is unsigned integer. The student must explicitly write "round to nearest integer" as a step. > 6. **Confusing CS (Chip Select) with CD (Carrier Detect).** CS is a per-slave active-low line in SPI. Examiners will not accept "Chip Select = Carrier Sense" or any mix-up. > 7. **Modulus misinterpretation** — When writing $\dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 f_{baud}}$, students sometimes invert it as $16 f_{baud} / f_{osc}$. Always double-check the inversion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Protocol = Rules + Format + Timing + Error Handling.** Not just software; not just hardware. - ✅ **Two main families:** Asynchronous (UART, RS-232, RS-485) and Synchronous (SPI, I²C). - ✅ **UART frame (8N1) = 1 start + 8 data + 0 parity + 1 stop = 10 bits total.** Efficiency = 80% for 8N1; 72.7% for 8N1P1. - ✅ **UBRR formula:** $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 f_{baud}} - 1$. Always verify with the achieved baud rate and compute error %. - ✅ **Baud rate = 9600 / 115200** are KTU-favorite values. The "16×" comes from the **16× oversampling** in the UART receiver. - ✅ **SPI = 4 wires, full-duplex, single master, no addressing overhead, fastest of the basic serial protocols.** Active-low CS per slave. - ✅ **I²C = 2 wires, half-duplex, open-drain, multi-master, 7-bit/10-bit addressing.** Needs **pull-up resistors** (4.7 kΩ typical). - ✅ **I²C SCL formula:** $f_{SCL} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{16 + 2 \cdot TWBR \cdot Prescaler}$. Must satisfy $f_{CPU}/f_{SCL} \ge 16$. - ✅ **CAN bus = dominant LOW / recessive HIGH, 11-bit or 29-bit ID, max 1 Mbps, max 8-byte payload (Classical).** Used in **automotive ECUs**. - ✅ **Modbus RTU = master/slave, RS-485, 247 slaves, function codes (0x03 = read holding registers).** - ✅ **Error detection methods:** Parity (1-bit, simple), Checksum (sum of bytes), **CRC-8 / CRC-16** (polynomial division, very robust). - ✅ **Always use `\vert` instead of `|` in markdown tables** for absolute values — the same rigor applies when you write answers on paper. - ✅ **Three pitfalls to avoid in exams:** Forgetting the "+1", wrong MSB/LSB, and treating baud rate = bit rate as a universal law. > [!TIP] > **Final Memory Aid:** "**UART is asynchronous, SPI is synchronous but simplex-isolated, I²C is synchronous but addressable**." If you remember this single sentence, you can answer 80% of KTU protocol questions confidently. ---

topics.title: Time Base and Database
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Base and Database

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Time-Based Database** (more formally, a **Temporal Database**) is a database system that is designed to **store, retrieve, and manipulate data whose semantics are dependent on time**. Unlike conventional (snapshot) databases which only reflect the *current* state of the modeled reality, a temporal database explicitly captures the **history** of data — recording when facts were true, when they were recorded, and when they were modified in the database itself.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a Time-Based Database is treated as an extension of the **Relational Model** in which every tuple is associated with one or more *time intervals* or *time instants*. Formally, a temporal relation is a mapping:

$$R: \text{TupleSchema} \times T \rightarrow \text{Boolean}$$

where $T$ denotes the **temporal domain** (discrete, dense, or continuous) over which the relation is interpreted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Three Pillars of Time in Databases**
> 1. **Valid Time (VT):** The real-world time during which a fact is *true* in the modeled reality.
> 2. **Transaction Time (TT):** The database-internal time when a fact was *stored* / *current in the database*.
> 3. **Bitemporal Time (BT):** The orthogonal combination of both Valid Time and Transaction Time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **bank manager** maintaining a *passbook* for a customer named Rahul.

- **Snapshot Database (Old-School Passbook):** The passbook only shows Rahul's *current* balance. Every withdrawal, deposit, or interest credit is overwritten. Once money is withdrawn, the previous balance is gone forever.
- **Time-Based (Temporal) Database:** A *digital ledger* is kept that records: *"On 5-Jan-2024, balance was ₹50,000"*; *"On 12-Feb-2024, ₹10,000 was withdrawn, so balance became ₹40,000 (valid from 12-Feb-2024 onward)"*; *"On 1-Mar-2024, the database logged this as a transaction at 14:32:10 IST"*. Here, every past state is *recoverable*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy** — Think of a temporal database as a **time machine for data**. A snapshot database is like a photograph (one instant), whereas a temporal database is like a **CCTV recording with timestamps** (the entire history).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time Instant** | $t$ | A point on the time line (e.g., 2024-01-15 10:30:00) |
| **Time Interval** | $[t_s, t_e)$ | A half-open period from start $t_s$ to end $t_e$ |
| **Chronon** | $\delta$ | The smallest indivisible unit of time (atomic time grain) |
| **Valid Time** | $VT$ | Real-world time dimension |
| **Transaction Time** | $TT$ | System time dimension |
| **Now / UC** | $\infty$ or `UC` | A variable representing the current point in time |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Time Dimension in Databases?

Conventional (snapshot) relational databases suffer from **historical data loss** the moment a tuple is `UPDATE`d or `DELETE`d. The two key motivations for adding a time base are:

1. **Auditing and Compliance:** Regulatory requirements (e.g., GDPR, SEBI, HIPAA) demand historical traceability.
2. **Decision Support Systems (DSS):** Trend analysis, forecasting, and OLAP queries depend on history.

## 2.2 The Three Time Dimensions — Structured Logic

### Dimension 1: Valid Time (VT)
- Represents the **real-world lifespan** of a fact.
- It is **user-controlled** — the user decides when a fact became true.
- **Example:** *"Ram was Professor of CSE at KTU from 2018-06-01 to 2023-05-31."*

### Dimension 2: Transaction Time (TT)
- Represents the **database storage lifespan** of a fact.
- It is **system-controlled** — managed by the DBMS clock, not the user.
- **Example:** *"This fact was inserted into the DB on 2024-01-10 and not yet deleted."*

### Dimension 3: Bitemporal Time (BT)
- Combination of VT and TT.
- A fact is associated with both intervals: $[VT_s, VT_e)$ and $[TT_s, TT_e)$.
- **Example:** *"On 2024-01-10, the DB recorded that 'Ram was Professor' for the valid period 2018-06-01 to 2023-05-31."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *Valid Time* = "When was the fact **true in reality**?"; *Transaction Time* = "When was the fact **known to the database**?"

## 2.3 Temporal Data Model — Structural Components

A temporal relation extends the standard relation as:

$$R^T = R \times VT \times TT$$

That is, every tuple $r$ in a temporal relation is augmented with a valid-time interval and a transaction-time interval:

$$r = \langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n, \ [VT_s, VT_e), \ [TT_s, TT_e) \rangle$$

## 2.4 Temporal Query Languages (TQL)

The standard SQL is **inadequate** for temporal queries. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following extensions:

| Query Class | Standard SQL | Temporal Extension |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Selection | `WHERE` | `VALID(relation, time)` or `AS OF` |
| Projection | `SELECT` | `SNAPSHOT` reduction |
| Join | `JOIN` | Temporal Equi-Join / Non-Equi Join |
| Aggregation | `GROUP BY` | Temporal Aggregation (`TIME-SLICE`) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Time interval | $I = [t_s, t_e)$ where $t_s \leq t_e$ | Time units |
| 2 | Duration of an interval | $\Delta t = t_e - t_s$ | Chronons ($\delta$) |
| 3 | Snapshot reduction at $t$ | $R(t) = \{ r \in R \mid t \in [VT_s(r), VT_e(r)) \}$ | Tuple set |
| 4 | Coalescing operation | $R_c = \text{COALESCE}(R)$ | Tuple set |
| 5 | Bitemporal tuple space | $B = R \otimes VT \otimes TT$ | Cartesian product |
| 6 | Time-slice query | $R \text{ AS OF } t \rightarrow \{ r \mid t \in [TT_s, TT_e) \}$ | Tuple set |
| 7 | Time intersection (Allen) | $[a, b) \cap [c, d) = [\max(a,c), \min(b,d))$ | Time interval |
| 8 | Allen's 13 relations | `before, after, meets, met-by, overlaps, overlapped-by,` | Categorical |
|   |   | `during, contains, starts, started-by, finishes, finished-by, equals` |   |
| 9 | Now variable | $VT_e = \text{UC}$ (Until Changed) | Symbol |
| 10 | Granularity conversion | $\delta_{new} = k \cdot \delta_{old}$, where $k \in \mathbb{N}$ | Time units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Temporal databases are foundational to **financial ledger systems, medical EHRs, supply chain tracking, satellite telemetry, IoT sensor logs, and SCADA systems** — all of which require time-stamped historical data.

## 2.6 Allen's Interval Algebra — The 13 Relations

Allen (1983) defined **13 mutually exclusive interval relations** that fully describe how two time intervals can interact. KTU frequently tests this.

| Relation | Symbol | Inverse | Pictorial |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| X before Y | $\prec$ | $\succ$ | `XXX` `YYY` |
| X meets Y | $m$ | $mi$ | `XXXYYY` |
| X overlaps Y | $o$ | $oi$ | `XX` `YY` overlap |
| X during Y | $d$ | $di$ | ` XX ` in `YYYY` |
| X starts Y | $s$ | $si$ | `XX` in `YYYYY` |
| X finishes Y | $f$ | $fi$ | ` XX` in `YYYYY` |
| X equals Y | $=$ | $=$ | `XXXXX`=`YYYYY` |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Snapshot Reduction (Time-Slice) Operation

The **snapshot reduction** extracts a non-temporal relation from a temporal relation at a specific time $t$.

**Given:** A temporal relation $R^T$ containing tuples with valid-time intervals $[VT_s, VT_e)$.

**Find:** The snapshot relation $R(t)$ at time $t$.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Define a temporal relation as a set of timestamped tuples.

$$R^T = \{ r_i \mid r_i = \langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n, \ [VT_{s,i}, VT_{e,i}) \rangle \}$$

**Step 2:** Define the membership condition. A tuple $r_i$ belongs to the snapshot at time $t$ if and only if $t$ lies within its valid-time interval.

$$r_i \in R(t) \iff t \in [VT_{s,i}, VT_{e,i})$$

**Step 3:** Express the interval membership as a logical predicate.

$$t \in [VT_{s,i}, VT_{e,i}) \iff (VT_{s,i} \leq t) \land (t < VT_{e,i})$$

> *Explanation:* This says $t$ must be **greater than or equal to** the start instant **and strictly less than** the end instant (the half-open interval convention).

**Step 4:** Substitute the predicate into the relation definition.

$$R(t) = \{ \langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \rangle \mid \exists r_i \in R^T, \ VT_{s,i} \leq t < VT_{e,i} \}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using set-builder notation.

$$\boxed{R(t) = \pi_{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n}(\sigma_{VT_s \leq t \ < \ VT_e}(R^T))}$$

Here, $\sigma$ is the relational selection operator and $\pi$ is the projection operator. The result $R(t)$ is a conventional non-temporal relation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Coalescing Operation

Multiple tuples with **identical non-temporal attributes** but **contiguous or overlapping** time intervals must be merged into a single tuple.

**Given:** Two tuples $r_1$ and $r_2$ with the same attribute values $A$ and time intervals $I_1 = [t_1, t_2)$, $I_2 = [t_3, t_4)$.

**Condition for coalescing:** Either $I_1$ and $I_2$ are adjacent ($t_2 = t_3$) or they overlap ($t_2 > t_3$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define contiguity condition.

$$C(r_1, r_2) = (A(r_1) = A(r_2)) \land (t_2 \geq t_3)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the merged interval.

$$I_{merged} = [\min(t_1, t_3), \ \max(t_2, t_4))$$

**Step 3:** When $t_2 = t_3$ (adjacent):

$$I_{merged} = [t_1, t_4) \quad \text{(exact union, no gap)}$$

**Step 4:** When $t_2 > t_3$ (overlap):

$$I_{merged} = [t_1, t_4) \quad \text{(union with internal overlap absorbed)}$$

**Step 5:** The coalesced relation is:

$$\boxed{R_c = \text{COALESCE}(R^T) = \{ r_{merged} \mid r_{merged} = \langle A, [t_{min}, t_{max}) \rangle, \ \forall \text{ adjacent/overlapping pairs} \}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Time Intersection of Two Intervals

The intersection of two time intervals is fundamental to **temporal joins**.

**Given:** $I_1 = [a, b)$ and $I_2 = [c, d)$ with $a \leq b$ and $c \leq d$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The intersection is non-empty only if the intervals overlap.

$$I_1 \cap I_2 \neq \emptyset \iff a < d \land c < b$$

**Step 2:** Define the intersection interval using $\max$ for the start and $\min$ for the end.

$$I_1 \cap I_2 = [\max(a, c), \ \min(b, d))$$

**Step 3:** Show this satisfies the half-open property.

$$\max(a, c) < \min(b, d) \implies \text{non-empty intersection}$$

**Step 4:** Worked numerical example. Let $I_1 = [5, 10)$ and $I_2 = [7, 12)$.

$$\max(5, 7) = 7, \quad \min(10, 12) = 10$$

$$I_1 \cap I_2 = [7, 10)$$

**Step 5:** Verify the duration.

$$\Delta t = 10 - 7 = 3 \text{ chronons}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Input | Operation | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time-slice | $R^T, t$ | Snapshot reduction $\sigma_{VT_s \leq t < VT_e}$ | Snapshot $R(t)$ |
| Coalescing | $R^T$ (fragmented) | $\text{COALESCE}$ | $R_c$ (compact) |
| Temporal join | $R^T, S^T$ | Equi-join + time intersection | $R \bowtie_T S$ |
| Time intersection | $[a,b), [c,d)$ | $[\max(a,c), \min(b,d))$ | Overlap interval |
| As-of query | $R^T, t$ | $\sigma_{TT_s \leq t < TT_e}$ | Historical snapshot |
| Granularity roll-up | $R^T$ (daily) | Group by month | $R^T_{monthly}$ |
| Versioning | $R^T$ (bitemporal) | Slice on TT | $R^{VT}_i$ per version |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Architecture of a Time-Based Database System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[User Application Layer] --> queryA[Temporal Query Engine]
    adminA[DB Administrator] --> queryA
    queryA --> parserA[SQL Temporal Parser]
    parserA --> optimA[Temporal Query Optimizer]
    optimA --> execA[Execution Engine]
    execA --> storageA[Temporal Storage Manager]
    storageA --> validTimeA[Valid Time Index B-Tree]
    storageA --> transTimeA[Transaction Time Log]
    storageA --> dataA[Current State Tables]
    validTimeA --> diskA[Persistent Storage Disk]
    transTimeA --> diskA
    dataA --> diskA
    execA --> resultA[Result Set with Time Stamps]
    resultA --> userA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Temporal Query Engine** is the heart of the system — it parses `AS OF`, `BETWEEN`, `VALID`, and `PERIOD` clauses; the **Valid-Time Index** is a B-Tree keyed on the start of the interval; and the **Transaction-Time Log** is append-only (immutable history).

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Bitemporal Concept Space (The Time Cube)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph vtAxis["Valid Time Axis (Reality)"]
        vtStart["VT_s = Past"] --> vtNow["VT_t = Present"] --> vtFut["VT_e = Future UC"]
    end
    subgraph ttAxis["Transaction Time Axis (Database)"]
        ttStart["TT_s = Logged"] --> ttNow["TT_t = Recorded"] --> ttEnd["TT_e = Not yet superseded"]
    end
    subgraph tupleSpace["Bitemporal Tuple Space"]
        fact1["Tuple 1: VT past + TT past"]
        fact2["Tuple 2: VT past + TT present"]
        fact3["Tuple 3: VT now + TT present"]
        fact4["Tuple 4: Corrected (VT now, TT retro)"]
    end
    vtAxis --- tupleSpace
    ttAxis --- tupleSpace
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A *bitemporal* relation can be visualized as a 2D plane. A **correction** to a past fact (e.g., salary revision backdated) does NOT delete the old tuple — it inserts a *new* tuple with the corrected valid time and current transaction time. The old tuple's transaction-time interval is closed (a *coalesced history*).

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Snapshot vs Temporal Query Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputQ[User Temporal Query] --> checkT{Contains Time Clause?}
    checkT -- Yes --> tQEngine[Temporal Query Engine]
    checkT -- No --> sQEngine[Standard SQL Engine]
    tQEngine --> intervalOp[Interval Algebra Operations]
    intervalOp --> coalesceOp[Coalescing]
    coalesceOp --> resultT[Temporal Result Set]
    sQEngine --> resultS[Snapshot Result Set]
    resultT --> outR[Combined Output]
    resultS --> outR
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Components of a Temporal DBMS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph external["External Interface"]
        uiA[User Interface]
        apiA[Application API]
    end
    subgraph logical["Logical Layer"]
        tschA[Temporal Schema Definition]
        tqlA[Temporal Query Language TSQL2]
        tconA[Temporal Integrity Constraints]
    end
    subgraph physical["Physical Layer"]
        tiA[Temporal Indices]
        tlogA[Transaction Time Log]
        tpartA[Time-Based Partitioning]
    end
    uiA --> logical
    apiA --> logical
    logical --> physical
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Temporal Database. Differentiate between Valid Time and Transaction Time with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Temporal Database (1 mark):** A database that supports the storage and querying of time-dependent data, capturing the history of facts rather than just the current state.
- **Valid Time (1 mark):** It denotes the real-world time period during which a fact is *true*. It is *user-controlled*. **Example:** *"Ravi was a Professor of CSE from 2018-01-01 to 2022-12-31."*
- **Transaction Time (1 mark):** It denotes the database-internal time when a fact was *recorded* in the DB. It is *system-controlled*. **Example:** *"This fact was inserted into the DB on 2023-01-15 10:00:00."*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of a 'Chronon' and 'Snapshot Reduction' in temporal databases.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Chronon (1.5 marks):** A chronon is the smallest, atomic, non-decomposable unit of time in a temporal database. It defines the *granularity* (e.g., second, day, year). All time instants are expressed as integer multiples of the chronon.
- **Snapshot Reduction (1.5 marks):** It is the operation of extracting a non-temporal (snapshot) relation from a temporal relation at a specific time instant $t$. The query is mathematically: $R(t) = \{ r \mid VT_s(r) \leq t < VT_e(r) \}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (14 Marks)

**Part (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three time dimensions** (Valid Time, Transaction Time, and Bitemporal Time) used in temporal databases. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b)** Discuss the **architecture of a Temporal DBMS** with reference to the role of the Temporal Query Engine, Valid-Time Index, and Transaction-Time Log. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question A — Part (a) [7 marks]

The three time dimensions are:

1. **Valid Time (VT) — 2 marks:** Valid time denotes the real-world time interval during which a fact is *true in the modeled reality*. It is controlled by the user, not the DBMS. For example, the statement *"The Project 'SmartIndia' was active from 2023-06-01 to 2024-03-31"* has $VT_s = \text{2023-06-01}$ and $VT_e = \text{2024-03-31}$.

2. **Transaction Time (TT) — 2 marks:** Transaction time denotes the time period during which a fact was *stored as current* in the database. It is system-controlled (via the DBMS clock) and is append-only — once stored, the record cannot be deleted, only superseded. For example, *"This fact was inserted into the DB on 2024-04-01 and is current as of 2024-09-01."*

3. **Bitemporal Time (BT) — 3 marks:** It is the orthogonal combination of valid time and transaction time. Every tuple is associated with a pair of intervals: $[VT_s, VT_e)$ and $[TT_s, TT_e)$. This allows the database to answer two questions simultaneously:
    - *What was true in reality at time $t_1$?* (Valid Time)
    - *What was believed by the database at time $t_2$?* (Transaction Time)
    
    A *correction* creates a new bitemporal tuple with the corrected VT and current TT, while the old tuple has its TT interval closed.

> **Diagram reference:** Use the **Bitemporal Time Cube** (from Section 4.2) to illustrate the orthogonal axes.

### Model Answer for Question A — Part (b) [7 marks]

A Temporal DBMS has three primary architectural layers (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Temporal Query Engine — 3 marks:** This is the topmost layer responsible for parsing, optimizing, and executing temporal queries. It understands clauses like `AS OF`, `VALID`, `PERIOD`, and `BETWEEN` which are extensions to standard SQL (TSQL2). The engine translates these into interval-algebra operations.

2. **Valid-Time Index — 2 marks:** A specialized **B+Tree index** keyed on the start of the valid-time interval. It allows efficient retrieval of all tuples whose valid-time overlaps with a query instant $t$. Without it, time-slice queries would require full-table scans.

3. **Transaction-Time Log — 2 marks:** An **append-only, immutable log** that records every change (insert, update, delete) to the database along with the system timestamp. It is the backbone of audit trails and recovery. The log supports the `AS OF $t$` query: *"What did the database look like at time $t$?"*

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks) — Alternative

**Part (a)** Define **Snapshot Reduction** and **Coalescing** operations. Derive the snapshot relation at time $t = 5$ for the given temporal relation. **(7 marks)**

| EmpID | Name | Dept | VT_s | VT_e |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E101 | Anu | CSE | 1 | 4 |
| E101 | Anu | CSE | 4 | 7 |
| E102 | Bala | ECE | 2 | 6 |

**Part (b)** With an example, explain **Allen's 13 interval relations**. Why is interval algebra important in temporal databases? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Question B — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define Snapshot Reduction (2 marks):**

$$R(t) = \{ r \in R^T \mid VT_s(r) \leq t < VT_e(r) \}$$

**Step 2 — Define Coalescing (2 marks):** Coalescing merges adjacent or overlapping tuples with identical non-temporal attributes into a single tuple with a unified time interval.

**Step 3 — Compute Snapshot at $t = 5$ (3 marks):**

Apply the predicate $VT_s \leq 5 < VT_e$ to each tuple:

| EmpID | Name | Dept | VT_s | VT_e | $5 \in [VT_s, VT_e)$? | Included? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E101 | Anu | CSE | 1 | 4 | $1 \leq 5 < 4$? No | ✗ |
| E101 | Anu | CSE | 4 | 7 | $4 \leq 5 < 7$? Yes | ✓ |
| E102 | Bala | ECE | 2 | 6 | $2 \leq 5 < 6$? Yes | ✓ |

**Resulting Snapshot Relation $R(5)$:**

| EmpID | Name | Dept |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E101 | Anu | CSE |
| E102 | Bala | ECE |

### Model Answer for Question B — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Allen's 13 Interval Relations (5 marks):** James Allen (1983) defined 13 mutually exclusive relations that completely characterize the relationship between any two time intervals $X = [x_s, x_e)$ and $Y = [y_s, y_e)$:

1. **X before Y ($\prec$):** $x_e < y_s$ — *Inverse: Y after X*
2. **X meets Y ($m$):** $x_e = y_s$ — *Inverse: Y met-by X*
3. **X overlaps Y ($o$):** $x_s < y_s < x_e < y_e$ — *Inverse: Y overlapped-by X*
4. **X during Y ($d$):** $x_s > y_s \land x_e < y_e$ — *Inverse: Y contains X*
5. **X starts Y ($s$):** $x_s = y_s \land x_e < y_e$ — *Inverse: Y started-by X*
6. **X finishes Y ($f$):** $x_s > y_s \land x_e = y_e$ — *Inverse: Y finished-by X*
7. **X equals Y ($=$):** $x_s = y_s \land x_e = y_e$ — *Self-inverse*

**Worked Example:** Let $X = [3, 7)$ and $Y = [5, 10)$.

- $x_s = 3, y_s = 5, x_e = 7, y_e = 10$
- Check: $3 < 5 < 7 < 10$ → $X$ **overlaps** $Y$ ($o$).

**Importance of Interval Algebra (2 marks):** Interval algebra is critical in temporal databases because it provides a *complete, sound, and tractable* framework for:
- **Temporal joins:** determining when two facts co-existed.
- **Temporal constraints:** expressing rules like *"A professor's tenure must precede their HOD period."*
- **Query optimization:** pre-filtering irrelevant time periods.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner's Observations):**
