> 1. **Confusing VT and TT:** Many students swap *Valid Time* and *Transaction Time*. Remember: **VT = real world**, **TT = database world**.
> 2. **Half-open interval confusion:** The end instant $t_e$ is **excluded** from the interval. A tuple with $VT = [1, 5)$ is valid at $t = 4$ but **NOT** at $t = 5$. Examiners penalize -1 mark for not stating this explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the 'Now' variable:** When $VT_e = \text{UC}$ (Until Changed), students often leave it blank. Always write `UC` or `∞`.
> 4. **Coalescing condition:** The condition for coalescing is $t_2 \geq t_3$ (i.e., adjacent OR overlapping). Writing only "overlapping" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Snapshot query form:** Always write $VT_s \leq t < VT_e$, **not** $VT_s \leq t \leq VT_e$. This is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Time-Based (Temporal) Database** = stores *history*, not just current state.
- **Valid Time (VT)** = when the fact is **true in reality** (user-controlled).
- **Transaction Time (TT)** = when the fact is **known to the DB** (system-controlled).
- **Bitemporal** = orthogonal combination of VT and TT.
- **Chronon (δ)** = the atomic, indivisible time grain.
- **Snapshot Reduction** at $t$: $R(t) = \{ r \mid VT_s(r) \leq t < VT_e(r) \}$.
- **Coalescing** merges contiguous/overlapping identical tuples: condition is $t_2 \geq t_3$.
- **Interval intersection:** $I_1 \cap I_2 = [\max(a,c), \min(b,d))$.
- **Allen's 13 relations** completely describe interval relationships.
- **Now / UC** is a variable, not a fixed time, updated upon modification.
- **Temporal Query Languages** (TSQL2) extend SQL with `AS OF`, `VALID`, `PERIOD`, `BETWEEN`.
- **Architecture:** Query Engine → Optimizer → Execution → Temporal Storage (VT-Index + TT-Log).
- **Use cases:** Finance, Healthcare (EHR), Supply Chain, IoT, Auditing, OLAP.
- **Snapshot databases lose history on UPDATE/DELETE; temporal databases preserve it via append-only logs.**
- **Bitemporal correction** = insert new tuple with updated VT and current TT; old tuple's TT is closed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Device Design Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Device Design Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of KTU's Power Electronics / Power Semiconductor Devices syllabus, the **Device Design Space** refers to the multidimensional engineering trade-off map that defines the feasible operating envelope for a power semiconductor switch. It is the graphical and mathematical construct that relates the four fundamental design variables of a power device:

1. **Breakdown Voltage ($V_{BR}$ or $V_{DSS}$)** — the maximum blocking voltage.
2. **Specific On-Resistance ($R_{on,sp}$)** — the conduction loss metric per unit active area.
3. **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$)** — the dynamic loss metric.
4. **Current Density ($J$ or $A/cm^2$)** — the conduction capability.

The "space" is bounded by **physical material limits** (chiefly the **silicon limit**), **thermal limits** (junction temperature $T_j \le 150^\circ C$), and **technological limits** (lithography, epitaxy, packaging). No single device can be optimal in all four dimensions simultaneously; choosing a coordinate in this space is the essence of power device engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The design space is the answer to the central question — *"Why doesn't a single power device dominate every application?"* The answer lies in fundamental trade-offs between breakdown voltage and conduction loss, switching speed and short-circuit ruggedness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel-Economy vs. Truck Size" Map

Imagine a vehicle design space plotted on two axes: **Cargo Capacity (tons)** on the X-axis and **Mileage (km/L)** on the Y-axis.

- A small Maruti Alto gives great mileage but cannot carry a refrigerator.
- A heavy Tata truck carries 20 tons but consumes diesel like water.
- A Toyota Innova sits comfortably in the middle.

The **Device Design Space** is exactly this for power switches:

- A **Power MOSFET** is the "Innova" — fast switching, medium voltage (up to ~1000 V), but high $R_{on,sp}$ at high voltages.
- An **IGBT** is the "Truck" — handles 600 V – 6500 V with low conduction loss, but switches slowly.
- A **Thyristor (SCR)** is the "Cargo Train" — handles massive currents at very high voltages, but cannot be turned off by the gate.
- A **GaN HEMT** or **SiC MOSFET** is the "Tesla Semi" — breaks the silicon limit entirely, giving truck-like capacity with car-like speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of the Trade-off:**
> - Silicon critical electric field: $E_{crit} \approx 3 \times 10^5$ **V/cm**
> - Silicon electron mobility: $\mu_n \approx 1400$ **cm²/V·s**
> - Silicon bandgap: $E_g = 1.12$ **eV**
> - Intrinsic carrier concentration at 300 K: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³**

These constants are the **"speed limit signs"** of the device design space. Wide-bandgap materials (SiC, GaN) push these limits further.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Design Space

#### Pillar 1: The Static (Conduction) Trade-off

For a vertical power device, the drift region must support the entire breakdown voltage. The minimum drift region width $W_d$ and doping $N_d$ are coupled by the **avalanche breakdown condition**:

$$W_d = \frac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}} \quad \text{and} \quad N_d = \frac{\varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}^2}{2 q V_{BR}}$$

The specific on-resistance (per unit area) of this drift region is:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{W_d}{q \mu_n N_d}$$

Substituting the relations above yields the famous **Silicon Limit** expression:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}$$

This $V_{BR}^{2.5}$ dependence is the **single most important equation** in the device design space — it explains why high-voltage MOSFETs have enormous conduction losses.

#### Pillar 2: The Dynamic (Switching) Trade-off

Switching losses are governed by the time constants of the device:

$$\tau = R_{on,sp} \cdot C_{oss} \quad \text{(often called the RC time constant)}$$

A figure of merit combining static and dynamic performance is **Baliga's Figure of Merit (BFOM)**:

$$BFOM = \frac{\varepsilon_s \mu E_{crit}^3}{4 V_{BR}^2 \cdot C_{oss}/A}$$

Higher BFOM $\Rightarrow$ better device. Silicon: 1 (normalized); **SiC: ~500; GaN: ~1000+**.

#### Pillar 3: The Thermal Trade-off

Power dissipation $P_{loss}$ heats the junction above ambient:

$$T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja}$$

Where $R_{th,ja}$ is the junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (°C/W). The thermal limit defines a hard ceiling on the design space.

### 2.2 The Four Application Quadrants

| Application Domain | Voltage Range | Current Range | Frequency | Optimal Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SMPS / DC-DC** | 30 – 200 V | 1 – 50 A | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | Power MOSFET, GaN HEMT |
| **Motor Drives (LV)** | 400 – 690 V | 10 – 500 A | 4 – 20 kHz | IGBT, SiC MOSFET |
| **Traction / HVDC** | 1.7 – 6.5 kV | 100 – 3000 A | 200 Hz – 2 kHz | IGBT press-pack, IGCT |
| **Grid / FACTS** | 3.3 – 8 kV | 1000 – 6000 A | 50 – 60 Hz | Thyristor (SCR), GTO |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}$ | Silicon limit of on-resistance | $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ |
| 2 | $W_d = \dfrac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}}$ | Minimum drift region width | $cm$ |
| 3 | $N_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}^2}{2 q V_{BR}}$ | Drift region doping | $cm^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $E_{crit} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{q N_d}{\varepsilon_{si}} \right) W_d$ | Peak electric field (avalanche) | $V/cm$ |
| 5 | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{on}$ | Conduction loss | $W$ |
| 6 | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching loss | $W$ |
| 7 | $T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja}$ | Junction temperature | $^\circ C$ |
| 8 | $BFOM = \varepsilon \mu E_{crit}^3$ | Baliga's Figure of Merit | $relative$ |
| 9 | $V_{BR} = \dfrac{1}{2} E_{crit} W_d$ | Parallel-plane breakdown | $V$ |
| 10 | $J_{max} = q N_d v_{sat}$ | Maximum current density | $A/cm^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The design space equations govern every decision in a variable-frequency drive, solar inverter, EV traction inverter, and induction cooktop. A wrong device choice (e.g., a Si MOSFET in a 1500 V solar inverter) leads to catastrophic conduction losses and thermal runaway.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Silicon Limit $R_{on,sp}$ vs. $V_{BR}$

This is the **mother derivation** of the device design space. We will derive it from Poisson's equation step-by-step.

**Step 1: Poisson's Equation in the Drift Region**

For a one-sided abrupt $p^+ n$ junction (the building block of every vertical power device), the electric field in the n-drift region satisfies:

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{q N_d}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

**Step 2: Integrate to Find the Electric Field Profile**

Integrating from $x = 0$ (junction) to $x = W_d$:

$$E(x) = \frac{q N_d}{\varepsilon_{si}} (W_d - x)$$

The peak field is at the junction: $E_{peak} = \dfrac{q N_d W_d}{\varepsilon_{si}}$.

**Step 3: Apply the Avalanche Breakdown Condition**

For avalanche breakdown, the peak field must equal the critical field $E_{crit}$:

$$\frac{q N_d W_d}{\varepsilon_{si}} = E_{crit}$$

Solve for the doping:

$$N_d = \frac{\varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}}{q W_d}$$

**Step 4: Integrate the Field to Get Breakdown Voltage**

The breakdown voltage is the area under the E-x curve (a triangle):

$$V_{BR} = \int_0^{W_d} E(x) \, dx = \frac{1}{2} E_{crit} W_d$$

Solving for $W_d$:

$$W_d = \frac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute Back to Find Doping as a Function of $V_{BR}$**

$$N_d = \frac{\varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}^2}{2 q V_{BR}}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Drift Region Resistance**

The resistance of the drift region per unit area is:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{W_d}{q \mu_n N_d}$$

**Step 7: Substitute Both Expressions**

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{ \dfrac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}} }{ q \mu_n \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}^2}{2 q V_{BR}} }$$

Cancel $q$ and simplify:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}} \cdot \frac{2 V_{BR}}{\mu_n \varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}^2}$$

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}$$

$$\boxed{R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}}$$

This is the **silicon limit** — the absolute minimum on-resistance achievable in a silicon power device for a given breakdown voltage. No clever design can beat it; only a different semiconductor (SiC, GaN) can.

### 3.2 Numerical Evaluation — Working Out a Design Point

**Given:** A silicon power MOSFET rated for $V_{BR} = 600$ V.
**Constants:** $\varepsilon_{si} = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm, $\mu_n = 1400$ cm²/V·s, $E_{crit} = 3 \times 10^5$ V/cm.

**Step 1: Compute $R_{on,sp}$**

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 \times (600)^2}{(1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1400 \times (3 \times 10^5)^3}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 3.6 \times 10^5 = 1.44 \times 10^6$ V².

Denominator: $(1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1400 \times (27 \times 10^{15}) = 1.04 \times 1400 \times 27 \times 10^{3}$

Compute stepwise:

$$1.04 \times 1400 = 1456$$

$$1456 \times 27 = 39312$$

$$39312 \times 10^{3} = 3.9312 \times 10^7$$

So:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^6}{3.9312 \times 10^7} = 0.0366 \; \Omega \cdot cm^2$$

**Step 2: Convert to milliohms for a typical die area**

If the die area is $A = 0.5$ cm² (1 cm × 0.5 cm):

$$R_{on} = \frac{R_{on,sp}}{A} = \frac{0.0366}{0.5} = 0.0732 \; \Omega = 73.2 \; m\Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute Conduction Loss at $I_{rms} = 20$ A**

$$P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{on} = (20)^2 \times 0.0732 = 29.3 \; W$$

This is the **design space reality** — at 600 V, even an ideal silicon MOSFET dissipates ~30 W at 20 A, which is why SiC MOSFETs dominate this voltage class in modern EV inverters.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Comparison Table

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $E_{crit}$ (V/cm) | $\mu_n$ (cm²/V·s) | $R_{on,sp}$ @ 600 V (rel.) | Thermal Cond. (W/cm·K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Silicon (Si) | 1.12 | $3 \times 10^5$ | 1400 | 1.0 (baseline) | 1.5 |
| Silicon Carbide (SiC, 4H) | 3.26 | $2.5 \times 10^6$ | 900 | $\approx 1/500$ | 4.9 |
| Gallium Nitride (GaN) | 3.4 | $3.5 \times 10^6$ | 1500 (2DEG) | $\approx 1/1000$ | 1.3 |
| Diamond (C) | 5.5 | $1 \times 10^7$ | 2200 | $\approx 1/10000$ | 22 |

> [!NOTE]
> The cubic dependence on $E_{crit}$ in the silicon limit formula is why wide-bandgap materials win so dramatically — a 10× higher $E_{crit}$ gives a $10^3 = 1000\times$ lower $R_{on,sp}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Device Design Space — 3D Trade-off Cube

Since the design space is inherently multi-dimensional, the following Mermaid **block-level functional architecture** maps the four design variables, the physical limits that bound them, the material choices, and the resulting device families.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Design Variable V_BR Breakdown Voltage"] --> CORE
    A2["Design Variable R_on_sp Specific On Resistance"] --> CORE
    A3["Design Variable f_sw Switching Frequency"] --> CORE
    A4["Design Variable J Current Density"] --> CORE

    CORE["Device Design Space Central Trade Off Engine"] --> B1
    CORE --> B2
    CORE --> B3
    CORE --> B4

    B1["Physical Limit Silicon Limit R_on_sp proportional to V_BR squared over E_crit cubed"] --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> C3

    C1["Material Family Silicon Si"] --> D1["Power MOSFET 30V to 1000V Fast switching High R_on at HV"]
    C1 --> D2["IGBT 600V to 6500V Slow switching Low conduction loss"]
    C1 --> D3["Thyristor SCR 1200V to 8500V No turn off Highest current"]

    C2["Material Family Silicon Carbide SiC 4H"] --> D4["SiC MOSFET 650V to 1700V EV inverters Solar"]
    C2 --> D5["SiC Schottky Diode 650V to 3300V Low Qrr"]

    C3["Material Family Gallium Nitride GaN"] --> D6["GaN HEMT 100V to 650V SMPS LiDAR RF amplifiers"]

    B2["Thermal Limit T_j less than 150 C Si 200 C SiC"] --> E1["Cooling Topology Forced air Heat sink Liquid cold plate"]
    B2 --> E2["Package Limit TO 247 D2PAK Module Press pack"]

    B3["Technological Limit Lithography Minimum feature size"] --> F1["Cell Pitch 1 to 5 microns"]
    B3 --> F2["Epitaxial Layer Uniformity"]

    B4["Application Quadrant"] --> G1["LV SMPS MOSFET GaN"]
    B4 --> G2["MV Motor Drive IGBT SiC"]
    B4 --> G3["HV Traction IGBT Press Pack"]
    B4 --> G4["EHV HVDC Thyristor"]

    D1 --> APPL
    D2 --> APPL
    D3 --> APPL
    D4 --> APPL
    D5 --> APPL
    D6 --> APPL
    E1 --> APPL
    E2 --> APPL
    F1 --> APPL
    F2 --> APPL
    G1 --> APPL
    G2 --> APPL
    G3 --> APPL
    G4 --> APPL

    APPL["Final Design Selection: Coordinates in Design Space"]
```

### 4.2 The Trade-off Loop — Why No Single Device Wins

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Increase V_BR"] --> L2["Increase drift width W_d"]
    L2 --> L3["Decrease drift doping N_d"]
    L3 --> L4["R_on_sp INCREASES as V_BR squared"]
    L4 --> L5["Conduction loss INCREASES"]
    L5 --> L6["Need larger die or new material"]
    L6 --> L1
```

This closed loop is the **"design space treadmill"** — improving one variable inevitably worsens another. Wide-bandgap materials are the only escape hatch.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Device Design Space** for a power semiconductor switch. Name the four primary design variables that define it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Device Design Space is the multidimensional trade-off map that defines the feasible operating envelope of a power semiconductor switch, bounded by physical (material), thermal, and technological limits.

The four primary design variables are:

1. **Breakdown Voltage ($V_{BR}$)** — maximum blocking capability.
2. **Specific On-Resistance ($R_{on,sp}$)** — conduction loss per unit area.
3. **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$)** — dynamic loss metric.
4. **Current Density ($J$)** — current handling per unit area.

*Valuation tip (1 mark):* Mention that the space is **bounded by the silicon limit**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State and explain the **Silicon Limit** for a power MOSFET. Why is it considered a fundamental barrier?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The silicon limit expresses the minimum achievable specific on-resistance of a silicon power device for a given breakdown voltage:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}$$

It is considered a fundamental barrier because:

- The constants $\varepsilon_{si}$, $\mu_n$, and $E_{crit}$ are **intrinsic material properties** of silicon and cannot be changed by clever design.
- The $V_{BR}^{2.5}$ dependence forces an exponential rise in conduction loss as voltage rating increases.
- It can only be overcome by switching to **wide-bandgap materials** like SiC or GaN, which have 8–10× higher $E_{crit}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **specific on-resistance** of the drift region of a vertical power MOSFET as a function of breakdown voltage, showing all steps. State the **Silicon Limit** clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon power MOSFET is rated for $V_{BR} = 1200$ V. Using the silicon limit formula, calculate the minimum specific on-resistance, and then the on-resistance for a die area of $0.8$ cm². Take $\varepsilon_{si} = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm, $\mu_n = 1400$ cm²/V·s, and $E_{crit} = 3 \times 10^5$ V/cm. If the device carries an RMS current of 15 A, compute the conduction loss. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The student must show:

1. Poisson's equation in the drift region: $\dfrac{dE}{dx} = \dfrac{q N_d}{\varepsilon_{si}}$ — **1 mark**.
2. Integration to get the triangular E-field profile: $E(x) = \dfrac{q N_d}{\varepsilon_{si}}(W_d - x)$ — **1 mark**.
3. Avalanche breakdown condition: $E_{peak} = E_{crit}$, giving $W_d = \dfrac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}}$ — **1 mark**.
4. Doping: $N_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si} E_{crit}^2}{2 q V_{BR}}$ — **1 mark**.
5. Drift resistance: $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{W_d}{q \mu_n N_d}$ — **1 mark**.
6. Final substitution: $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}$ — **1.5 marks**.
7. Statement of silicon limit and its physical meaning — **0.5 mark**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Substitute into the silicon limit formula (1 mark)**

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 \times (1200)^2}{(1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1400 \times (3 \times 10^5)^3}$$

**Step 2: Compute numerator (0.5 mark)**

$$4 \times 1.44 \times 10^6 = 5.76 \times 10^6 \; V^2$$

**Step 3: Compute denominator (1.5 marks)**

$$(3 \times 10^5)^3 = 27 \times 10^{15} = 2.7 \times 10^{16}$$

$$1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 1400 = 1.456 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$1.456 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.7 \times 10^{16} = 3.9312 \times 10^7$$

**Step 4: Divide (1 mark)**

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^6}{3.9312 \times 10^7} = 0.1465 \; \Omega \cdot cm^2$$

**Step 5: Convert to absolute $R_{on}$ for $A = 0.8$ cm² (1 mark)**

$$R_{on} = \frac{0.1465}{0.8} = 0.183 \; \Omega = 183 \; m\Omega$$

**Step 6: Compute conduction loss at 15 A RMS (2 marks)**

$$P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \times R_{on} = (15)^2 \times 0.183 = 225 \times 0.183 = 41.2 \; W$$

**Final Answer:** $R_{on,sp} = 0.1465 \; \Omega \cdot cm^2$, $R_{on} = 183 \; m\Omega$, $P_{cond} = 41.2 \; W$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $(V_{BR})^2$ in the numerator — students often write $V_{BR}$ instead of $V_{BR}^2$ (**–2 marks**).
> 2. Mixing up $\mu_n$ and $\mu_p$ — for an n-channel MOSFET, it is **electron** mobility, not hole mobility (**–1 mark**).
> 3. Using $E_{crit}$ in V/m instead of V/cm — silent unit error leading to numerical answer off by 6 orders of magnitude (**–3 marks**).
> 4. Forgetting the factor of 4 in the silicon limit formula (some textbooks write 4, others write 2 depending on the assumption of triangular vs. trapezoidal field) — state the assumption clearly (**–1 mark**).
> 5. Not specifying that the result is a **lower bound**, not an achievable value — real devices are 2–5× worse due to contact resistance and channel resistance (**–1 mark**).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **Device Design Space** in power electronics. With a neat sketch, show how the trade-off between breakdown voltage, on-resistance, switching frequency, and current density defines application-specific device selection. Mention Baliga's Figure of Merit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Silicon, SiC, and GaN** power devices in terms of their position in the design space. Why are SiC and GaN called "wide-bandgap" devices, and what specific advantage does each offer over silicon? Compute the theoretical ratio of $R_{on,sp}$ for GaN vs. Si at $V_{BR} = 600$ V, given $E_{crit,Si} = 3 \times 10^5$ V/cm and $E_{crit,GaN} = 3.5 \times 10^6$ V/cm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Space Concept (7 marks):**

The Device Design Space is a **4-D trade-off envelope** bounded by:

- The **silicon limit** curve: $R_{on,sp} \propto V_{BR}^{2.5}$ (static trade-off).
- The **RC time constant** limit: $R_{on,sp} \cdot C_{oss}$ (dynamic trade-off).
- The **thermal limit**: $T_j \le 150^\circ C$ (for Si).
- The **technological limit**: minimum cell pitch set by lithography.

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A 2-D plot with $V_{BR}$ on X-axis (log scale) and $R_{on,sp}$ on Y-axis (log scale). The silicon limit is a straight line with slope 2 on log-log axes. Real devices lie above this line. The student's sketch should label:

- Power MOSFET region (LV, fast).
- IGBT region (MV/HV, slow).
- Thyristor region (EHV, no turn-off).
- SiC and GaN regions (below the silicon limit).

**Baliga's Figure of Merit (2 marks):**

$$BFOM = \varepsilon_s \mu E_{crit}^3$$

Higher BFOM $\Rightarrow$ lower $R_{on,sp}$ at given $V_{BR}$. SiC and GaN have BFOMs 100–1000× that of silicon.

**Application mapping (1.5 marks):** Tie each device family to its natural quadrant — e.g., MOSFETs in SMPS, IGBTs in motor drives, SCRs in HVDC.

**(b) Material Comparison (7 marks):**

**Wide-bandgap definition (1.5 marks):** SiC has $E_g = 3.26$ eV, GaN has $E_g = 3.4$ eV, vs. Si at $E_g = 1.12$ eV. "Wide" refers to the larger bandgap, which leads to:

- Higher $E_{crit}$ (can sustain higher fields without avalanche).
- Higher breakdown field.
- Higher operating temperature.

**Comparison table (2.5 marks):**

| Property | Si | SiC | GaN |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_g$ (eV) | 1.12 | 3.26 | 3.4 |
| $E_{crit}$ (V/cm) | $3 \times 10^5$ | $2.5 \times 10^6$ | $3.5 \times 10^6$ |
| Thermal cond. (W/cm·K) | 1.5 | 4.9 | 1.3 |
| Best voltage class | < 1.7 kV | 650 V – 15 kV | < 650 V |
| Switching speed | Med | High | Very high |

**Numerical ratio (3 marks):**

Since $R_{on,sp} \propto 1/E_{crit}^3$ (other terms assumed similar):

$$\frac{R_{on,sp,GaN}}{R_{on,sp,Si}} = \left( \frac{E_{crit,Si}}{E_{crit,GaN}} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{3 \times 10^5}{3.5 \times 10^6} \right)^3$$

$$= \left( \frac{3}{35} \right)^3 = \left( 0.0857 \right)^3 = 6.3 \times 10^{-4}$$

So $R_{on,sp,GaN} \approx \dfrac{1}{1587}$ of silicon — roughly a **1500× reduction** in theoretical on-resistance. (The actual achieved ratio in commercial devices is closer to 10–50× due to packaging and channel resistance, but the theoretical scaling is the design-space argument.)

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** GaN devices occupy the "high-frequency, low-loss" corner of the design space that silicon cannot reach.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Device Design Space** is a 4-D trade-off map: $V_{BR}$, $R_{on,sp}$, $f_{sw}$, $J$.
- **The silicon limit** is the hard floor: $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_{si} \mu_n E_{crit}^3}$.
- The $V_{BR}^{2.5}$ dependence makes high-voltage Si devices lossy — this is the **central problem** of power electronics.
- **Baliga's Figure of Merit** ($BFOM = \varepsilon \mu E_{crit}^3$) ranks materials: Si < SiC < GaN < Diamond.
- **Power MOSFET** = fast, low voltage (< 1000 V), high conduction loss at HV.
- **IGBT** = medium voltage (600 V – 6.5 kV), low conduction loss, slower switching.
- **Thyristor (SCR)** = highest voltage and current, no gate turn-off, used in HVDC.
- **SiC MOSFET** = EV traction inverters, solar inverters (650 V – 1700 V).
- **GaN HEMT** = SMPS, LiDAR, RF (100 V – 650 V), highest switching frequency.
- **Thermal limit** $T_j \le 150^\circ C$ (Si) and $\le 200^\circ C$ (SiC) is the second hard boundary.
- **Wide-bandgap materials** break the silicon limit through 8–10× higher $E_{crit}$.
- **The design space treadmill:** improving one variable always worsens another — escape only via material change.
- Always check units: $E_{crit}$ in **V/cm**, $V_{BR}$ in **V**, $R_{on,sp}$ in **$\Omega \cdot cm^2$**.
- For KTU numerical problems, the silicon limit formula is worth **5–7 marks** in a typical 14-mark question.
- The choice of device family in a 14-mark question should be **justified with quantitative reasoning** (compute $R_{on,sp}$ or $P_{loss}$), not just opinion.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** Whenever a 14-mark question asks about "device selection" for a given application, always structure your answer as: (1) Identify the design-space coordinates ($V_{BR}$, $f_{sw}$, $P_{load}$), (2) Compute $R_{on,sp}$ using the silicon limit, (3) Compute $P_{loss}$, (4) Compare with SiC/GaN to justify the choice. This is the **valuation key pattern** used by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Cost of Ownership and Power with Conventional Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Cost of Ownership and Power with Conventional Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBPET604 (Power Electronics and Electric Transportation / Electric Vehicle Technology)**, **Module 3 — Comparison of Cost of Ownership and Power with Conventional Systems** refers to the systematic, quantitative evaluation of an Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain against a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle powertrain across two principal engineering axes:

1. **Economic Axis — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** The aggregate lifetime expenditure incurred by an end-user over the vehicle's useful life, encompassing **acquisition cost, fuel/energy cost, maintenance cost, insurance, depreciation, financing, and residual value**.
2. **Technical Axis — Power Performance:** A direct comparison of drivetrain power metrics including **peak power, continuous power, torque-speed envelope, specific energy (Wh/kg), energy efficiency (%), and energy consumption (kWh/100 km vs. L/100 km)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 3):** Students are expected to quantitatively justify, with numerical examples, why the EV powertrain — despite higher upfront cost — achieves **lower TCO** within a 5–8 year horizon due to the lower per-km energy cost and drastically reduced maintenance overhead. The power comparison must establish the **flat torque curve** of electric machines as a structural advantage over the **narrow peak torque band** of ICE engines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing between two vehicles for a daily 60 km office commute lasting 7 years:

- **Vehicle A (Conventional ICE):** You pay less at the showroom, but every month you visit a petrol pump (paying ₹110/L) and a service centre (paying for engine oil, spark plugs, timing belts, clutches, exhaust repairs).
- **Vehicle B (Electric):** You pay more at the showroom because of the expensive lithium-ion battery, but every night you "fill" the tank from a 15-ampere socket at ₹7/unit. The "engine" has only **one moving part** (the rotor), so the service centre almost forgets your face.

After 7 years, the cumulative ledger of Vehicle B — its **Total Cost of Ownership** — is actually **lower** than Vehicle A, even though its sticker price is higher. This is the central insight of this module.

For **power**, think of two horses pulling a cart:
- The **ICE horse** sprints in a narrow gallop range (2000–5000 RPM) and needs a **multi-speed gearbox** to stay in that band while climbing hills.
- The **EV horse (electric motor)** delivers its **maximum torque from a standstill** and maintains it across nearly the entire speed range, so it can pull the cart up a hill without ever changing gears.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Anchor (Bold Constants):**
> - Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) Efficiency of ICE: **~ 20–35 %**
> - Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency of ICE: **~ 12–25 %**
> - Grid-to-Wheel Efficiency of EV: **~ 70–85 %**
> - Battery Round-Trip Efficiency: **~ 85–95 %**
> - Standard Test Cycle: **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)** / **WLTP**

## 1.3 Scope of Comparison

The KTU Module 3 framework structures the comparison across the following engineering sub-domains:

| Sub-Domain | EV Characteristic | ICE Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Carrier | Electricity (kWh) | Petrol / Diesel (Litre) |
| Prime Mover | 3-Phase AC Induction / PMSM | 4-Stroke Reciprocating Engine |
| Energy Storage | Li-ion / LFP Battery Pack | Liquid Hydrocarbon Fuel Tank |
| Transmission | Single-Speed Reducer | Multi-Speed Gearbox (5/6/8) |
| Peak Efficiency Point | **90–95 %** | **~35–40 %** |
| Auxiliary Loads | Electric AC, Vacuum Pump | Mechanical + Vacuum from Engine |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Axes of Comparison — Structured Logic

### Axis 1: Cost of Ownership (TCO Framework)

The **Total Cost of Ownership** is a **time-integrated financial model** that captures all expenses a vehicle owner incurs from purchase to disposal. KTU expects students to decompose TCO into the following additive components:

**Step 1 — Acquisition Cost ($C_{acq}$):**
The manufacturer's ex-showroom price, including the battery cost for EVs. For an EV, the battery alone contributes **30–40 %** of the total vehicle cost.

**Step 2 — Energy Cost ($C_{E}$):**
The cumulative fuel/electricity expense over the ownership period.

$$C_{E} = D_{annual} \times N_{years} \times P_{fuel}$$

where $D_{annual}$ is the annual distance driven, $N_{years}$ is the ownership period, and $P_{fuel}$ is the per-unit energy price (₹/kWh for EV, ₹/L for ICE, with a calorific conversion factor for ICE).

**Step 3 — Maintenance Cost ($C_{M}$):**
For ICE, this includes oil change, filter replacement, brake pad wear, clutch wear, timing belt, exhaust system. For EV, this is dominated by **tyre wear, brake fluid, and 12V auxiliary battery replacement** — drastically lower.

**Step 4 — Insurance & Financing ($C_{I+F}$):**
Annualized insurance premium plus financing interest.

**Step 5 — Residual Value ($-C_{R}$):**
Salvage value at the end of ownership — recovered from the total cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Master Equation:**
> $$TCO = C_{acq} + C_{E} + C_{M} + C_{I+F} - C_{R}$$
> The crossover point where $TCO_{EV} < TCO_{ICE}$ is called the **Break-Even Mileage** or **Payback Mileage**.

### Axis 2: Power & Performance Comparison

**Step 1 — Peak Power ($P_{peak}$):**
Maximum mechanical power output. EV motors deliver peak power typically for 30–60 seconds; ICE delivers it at Wide Open Throttle (WOT).

**Step 2 — Continuous Power ($P_{cont}$):**
Sustained power without thermal damage. For an EV, $P_{cont} \approx 0.5 \times P_{peak}$ to protect the motor windings and battery C-rate.

**Step 3 — Torque-Speed Envelope:**
The defining differentiator. EV motors have a **constant-torque region** from 0 RPM to base speed $N_{base}$, followed by a **constant-power region** up to maximum speed $N_{max}$. ICE engines have a narrow usable band and need a transmission.

**Step 4 — Acceleration (0–100 km/h):**
For a vehicle of mass $m$:
$$t_{0\text{-}100} \approx \frac{m \cdot v_{f}}{\eta \cdot P_{peak} \cdot 2.5}$$
EVs typically achieve 0–100 km/h in **6–8 seconds** (performance segment) due to instant peak torque availability.

**Step 5 — Specific Energy & Energy Density:**
- Petrol: **~ 12,000 Wh/kg**
- Li-ion Battery: **~ 150–250 Wh/kg**

This is the **fundamental thermodynamic disadvantage** of EVs — the energy carrier is ~50× heavier for the same range.

**Step 6 — Energy Consumption Conversion:**
$$E_{EV}\,[\text{kWh/100 km}] \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad F_{ICE}\,[\text{L/100 km}]$$

Using the calorific value of petrol: **1 L petrol ≈ 8.9 kWh** (Lower Heating Value, LHV).
So an EV consuming 15 kWh/100 km is energetically equivalent to:
$$F_{ICE} = \frac{15}{8.9 \times \eta_{ICE} / \eta_{EV}} \approx 5.0\,\text{L/100 km (TTW equivalent)}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TCO = C_{acq} + C_E + C_M + C_{I+F} - C_R$ | Total Cost of Ownership | ₹ (INR) |
| 2 | $C_E = D \cdot N \cdot P_{fuel}$ | Cumulative Energy Cost | ₹ |
| 3 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical Power from Torque | W (T in Nm, ω in rad/s) |
| 4 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Electric Motor Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{m_{battery}}$ | Battery Specific Energy | Wh/kg |
| 6 | $1\,\text{L petrol} \approx 8.9\,\text{kWh}$ (LHV) | Calorific Conversion | kWh/L |
| 7 | $E_{cons,EV} = \dfrac{V \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t}{D}$ | EV Energy Consumption | kWh/km |
| 8 | $C_{per\,km,EV} = \dfrac{P_{grid}\,[\text{₹/kWh}]}{E_{range}}\,[\text{km/kWh}]$ | EV Per-km Energy Cost | ₹/km |
| 9 | $C_{per\,km,ICE} = \dfrac{P_{petrol}\,[\text{₹/L}]}{\text{Mileage}}\,[\text{km/L}]$ | ICE Per-km Energy Cost | ₹/km |
| 10 | $N_{payback} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{acq}}{C_{per\,km,ICE} - C_{per\,km,EV}}$ | Payback Period (years) | years |
| 11 | $P_{peak,cont} = \dfrac{1}{2} P_{peak,30s}$ | Continuous vs Peak Power Rule | kW |
| 12 | $\text{Regen Efficiency} = \dfrac{E_{rec}}{E_{dec}}$ | Regenerative Braking Factor | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The TCO model is used by fleet operators (Ola Electric, Uber, Amazon Logistics) to procure vehicles, and by national policy-makers (NITI Aayog, Ministry of Heavy Industries) to design **FAME-II subsidy** slabs. The payback mileage analysis is the single most common 14-mark question in KTU board exams for this module.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Fleet Electrification:** Companies like **Amazon (Rivian EDV)** and **Flipkart (Tata Ace EV)** have publicly stated that their EV fleets achieve TCO parity with ICE at **~50,000–70,000 km/year** annual mileage, driven by 4× lower energy cost per km.
- **Power Electronics Impact:** The introduction of **Silicon Carbide (SiC) MOSFET** inverters has pushed motor-inverter combined efficiency from 85 % to **>95 %**, further widening the gap against ICE.
- **Grid Integration:** Comparing grid-to-wheel (EV) vs well-to-wheel (ICE) efficiencies, the EV is **~3× more efficient** even when India's coal-heavy grid (~70 % thermal) is considered.
<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Mileage / Payback Period

Let $C_{acq,EV}$ and $C_{acq,ICE}$ be the ex-showroom prices.
Let $c_{ICE}$ and $c_{EV}$ be the per-km operating cost (energy + maintenance).
Let $D_{annual}$ be the annual distance driven.

**Step 1:** Cumulative cost of ICE after $t$ years:
$$C_{ICE}(t) = C_{acq,ICE} + c_{ICE} \cdot D_{annual} \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Cumulative cost of EV after $t$ years:
$$C_{EV}(t) = C_{acq,EV} + c_{EV} \cdot D_{annual} \cdot t$$

**Step 3:** At the break-even point, $C_{EV}(t^*) = C_{ICE}(t^*)$:
$$C_{acq,EV} + c_{EV} \cdot D_{annual} \cdot t^* = C_{acq,ICE} + c_{ICE} \cdot D_{annual} \cdot t^*$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging the equation:
$$C_{acq,EV} - C_{acq,ICE} = (c_{ICE} - c_{EV}) \cdot D_{annual} \cdot t^*$$

**Step 5:** Defining the upfront cost differential $\Delta C = C_{acq,EV} - C_{acq,ICE}$:
$$\Delta C = (c_{ICE} - c_{EV}) \cdot D_{annual} \cdot t^*$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the break-even time $t^*$:
$$\boxed{\,t^* = \dfrac{\Delta C}{(c_{ICE} - c_{EV}) \cdot D_{annual}}\,}$$

**Step 7:** Equivalently, the **break-even cumulative mileage** $M^* = D_{annual} \cdot t^*$:
$$\boxed{\,M^* = \dfrac{\Delta C}{c_{ICE} - c_{EV}}\,}$$

**Step 4 Commentary:** This is the most important derivation in the KTU Module 3 syllabus. It quantifies exactly how many kilometres must be driven before the EV's higher sticker price is recovered through cheaper running costs. For Indian conditions (Tata Nexon EV vs Tata Nexon Petrol), $\Delta C \approx ₹10,00,000$ and $c_{ICE} - c_{EV} \approx ₹5/\text{km}$, giving $M^* \approx 2,00,000$ km — which is reached in ~6 years of typical 35,000 km/year driving.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power-to-Weight Ratio & Acceleration

**Step 1:** Net tractive force on a vehicle of mass $m$ (kg) on level road:
$$F_{net} = F_{trac} - F_{roll} - F_{aero} = ma$$

**Step 2:** Resistive forces:
$$F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \quad \text{where} \; C_{rr} \approx 0.012 \;\text{(car tyre on asphalt)}$$
$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \quad \text{where} \; \rho_{air} = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** For the 0–100 km/h acceleration phase, we approximate the resistive terms as constant at the average velocity $v_{avg} = 50$ km/h = 13.89 m/s:
$$F_{resist,avg} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v_{avg}^2$$

**Step 4:** Acceleration time from $v_1$ to $v_2$:
$$t_{acc} = \dfrac{m}{\eta \cdot P_{peak}} \cdot \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{v \, dv}{v + \dfrac{F_{resist,avg}}{m} \cdot v^{-1}} \approx \dfrac{m \cdot (v_2 - v_1)}{\eta \cdot P_{peak} - F_{resist,avg} \cdot v_{avg}}$$

**Step 5:** For $v_2 \gg v_1$, the simplified form becomes:
$$t_{0\text{-}100} \approx \dfrac{m \cdot v_f}{P_{acc,eff}}$$

**Step 6 Commentary:** An EV delivering 150 kW peak to a 1600 kg vehicle achieves:
$$t_{0\text{-}100} \approx \dfrac{1600 \times 27.78}{150{,}000 - 200 \times 13.89} = \dfrac{44{,}448}{147{,}222} \approx 0.30\,\text{s/m} \implies \approx 8.5\,\text{s}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Board Style (14-Mark Format)

**Problem:** A buyer compares a **Tata Nexon EV Prime** (₹14,00,000 ex-showroom, 30 kWh battery, certified range 312 km, energy consumption 9.6 kWh/100 km) with a **Tata Nexon Petrol** (₹10,00,000 ex-showroom, mileage 17 km/L). Electricity = ₹7/kWh, Petrol = ₹110/L, annual distance = 30,000 km, ownership period = 7 years. Maintenance: EV = ₹3,000/year, ICE = ₹12,000/year. Compute the TCO for both vehicles and the break-even mileage.

**Solution — Step 1: Annual Energy Cost**

EV per km energy cost:
$$c_{E,EV} = \dfrac{7\,[\text{₹/kWh}]}{100\,[\text{km/kWh}]} \times 9.6 = \dfrac{7 \times 9.6}{100} = 0.672\,[\text{₹/km}]$$

Wait — re-derivation: 9.6 kWh/100 km = 0.096 kWh/km.
Energy cost per km:
$$c_{E,EV} = 0.096 \times 7 = 0.672\,[\text{₹/km}]$$

ICE per km energy cost:
$$c_{E,ICE} = \dfrac{110\,[\text{₹/L}]}{17\,[\text{km/L}]} = 6.47\,[\text{₹/km}]$$

**Step 2: Total Per-km Operating Cost**
$$c_{EV} = 0.672 + \dfrac{3{,}000}{30{,}000} = 0.672 + 0.100 = 0.772\,[\text{₹/km}]$$
$$c_{ICE} = 6.47 + \dfrac{12{,}000}{30{,}000} = 6.47 + 0.40 = 6.87\,[\text{₹/km}]$$

**Step 3: TCO over 7 years (210,000 km)**
$$TCO_{EV} = 14{,}00{,}000 + 0.772 \times 2{,}10{,}000 = 14{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}62{,}120 = 15{,}62{,}120\,[\text{₹}]$$
$$TCO_{ICE} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 6.87 \times 2{,}10{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}42{,}700 = 24{,}42{,}700\,[\text{₹}]$$

**Step 4: Savings**
$$\Delta TCO = TCO_{ICE} - TCO_{EV} = 24{,}42{,}700 - 15{,}62{,}120 = 8{,}80{,}580\,[\text{₹}]$$

**Step 5: Break-Even Mileage**
$$M^* = \dfrac{14{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{6.87 - 0.772} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{6.098} \approx 65{,}600\,\text{km}$$

**Step 6 Commentary:** The EV pays back its price premium at ~65,600 km (~2.2 years of 30,000 km/yr driving) and saves **₹8.8 Lakh** over 7 years. This is a canonical KTU problem structure — note how the break-even comes out under 3 years, which is why the Indian EV market is growing rapidly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TCO Component Decomposition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["TCO Comparison Framework"] --> nodeB["Acquisition Cost"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Operational Cost"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["End of Life Cost"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["EV: Ex-showroom + Battery Cost"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["ICE: Ex-showroom + GST"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Energy Cost per km"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Maintenance Cost per year"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Insurance Premium"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a["EV: 0.7 to 1.2 Rupee per km"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b["ICE: 5 to 8 Rupee per km"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a["EV: 3000 Rupee per year"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b["ICE: 12000 Rupee per year"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Residual Value minus Scrap"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Battery Replacement if needed"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Break Even Mileage"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Delta C divided by per km savings"]
```

## 4.2 Power & Torque Envelope Comparison (EV vs ICE)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["Torque Speed Envelope Comparison"] --> nodeY["Electric Motor PMSM"]
    nodeX --> nodeZ["ICE Petrol Engine"]
    nodeY --> nodeY1["Region 1: 0 to N base constant torque at peak T"]
    nodeY --> nodeY2["Region 2: N base to N max constant power with falling T"]
    nodeY --> nodeY3["Single speed reducer sufficient"]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1["Region 1: Idle to 1500 rpm negligible torque"]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ2["Region 2: 2000 to 5000 rpm narrow peak torque band"]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ3["Requires 5 or 6 speed gearbox"]
    nodeY1 --> nodeResult["EV has flat torque from zero RPM"]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeResult
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: EV Powertrain vs ICE Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop["Powertrain Architecture Comparison"] --> nodeEV["EV Powertrain"]
    nodeTop --> nodeICE["ICE Powertrain"]
    nodeEV --> nodeEV1["Battery Pack 300 to 800 V DC"]
    nodeEV1 --> nodeEV2["VFD Inverter SiC MOSFET"]
    nodeEV2 --> nodeEV3["3 Phase AC Motor PMSM or IM"]
    nodeEV3 --> nodeEV4["Single Speed Reducer"]
    nodeEV4 --> nodeEV5["Wheels"]
    nodeEV2 -.-> nodeEV6["VCU for torque command"]
    nodeICE --> nodeICE1["Fuel Tank Petrol or Diesel"]
    nodeICE1 --> nodeICE2["Engine 4 Stroke Reciprocating"]
    nodeICE2 --> nodeICE3["Multi Speed Gearbox 5 or 6 ratios"]
    nodeICE3 --> nodeICE4["Clutch and Torque Converter"]
    nodeICE4 --> nodeICE5["Differential and Half Shafts"]
    nodeICE5 --> nodeICE6["Wheels"]
    nodeICE2 -.-> nodeICE7["ECU for fuel injection"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The EV drivetrain has **5 functional blocks** from energy source to wheels; the ICE drivetrain has **7 functional blocks**. Each additional mechanical stage introduces **frictional losses of 8 to 12 percent**, which is why the EV achieves 3× higher drivetrain efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the term **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** of a vehicle and list its four major components.

**Model Answer:**
Total Cost of Ownership is the aggregate financial expenditure incurred by a vehicle owner over the entire useful life, expressed in present-value terms. Its four major components are:
1. **Acquisition Cost** — the ex-showroom price including the battery for EVs.
2. **Energy Cost** — cumulative fuel or electricity expense over the ownership period.
3. **Maintenance Cost** — periodic service, parts replacement, and labour.
4. **Residual Value (negative cost)** — salvage value recovered at end-of-life.

TCO is the **gold-standard metric** used by fleet operators and policy makers to compare EV vs ICE because it normalizes the higher upfront EV cost against the lower running cost.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** State two structural advantages of an electric motor's torque-speed envelope over an ICE engine. Why does this eliminate the need for a multi-speed gearbox in most EVs?

**Model Answer:**
1. **Flat Peak Torque from Zero RPM:** The PMSM or Induction motor delivers maximum torque at standstill, whereas the ICE must rev to ~2000 RPM before producing useful torque. This is why EVs accelerate faster from a traffic signal without clutch slip.
2. **Constant-Power Region up to Maximum Speed:** Above the base speed, the motor enters a field-weakening region where power remains constant as speed rises, allowing the vehicle to cruise at highway speeds without gear changes.

Because the motor's torque is high across the entire operating range, a **single fixed-ratio reducer (typically 8:1 to 12:1)** is sufficient, eliminating the 5/6-speed gearbox, clutch, and torque converter — saving weight, cost, and ~10 % drivetrain loss.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, compare the **powertrain architecture of an EV and a conventional ICE vehicle**. Identify the components that are **eliminated** in the EV powertrain and explain their contribution to drivetrain losses.

**Model Answer:**

The EV powertrain consists of: **Battery Pack → VFD Inverter → 3-Phase AC Motor → Single-Speed Reducer → Wheels**.

The ICE powertrain consists of: **Fuel Tank → Engine → Clutch → Multi-Speed Gearbox → Propeller Shaft → Differential → Half-Shafts → Wheels**.

**Components eliminated in EV:** Clutch, multi-speed gearbox, propeller shaft, differential (in some architectures), and the torque converter.

**Contribution to losses:**
- Clutch slip loss: ~3–5 %
- Gearbox meshing loss: ~5–8 %
- Differential loss: ~2–3 %
- Propeller shaft frictional loss: ~1–2 %

**Cumulative ICE drivetrain loss: 11–18 %**
**EV drivetrain loss: 3–5 % (mostly in the single-speed reducer)**
**Net drivetrain efficiency gain: ~10–13 %**

This translates directly to energy savings of ~1.5 kWh per 100 km for every 10 % efficiency gain, which is **₹10.5 per 100 km saved** at ₹7/kWh.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]** A fleet operator is comparing a **Tata Ace EV** (₹9,50,000 ex-showroom, energy cost ₹1.2/km, maintenance ₹4,000/year) with a **Tata Ace Diesel** (₹6,50,000 ex-showroom, mileage 18 km/L, diesel ₹92/L, maintenance ₹18,000/year). If the annual distance is 40,000 km, compute:
(i) The break-even cumulative mileage
(ii) The TCO over 5 years
(iii) The annual savings at the end of year 5

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Per-km operating costs:**

EV:
$$c_{EV} = 1.2 + \dfrac{4{,}000}{40{,}000} = 1.2 + 0.10 = 1.30\,[\text{₹/km}]$$

ICE:
$$c_{ICE} = \dfrac{92}{18} + \dfrac{18{,}000}{40{,}000} = 5.11 + 0.45 = 5.56\,[\text{₹/km}]$$

**Step 2 — Break-even cumulative mileage:**
$$M^* = \dfrac{9{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}50{,}000}{5.56 - 1.30} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{4.26} \approx 70{,}422\,\text{km}$$

**Step 3 — TCO over 5 years (200,000 km):**
$$TCO_{EV} = 9{,}50{,}000 + 1.30 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}60{,}000 = 12{,}10{,}000\,[\text{₹}]$$
$$TCO_{ICE} = 6{,}50{,}000 + 5.56 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000 + 11{,}12{,}000 = 17{,}62{,}000\,[\text{₹}]$$

**Step 4 — Annual savings at end of year 5:**
$$\Delta TCO = 17{,}62{,}000 - 12{,}10{,}000 = 5{,}52{,}000\,[\text{₹ over 5 years}]$$
$$\text{Annual savings} = \dfrac{5{,}52{,}000}{5} = 1{,}10{,}400\,[\text{₹/year}]$$

**Interpretation:** The fleet operator recovers the ₹3 Lakh price premium in **~70,422 km** (~1.76 years at 40,000 km/yr) and then saves **₹1.10 Lakh per year** thereafter. This is why commercial last-mile delivery is the **fastest-growing EV segment** in India.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **break-even mileage** of an EV relative to an ICE vehicle, defining all symbols. List **three government incentives** in India that reduce $\Delta C$ and accelerate break-even.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 of these notes):**

Let $C_{acq,EV}$ and $C_{acq,ICE}$ be the acquisition costs in ₹.
Let $c_{EV}$ and $c_{ICE}$ be the per-km operating costs in ₹/km.
Let $D$ be the annual distance in km.

Cumulative cost of ICE after $t$ years: $C_{ICE}(t) = C_{acq,ICE} + c_{ICE} \cdot D \cdot t$
Cumulative cost of EV after $t$ years: $C_{EV}(t) = C_{acq,EV} + c_{EV} \cdot D \cdot t$

At break-even: $C_{EV}(t^*) = C_{ICE}(t^*)$
$$\Rightarrow C_{acq,EV} - C_{acq,ICE} = (c_{ICE} - c_{EV}) \cdot D \cdot t^*$$
$$\Rightarrow M^* = D \cdot t^* = \dfrac{C_{acq,EV} - C_{acq,ICE}}{c_{ICE} - c_{EV}}$$

This is the **break-even cumulative mileage** in km.

**Three Indian government incentives reducing $\Delta C$:**
1. **FAME-II Subsidy (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles):** ₹10,000 per kWh of battery capacity for 2-wheelers, ₹20,000 per kWh for 4-wheelers (capped).
2. **GST Reduction:** EVs attract **5 % GST** vs **28 % GST + cess (up to 22 %)** for ICE vehicles — direct ~20 % price reduction.
3. **State Subsidies:** Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, Delhi, and Karnataka offer **road tax exemption** (saves 8–12 % of vehicle cost) and **registration fee waiver**.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **peak efficiency** and **part-load efficiency** of an electric motor and an ICE engine. Use a sketch of efficiency vs % of rated load to illustrate.

**Model Answer:**

| Load (% of Rated) | EV Motor Efficiency | ICE Engine Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| 10 % | 75 % | 5 % |
| 25 % | 88 % | 18 % |
| 50 % | 92 % | 28 % |
| 75 % | 93 % | 33 % |
| 100 % | 90–95 % | 35 % |
| Peak | **95 %** at 60–80 % load | **38 %** at 80–90 % load |

**Sketch (efficiency curve):**

```
Efficiency (%)
100 |          ___
 90 |         /   \____
 80 |        /         \____
 70 |       /               \____ EV Motor
 60 |      /                     \____
 50 |     /                           \____
 40 |    /_____________________________________ ICE Engine Peak
 30 |   /                          _____________
 20 |  /                ___________
 10 | /___________
  0 |________________________________________
    0    20    40    60    80    100  (% Load)
```

**Critical Insight:** The EV motor maintains **>85 % efficiency across 25–100 % load**, while the ICE engine drops below 20 % at part load. This is because:
- EV motor losses are dominated by **I²R copper losses**, which scale with load current squared, but the base iron losses are low.
- ICE engine losses include **pumping loss, friction loss, and heat loss**, all of which persist even at low load, making the ICE extremely inefficient in city driving with frequent stops.

This is the **single most important reason** why EVs are more energy-efficient in urban Indian driving cycles, even though their on-paper tank-to-wheel range appears lower.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Board Exams (Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing per-kWh and per-km:** Students often write $P_{grid} \times D / 100$ directly without converting 9.6 kWh/100 km to 0.096 kWh/km. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to annualize maintenance:** Maintenance is annual (₹/year), not per km. Always convert: $c_{M,per\,km} = M_{annual} / D_{annual}$.
> 3. **Using Higher Heating Value (HHV) instead of LHV:** Petrol LHV = 8.9 kWh/L, HHV = 10.4 kWh/L. KTU board accepts either, but **always declare which you are using**.
> 4. **Ignoring residual value in TCO:** The salvage value at end-of-life can be **15–20 % of acquisition cost** for ICE but only **5–10 % for EV** (battery degradation). Always subtract $C_R$ from the final TCO.
> 5. **Confusing peak and continuous power:** A 100 kW EV motor cannot deliver 100 kW continuously. Always state "$P_{cont} \approx 0.5 \times P_{peak}$ for thermal limit" in your answer.
> 6. **Wrong break-even formula direction:** Make sure $\Delta C = C_{acq,EV} - C_{acq,ICE}$ is positive (EV is more expensive) and the denominator $(c_{ICE} - c_{EV})$ is positive (ICE is more expensive to run). If both become negative, your break-even interpretation is inverted.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TCO Definition:** $TCO = C_{acq} + C_E + C_M + C_{I+F} - C_R$ — must be the first equation in any answer.
- **Break-Even Mileage Formula:** $M^* = \Delta C / (c_{ICE} - c_{EV})$ — memorize this verbatim; appears in 80 % of KTU questions on this module.
- **Payback Period Formula:** $t^* = \Delta C / [(c_{ICE} - c_{EV}) \cdot D_{annual}]$ — annual-distance variant.
- **Calorific Anchor:** 1 L petrol = **8.9 kWh (LHV)** = 10.4 kWh (HHV). State explicitly.
- **Efficiency Anchors:** ICE TTW = **20–35 %**; EV Grid-to-Wheel = **70–85 %**; EV Motor-Inverter Peak = **95 %**.
- **Torque-Speed Envelope:** EV has **constant-torque region (0 to $N_{base}$)** + **constant-power region ($N_{base}$ to $N_{max}$)**; ICE has a narrow peak band requiring a multi-speed gearbox.
- **Specific Energy:** Petrol **~12,000 Wh/kg** vs Li-ion **~150–250 Wh/kg** — fundamental reason EVs are heavier for equal range.
- **Energy Consumption Conversion:** EV 15 kWh/100 km ≈ ICE 5 L/100 km (TTW equivalent).
- **Indian Subsidies:** FAME-II (₹10k–₹20k per kWh), 5 % GST, state road-tax exemption.
- **Maintenance Ratio:** EV maintenance is typically **1/4 to 1/5 of ICE** — quote this in any TCO answer.
- **Commercial Fleet Threshold:** TCO parity typically achieved at **50,000–70,000 km/year** mileage.
- **Power Electronics Anchor:** SiC inverters achieve **>97 % efficiency** at 50 % load — the technology driver behind modern EV powertrain superiority.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Always solve with **explicit numerical substitution**, **unit annotations on every term**, and a **final boxed answer** with units in ₹ or km.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Network Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT Network Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an IoT Network Model?

An **IoT Network Model** is a formalized, layered architectural blueprint that defines how billions of heterogeneous physical "Things" (sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart devices) discover each other, transmit data, get processed, and finally deliver actionable intelligence to applications, businesses, and end-users across the global internet infrastructure.

Unlike a traditional TCP/IP client-server network, an IoT network must simultaneously support:
- Massive device density (often **millions of nodes per square kilometre**).
- Constrained energy budgets (battery-operated nodes, **often < 1 W peak**).
- Intermittent and lossy links (wireless, low-power, high-latency).
- Heterogeneous protocols (IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT, MQTT, CoAP, HTTP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must be able to draw, label, and compare the major reference architectures: the **3-layer model**, the **5-layer model**, the **ITU-T Y.2060 model**, and the **IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-layer model**. Examination questions are routinely asked on layer responsibilities and cross-layer data flow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart City Postal System"

Imagine a smart city as a giant postal system:

| Postal Element | IoT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Houses generating letters | **Perception Layer** (sensors collecting raw data) |
| Local post offices & trucks | **Network/Transport Layer** (gateways, routers, wireless links) |
| Sorting & translation hubs | **Middleware / Processing Layer** (edge/fog computing, data aggregation) |
| The addressee's app/mailbox | **Application Layer** (smart home dashboard, health monitor) |
| The CEO of the postal service reading reports | **Business Layer** (analytics, profit models, strategy) |

Just as the postal service has distinct functional layers that move a letter from sender to receiver, the IoT Network Model stratifies the journey of a single sensor reading (e.g., a temperature of **27.4 °C**) from a field device all the way to a cloud dashboard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Every IoT architecture is essentially a *contract* between layers — each layer offers services to the layer above and consumes services from the layer below. The KTU examiner frequently tests this *service-provider / service-consumer* relationship.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a Standardized Network Model?

Without a standardized model:
- Hardware vendors (Texas Instruments, Intel, ARM) and software developers would create incompatible silos.
- Scalability would collapse — a system designed for 100 devices cannot organically grow to **10 million**.
- Security, management, and interoperability would become ad-hoc and un-maintainable.

A standardized model ensures **modularity**, **interoperability**, **scalability**, and **manageability** — the four pillars of any production-grade IoT deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major IoT Network Reference Models

The IoT ecosystem has converged on **four canonical reference models**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects mastery of all four.

### 2.1.1 The 3-Layer Model (Simplest, Foundational)

This is the **academic baseline** model introduced in virtually every IoT textbook.

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Physical / Sensor Layer)**
- **Role:** The "eyes, ears, and hands" of IoT.
- **Hardware:** Sensors (temperature, humidity, motion, gas), actuators, RFID readers, GPS modules, cameras.
- **Function:** Converts physical phenomena → electrical/digital signals.
- **Typical protocols/standards:** IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, BLE, NFC.

**Layer 2 — Network Layer (Transmission Layer)**
- **Role:** The "nervous system" that ferries data.
- **Hardware:** Gateways, routers, base stations, switches, cloud servers.
- **Function:** Routes and transmits aggregated sensor data over wired/wireless media (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, cellular, satellite).
- **Typical protocols:** IPv4/IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT.

**Layer 3 — Application Layer**
- **Role:** The "brain's interface" to the user.
- **Function:** Delivers application-specific services (smart home, smart agriculture, health monitoring, industrial automation).
- **Typical protocols:** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS, WebSockets, XMPP.

### 2.1.2 The 5-Layer Model (Industry Standard, KTU-Favoured)

The 3-layer model is academically clean but too coarse for engineering practice. The **5-layer model** introduces two crucial intermediate layers.

| # | Layer | Primary Function | KTU Frequently Tested Points |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Perception (Sensing)** | Physical sensing, actuation, RFID, GPS | Sensor types, sampling rate, ADC resolution |
| 2 | **Transport (Network)** | Data transfer via WAN/MAN/LAN/PAN | 6LoWPAN, RPL routing, cellular IoT |
| 3 | **Processing (Middleware)** | Data storage, analytics, decision-making, security | Edge vs Cloud computing, fog nodes |
| 4 | **Application** | Service delivery to end-users | Smart home, smart city, e-Health |
| 5 | **Business** | Manages overall system, ROI, privacy, strategy | Business models, data monetization |

### 2.1.3 The ITU-T Y.2060 Model (Standardization View)

The **International Telecommunication Union (ITU-T)** in recommendation **Y.2060** defined a 4-layer reference model widely referenced in KTU answers:

1. **Device Layer** — sensors/actuators/devices.
2. **Network Layer** — networking support & transport capabilities.
3. **Service Support and Application Support Layer** — generic IoT services (data storage, processing).
4. **Application Layer** — vertical applications (smart transport, e-Health).

The ITU-T model uniquely emphasizes **service support capabilities** like authentication, device management, and geo-location as first-class citizens.

### 2.1.4 The IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-Layer Model

Cisco and the IoT World Forum consortium proposed a **7-layer decomposed model**, which is *the* model for KTU Part B 14-mark questions on architecture.

| Layer | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physical Devices & Controllers** | The "Things" — sensors, actuators, edge nodes |
| 2 | **Connectivity** | Communications between devices and the network |
| 3 | **Edge / Fog Computing** | Pre-processing at the network edge (latency-critical) |
| 4 | **Data Accumulation** | In-motion + at-rest data storage, normalization |
| 5 | **Data Abstraction** | APIs, virtualization, data models for app developers |
| 6 | **Application** | Reporting, analytics, control, dashboards |
| 7 | **Collaboration & Processes** | People, business processes, end-user experience |

## 2.2 Cross-Layer Data Flow — "The Why and The How"

To ground these abstractions, trace the journey of a single data packet:

1. **Generation (Layer 1):** A DHT22 sensor measures temperature **27.4 °C** with ±0.5 °C accuracy. The onboard ADC converts the analog voltage to a **10-bit digital value**.
2. **Encoding (Layer 1 → Layer 2):** The value is wrapped in an IEEE 802.15.4 MAC frame and transmitted via 6LoWPAN over IPv6.
3. **Routing (Layer 2):** RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) constructs a DODAG (Destination Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) and routes the packet to a 6LoWPAN border router.
4. **Pre-processing (Layer 3 of 5-layer / Edge of IoTWF):** A fog node running at the gateway averages 100 readings, drops outliers beyond **3σ**, and forwards a single compressed payload.
5. **Storage (Layer 4 of 5-layer / Data Accumulation of IoTWF):** Cloud broker (e.g., Mosquitto MQTT broker on AWS EC2) persists the data into an InfluxDB time-series store.
6. **Abstraction (Layer 5 of 5-layer / Data Abstraction of IoTWF):** A REST API exposes `/api/v1/temperature?device_id=X` for downstream apps.
7. **Application (Layer 4 of 5-layer / Layer 6 of IoTWF):** Grafana dashboard plots the live trend; a rule engine triggers an SMS if temperature > **60 °C**.
8. **Business (Layer 5 of 5-layer / Layer 7 of IoTWF):** SLA monitoring, billing, predictive maintenance scheduling, ROI dashboards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-layer interactions** are not strictly forbidden. For example, security (TLS/DTLS) typically spans Layers 2 through 5. KTU answers that mention this earn additional appreciation marks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Metric Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of IoT devices (2025 projection) | $N_{dev}$ | **≈ 75 billion** | Stress-test scale for IPv6 + 6LoWPAN |
| Data rate for low-power IoT | $R$ | **0.3 kbps – 50 kbps** | Below 802.15.4's 250 kbps ceiling |
| Battery life target | $T_{bat}$ | **5 – 10 years** (2 × AA cells) | Drives energy harvesting & duty-cycling |
| Maximum packet size (802.15.4) | $L_{max}$ | **127 bytes** | Forces IPv6 header compression via 6LoWPAN |
| Maximum transmission unit (6LoWPAN) | $MTU_{6LN}$ | **81 bytes** (after compression) | Causes IPv6 fragmentation at adaptation layer |
| ADC resolution | $N_{ADC}$ | **10 – 16 bits** | Defines quantization step: $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N_{ADC}}}$ |
| Quantization step | $\Delta$ | mV range | Smallest detectable analog change |
| Average end-to-end latency (target) | $\tau_{e2e}$ | **< 100 ms** for control loops | Determines edge vs cloud processing placement |
| Coverage radius (LoRa) | $r_{cov}$ | **2 – 15 km** (rural) | Trades data rate for range via SF (Spreading Factor) |
| Airtime per packet | $T_{air}$ | **0.1 – 2 s** (LoRa) | Impacts duty cycle & energy budget |
| Energy per bit | $E_b$ | **μJ range** | Battery-lifetime projection driver |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF$ | **100,000 hrs** | Industrial-grade reliability target |

### 2.3.1 Fundamental Equation — Quantization

The resolution of any digital sensor is governed by:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N_{ADC}}}$$

Where:
- $V_{ref}$ is the analog reference voltage (e.g., **3.3 V**).
- $N_{ADC}$ is the number of bits of the analog-to-digital converter.

**Engineering Utility:** A 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$ yields $\Delta = 3.22\text{ mV}$. A 16-bit ADC on the same reference yields $\Delta = 50.35\text{ \mu V}$ — a **64× improvement** in measurement granularity, often critical for strain gauges and biomedical sensors.

### 2.3.2 Battery Lifetime Estimate

A simplified first-order battery-lifetime model used in IoT planning:

$$T_{bat} \approx \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$$

Where:
- $C_{bat}$ is battery capacity in **mAh** (e.g., 2400 mAh for a typical AA).
- $V_{bat}$ is nominal voltage (**1.5 V** for alkaline AA).
- $I_{avg}$ is average current draw in **mA**, dominated by sleep current plus active transmission bursts.

**Caveat (examiner trap):** Real lifetime is shortened by self-discharge (typically **2 – 5 %/year**), temperature derating, and pulse-load effects. Use this equation only for back-of-envelope estimates.

### 2.3.3 Link Budget for Wireless IoT

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} - L_{fs} - L_{misc} + G_{rx}$$

Where:
- $P_{rx}$ = received power (dBm).
- $P_{tx}$ = transmit power (typically **14 – 20 dBm** for LoRa).
- $G_{tx}, G_{rx}$ = antenna gains.
- $L_{fs}$ = free-space path loss (FSPL).
- $L_{misc}$ = miscellaneous losses (cables, connectors, fading margin).

The **free-space path loss** itself is:

$$L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \quad \text{(dB, with d in km and f in MHz)}$$

For non-line-of-sight, the **Log-Distance Path Loss Model** is used:

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 \, n \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

Where $n$ is the **path-loss exponent** (2.0 free space, 2.7–3.5 urban, 4–6 indoor obstructed) and $X_\sigma$ is a zero-mean Gaussian shadow-fading term in dB.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency in a Multi-Layer IoT Pipeline

Consider a temperature reading traveling from sensor to dashboard through the **5-layer model**. The total end-to-end latency is the sum of per-layer contributions.

**Step 1 — Define per-layer latency components:**

Let us denote the latency contribution of each layer $i$ as $\tau_i$, broken into:
- $\tau_{proc,i}$ = processing/serialization delay.
- $\tau_{queue,i}$ = queuing delay (often near-zero for IoT).
- $\tau_{trans,i}$ = transmission/airtime delay.

**Step 2 — Express the total latency:**

The system-level latency is the cascade of all layer delays:

$$
\tau_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \tau_i = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \left( \tau_{proc,i} + \tau_{queue,i} + \tau_{trans,i} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute realistic values:**

For a representative LPWAN (LoRa) link with cloud processing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= \tau_{sensor} + \tau_{MAC} = 5 \text{ ms} + 50 \text{ ms} = 55 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_2 &= \tau_{airtime} = 1.2 \text{ s (LoRa SF7, 50-byte payload)} \\
\tau_3 &= \tau_{broker} + \tau_{edge} = 10 \text{ ms} + 20 \text{ ms} = 30 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_4 &= \tau_{app\,poll} + \tau_{DB} = 100 \text{ ms} + 15 \text{ ms} = 115 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_5 &= \tau_{dashboard} = 50 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and convert to a meaningful engineering quantity:**

$$
\tau_{e2e} = 55 + 1200 + 30 + 115 + 50 = 1450 \text{ ms} \approx 1.45 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

For a **slow-changing** quantity like ambient temperature, **1.45 s** is acceptable. For a **safety-critical loop** (e.g., industrial emergency shutoff), the same architecture is unworkable — pushing processing to the **edge (Layer 3)** reduces $\tau_{e2e}$ by an order of magnitude (typically **< 100 ms**). This is the quantitative justification for **edge computing**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Number of Gateways via Link Budget

**Problem:** A KTU municipal IoT deployment plans to cover a **circular zone** of radius **5 km** with LoRaWAN nodes operating at **868 MHz** with $P_{tx} = 14 \text{ dBm}$, $G_{tx} = G_{rx} = 2 \text{ dBi}$, receiver sensitivity $S_{min} = -137 \text{ dBm}$ (SF12, BW 125 kHz), and path-loss exponent $n = 3.0$ (suburban). Find the maximum cell radius $R_{max}$ and number of gateways needed for full coverage.

**Step 1 — Compute maximum allowable path loss $L_{max}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - S_{min} - M_{fade} \\
&= 14 + 2 + 2 - (-137) - 10 \\
&= 145 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $M_{fade} = 10 \text{ dB}$ is an additional **shadow-fading margin**.

**Step 2 — Apply the Log-Distance Path Loss model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= L(d_0) + 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_{max}}{d_0}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Use the free-space reference at $d_0 = 1$ km with $f = 868$ MHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(d_0) &= 20 \log_{10}(1) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44 \\
&= 0 + 58.77 + 32.44 \\
&= 91.21 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_{max}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
145 &= 91.21 + 10 \cdot 3.0 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_{max}}{1}\right) \\
145 - 91.21 &= 30 \cdot \log_{10}(R_{max}) \\
53.79 &= 30 \cdot \log_{10}(R_{max}) \\
\log_{10}(R_{max}) &= \frac{53.79}{30} = 1.793 \\
R_{max} &= 10^{1.793} \approx 62.1 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decide coverage strategy:**

A single gateway can theoretically cover up to **62.1 km** in radius — but this is the noise-limited free-space case. Practical terrain, building clutter, and antenna height reduce this to **3 – 8 km** in suburban deployments. A safe engineering design uses $R_{design} = 4$ km.

**Step 5 — Number of gateways for full coverage:**

Area of full zone $A_{zone} = \pi R_{zone}^2 = \pi (5)^2 \approx 78.54 \text{ km}^2$.

Area covered per gateway $A_{gw} = \pi R_{design}^2 = \pi (4)^2 \approx 50.27 \text{ km}^2$.

Number of gateways:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{gw} &= \left\lceil \frac{A_{zone}}{A_{gw}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{78.54}{50.27} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.56 \rceil = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** **2 gateways** are needed, with one centrally placed and one offset to provide overlap and redundancy.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selecting the Right Model

| Criterion | 3-Layer | 5-Layer | ITU-T Y.2060 | IoTWF 7-Layer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of layers | 3 | 5 | 4 | 7 |
| Best for academic intro | ✅✅✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Best for industry design | ❌ | ✅✅ | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ |
| Handles security explicitly | ❌ | ❌ | ✅✅ | ✅✅✅ |
| Includes business layer | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Separation of edge/fog | ❌ | ⚠️ (Layer 3) | ⚠️ | ✅✅✅ |
| KTU question frequency | Medium | High | Medium | High |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Quantization for a Soil Moisture Sensor

**Problem:** An IoT soil-moisture node uses a **12-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V. The analog output range from the capacitive sensor is **0.5 V to 2.5 V**. Find (a) quantization step, (b) digital value for a measured **1.4 V**, (c) minimum detectable moisture change.

**Step (a) — Quantization step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N_{ADC}}} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} \\
&\approx 0.8057 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Digital value for 1.4 V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \text{round}\!\left( \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{N_{ADC}} - 1) \right) \\
&= \text{round}\!\left( \frac{1.4}{3.3} \cdot 4095 \right) \\
&= \text{round}\!\left( 0.4242 \cdot 4095 \right) \\
&= \text{round}(1737.18) = 1737
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Minimum detectable voltage change:**

$$
\Delta V_{min} = \Delta = 0.806 \text{ mV}
$$

If the sensor has a transfer function of **25 mV per 1 % VWC (Volumetric Water Content)**, the minimum detectable moisture change is:

$$
\Delta\text{VWC}_{min} = \frac{0.806}{25} \approx 0.032 \, \% \text{ VWC}
$$

This is more than adequate for precision irrigation.

---
