<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**  
*Define a Functional Domain. List any two distinguishing characteristics of a Core Domain.*

**Model Answer:**

A *Functional Domain* is a logically bounded area of a software system that encapsulates a cohesive set of business capabilities, data, and rules. It represents a unified sphere of responsibility aligned with a specific business function.

**Distinguishing characteristics of a Core Domain:**

1. **Strategic Business Value** — It embodies the unique differentiator of the system and provides competitive advantage to the organization.
2. **High Investment Priority** — Core domains receive the most skilled developers, the most rigorous architectural review, and the highest test coverage, since failures here directly impact revenue and reputation.

*(Awarded: Definition — 1.5 marks; Two characteristics — 1.5 marks. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
*Differentiate between Core Domain and Generic Domain with a suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect**         | **Core Domain**                                              | **Generic Domain**                                  |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| **Purpose**        | Encapsulates the unique value proposition of the system      | Provides commonly available, non-differentiating functionality |
| **Investment**     | High — in-house development by best teams                    | Low — often outsourced or use third-party libraries |
| **Example**        | The recommendation engine in an e-commerce platform          | The authentication/login module using OAuth2        |
| **Reusability**    | Low (specific to one product family)                         | High (reusable across many systems)                |
| **Failure Impact** | High (direct revenue loss)                                   | Low (graceful degradation possible)                |

*(Awarded: 1.5 marks for differentiation + 1.5 marks for examples. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Domain Engineering Process** in detail with a suitable block diagram. Identify and briefly describe each of its four phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a feature model for an **Online Learning Platform (LMS)**. The platform must support the following features:
- **Course Delivery** (mandatory): alternatives — *Live Classes* OR *Recorded Lectures*
- **Assessment** (mandatory): alternatives — *Quiz Module* OR *Assignment Module* OR *Proctored Exam*
- **Certification** (optional): sub-options — *PDF Certificate* OR *Blockchain-Verified Certificate*
- **Discussion Forum** (optional)

Compute the **Total Valid Configurations (VCC)** and the **Domain Reusability Index (DRI)** assuming 42 features are defined in the domain model, and 31 of them have been reused across deployed products. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

The **Domain Engineering Process** is a systematic, four-phase methodology for engineering a family of related software systems (a Software Product Line) by identifying, designing, implementing, and deploying reusable domain assets. The four phases are:

**Phase 1 — Domain Analysis:**  
This is the *what* phase. Analysts study the market, existing systems, and stakeholder needs to identify commonalities (features common to all products in the family) and variabilities (features that differ across products). The output includes a **Feature Model**, a **Glossary of Terms**, and a **Domain Definition Document**.

**Phase 2 — Domain Design:**  
This is the *how* phase. Architects define a **Reference Architecture** for the family, specifying components, connectors, inter-domain contracts, and architectural styles. The output is a **Reference Architecture Document** and **Component Specifications**.

**Phase 3 — Domain Implementation:**  
This is the *build* phase. Developers create the **Reusable Asset Library** (frameworks, services, libraries, domain-specific languages) that will be reused across all products in the family.

**Phase 4 — Application Engineering / Deployment:**  
This is the *instantiate* phase. Product teams select features from the domain model, configure them, and produce concrete products from the SPL.

*(Awarded: 4 marks for the four-phase description + 3 marks for the block diagram. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Step 1: Identify the cardinality of each alternative group.**

- **Course Delivery Group:** $\vert \text{AltChoices} \vert = 2$ (Live OR Recorded)
- **Assessment Group:** $\vert \text{AltChoices} \vert = 3$ (Quiz OR Assignment OR Proctored)
- **Certification Group (optional):** $1 + 2 = 3$ (None OR PDF OR Blockchain)
- **Discussion Forum (optional):** $1 + 1 = 2$ (Disabled OR Enabled)

**Step 2: Compute VCC using the product rule.**

$$ \text{VCC} = 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 2 $$

$$ \text{VCC} = 36 $$

**Step 3: Compute DRI using the formula.**

$$ \text{DRI} = \dfrac{31}{42} $$

$$ \text{DRI} = 0.7381 \approx 73.81\% $$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The LMS feature model supports **36 valid product configurations**, indicating high flexibility. The **DRI of 73.81%** signifies a **mature Software Product Line** with strong asset reuse — comfortably above the 70% benchmark recommended in the KTU 2024 software engineering guidelines.

*(Awarded: 4 marks for VCC computation + 3 marks for DRI computation and interpretation. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **Commonality and Variability Analysis (CVA)** technique. How is it applied during Domain Analysis? Provide one example for each type of variability (mandatory optional, alternative). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable **Mermaid-style block diagram**, depict the **Bounded Context Map** of a **Healthcare Management System** showing at least four functional domains. For each domain, classify it as Core, Supporting, Generic, or Auxiliary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Commonality and Variability Analysis (CVA)** is a structured technique used during **Phase 1 (Domain Analysis)** of the Domain Engineering Process. It involves systematically identifying:

- **Commonalities:** Features, behaviours, and structures that are **shared by all products** in the software product line.
- **Variabilities:** Features, behaviours, and structures that **differ across products** in the family.

**Application Steps:**

1. Gather stakeholder requirements from multiple product visions.
2. Cluster requirements into common vs. variable categories.
3. Represent variabilities in a **Variability Table** with binding time (compile-time, deployment-time, run-time).
4. Trace each variability back to a stakeholder need.

**Examples of Variability Types:**

| **Variability Type**  | **Description**                                                  | **Example**                                                |
|-----------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Mandatory Optional**| Feature may or may not be present in a product                   | *Two-Factor Authentication* — optional in basic plan      |
| **Alternative**       | Exactly one of a set of mutually exclusive features must be chosen | *Payment Gateway* — Razorpay OR Stripe OR PayPal          |
| **Parameterised**     | Feature's behaviour varies by a numeric/string parameter         | *Tax Calculator* — tax rate varies by region (5%–28%)     |
| **External**          | Variability resolved by an external service or device            | *Map Provider* — Google Maps OR OpenStreetMap             |

*(Awarded: 3 marks for CVA explanation + 4 marks for the table and examples. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Bounded Context Map for a Healthcare Management System:**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.4** of these notes, adapted as follows:

- **Patient Records Domain** → **Core** (strategic, regulatory-critical).
- **Clinical Workflow Domain** → **Core** (appointment, prescription, treatment plans).
- **Billing & Insurance Domain** → **Supporting** (depends on Clinical Workflow).
- **Telemedicine Domain** → **Supporting** (uses Patient Records).
- **Notification Domain** → **Generic** (third-party SMS/email gateway).
- **Audit & Logging Domain** → **Auxiliary** (background regulatory compliance).

**Integration patterns:**
- *Patient Records* and *Clinical Workflow* share a **Shared Kernel** (patient demographics).
- *Billing* consumes *Clinical Workflow* events via **Published Language (HL7 FHIR)**.
- *Telemedicine* validates sessions via the *Authentication* context using **Customer-Supplier** pattern.
- *Notification* subscribes to events from all other domains via an **Event Bus**.

*(Awarded: 3 marks for the diagram + 4 marks for classifications and integration patterns. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Module" with "Domain":** A *module* is a *technical* decomposition (e.g., a Java package). A *domain* is a *business-aligned* boundary. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Bounded Context" aspect:** When defining a domain, you MUST mention the data boundary. Stating it as merely "a group of features" is worth only **1 of 3 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong formula application in VCC:** Students often multiply without accounting for the **optional groups** (the "+1" for the "not selected" case). This loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the FODA diagram:** In CVA-related 7-mark questions, a *visual* representation of commonalities and variabilities is **mandatory** for full marks. A text-only answer scores at most **4 of 7 marks**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition:** A Functional Domain = Cohesive Business Capability + Bounded Data Context + Explicit Integration Contract.
- **Domain Engineering Process (DEP):** Analysis → Design → Implementation → Deployment (Application Engineering).
- **Core Domain = strategic**; **Supporting = necessary but not differentiating**; **Generic = reusable across systems**; **Auxiliary = background** (Mnemonic: **CSGA**).
- **Feature Model Elements:** Mandatory, Optional, Alternative, Requires, Excludes.
- **VCC Formula:** $\text{VCC} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (\text{AltChoices}_i + 1_{i \text{ optional}})$.
- **DRI Formula:** $\text{DRI} = \dfrac{\text{Reused Features}}{\text{Total Features}}$.
- **FCR Formula:** $\text{FCR} = \dfrac{\text{Implemented Features}}{\text{Planned Features}}$.
- **DDD Bounded Context:** Eric Evans; a domain has *one model* internally but is *integrated via context maps*.
- **CVA** identifies commonalities and variabilities — produce a **Variability Table** with **binding time**.
- **Reference Architecture** is the *blueprint* for the SPL — never confuse with concrete product architecture.
- **12-Factor App** methodology supports domain-based deployment (factor II: dependencies, factor V: build-release-run).
- **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity threshold:** $\leq 10$ per domain module for maintainability.
- **Cohesion vs. Coupling:** High cohesion **within** a domain, low coupling **between** domains.
- **FODA** = Feature-Oriented Domain Analysis (Kang et al., 1990) — the foundational KTU reference.
- **HL7 FHIR** is the **Published Language** used in healthcare domain integration.
- **Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL):** Use it when integrating with legacy or third-party domains to prevent foreign schema leakage.
- **Mermaid-safe identifiers:** Always use alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`); never use `end` as a node name.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RFID
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** RFID

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** is a non-contact, automatic identification technology that uses electromagnetic fields (specifically radio waves) to uniquely identify, track, and retrieve data stored on **tags** (also called transponders) attached to objects, animals, or persons. The system fundamentally relies on the transfer of modulated radio frequency energy between an **interrogator/reader** (transceiver) and a passive or active **tag** to enable identification without physical contact or line-of-sight visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** RFID is a wireless communication technology classified under *Automatic Identification and Data Capture (AIDC)* systems, primarily used in IoT, supply chain management, access control, and asset tracking, operating in frequency bands standardized by ISO/IEC and EPCglobal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian and you have **10,000 books** in a giant warehouse. Normally, to find a specific book, you must scan its barcode with a laser (Barcode technology). But this has a problem:

- The laser needs **line-of-sight** (you must see the barcode).
- It can scan only **one book at a time**.
- The barcode **cannot be rewritten** once printed.

Now, imagine giving each book a **tiny electronic whisperer (the tag)** that constantly listens for a "shout" from your walkie-talkie (the **reader**). The moment you shout its unique ID, it whispers back its name and information *through the wall, from inside a closed box, even if 50 books are stacked together*. That, in essence, is **RFID**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike barcodes which need optical contact, RFID uses **radio waves** that pass through non-metallic materials (plastic, cardboard, fabric), enabling **bulk scanning** and **hidden tagging**.

## 1.3 Core Components of an RFID System

| Component | Function | Key Characteristic |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Tag / Transponder** | Stores unique ID + data, transmits when interrogated | Passive (no battery) or Active (with battery) |
| **Reader / Interrogator** | Emits RF signal, receives tag response | Has antenna + transceiver + microprocessor |
| **Antenna** | Mediates RF energy transfer | Tuned to specific frequency band |
| **Host Computer / Middleware** | Processes, filters, and routes tag data to enterprise systems | Application layer software |
| **Communication Protocol** | Defines how reader and tag "talk" (e.g., EPC Class-1 Gen-2) | Standardized by ISO 18000 series |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Frequency Bands (Bolded Key Metrics):**
> - **LF (Low Frequency): 125 kHz – 134 kHz** | Range: ~10 cm
> - **HF (High Frequency): 13.56 MHz** | Range: ~10 cm – 1 m
> - **UHF (Ultra High Frequency): 860 MHz – 960 MHz** | Range: ~1 m – 12 m
> - **Microwave: 2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz** | Range: ~1 m – 100 m (active tags)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The communication in a passive RFID system follows a structured sequence:

1. **Reader Activation:** The reader energizes its antenna with an alternating current at a specific carrier frequency $f_c$, producing a time-varying electromagnetic field in the surrounding region.
2. **Tag Coupling:** When a passive tag enters this field, the **coil antenna** on the tag intercepts the flux. According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an alternating EMF is induced in the tag's antenna coil.
3. **Power Harvesting:** The induced AC voltage is rectified by a Schottky diode bridge on the tag's IC, charging a small internal capacitor that acts as the tag's temporary power source.
4. **Data Modulation:** The tag's microcontroller modulates the load (impedance) on its antenna, known as **Load Modulation** (specifically **Amplitude Shift Keying – ASK** or **Phase Shift Keying – PSK**). This modulates the back-scattered or re-radiated signal.
5. **Reader Demodulation:** The reader detects the subtle variation in its antenna's voltage/current caused by the tag's load modulation, then amplifies and demodulates it to recover the tag's unique ID (e.g., 96-bit EPC code).
6. **Data Forwarding:** The decoded ID is forwarded to the host system (cloud, database) for action.

## 2.2 Coupling Mechanisms — The "How" Behind Wireless Power Transfer

RFID uses **two fundamental coupling methods** depending on the frequency:

- **Inductive Coupling (Near Field):** Used in LF and HF. Based on the transformer principle, where the magnetic field component $H$ dominates. Range is governed by:

$$\frac{H}{H_0} \approx \left(\frac{r}{\lambda}\right)^{-3}$$

where $r$ is the distance and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. Range is typically **less than $\lambda / (2\pi)$**.

- **Backscatter Coupling (Far Field):** Used in UHF and Microwave. The tag reflects (backscatters) a portion of the reader's transmitted EM wave, with the reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ modulated by the tag's antenna load.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Carrier frequency vs wavelength ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | Hz, m |
| 2 | $\lambda / (2\pi)$ | Approximate boundary between near-field and far-field | m |
| 3 | $V_{induced} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | Faraday's induced EMF in tag coil | Volts |
| 4 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_a}{Z_L + Z_a}$ | Reflection coefficient for backscatter modulation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation — Received power | Watts |
| 6 | $R_{max} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{P_{threshold}}}$ | Theoretical max read range (FRIIS-derived) | meters |
| 7 | $N_{tags/sec} = \dfrac{1}{T_{inventory}}$ | Anti-collision throughput | tags/second |
| 8 | $DR = \dfrac{\text{Data bits}}{\text{Time slot}}$ | Data Rate (typically 40 kbps – 640 kbps) | bps |
| 9 | $E_{tag} = \int P_{harvested}\, dt$ | Energy harvested by passive tag | Joules |
| 10 | $BER_{ASK} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate for ASK modulation | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind Formula 1 — $f_c = c/\lambda$:**  
> Lower frequency → longer wavelength → better penetration through materials but smaller bandwidth and shorter range. Higher frequency → shorter wavelength → longer range and higher data rate but weaker penetration. This is why **LF is used for livestock** (penetrates tissue) and **UHF is used for pallets** (longer range in open air).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Walmart, Amazon, and Maersk use UHF RFID to track pallets in real-time.
- **Healthcare:** Tracking surgical instruments, patient wristbands, blood bags.
- **Access Control:** Employee ID badges, contactless toll collection (FASTag in India).
- **Retail:** Inventory management, anti-theft EAS (Electronic Article Surveillance).
- **IoT & Industry 4.0:** M2M identification, smart factories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Read Range of an RFID System (Friis-Based)

The maximum read range $R_{max}$ of a passive RFID tag is derived from the condition that the **power received by the tag** equals (or exceeds) the **tag's activation threshold power**.

### Step 1: Received Power at the Tag (Friis Transmission Equation)

For a far-field, line-of-sight RFID link, the power $P_r$ received by the tag's antenna is given by the **Friis Transmission Equation**:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2
$$

**Explanation:** $P_t$ is the power transmitted by the reader antenna, $G_t$ is the reader antenna gain, $G_r$ is the tag antenna gain, $\lambda$ is the carrier wavelength, and $R$ is the distance between the reader and the tag. The term $\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ represents the free-space path loss (FSPL).

### Step 2: Minimum Power Required for Tag Activation

The tag can only respond if the received power is at least the **tag's sensitivity threshold** $P_{th}$:

$$
P_r \geq P_{th}
$$

**Explanation:** $P_{th}$ is determined by the tag IC's chip design, typically ranging from **-10 dBm to -20 dBm** for modern passive UHF tags.

### Step 3: Set Power Equal to Threshold for $R_{max}$

For the maximum read range, we set $P_r = P_{th}$ and solve for $R$:

$$
P_{th} = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^2
$$

**Explanation:** This expresses the boundary condition — any greater distance would deliver power below the tag's activation threshold.

### Step 4: Isolate $R_{max}$

$$
\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{P_{th}}{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}
$$

**Explanation:** Dividing both sides by $P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r$ to isolate the path-loss term.

### Step 5: Take the Square Root

$$
\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{th}}{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}}
$$

**Explanation:** The square root removes the squared term, giving the ratio of wavelength to circumference $4\pi R_{max}$.

### Step 6: Rearrange for $R_{max}$

$$
4\pi R_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\sqrt{\dfrac{P_{th}}{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}}}
$$

**Explanation:** Multiplying both sides by $R_{max} \cdot 4\pi$ and the square-root term to invert the fraction.

### Step 7: Final Expression

$$
R_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{th}}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **fundamental passive RFID range equation**, showing that $R_{max}$ is directly proportional to $\lambda$ and the square root of the reader's EIRP, and inversely proportional to the square root of the tag sensitivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Doubling the reader's transmit power $P_t$ increases the range by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times, *not* 2 times — a classic diminishing-returns lesson in RF design.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tag Type Comparison

| Property | Passive Tag | Semi-Passive (Semi-Active) Tag | Active Tag |
|----------|-------------|-------------------------------|------------|
| **Power Source** | Harvested from reader RF | Battery for IC, RF from reader | Internal battery powers everything |
| **Battery** | None | Yes (small) | Yes (large) |
| **Read Range** | Up to **10 m** (UHF) | Up to **25 m** | Up to **100 m** |
| **Cost** | **$0.05 – $0.50** | $1 – $10 | $15 – $100+ |
| **Lifespan** | Unlimited | 2 – 5 years | 3 – 10 years |
| **Data Storage** | 64 – 96 bits typical | Up to 64 KB | Up to 1 MB+ |
| **Sensors on tag** | No | Yes (temp, humidity) | Yes (multi-sensor) |
| **KTU Use Case** | Library books, ID cards | Cold chain monitoring | Vehicle tolling, container tracking |

## 3.3 Anti-Collision Protocol (Slotted Aloha — KTU High-Yield)

When multiple tags enter the reader's field simultaneously, **signal collision** occurs. The **EPC Class-1 Gen-2** standard uses a **slotted Aloha-based Q-algorithm**:

$$
N_{slots} = 2^Q
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The reader broadcasts a **Query** command with a parameter $Q \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 15\}$.
2. The tag randomly picks one of the $N_{slots} = 2^Q$ time slots to respond.
3. If only one tag picks a given slot, the reader successfully reads it (no collision).
4. If two or more tags pick the same slot, a collision occurs and the reader increments $Q$ (more slots, lower collision probability).
5. If no tag responds, the reader decrements $Q$.

**Optimization target:** The system dynamically adjusts $Q$ to maximize the throughput $S$, with theoretical maximum throughput of a slotted Aloha system being:

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%
$$

at $G = 1$ (offered load = 1 packet per slot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "Q goes UP when CHAOS (collision) happens, and DOWN when there's SILENCE (no response)."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — RFID System Architecture (Mermaid Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Host Computer\nMiddleware\nApplication Layer] -->|Command and Data| B[RFID Reader\nTransceiver + MCU]
    B -->|Carrier Signal\nf_c 860-960 MHz| C[Reader Antenna\nGain G_t]
    C -->|Electromagnetic Wave\nFar Field| D[RFID Tag\nIC + Antenna]
    D -->|Backscatter\nModulated Signal| C
    C -->|Received Echo| B
    B -->|Decoded EPC Data| A
    A -->|HTTP / MQTT / SQL| E[Cloud Database\nEnterprise ERP]
    D -.->|Inductive Harvest| F[Tag Power Module\nRectifier + Capacitor]
    F -.->|DC Supply 1.2V| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Solid arrows show **active data/signal flow**; dashed arrows show **power flow** on the tag side (the tag has no external battery — it harvests energy from the reader's RF field).

## 4.2 Process Flow — Tag Inventory and Anti-Collision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Reader Power On]) --> Q[Reader sends Query\nwith initial Q value]
    Q --> R[Tags in field\nrandomly select slot]
    R --> D{Slot Decision}
    D -->|Empty Slot| S1[Reader detects silence\nQ decreases by 1]
    D -->|Single Tag| S2[Reader reads tag EPC\ntag enters Acknowledged state]
    D -->|Collision| S3[Reader detects collision\nQ increases by c-value]
    S1 --> Q
    S2 --> Q
    S3 --> Q
    S2 --> END([Inventory Complete])
```

## 4.3 Frequency Band Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Need] --> B{Range Required?}
    B -->|Less than 1 m| C{LF 125 kHz\nor HF 13.56 MHz}
    B -->|Greater than 1 m| D{Active or Passive?}
    D -->|Passive| E[UHF 860-960 MHz\nRange up to 12 m]
    D -->|Active| F[UHF or Microwave 2.45 GHz\nRange up to 100 m]
    C --> G{Material Penetration?}
    G -->|Through water/tissue| H[LF 125 kHz\nLivestock, Implant]
    G -->|Smart cards/tickets| I[HF 13.56 MHz\nNFC, MIFARE]
```

## 4.4 Tag Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ANT[Tag Antenna\nDipole or Coil] --> RECT[Rectifier Circuit\nSchottky Diode Bridge]
    RECT --> CAP[Storage Capacitor\n1 to 100 microfarad]
    CAP --> REG[Voltage Regulator\nOutput 1.2 to 3V DC]
    REG --> MCU[Tag IC Microcontroller]
    ANT --> MOD[Load Modulator\nASK / PSK switch]
    MOD --> ANT
    MCU --> MEM[Non-Volatile Memory\nEPC 96 bits, TID 64 bits, User Memory]
    MEM --> MCU
    MCU --> MOD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for KTU:** Memorize the **three memory banks** of an EPC Gen-2 tag: **EPC** (Electronic Product Code — the unique ID), **TID** (Tag ID — factory-locked, identifies the manufacturer), and **User Memory** (rewritable, application-specific data).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define RFID. List any four applications of RFID technology.**

**Model Answer:**

**Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** is a wireless, non-contact automatic identification technology that uses radio frequency electromagnetic fields to transfer data between a **reader (interrogator)** and a **tag (transponder)** for the purpose of identifying, tracking, and managing objects, animals, or persons.

**Four Applications:**
1. **Supply Chain Management:** Real-time tracking of pallets and containers in warehouses (used by Walmart, Amazon).
2. **Access Control:** Employee ID badges, hotel key cards, and contactless FASTag toll collection.
3. **Healthcare:** Tracking surgical instruments, patient identification wristbands, and blood bag management.
4. **Library Management:** Automated book issue/return and anti-theft detection in libraries.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for any 2 applications = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare passive and active RFID tags based on power source, range, cost, and lifespan.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Passive Tag | Active Tag |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Power Source** | Harvested from reader's RF field | Internal battery powers the tag |
| **Read Range** | Up to **10 m** (UHF) | Up to **100 m** |
| **Cost per tag** | **$0.05 – $0.50** | **$15 – $100+** |
| **Lifespan** | Practically unlimited | 3 – 10 years (limited by battery) |
| **Tag Size** | Very small (can be paper-thin) | Larger due to battery |

*(3 marks: Comparison table covering 4 parameters with values)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **block diagram and working principle of an RFID system**. Discuss the role of the reader, tag, and antenna in the system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Friis-based maximum read range equation** for a passive RFID tag and explain how the range depends on wavelength, transmit power, and tag sensitivity. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The RFID system consists of the following functional blocks:

**1. Reader / Interrogator:**
The reader is the "master" device. It houses a **microcontroller**, a **transceiver** (transmitter + receiver), and an **RF front-end**. Its functions are:
- Generate a stable carrier signal at the chosen frequency (e.g., 866 MHz for Indian UHF RFID).
- Modulate this carrier with command data (downlink to tags).
- Emit the modulated signal through its antenna.
- Detect the weak backscattered signal from the tag.
- Demodulate, decode, and forward the tag's EPC code to the host system.

**2. Antenna:**
The antenna converts the electrical current from the transceiver into a propagating electromagnetic wave (and vice versa). Its key parameters are:
- **Gain $G_t$:** Directivity of radiation (typical UHF reader: 6 – 8 dBi).
- **Polarization:** Linear (vertical/horizontal) or circular — circular is preferred in RFID because tag orientation is uncontrolled.
- **Beamwidth:** Defines the read zone geometry (e.g., 60° conical).

**3. Tag / Transponder:**
The tag is the "slave" device. It contains:
- A small antenna (coil for HF, dipole for UHF).
- A rectifier to harvest DC power from the RF field.
- An IC with non-volatile memory storing the EPC.
- A load modulator that changes the antenna impedance to backscatter data.

**4. Working Principle:**
- The reader transmits continuous-wave RF energy.
- The tag, upon entering the field, harvests energy and powers up.
- The tag modulates its antenna load (changing between two impedances) to backscatter a coded signal back to the reader.
- The reader decodes the unique tag ID and forwards it to the host application.

*(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for component description, 2 for working principle)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

We derive the maximum read range using the Friis transmission equation.

**Step 1:** Power received by the tag antenna at distance $R$:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2:** For the tag to be activated, the received power must be at least the tag's sensitivity threshold $P_{th}$:

$$
P_r \geq P_{th}
$$

**Step 3:** At maximum read range, $P_r = P_{th}$:

$$
P_{th} = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $R_{max}$:

$$
R_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{th}}}
$$

**Discussion of Dependencies:**

- **Proportional to $\lambda$:** Doubling the wavelength (halving the frequency) **doubles** the read range. This is why LF tags (long wavelength, ~2400 m at 125 kHz) can theoretically achieve longer inductive range, but their huge antennas and low data rates make UHF more practical.
- **Proportional to $\sqrt{P_t}$:** Increasing reader transmit power only yields a **square-root improvement** in range — diminishing returns.
- **Inversely proportional to $\sqrt{P_{th}}$:** Lower tag sensitivity dramatically extends range. Modern UHF tags with $P_{th} = -20$ dBm (10 μW) achieve much longer ranges than older $-10$ dBm tags.

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation + 3 for physical interpretation)*

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** Explain the **different frequency bands used in RFID** (LF, HF, UHF, Microwave) with their typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the **anti-collision problem in RFID**? Explain the **slotted Aloha-based Q-algorithm** used in EPC Class-1 Gen-2 tags with a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

RFID operates in four globally regulated frequency bands, each suited to specific applications:

**1. Low Frequency (LF) — 125 kHz to 134 kHz:**
- **Range:** < 10 cm (very short, near-field only).
- **Wavelength:** ~2400 m (huge!).
- **Properties:** Excellent penetration through water, animal tissue, and wood. Immune to metal interference (in small doses).
- **Applications:** Livestock identification (cattle ear tags), pet implants, car immobilizers, access control badges (older systems).
- **Standard:** ISO 11784 / ISO 11785.

**2. High Frequency (HF) — 13.56 MHz:**
- **Range:** 10 cm to 1 m.
- **Wavelength:** ~22 m.
- **Properties:** Moderate data rate, good balance of range and penetration. Standardized globally (no regional restrictions).
- **Applications:** **Contactless smart cards** (MIFARE), NFC payments (Apple Pay, Google Pay), library books, passports (e-passports), and product authentication.
- **Standard:** ISO 14443 (proximity cards), ISO 15693 (vicinity cards), NFC Forum standards.

**3. Ultra High Frequency (UHF) — 860 MHz to 960 MHz:**
- **Range:** 1 m to 12 m (passive), up to 100 m (active).
- **Wavelength:** ~33 cm.
- **Properties:** Long range, high data rate (40 – 640 kbps), but **regionally regulated** (different bands in EU, US, India).
- **Applications:** **Supply chain management**, retail inventory (Walmart mandate), pallet tracking, toll collection (FASTag in India), baggage handling.
- **Standard:** EPC Class-1 Gen-2, ISO 18000-63.

**4. Microwave — 2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz:**
- **Range:** Up to 100 m (active tags).
- **Wavelength:** ~12 cm (at 2.45 GHz).
- **Properties:** Very high data rate, very short wavelength allows small antenna designs.
- **Applications:** **Active RFID for vehicle identification** (AVI), container tracking, real-time location systems (RTLS).
- **Standard:** ISO 18000-4.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per band, with 0.5 for application)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Anti-Collision Problem:**
When multiple RFID tags enter the reader's interrogation zone simultaneously, they all try to respond at once. Their signals **collide** at the reader, causing a **garbled waveform** that cannot be decoded. This is the **tag collision problem**. It must be solved to enable efficient bulk reading.

**Slotted Aloha-based Q-Algorithm (EPC Gen-2):**

The EPC Class-1 Gen-2 standard uses a probabilistic anti-collision algorithm based on **slotted Aloha**:

1. **Initialization:** The reader broadcasts a `Query` command with an initial slot-count parameter $Q$ (integer, 0 to 15).
2. **Slot Frame:** The time interval is divided into $2^Q$ discrete time slots. For example, $Q = 4$ means $2^4 = 16$ slots per frame.
3. **Tag Randomization:** Each tag in the field generates a random 16-bit number, takes it modulo $2^Q$, and replies in that chosen slot using a 16-bit random number (RN16).
4. **Three Slot Outcomes:**
   - **Single Reply:** Reader successfully receives the tag's RN16 → sends `ACK` → tag transmits full EPC → tag enters "Acknowledged" state and remains silent for the rest of the inventory round.
   - **Collision (Multiple Replies):** Reader detects corrupted waveform → increments $Q$ by a constant $c$ (typical $c = 0.3$ to $0.8$) → next frame has more slots, reducing collision probability.
   - **Empty Slot (No Reply):** Reader detects silence → decrements $Q$ by $c$ → next frame has fewer slots, saving time.
5. **Termination:** The process repeats until all tags are read.

**Theoretical Maximum Throughput:** The slotted Aloha protocol achieves a maximum channel utilization of:

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%
$$

at an offered load $G = 1$ packet per slot, meaning the algorithm is inherently inefficient at high loads, but the Q-algorithm dynamically approaches this optimum.

*(7 marks: 1 for problem statement, 4 for algorithm steps, 2 for flowchart + throughput formula)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "RFID uses barcode scanning" — RFID is **not** a barcode technology; it uses **RF signals**. Students commonly lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse the Friis equation path-loss exponent. The term $\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ uses **square of wavelength**, not $R^2$ alone. Miswriting this gives zero marks for derivation.
> 3. **Do NOT** say "active tags have infinite range" — be precise: typically **up to 100 m**, governed by regulatory EIRP limits (e.g., 4 W EIRP in India for 866 MHz UHF).
> 4. **Do NOT** confuse EPC memory (rewritable in the field) with TID memory (factory-locked, read-only). Many students mix these up.
> 5. **Always mention units** in the formula sheet — KTU examiners award 0.5 marks per correct unit notation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read 5 Minutes Before Exam:**

- ✅ **RFID = Radio Frequency Identification**, a non-contact AIDC technology.
- ✅ **Two coupling types:** Inductive (LF/HF, near-field, transformer principle) and Backscatter (UHF/Microwave, far-field, radar principle).
- ✅ **Four frequency bands:** LF (125 kHz), HF (13.56 MHz), UHF (860–960 MHz), Microwave (2.45 / 5.8 GHz).
- ✅ **Passive tags** = no battery, powered by reader RF, range up to 10 m.
- ✅ **Active tags** = internal battery, range up to 100 m.
- ✅ **Maximum read range formula:** $R_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{th}}}$
- ✅ **Friis equation** governs all passive RFID link budgets.
- ✅ **Anti-collision protocol:** Slotted Aloha with Q-algorithm (EPC Gen-2), $2^Q$ slots per frame, max throughput $1/e \approx 36.8\%$.
- ✅ **EPC Gen-2 memory banks:** EPC (96-bit unique ID), TID (64-bit factory-locked), User Memory (rewritable).
- ✅ **Key standards:** ISO 14443, ISO 15693, ISO 18000-63, EPC Class-1 Gen-2, NFC Forum.
- ✅ **Faraday's law on tag:** $V = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ — the induced voltage powers the tag.
- ✅ **Modulation used by tag:** Load modulation via ASK or PSK on backscattered signal.
- ✅ **Best penetration through water/tissue:** LF (used for livestock).
- ✅ **Best for long-range logistics:** UHF (used in supply chain).
- ✅ **Best for payments and NFC:** HF at 13.56 MHz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WSN Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** WSN Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes (motes)** that use sensors to cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations. Each node in the network is equipped with a radio transceiver, a micro-controller, an energy source (usually a battery), and one or more sensing elements. The nodes communicate wirelessly and pass their data through the network to a central sink or base station (Gateway) for further processing and analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A WSN is defined as a collection of **low-power**, **low-cost**, **multi-functional** sensor nodes that are densely deployed in a sensor field to sense, process, and communicate data over short distances via a wireless medium to a **Base Station (BS)** / **Sink Node**.

### Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus:
- **Sensor Field:** The physical area where sensor nodes are deployed for monitoring.
- **Base Station (BS) / Sink:** The central data collection point that interfaces the WSN with the outside world (e.g., the internet or a control server).
- **Mote:** A single sensor node hardware unit.
- **Sink Node:** A node with enhanced resources (power, memory, processing) that aggregates data.
- **Source Node:** A node that originates the data through sensing.
- **Cluster Head (CH):** A node selected within a cluster to aggregate data from member nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Smart Nervous System of a City"**
> Imagine you sprinkle thousands of tiny, battery-operated "smart dust" devices across a vast forest to detect fire. Each device has:
> 1. **Eyes/Ears (Sensors)** to sense temperature and smoke,
> 2. **A tiny Brain (Microcontroller)** to decide if a reading is dangerous,
> 3. **A Mouth (Radio Transceiver)** to talk to neighboring devices, and
> 4. **A Heart (Battery)** that must last for years.
>
> The devices don't talk to a central tower directly. Instead, they whisper to their immediate neighbors, and the warning message "hops" from one device to another until it reaches the forest ranger's office (Base Station). Because each device is energy-constrained, they take turns being a "team leader" (Cluster Head) to avoid draining any one battery. This entire ecosystem of self-organizing, battery-limited, whispering devices is a **Wireless Sensor Network**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Design Constraints

- **Energy Efficiency:** Since nodes are battery-powered, **energy is the scarcest resource**. Protocols are designed to minimize transmission power.
- **Self-Organization:** Nodes must configure themselves into a network without human intervention (auto-configuration, auto-routing).
- **Multi-Hop Communication:** Due to limited transmission range, data travels through intermediate nodes.
- **Scalability:** The network must support hundreds to thousands of nodes.
- **Data-Centric Routing:** Queries are sent to the *data*, not to specific nodes (e.g., "What is the temperature in Region A?" rather than "Node 15, what is your temperature?").
- **Hardware Constraints:** Limited memory, processing speed, and bandwidth.

## 1.4 Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard WSN Performance Metrics (Board-Exam Frequently Asked):**
> - **Network Lifetime:** The time until the first/last/half of the nodes die.
> - **Energy Consumption per Bit (J/bit):** Critical efficiency indicator.
> - **Latency:** End-to-end delay from sensing to sink.
> - **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR):** Ratio of packets received to packets sent.
> - **Network Throughput (bps):** Successful data delivery rate.
> - **Jitter:** Variation in packet arrival time.
> - **Coverage Area:** The region effectively monitored by the sensors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WSN Architecture and Components

A WSN is typically structured into **three primary components**:

1. **Hardware Components (Sensor Nodes):** The physical devices deployed in the field.
2. **Software Components:** Operating systems (TinyOS, Contiki), middleware, and application software.
3. **Communication Protocols:** The rules governing data exchange (routing, MAC, transport).

### 2.1.1 Internal Architecture of a Sensor Node

A sensor node consists of **four fundamental subsystems**:

| Subsystem | Function | Key Components |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensing Unit** | Acquires data from the physical environment | Sensors + Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) |
| **Processing Unit** | Processes, stores, and manages data | Microcontroller/Microprocessor + Memory |
| **Transceiver Unit** | Provides wireless communication | Radio Transmitter/Receiver (RF), Antenna |
| **Power Unit** | Supplies energy to all other units | Battery (AA, Li-ion), Solar Cell (Energy Harvesting) |

> **Additional Units (Optional but vital):**
> - **Location Finding System:** GPS or localization algorithm to determine node position.
> - **Mobilizer / Actuator:** Allows node movement or physical action (in Mobile WSN).
> - **Power Generator / Harvester:** Solar, vibration, or thermal energy harvesters for extended life.

## 2.2 Classification of Wireless Sensor Networks

WSNs are classified based on several criteria:

### A. Based on Deployment Environment:
- **Terrestrial WSNs:** Deployed on land; high density; used in environmental monitoring, agriculture.
- **Underground WSNs:** Nodes buried underground; used in landslide detection, soil monitoring. Requires careful energy management because RF signals attenuate through soil/rock.
- **Underwater WSNs:** Deployed in water bodies; use acoustic (sound) waves instead of radio. Used in tsunami warning, marine biology.
- **Multi-Media WSNs:** Nodes equipped with multimedia sensors (cameras, microphones); used in surveillance. High bandwidth required.
- **Mobile WSNs (MWSN):** Nodes can move; can dynamically reposition themselves.

### B. Based on Network Topology:
- **Star Topology:** All nodes communicate directly with the BS. Simple, low latency, but BS is a single point of failure.
- **Mesh Topology:** Multi-hop; nodes route data through neighbors. Highly robust but energy-intensive.
- **Hybrid (Star-Mesh):** Combination; nodes form clusters, and CHs communicate with each other.
- **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology:** Nodes are arranged in a tree; data flows upward to the root (BS).

## 2.3 WSN Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the 5-layer protocol stack:

| Layer | Function | Standard Protocols/Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Application Layer** | User interaction, data aggregation, time synchronization | SMP (Sensor Management Protocol), TADAP, SQDDP |
| **Transport Layer** | Reliable end-to-end delivery, congestion control | TCP (rarely used), RMST (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport), PSFQ (Pump-Slowly Fetch-Quickly) |
| **Network Layer** | Routing of data from source to sink | LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion, GPSR |
| **Data Link Layer** | MAC (Medium Access Control), error control, framing | S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 |
| **Physical Layer** | Frequency selection, modulation, signal detection | ZigBee (868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz), Bluetooth, IrDA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Layer Functional Planes:** Alongside the 5 layers, three management planes operate:
> - **Power Management Plane:** Manages node energy consumption.
> - **Mobility Management Plane:** Detects and registers node movement.
> - **Task Management Plane:** Schedules sensing tasks among nodes in a region.

## 2.4 Energy Consumption Analysis

Energy in a WSN node is consumed in three main areas: **Sensing ($E_{sense}$)**, **Processing ($E_{proc}$)**, and **Communication ($E_{comm}$)**.

The communication energy is the dominant factor. For a transmitter to send a $k$-bit packet over a distance $d$, the energy consumed is modeled using the **First Order Radio Model (Heinzelman Model)**:

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & \text{if } d \le d_0 \text{ (Free Space)} \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & \text{if } d > d_0 \text{ (Multipath)} \end{cases}$$

The receiving energy is:

$$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** The 4th-power law in multipath model means that doubling the distance increases transmission energy by 16x. This is the mathematical reason why **multi-hop short-distance transmission is far more energy-efficient** than long-distance direct transmission in WSNs.

The threshold distance $d_0$ is the crossover point:

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Free Space Energy** | $E_{TX}^{fs} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ | Transmit energy (short range) | Joule (J) |
| **Multipath Energy** | $E_{TX}^{mp} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ | Transmit energy (long range) | Joule (J) |
| **Receive Energy** | $E_{RX} = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits | Joule (J) |
| **Threshold Distance** | $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} \,/\, \epsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover between models | meter (m) |
| **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}$ | Signal attenuation in vacuum | dimensionless (linear) |
| **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} G_{r} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Power received at antenna | Watt (W) |
| **Path Loss (dB)** | $PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Path loss in dB (f in MHz, d in km) | dB |
| **Network Lifetime (LEACH)** | $L_{net} \approx \frac{E_{total}}{E_{per\,round}}$ | Total energy divided by per-round cost | rounds |
| **Energy Harvested** | $E_h = P_{src} \cdot \eta \cdot t$ | Energy from source (solar/wind) | Joule (J) |
| **Sleep Duty Cycle** | $D = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Fraction of time node is awake | dimensionless |
| **Cluster Area (Circular)** | $A_c = \pi r^{2}$ | Area covered by a cluster | $m^{2}$ |
| **Node Density** | $\rho = N \,/\, A$ | Nodes per unit area | nodes/$m^{2}$ |
| **Coverage Ratio** | $C_r = A_{covered} \,/\, A_{total}$ | Fraction of area monitored | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU board exams, the path loss equation $PL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d)$ (with $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km) is a **favourite 3-mark question**. Memorize the constant 32.45.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** Monitoring soil moisture, pH, temperature across a farm to optimize irrigation and fertilization.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance in factories by sensing vibration, temperature of motors.
- **Healthcare:** Body Area Networks (BAN) for continuous patient monitoring.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Volcano eruption prediction, forest fire detection, glacier monitoring.
- **Smart Cities:** Smart parking, smart lighting, structural health monitoring of bridges and buildings.
- **Military Surveillance:** Battlefield monitoring, intrusion detection.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Multi-Hop is More Energy Efficient

Let us analytically prove that breaking a long transmission into two short hops saves energy. Consider sending a $k$-bit message over a total distance $2d$ in two scenarios:

**Scenario A: Direct Single-Hop Transmission**
Distance covered = $2d$ (assumed to be in the multipath region for a long link)

$$E_{direct} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot (2d)^{4}$$

Expanding the fourth power:

$$E_{direct} = k \cdot E_{elec} + 16 \cdot k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

**Scenario B: Two-Hop Transmission via a Relay**
Distance covered = $d$ + $d$ (two short links in free-space region, so use $\epsilon_{fs}$ for the transmission leg). Each transmission also costs reception at the relay.

$$E_{2hop} = E_{TX}(k, d) + E_{RX}(k) + E_{TX}(k, d)$$

$$E_{2hop} = 2\left(k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}\right) + k \cdot E_{elec}$$

Simplifying:

$$E_{2hop} = 3k \cdot E_{elec} + 2k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

**Energy Savings Ratio:**

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{2hop}} = \frac{k E_{elec} + 16 k \epsilon_{mp} d^{4}}{3k E_{elec} + 2k \epsilon_{fs} d^{2}}$$

For long distances, the $d^{4}$ term in the numerator dominates over the $d^{2}$ term in the denominator. Hence, $E_{direct} \gg E_{2hop}$, mathematically justifying multi-hop routing as the energy-efficient choice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** Let $k = 2000$ bits, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $d = 100$ m.
> - Direct: $E = 2000(50\times10^{-9}) + 2000(0.0013\times10^{-12})(200)^{4} = 1.0\times10^{-4} + 0.416 = 0.4161$ J
> - Two-hop: $E = 3(2000)(50\times10^{-9}) + 2(2000)(10\times10^{-12})(100)^{2} = 3.0\times10^{-4} + 4.0\times10^{-4} = 7.0\times10^{-4}$ J
> - **Savings: ~594x more energy consumed by direct transmission.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Number of Hops for Minimum Energy

We now derive the optimal number of hops $n$ to cover a total distance $D$ such that total energy is minimized. Each hop covers distance $D/n$, assumed to be in the free-space region.

$$E_{total}(n) = n \left[ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)^{2} \right] + (n-1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}$$

The $(n-1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}$ term accounts for reception at intermediate relay nodes. Expanding:

$$E_{total}(n) = 2n \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} - k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot D^{2}}{n}$$

To minimize, take the derivative with respect to $n$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d E_{total}}{dn} = 2k E_{elec} - \frac{k \epsilon_{fs} D^{2}}{n^{2}} = 0$$

Solving for the optimal $n$:

$$n_{opt} = \frac{D \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}}}{\sqrt{2 E_{elec}}}$$

The corresponding optimal hop distance is:

$$d_{opt} = \frac{D}{n_{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{elec}}{\epsilon_{fs}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** The result $d_{opt} = \sqrt{2E_{elec}/\epsilon_{fs}}$ is a constant independent of the total distance $D$. This means **for any given hardware, there is a fixed optimal transmission range**, and it is the cluster head's job to maintain that range.

## 3.3 Derivation: Network Lifetime in a Hierarchical Clustered Network (LEACH Model)

In LEACH, the network is divided into $c$ clusters. The number of nodes in each cluster is $N/c$. Per round, the energy spent in a non-CH node (one transmission of $k$ bits over distance $d_{toCH}$) is:

$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}$$

The CH node receives data from $(N/c - 1)$ nodes, performs data aggregation (cost $E_{DA}$ per bit), and transmits the aggregated $k$ bits to the BS at distance $d_{toBS}$:

$$E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{c} - 1\right) k E_{RX} + \frac{N}{c} k E_{DA} + E_{TX}(k, d_{toBS})$$

Total energy per round:

$$E_{round} = c \cdot E_{CH} + (N - c) \cdot E_{nonCH}$$

Number of rounds before death (Network Lifetime):

$$L = \left\lfloor \frac{E_{total}}{E_{round}} \right\rfloor$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Routing Protocol Table

| Protocol | Type | Energy Efficiency | Scalability | Data Aggregation | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LEACH** | Hierarchical Cluster-based | High (via clustering) | Limited by clusters | Yes (at CH) | ⭐⭐⭐ Most important |
| **PEGASIS** | Chain-based | Very High | Low (chain) | Yes (greedy) | ⭐⭐ Frequently asked |
| **SPIN** | Data-centric negotiation | Medium (metadata exchange) | Good | No (raw data) | ⭐⭐ |
| **Directed Diffusion** | Data-centric, interest-based | Medium | Good | Yes (in-network) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **GPSR** | Geographic / Greedy | High for dense networks | Excellent | No | ⭐⭐ |
| **Flooding** | Classical | Very Low | Excellent | No | ⭐⭐ |
| **Gossiping** | Probabilistic | Low | Good | No | ⭐⭐ |
| **TEEN** | Reactive, threshold-based | High for event detection | Limited | Yes (CH) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **APTEEN** | Hybrid (periodic + threshold) | Balanced | Limited | Yes (CH) | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Distinctions (Board Exam Favourite):**
> - **LEACH vs PEGASIS:** LEACH uses 1-hop cluster-to-BS; PEGASIS uses chain to BS. PEGASIS saves more energy but has high delay.
> - **TEEN vs APTEEN:** TEEN is purely reactive (only reports when threshold is crossed). APTEEN combines periodic transmission with thresholds.
> - **SPIN vs Directed Diffusion:** SPIN uses 3-way metadata handshake (ADV-REQ-DATA). Directed Diffusion uses interest gradients and reinforcements.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 x 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List the four main components of a sensor node.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-organizing network of spatially distributed, autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively sense, process, and communicate data about physical phenomena to a central **Base Station (Sink)** over a wireless medium. Each node is energy-constrained and uses multi-hop communication to extend network lifetime.

The four main components of a sensor node are:

1. **Sensing Unit** – Comprises sensors and ADC for acquiring real-world data.
2. **Processing Unit** – Includes a microcontroller and memory for data computation and storage.
3. **Transceiver Unit** – Contains a radio transmitter/receiver and antenna for wireless communication.
4. **Power Unit** – Provides energy via battery or energy harvesting mechanisms.

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **Explain the concept of data-centric routing in WSN. How does it differ from address-centric routing?**

**Model Answer:**

In **address-centric (traditional) routing**, queries are sent to a specific node identified by its address (e.g., IP). This is suitable for wired or addressable networks.

In **data-centric routing**, queries are addressed to the *data* itself, not the node. Nodes are queried by attribute-value pairs (e.g., "Temperature > 30°C in Region A"). This is necessary because:
- Sensor nodes are anonymous and densely deployed.
- Node failures are common; querying data ensures reliability.
- Energy efficiency is achieved by suppressing redundant data at intermediate nodes (in-network aggregation).

**Examples:** Directed Diffusion, SPIN (Sensor Protocol for Information via Negotiation).

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

---

### **OR Choice 1: Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a sensor node. List the four main subsystems and the role of each. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) Explain the LEACH routing protocol for WSN in detail. How does it achieve energy efficiency? (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

The architecture of a sensor node consists of four main subsystems as shown in the block diagram below:

```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                 SENSOR NODE ARCHITECTURE                     |
|                                                              |
|  [Sensing]  --> [Processing]  <-->  [Transceiver]            |
|     ^              ^                       |                 |
|     |              |                       v                 |
|  [ADC]        [Memory]              [RF Antenna]             |
|     ^              ^                       |                 |
|     |              |                       v                 |
|     +--------------+-----------------> [Power Unit]          |
|                                       (Battery)              |
|  [Location Unit] ----> [Processing]                           |
|  [Mobilizer] --------> [Physical World]                       |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**1. Sensing Unit:**
- Comprises one or more **sensors** (e.g., temperature, humidity, vibration) and an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
- The sensor produces an analog signal proportional to the physical phenomenon.
- The ADC converts the analog signal into digital form for the processing unit.
- Example: TMP36 for temperature, MQ-135 for gas sensing.

**2. Processing Unit:**
- Includes a **microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex)** and **memory (RAM/Flash)**.
- Performs data processing, local computation, and packet formatting.
- Executes communication protocols and manages the node's energy-aware behavior (sleep/wake cycles).
- Storage: Flash memory for code; RAM for transient data.

**3. Transceiver Unit:**
- Consists of an **RF Transmitter/Receiver**, an **antenna**, and a **modulator/demodulator**.
- Provides short-range wireless communication (typical range: 10–100 m).
- Operating frequencies: 433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, or 2.4 GHz ISM band.
- Modulation schemes: OOK, FSK, BPSK, QPSK.
- Has four operational states: Transmit, Receive, Idle, Sleep.

**4. Power Unit:**
- The most critical unit because sensor nodes are battery-powered.
- Typically uses **AA/Li-ion batteries** with a capacity of 2,200–3,000 mAh.
- May include **energy harvesting** (solar, vibration, thermal) for extended life.
- Power is also supplied to the ADC, transceiver, and microcontroller.

**Additional Units (Optional):**
- **Location Finding System:** GPS or localization algorithms (e.g., RSSI-based).
- **Mobilizer / Actuator:** Allows the node to move (mobile WSN) or perform physical actions.
- **Power Generator / Harvester:** Extends battery life by harvesting ambient energy.

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** is a hierarchical, cluster-based routing protocol designed by **Heinzelman et al. (2000)** to minimize energy dissipation in WSNs. It uses **distributed cluster formation** and **rotation of cluster head (CH) responsibility** to evenly distribute the energy load among all nodes.

**Key Features of LEACH:**

1. **Clustering:** Nodes organize themselves into local clusters. Each cluster has one Cluster Head (CH) and several member nodes.
2. **CH Random Rotation:** Cluster Head selection is random and rotated periodically, preventing any single node from dying quickly.
3. **Data Aggregation:** The CH aggregates data from all members before sending to BS, reducing transmission volume.
4. **Localized Coordination:** Each node makes decisions based only on local information.

**LEACH Operation is Divided into Two Phases per Round:**

**Phase 1: Set-Up Phase (Cluster Formation)**

Each node $n$ generates a random number between 0 and 1 and compares it with a threshold $T(n)$:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot (r \mod 1/p)}, & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $p$ = desired percentage of CHs (e.g., 0.05 = 5%)
- $r$ = current round number
- $G$ = set of nodes that have not been CHs in the last $1/p$ rounds

If the random number is less than $T(n)$, the node becomes a CH for that round.

**CH Advertisement and Cluster Joining:**
- The elected CH broadcasts an **ADV (Advertisement)** message using CSMA MAC.
- Non-CH nodes choose the nearest CH based on **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** and send a **Join-REQ** message.
- The CH creates a **TDMA schedule** and assigns each member a time slot.

**Phase 2: Steady-State Phase (Data Transmission)**

- Each member node transmits its data to the CH only in its assigned TDMA slot.
- The CH aggregates (using data fusion) the received data into a single signal.
- The CH transmits the aggregated data to the BS using a **spread spectrum** technique to save energy.

**How LEACH Achieves Energy Efficiency:**

1. **Multi-hop within cluster and direct CH-to-BS:** Local data aggregation minimizes long-distance transmissions.
2. **CH Rotation:** Prevents the "hot spot" problem where the CH dies quickly.
3. **Sleep Mode:** Non-active nodes turn off their radios to save energy.
4. **Data Aggregation:** Reduces redundancy and the number of bits transmitted.
5. **Localized Decisions:** Avoids global information exchange.

**Disadvantages of LEACH:**
- Assumes uniform energy distribution; not suitable for heterogeneous networks.
- Single-hop CH-to-BS communication is inefficient for large networks.
- Cluster heads selected randomly may result in uneven clusters.
- Not scalable for very large sensor fields.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the "**advantages**" of LEACH and forget the **threshold equation $T(n)$**. This is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**. Also, do not confuse LEACH with PEGASIS — PEGASIS is a **chain-based** protocol, while LEACH is a **cluster-based** protocol. Always state the random threshold formula explicitly when discussing CH selection.

### **OR Choice 2: Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **(a) Explain the WSN protocol stack in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the function of each layer. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) Derive the energy consumption for transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$ using the first-order radio model. Show why multi-hop is more energy-efficient than direct transmission. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

The WSN protocol stack consists of **5 layers** and **3 management planes** that operate across all layers.

**5-Layer Protocol Stack:**

**1. Physical Layer:**
- Responsible for **frequency selection**, **carrier frequency generation**, **signal detection**, and **modulation**.
- Handles **transmission and reception** of raw bits over the wireless medium.
- Common modulation schemes: **BFSK (Binary Frequency Shift Keying)**, **OQPSK**, **DSSS**.
- Operates in ISM bands: 433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz.
- Major challenge: minimizing energy in transmission and reception.

**2. Data Link Layer:**
- Performs **Medium Access Control (MAC)** and **Error Control**.
- Responsible for reliable point-to-point and point-to-multipoint connections.
- Combines data into frames, detects errors using CRC.
- Key MAC protocols: **S-MAC (Sensor MAC)**, **B-MAC (Berkeley MAC)**, **IEEE 802.15.4**.
- Addresses the **hidden terminal** and **exposed terminal** problems.

**3. Network Layer:**
- The most crucial layer for WSNs; implements **routing** of data from source to sink.
- Energy-efficient routing is the primary concern.
- Protocols: **LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion, GPSR**.
- Supports multi-hop forwarding and in-network data aggregation.
- IP-based routing (IPv6, RPL) is also emerging.

**4. Transport Layer:**
- Ensures **reliable end-to-end delivery** of data.
- Provides **congestion control** and **loss recovery**.
- Standard TCP is rarely used due to overhead; WSN-specific protocols are preferred.
- Examples: **RMST (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport)**, **PSFQ (Pump-Slowly Fetch-Quickly)**, **CODA (Congestion Detection and Avoidance)**.

**5. Application Layer:**
- Top layer where the user interacts with the network.
- Provides **time synchronization**, **node localization**, and **application-specific processing**.
- Protocols: **SMP (Sensor Management Protocol)**, **TADAP (Task Assignment and Data Advertisement Protocol)**, **SQDDP (Sensor Query and Data Dissemination Protocol)**.

**Three Management Planes (Cross-Layer):**

**1. Power Management Plane:**
- Monitors and manages **energy consumption** of the node.
- Decides when to power down components (e.g., radio sleep mode).
- Works with all 5 layers to minimize energy use.

**2. Mobility Management Plane:**
- Detects and registers **node movement**.
- Maintains a registry of node positions and their neighbors.
- Critical for **Mobile WSNs (MWSN)** and tracking applications.

**3. Task Management Plane:**
- **Schedules sensing tasks** among nodes in a specific region.
- Balances the workload so that no single node is over-utilized.
- Coordinates with the application layer.

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

```
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              Application Layer                    |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              Transport Layer                      |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              Network Layer                        |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              Data Link Layer                      |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              Physical Layer                       |
+---------------------------------------------------+
| Power Mgmt | Mobility Mgmt | Task Mgmt           |
+---------------------------------------------------+
```

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**First-Order Radio Model Derivation:**

Consider a node transmitting $k$ bits over a distance $d$. The total energy consumed is the sum of **electronic circuit energy** and **amplifier energy**:

**1. Transmitter Energy $E_{TX}(k, d)$:**

The transmitter has two parts: the electronic circuitry (digital processing) and the power amplifier (signal amplification). The amplifier energy depends on distance because of propagation loss.

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{TX-elec}(k) + E_{TX-amp}(k, d)$$

The electronic energy is linearly proportional to bits:

$$E_{TX-elec}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

The amplifier energy depends on the path loss model. For **Free-Space Propagation** ($d^{2}$ loss):

$$E_{TX-amp}(k, d) = k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

For **Multipath Propagation** ($d^{4}$ loss, used at longer distances):

$$E_{TX-amp}(k, d) = k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$$

**Combined Transmit Energy:**

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + \begin{cases} k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d \le d_0 \\ k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d > d_0 \end{cases}$$

**2. Receiver Energy $E_{RX}(k)$:**

The receiver only dissipates electronic energy:

$$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

**3. Threshold Distance $d_0$:**

Setting the two transmission models equal to find the crossover:

$$k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}$$

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Multi-Hop Energy Savings Proof:**

We compare two scenarios for sending a $k$-bit message over a total distance $D$:

**Scenario 1: Direct Transmission (1 hop)**
Distance = $D$, assumed in multipath region:

$$E_{direct} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4}$$

**Scenario 2: Two-Hop Transmission (via 1 relay)**
Each hop covers $D/2$, assumed in free-space region. The relay also incurs reception cost.

$$E_{TX, hop} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2}$$

$$E_{RX, hop} = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

$$E_{2hop} = 2 \cdot E_{TX, hop} + 1 \cdot E_{RX, hop}$$

$$E_{2hop} = 3k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{2}$$

**Comparison:** For large $D$, the $D^{4}$ term in $E_{direct}$ dominates the $D^{2}$ term in $E_{2hop}$:

$$\frac{E_{direct}}{E_{2hop}} \approx \frac{\epsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4}}{0.5 \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot D^{2}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\epsilon_{mp}}{\epsilon_{fs}} \cdot D^{2} \gg 1$$

**Conclusion:** Multi-hop transmission is exponentially more energy-efficient than direct long-distance transmission. This mathematical result is the foundation of hierarchical and multi-hop routing protocols like LEACH, PEGASIS, and Directed Diffusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to forget the **$E_{RX}$** term in the multi-hop derivation. The relay node must *receive* the packet before re-transmitting, which adds an extra $k \cdot E_{elec}$ cost. Omitting this leads to a wrong answer and a **2-mark penalty** in the 14-mark question.
