topics.title: Project Topic: Selection of Hydraulic Cylinders for Specific Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Topic: Selection of Hydraulic Cylinders for Specific Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **hydraulic cylinder** is a mechanical actuator that converts hydraulic energy (hydraulic fluid under pressure) into unidirectional or bidirectional linear mechanical force and motion. It consists of a cylindrical barrel, a piston, a piston rod, and end caps, forming the fundamental linear-motion producing element in any fluid power system governed by **Pascal's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A hydraulic cylinder is a double-acting or single-acting linear actuator in which a pressurized hydraulic fluid acting on a piston produces a force proportional to the effective piston area and the supply pressure, mathematically expressed as **F = P × A**, where the effective area differs on the cap-end and rod-end due to the cross-sectional area of the piston rod.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump" Intuition

Imagine a bicycle pump pushing air to lift a car. Now reverse the idea: instead of your hands pushing a small piston, imagine high-pressure oil (which cannot be compressed) pushing a much larger piston. Because oil is essentially incompressible, every drop of force you put in comes out the other side, multiplied by the ratio of the areas. 

- **Your hand force on a small syringe** = Supply pressure × small area
- **The huge lifting force on a car jack** = Same pressure × large area
- This is exactly how a hydraulic press works, and a hydraulic cylinder is simply a "press" designed for straight-line pushing or pulling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why oil, and not water or air?** Hydraulic oil is used because it is **incompressible** (gives instant, rigid motion), it **lubricates** moving parts, it **carries heat away** from the system, and it prevents rust and wear inside the cylinder.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following constants and standard values must be memorized for KTU 2024 scheme examinations:

- **Standard System Pressure:** Typically **70 bar to 210 bar (7 MPa to 21 MPa)** for industrial hydraulics; mobile equipment often uses **350 bar (35 MPa)**.
- **Standard System Pressure (Low-Pressure):** **25 bar to 70 bar** for low-cost, low-duty applications.
- **Standard Bore Sizes (ISO 3320):** 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 320 mm.
- **Standard Rod Diameters (ISO 3320):** Typically 50% to 60% of bore diameter for standard duty.
- **Bulk Modulus of Hydraulic Oil:** Approximately **1.5 × 10⁹ N/m² to 1.7 × 10⁹ N/m² (Pa)**.
- **Standard Fluid:** ISO VG 32, ISO VG 46, or ISO VG 68 mineral oil.
- **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination), numerical answers expected in **Newtons (N)** for force, **Pascals (Pa)** or **bar** for pressure (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa), and **metres (m)** for dimensions. Always convert mm to m before area calculation.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Hydraulic cylinders are primarily classified as follows:

1. **Single-Acting Cylinder** — Hydraulic fluid acts on one side only; return is by gravity or spring.
2. **Double-Acting Cylinder** — Fluid acts on both sides, providing force in both directions.
3. **Telescopic Cylinder** — Multi-stage nested cylinders for long strokes from short retracted lengths.
4. **Differential Cylinder** — A double-acting cylinder where the rod-end area is less than the cap-end area, producing different forces in each direction.
5. **Cushioned Cylinder** — Includes built-in cushioning at end-of-stroke to absorb kinetic energy.
6. **Tandem Cylinder** — Two pistons in series, providing higher force at the same pressure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Pressure Generation
A hydraulic pump (driven by an electric motor or engine) draws oil from a reservoir and delivers it at a set pressure, governed by the **pressure relief valve** setting.

### Step 2: Directional Control
A **directional control valve (DCV)** routes the pressurized oil to the **cap end** (for extension) or the **rod end** (for retraction) of the cylinder.

### Step 3: Force Generation (Pascal's Law Applied)
The pressurized fluid acts on the effective piston area. Since fluid pressure acts equally in all directions, the force generated is the product of pressure and projected area.

### Step 4: Motion
The piston moves linearly, pushing the rod and attached load. The velocity of motion is governed by the flow rate and the effective area: **v = Q / A**.

### Step 5: Return
In a double-acting cylinder, oil is directed to the opposite side; in a single-acting cylinder, the load returns by gravity or an internal return spring.

## 2.2 Why Effective Area Matters — The Asymmetry Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A double-acting cylinder is **asymmetric** because the piston rod occupies area on one side. Therefore, the effective area on the cap-end (extension) is **greater** than the effective area on the rod-end (retraction). This results in:
> - **Higher extension force** (F₁ = P × A₁)
> - **Lower retraction force** (F₂ = P × A₂)
> - **Faster retraction speed** (v₂ > v₁) because the same flow must fill a smaller area.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Critical)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Extension Force (F₁) | $F_1 = P \times A_1$ | P = Pressure (Pa or N/m²); A₁ = Cap-end area (m²) | Force pushing piston outward |
| 2 | Retraction Force (F₂) | $F_2 = P \times A_2$ | A₂ = Rod-end effective area (m²) | Force pulling piston inward |
| 3 | Cap-end Area (A₁) | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | D = Bore diameter (m) | Full piston area |
| 4 | Rod-end Area (A₂) | $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | d = Rod diameter (m) | Area minus rod cross-section |
| 5 | Extension Velocity (v₁) | $v_1 = \dfrac{Q}{A_1}$ | Q = Flow rate (m³/s) | Speed during extension |
| 6 | Retraction Velocity (v₂) | $v_2 = \dfrac{Q}{A_2}$ | — | Speed during retraction |
| 7 | Time for Full Stroke (t) | $t = \dfrac{L \times A}{Q}$ | L = Stroke length (m) | Useful for cycle-time design |
| 8 | Hydraulic Power (P_h) | $P_h = \dfrac{F \times v}{\eta}$ | η = Overall efficiency | Power at the load |
| 9 | Pump Power Required | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{P \times Q}{\eta_{pump}}$ | η_pump = Pump efficiency | Prime mover sizing |
| 10 | Buckling Load (P_cr) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$ | E = Young's modulus; I = Area moment; K = End condition factor | Critical for long-stroke columns |
| 11 | Cylinder Tonnage (T) | $T = \dfrac{F_1}{10{,}000}$ | T = Tonnage (metric) | Press/Clamp selection |
| 12 | Cushioning Pressure (P_c) | $P_c = P_1 + \dfrac{\rho v^2 A_1}{2 A_c}$ | P₁ = Supply pressure; A_c = Cushion area | End-of-stroke deceleration |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule for Tables:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is forbidden inside table cells. For absolute value or "such that", always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX. Example: $\vert F \vert$ instead of `|F|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydraulic cylinders are the **workhorses** of heavy industry. Their selection impacts:

- **Manufacturing:** Presses, injection moulding machines, CNC clamping.
- **Construction:** Excavators, bulldozers, cranes, loaders.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control actuators, landing gear.
- **Marine:** Steering gear, hatch covers, deck machinery.
- **Automotive:** Lifts, presses, assembly line tooling.
- **Agriculture:** Tractor implements, harvesters.
- **Mining & Drilling:** Long-stroke push-feed cylinders.

The selection is not arbitrary — it is a multi-criteria optimization balancing **force, speed, stroke, duty cycle, environment, and cost**.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Generated by a Double-Acting Cylinder

**Starting Premise (Pascal's Law):** Pressure transmitted through a fluid acts with equal intensity on all areas in contact with the fluid.

**Step 1:** Define the cap-end area. The piston is a circle of diameter D.

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

*Textual Explanation:* This is the geometric area of the piston face, ignoring the rod because the rod is on the opposite side of the piston during extension.

**Step 2:** The force on the cap-end is pressure multiplied by cap-end area.

$$F_1 = P \times A_1$$

*Textual Explanation:* Each unit of pressure acting on each unit of area contributes one unit of force. Summing across the entire piston face gives total force.

**Step 3:** Substitute the area expression into the force equation.

$$F_1 = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi P D^2}{4}$$

*Textual Explanation:* This is the engineering form most commonly used in KTU numerical problems.

**Step 4:** Define the rod-end effective area. The rod occupies area on the rod side, so effective area is reduced.

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

*Textual Explanation:* Total piston area minus the area stolen by the rod (also a circle of diameter d).

**Step 5:** Retraction force.

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = \frac{\pi P}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

*Textual Explanation:* Because of rod area subtraction, F₂ is always less than F₁ for the same pressure.

**Step 6:** The **force ratio** (a key KTU exam quantity).

$$\frac{F_1}{F_2} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$$

*Textual Explanation:* This ratio depends purely on geometry, not pressure. For standard d = 0.5D, the ratio is 1.333.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity from Continuity Equation

**Starting Premise:** Hydraulic fluid is incompressible. Therefore, the volume of fluid entering the cylinder per second must equal the rate of volume swept by the piston.

**Step 1:** Volume swept per unit time = Area × Velocity.

$$Q = A \times v$$

*Textual Explanation:* The piston sweeps a cylindrical volume as it moves; rate of sweeping gives flow rate.

**Step 2:** Solve for velocity.

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

*Textual Explanation:* This is the fundamental kinematic relationship for any hydraulic actuator.

**Step 3:** Apply to extension and retraction.

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}$$

*Textual Explanation:* Since A₂ < A₁, the retraction speed is greater than the extension speed for the same flow rate.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time for a Complete Stroke

**Step 1:** Distance to be covered = L (stroke length).

**Step 2:** Time = Distance / Velocity.

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \times A}{Q}$$

*Textual Explanation:* Multiply both sides of v = Q/A by A/Q to get the time expression directly.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Buckling of the Piston Rod (Euler's Theory)

For long-stroke cylinders, the rod behaves as a column. If compressive load exceeds a critical value, it buckles.

**Step 1:** Euler's critical load for a long column with both ends pinned (K = 1).

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$$

*Textual Explanation:* E is material stiffness; I is the second moment of area of the rod's cross-section.

**Step 2:** For a solid circular rod of diameter d:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

*Textual Explanation:* Standard formula for the area moment of a circle.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the critical load.

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{L^2} \times \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi^3 E d^4}{64 L^2}$$

*Textual Explanation:* This shows that buckling load depends on the **fourth power of rod diameter** but only the **square of length**. Doubling the rod diameter increases buckling strength by 16 times.

**Step 4:** Apply end-condition factor K (typically 0.7 to 2.0 for cylinder rods).

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$$

*Textual Explanation:* K accounts for the degree of end-fixity. A cylinder with both ends pinned has K = 1; fixed-guided has K = 0.7; one-fixed-one-free has K = 2.

**Step 5:** Factor of Safety application.

$$F_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{n}$$

*Textual Explanation:* KTU 2024 recommends n = 3 to 5 for hydraulic cylinder rods.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 100 mm and a rod diameter of 60 mm. It is supplied with oil at 100 bar and a flow rate of 30 L/min. Calculate:
(a) Extension force
(b) Retraction force  
(c) Extension velocity
(d) Retraction velocity
(e) Time to complete a 500 mm stroke during extension

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- D = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- d = 60 mm = 0.06 m
- P = 100 bar = 100 × 10⁵ Pa = 10⁷ Pa
- Q = 30 L/min = 30 × 10⁻³ / 60 m³/s = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s
- L = 500 mm = 0.5 m

**(a) Extension Force:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_1 = P \times A_1 = 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 78{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 78.54 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Retraction Force:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.01 - 0.0036) = 5.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = 10^7 \times 5.026 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}$$

**(c) Extension Velocity:**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0637 \text{ m/s} \approx 63.7 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(d) Retraction Velocity:**

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.026 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 99.5 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(e) Stroke Time (Extension):**

$$t = \frac{L \times A_1}{Q} = \frac{0.5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.854 \text{ s}$$

**Observations:**
- F₁ is 1.563 times F₂ (matches D²/(D²-d²) = 0.01/0.0064 ≈ 1.5625).
- v₂ is 1.563 times v₁ — same ratio, confirming force-velocity trade-off.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reservoir Tank] -->|Suction Line| B1[Hydraulic Pump]
    B1 -->|High Pressure Oil| C1[Pressure Relief Valve Set at 100 bar]
    C1 -->|Controlled Flow| D1[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3]
    D1 -->|Port A Pressurized| E1[Cap End Chamber]
    D1 -->|Port B Vented| F1[Rod End Chamber to Tank]
    E1 -->|Force on Full Piston Area| G1[Piston with Bore D]
    G1 -->|Linear Motion| H1[Piston Rod Diameter d]
    H1 -->|Mechanical Output| I1[External Load]
    F1 -->|Return Oil| A1
    D1 -->|Reverse Position| J1[Rod End Pressurized]
    J1 -->|Retraction Stroke| G1
    I1 -.->|Resistive Load| K1[Deceleration via Cushion]
    K1 -->|Exhaust| A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram follows the **KTU Functional Block Architecture** convention: pressurized flow path uses solid arrows; return flow uses thinner solid arrows. The cushion appears at the end of the stroke, where the trapped oil must escape through a small orifice to decelerate the load.

## 4.2 Cylinder Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Application Requirements] --> B0[Determine Required Force F in N]
    B0 --> C0[Determine Required Stroke Length L in m]
    C0 --> D0[Determine Required Speed v in m per s]
    D0 --> E0[Determine Duty Cycle and Environment]
    E0 --> F0{Single Acting or Double Acting?}
    F0 -->|Gravity or Spring Return| G0[Choose Single Acting Type]
    F0 -->|Bidirectional Force| H0[Choose Double Acting Type]
    G0 --> I0[Calculate Bore D from F equals P times A]
    H0 --> J0[Calculate Bore D from F1 equals P times A1]
    J0 --> K0[Check Buckling for Long Stroke]
    K0 --> L0{Slenderness Ratio within Safe Limit?}
    L0 -->|No| M0[Increase Rod Diameter or Add Stop Tube]
    L0 -->|Yes| N0[Select Standard Bore from ISO 3320]
    N0 --> O0[Verify Cushioning Required]
    O0 --> P0[End Cylinder Selection]
    I0 --> P0
    M0 --> J0
```

## 4.3 Force-Velocity Trade-Off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT[Extension Stroke]
        E1A[Cap end Area A1 large] --> E1B[Force F1 high] 
        E1A --> E1C[Velocity v1 low]
    end
    subgraph RET[Retraction Stroke]
        R1A[Rod end Area A2 small] --> R1B[Force F2 low] 
        R1A --> R1C[Velocity v2 high]
    end
    P1[Same Pressure P] --> EXT
    P1 --> RET
    P2[Same Flow Rate Q] --> EXT
    P2 --> RET
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualizing the Trade-Off:** When the same flow and pressure are used, the extension side has more area to fill (slow but strong), while the retraction side has less area (fast but weak). This is the **fundamental asymmetric behaviour** of double-acting cylinders.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. Why is the retraction force always less than the extension force at the same supply pressure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** is a hydraulic actuator in which pressurized fluid can be applied to either side of the piston, producing force in both extending and retracting directions.

The retraction force is less than the extension force because:
- During **extension**, the effective area is the full piston area $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$.
- During **retraction**, the effective area is reduced by the rod area: $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$.
- Since $A_2 < A_1$ and force $F = P \times A$, the retraction force $F_2 = P \times A_2$ is always less than $F_1 = P \times A_1$ at the same supply pressure P.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any four selection criteria for choosing a hydraulic cylinder for a given application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four key selection criteria are:
1. **Required Force Output** — Determined by load and safety factor; dictates bore size.
2. **Required Stroke Length** — Determines overall cylinder length; affects buckling considerations.
3. **Required Operating Speed** — Dictates flow rate requirement; affects pump sizing.
4. **Operating Pressure and Duty Cycle** — Higher pressure reduces cylinder size but increases sealing and safety requirements; duty cycle affects heat generation and seal life.

*(Acceptable additional criteria: mounting style, environment, cushioning needs, rod end type.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder**. State its industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder consists of:
1. **Cylinder Barrel** — A precision-honed steel tube providing the bore surface.
2. **Piston** — A close-fitting disc that divides the cylinder into two chambers; carries sealing rings (usually polyurethane or PTFE).
3. **Piston Rod** — A hard-chrome plated steel rod connected to the piston, transmitting force to the external load.
4. **End Caps (Head and Cap)** — Close both ends of the barrel; the head end contains a rod seal and wiper; the cap end is closed.
5. **Ports** — Two ports (A and B) for inlet/outlet of hydraulic fluid.
6. **Seals** — Piston seals prevent internal leakage; rod seals prevent external leakage; wipers keep contaminants out.

**Working:**
- In **extension stroke**, pressurized oil enters port A (cap end), pushes the piston outward, and oil from the rod end exits through port B to the tank.
- In **retraction stroke**, the directional control valve reverses flow: oil enters port B (rod end), pushes the piston inward, and oil from the cap end returns to the tank through port A.
- Cushioning at both ends absorbs kinetic energy near end-of-stroke to prevent shock and mechanical damage.

**Applications:** Injection moulding machines, hydraulic presses, excavators, machine tools, mobile cranes, agricultural machinery, and ship steering systems.

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. The cylinder is supplied with oil at a pressure of 120 bar. Calculate:
- (i) The extension and retraction forces.
- (ii) If the pump delivers 25 L/min, determine the extension and retraction velocities.
- (iii) The time required for a 400 mm extension stroke. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- D = 80 mm = 0.08 m
- d = 45 mm = 0.045 m
- P = 120 bar = 120 × 10⁵ Pa = 1.2 × 10⁷ Pa
- Q = 25 L/min = 25 × 10⁻³ / 60 = 4.167 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s
- L = 400 mm = 0.4 m

**Step 1: Calculate areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.08)^2 - (0.045)^2)$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0064 - 0.002025) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.004375) = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Forces**

$$F_1 = P \times A_1 = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 60{,}320 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 3.436 \times 10^{-3} = 41{,}230 \text{ N} \approx 41.23 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Velocities**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829 \text{ m/s} \approx 82.9 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{3.436 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1213 \text{ m/s} \approx 121.3 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4: Stroke Time**

$$t = \frac{L}{v_1} = \frac{0.4}{0.0829} = 4.825 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- F₁ = 60.32 kN, F₂ = 41.23 kN
- v₁ = 82.9 mm/s, v₂ = 121.3 mm/s
- t = 4.825 seconds

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **selection procedure for hydraulic cylinders** for specific industrial applications. Discuss the role of bore size, rod diameter, stroke length, and end-cushioning in this selection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Application Requirements**
- Determine the maximum load (F) to be moved, including any safety factors (typically 1.25 to 1.5 for static loads; 2.0 to 3.0 for dynamic or shock loads).
- Determine the available installation space (mounting envelope, retracted and extended lengths).
- Determine the required stroke length L.
- Determine the required operating speed v and duty cycle.

**Step 2: Choose Operating Pressure**
- For industrial fixed machinery: 100 to 210 bar is standard.
- For mobile equipment: 250 to 350 bar for compactness.
- Higher pressure allows smaller bore (cost saving on cylinder) but increases pump and sealing costs.

**Step 3: Calculate Bore Diameter**
- From $F_1 = P \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, solve for D:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 F_1}{\pi P}}$$

- Round up to the next standard ISO 3320 size.

**Step 4: Select Rod Diameter**
- For standard duty: d = 0.5 D to 0.6 D.
- For long-stroke or high-buckling-risk applications: d = 0.7 D to 1.0 D.
- Verify Euler buckling load with a safety factor of 3 to 5.

**Step 5: Verify Stroke and Speed**
- From $v_1 = Q / A_1$, determine required pump flow.
- From $t = L \times A_1 / Q$, check whether cycle time meets production requirements.

**Step 6: Decide on Cushioning**
- For high-speed, high-mass loads: cushioned ends are essential.
- For low-speed, low-mass loads: cushioning is optional.
- Cushioning length is typically 10% to 15% of stroke.

**Step 7: Choose Mounting Style**
- Common types: tie-rod, welded, clevis, trunnion, flange. Selection depends on load direction and motion geometry.

**Step 8: Select Seals and Materials**
- Based on fluid compatibility, temperature range, and pressure.

**Role of Bore Size:**
Larger bore → higher force and lower pressure, but slower speed and larger installation envelope.

**Role of Rod Diameter:**
Larger rod → better buckling resistance and stronger load handling, but reduces retraction force and increases retraction speed.

**Role of Stroke Length:**
Longer stroke → need to check buckling and column strength; may need stop tubes.

**Role of End-Cushioning:**
Decelerates load at end-of-stroke; reduces shock, noise, and mechanical wear; mandatory for high-inertia loads.

**(b)** A hydraulic press requires a clamping force of 200 kN. The system pressure is available at 160 bar. Select a suitable standard bore diameter from ISO 3320 series. If the rod diameter is 0.6 times the bore, calculate the retraction force. Also, determine the pump flow rate required for a clamping time of 5 seconds over a 200 mm stroke. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- F₁ = 200 kN = 200,000 N
- P = 160 bar = 160 × 10⁵ Pa = 1.6 × 10⁷ Pa
- d = 0.6 D
- t = 5 s
- L = 200 mm = 0.2 m

**Step 1: Calculate Required Bore**

$$A_1 = \frac{F_1}{P} = \frac{200{,}000}{1.6 \times 10^7} = 0.0125 \text{ m}^2 = 125 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_1}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.0125}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.01592} = 0.1262 \text{ m} = 126.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Select Standard Bore**
- ISO 3320 series: 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, **125**, 160, 200, 250, 320 mm
- **Select D = 125 mm** (next standard size above 126.2 mm is 160, but 125 is closer to design; if strict, use 160 mm — both are accepted, document the choice).

For this solution, we use D = 125 mm = 0.125 m to be more economical.

**Step 3: Recalculate Actual Forces with D = 125 mm**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.125)^2 = 0.01227 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.125)^2 - (0.075)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.015625 - 0.005625) = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Retraction Force**

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 0.007854 = 125{,}664 \text{ N} \approx 125.66 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Pump Flow Rate**

$$Q = \frac{L \times A_1}{t} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.01227}{5} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to L/min:

$$Q = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 29.45 \text{ L/min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Selected bore: D = 125 mm (standard ISO 3320)
- Retraction force: F₂ = 125.66 kN
- Required pump flow: Q ≈ 29.45 L/min

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hydraulic Cylinder Selection Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert mm to m before area calculation. Bore 100 mm must be entered as 0.1 m, not 100. Pressure in bar must be converted to Pa (×10⁵) before using in F = P × A.
> 2. **Confusion Between Extension and Retraction Area (–2 marks):** Students often use A₁ for both strokes, leading to wrong retraction force. Always check: rod end uses $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$.
> 3. **Forgetting Rod Area in Volume Calculation (–1 mark):** During retraction, the displaced volume per unit stroke is $A_2 \times L$, not $A_1 \times L$.
> 4. **Not Specifying Standard Bore (–1 mark):** KTU examiners penalize if the calculated bore is not rounded up to the nearest ISO 3320 standard size.
> 5. **No Factor of Safety for Buckling (–2 marks):** For long strokes, ignoring rod buckling checks is a major mark loss.
> 6. **Skipping Free-Body Diagram or Justification (–1 mark):** Even in numericals, brief justification of chosen formula earns full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Pascal's Law** is the foundation: Pressure × Area = Force.
- ✅ **Bore diameter D** determines extension force: $F_1 = P \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.
- ✅ **Rod diameter d** reduces retraction area: $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$.
- ✅ **Standard ISO 3320 bore sizes:** 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 320 mm.
- ✅ **Standard pressure range:** 70–210 bar (industrial); 250–350 bar (mobile).
- ✅ **Velocity** comes from continuity: $v = Q / A$. Retraction is always faster than extension for the same flow.
- ✅ **Cycle time** is governed by $t = L \times A / Q$.
- ✅ **Cylinder selection order:** Force → Bore → Stroke → Rod (with buckling check) → Cushioning → Mounting → Seals.
- ✅ **Single-acting** cylinders have fluid on one side only; **double-acting** have fluid on both sides.
- ✅ **Cushioning** is mandatory for high-inertia or high-speed loads.
- ✅ **Buckling** is critical for strokes > 10 × bore diameter — apply Euler's formula with K = 1 and FoS = 3 to 5.
- ✅ **Telescopic cylinders** are used for long strokes (e.g., dump trucks) where retracted length must be small.
- ✅ **Tandem cylinders** double the force at the same pressure (used in landing gear).
- ✅ **Hydraulic oil grade:** ISO VG 32, 46, or 68 — for viscosity index and lubrication.
- ✅ **Always check** for standard sizing, factor of safety, and buckling before finalizing selection.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Selection of Hydraulic Cylinders for Specific Applications**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Topic: Identification of Robotised Motion and Mapping into Multiple Cylinder Actuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - Industrial Hydraulics, Pneumatics and Automation
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Topic: Identification of Robotised Motion and Mapping into Multiple Cylinder Actuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robotised Motion Identification** is the systematic engineering process of decomposing a complex robotic task (such as pick-and-place, palletizing, or assembly) into its fundamental **kinematic sub-motions** (linear extension, linear retraction, rotation, gripping, releasing). Each identified sub-motion is then **mapped** — that is, assigned — to a dedicated **fluid-power actuator** (hydraulic or pneumatic cylinder/motor). The collective orchestration of these cylinders, governed by a programmable logic controller (PLC) or relay-based sequencing logic, is termed **Multiple Cylinder Actuation**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the project workflow comprises four canonical phases:
1. **Motion Audit** — decomposition of robotized tasks into atomic kinematic events.
2. **Actuator Allocation** — mapping of each kinematic event to a cylinder.
3. **Sequence Synthesis** — design of the temporal control logic (sequential, cascade, or PLC-based).
4. **Circuit Realization** — drafting the electro-hydraulic / electro-pneumatic circuit diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners specifically test the student's ability to (a) draw a clear **Motion-Action Table**, (b) assign correct **5/2 or 5/3 directional control valves**, and (c) draw the **fully labeled circuit** with limit switches, pilot-operated check valves, and pressure relief valves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robotic Orchestra

Imagine a **symphony orchestra** performing a complex musical piece:
- Each **musician** (violinist, flutist, drummer) is analogous to a **single cylinder**.
- The **musical score** is the **motion sequence** required by the robot.
- The **conductor's baton** is the **PLC or relay sequencer** that cues each musician at the precise instant.
- The **concert hall** is the **hydraulic/pneumatic power unit (HPU)** supplying energy to all musicians.

Just as a poorly-timed orchestra produces chaos, a poorly-sequenced multi-cylinder system results in **collision, jamming, or out-of-order motion**. The engineer's role is to be the "conductor" — ensuring every cylinder extends and retracts at the right moment, in the right stroke, and with the right force.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Terminology (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent motions a robot can perform. |
| **Stroke Length** | The maximum linear distance travelled by a cylinder piston. |
| **Pilot-Operated Check Valve** | A valve that holds cylinder position when center/offset is selected. |
| **5/2 DCV** | A 5-port, 2-position Directional Control Valve (most common in sequencing). |
| **Limit Switch (LS)** | A sensor signaling the end of cylinder stroke. |
| **Cascade Method** | A sequencing technique where the last signal of one step initiates the next. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in industry:** Modern manufacturing cells (automotive welding, FMCG packaging, CNC tending) rely on **synchronized multi-cylinder robots**. A misaligned sequence can cause a **20–30 % productivity loss** and severe mechanical damage. Mastery of this mapping is therefore a **core employability skill** for KTU Production/Mechanical engineers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Phase Engineering Methodology

### Phase 1 — Motion Audit (Identifying Robotized Motion)

The robotized task is broken down into a **Motion-Action Table**. For a typical **2-DOF pick-and-place robot** with a gripper, the audit yields:

| Step | Sub-Motion | Type | Direction | Trigger | End Signal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical arm descent | Linear | Downward | Start PB | LS1 (lower) |
| 2 | Gripper close | Linear | Inward | LS1 | LS2 (gripped) |
| 3 | Vertical arm ascent | Linear | Upward | LS2 | LS3 (upper) |
| 4 | Horizontal traverse | Linear | Rightward | LS3 | LS4 (right end) |
| 5 | Vertical descent | Linear | Downward | LS4 | LS5 (lower pos 2) |
| 6 | Gripper open | Linear | Outward | LS5 | LS6 (released) |
| 7 | Vertical ascent | Linear | Upward | LS6 | LS3 (home) |
| 8 | Return traverse | Linear | Leftward | LS3 | LS1 (home) |

Each row corresponds to **one cylinder actuation event**.

### Phase 2 — Actuator Allocation (Mapping)

Each sub-motion is mapped to a specific cylinder:
- **Cylinder A** (Double-Acting, DA) → Vertical arm motion.
- **Cylinder B** (DA) → Gripper open/close.
- **Cylinder C** (DA) → Horizontal traverse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Criteria for Cylinder Type:**
> - **Single-Acting (SA)**: For unidirectional motion under spring return (used only in simple clamping).
> - **Double-Acting (DA)**: For **bidirectional motion under load** — used in **90 % of robotized applications**.
> - **Tandem Cylinder**: For double force output in confined space.
> - **Rotary Actuator**: When the motion is **rotational** (limited angle, e.g., 90°, 180°).

### Phase 3 — Sequence Synthesis (Control Logic)

The mapping is complete only when a **temporal logic** is imposed. KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes three principal methods:

**A) Cascade Method (Step-by-Step)**
- The last limit switch signal of step *n* acts as the **trigger** for step *n+1*.
- Implemented using **relay logic** (electromechanical).
- **Advantages:** Simple, no PLC required.
- **Disadvantages:** Not easily modified; bulky panel.

**B) PLC-Based Method**
- The sequence is programmed in **Ladder Logic (LAD)** or **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**.
- **Advantages:** Flexible reprogramming, diagnostics, networking.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher initial cost.

**C) Pneumatic Timer-Based (for DA + SA mixing)**
- Uses **pneumatic time-delay valves** when physical limit switches are impractical.

### Phase 4 — Circuit Realization

The final circuit includes:
- **Power Unit**: Electric motor + pump (hydraulic) or compressor (pneumatic).
- **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)**: Set at **110 %** of max working pressure.
- **Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL)** unit: For pneumatics, ensures clean, regulated, lubricated air (**6 bar standard**).
- **Directional Control Valves (DCVs)**: One per cylinder (typically **5/2 pilot-operated, spring return or double pilot**).
- **Flow Control Valves (FCVs)**: Meter-in / meter-out to control **cylinder speed**.
- **Limit Switches / Sensors**: Feedback to sequencer.
- **Silencers / Quick-Exhaust Valves**: For noise reduction and fast retraction.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cheat Sheet — Master These Six Equations for the Board Exam.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = Cylinder thrust, $P$ = Supply pressure, $A$ = Piston area | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² | **Force calculation** during extension |
| 2 | $F_r = P \times A_r$ | $F_r$ = Retract force, $A_r$ = Annular (rod-side) area | Same as above | **Retract force** (lower due to rod) |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4}$ | Piston full-bore area | m² | Piston area computation |
| 4 | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left(D^2 - d^2\right)$ | Annular area, $d$ = rod diameter | m² | Rod-side area computation |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $v$ = Piston velocity, $Q$ = Flow rate | $v$ in m/s, $Q$ in m³/s | **Speed of cylinder** |
| 6 | $P_{out} = F \times v$ | Hydraulic power output | Watts | Power required at actuator |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | System efficiency (typically **0.6 – 0.85**) | Dimensionless | Motor-pump sizing |
| 8 | $P_{PRV} = 1.1 \times P_{max}$ | Relief valve setting | Pa or bar | Safety circuit design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** Absolute value bars in formulas are written as `\vert x \vert` — never as `|x|` — to avoid breaking KTU markdown table formatting.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Spot-Welding Robots**: 4–6 synchronized cylinders per station.
- **Bottling Plants**: Filling, capping, labeling — each a multi-cylinder sequence.
- **CNC Tool-Changers**: 3-cylinder sequence (unlock, swing, lock).
- **Aerospace Testing Rigs**: Hydraulic multi-cylinder load application.
- **Agricultural Harvesters**: Pneumatic cutter-bar actuation with sequenced cylinders.

In every case, the **mapping** is the bridge between the **kinematic intent** of the robot designer and the **fluid-power reality** of the actuator engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Thrust Force During Extension

A double-acting cylinder of bore **D** is supplied with fluid at gauge pressure **P**. The piston experiences a force equal to pressure times effective area.

**Starting Point (Newton's 2nd Law for fluid column at piston):**

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{piston}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the geometric expression for circular piston area:

$$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore:

$$F_{ext} = P \times \frac{\pi \times D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3:** Accounting for **friction** and **back-pressure load**, the *effective* thrust is:

$$F_{effective} = F_{ext} - F_{friction} - F_{back} = \left(P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right) - \left(\mu \times W\right) - \left(P_b \times A_r\right)$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically **0.05 – 0.15** for seals)
- $W$ = weight of moving load (N)
- $P_b$ = back pressure (Pa)
- $A_r$ = rod-side annular area

**Step 4:** Numerical Example (KTU Typical):

> *Given:* $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $\mu = 0.1$, $W = 500 \text{ N}$, $P_b = 0.2 \text{ bar} = 0.2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, rod diameter $d = 25 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 4a — Piston area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4b — Annular area:**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.05)^2 - (0.025)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[0.0025 - 0.000625\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.001875 = 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4c — Raw thrust:**

$$F_{ext} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 1178.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4d — Friction loss:**

$$F_{friction} = 0.1 \times 500 = 50 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4e — Back-pressure loss:**

$$F_{back} = (0.2 \times 10^5) \times (1.4726 \times 10^{-3}) = 29.45 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4f — Effective thrust:**

$$F_{effective} = 1178.1 - 50 - 29.45 = 1098.65 \text{ N}$$

> **Result:** The cylinder can deliver approximately **1099 N** of useful force during extension — sufficient to lift a ~112 kg load against gravity (since $m = F/g = 1098.65 / 9.81 = 112$ kg).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder Velocity and Cycle Time

The velocity of the piston is determined by **flow rate divided by piston area**.

**Starting Equation:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 1:** Express flow rate in compatible units. If $Q$ is in **L/min** and $A$ in **cm²**:

$$v \text{ (m/s)} = \frac{Q \text{ (L/min)} \times 10^{-3}/60}{A \text{ (m}^2)} = \frac{Q}{60000 \times A}$$

**Step 2:** Time to traverse a stroke of length $L$:

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \times A}{Q}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**

> *Given:* Stroke $L = 0.3 \text{ m}$, $A = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$, $Q = 20 \text{ L/min} = 20 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Step 3a — Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1697 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3b — Stroke time:**

$$t = \frac{0.3}{0.1697} = 1.768 \text{ s} \approx 1.77 \text{ s}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** For a **8-step sequence**, if each step averages ~1.77 s, the **total cycle time ≈ 14.16 s**, giving a throughput of **~254 cycles/hour**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Cylinder Synchronization Check (Analytical Matrix)

For synchronized motion, the system designer must verify that the **flow demands** of all simultaneously active cylinders do not exceed the **pump capacity**.

Let:
- $n$ = number of cylinders simultaneously active
- $Q_i$ = flow demand of cylinder $i$ (m³/s)
- $Q_{pump}$ = pump flow capacity

**Synchronization Constraint:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \leq Q_{pump} \times 0.85$$

(The 0.85 factor accounts for volumetric efficiency and leakages.)

**Example:** 3 cylinders operating simultaneously with demands $Q_1 = 5 \text{ L/min}$, $Q_2 = 8 \text{ L/min}$, $Q_3 = 6 \text{ L/min}$.

$$\sum Q_i = 5 + 8 + 6 = 19 \text{ L/min}$$

Required pump capacity:

$$Q_{pump} \geq \frac{19}{0.85} = 22.35 \text{ L/min}$$

> **Choose a standard 25 L/min pump** — providing a 12 % safety margin.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Valve Selection

| Application Requirement | Recommended DCV Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Simple extend/hold/retract, spring centred preferred | **5/3 Closed-Center DCV** | Holds load hydraulically, no pump heating |
| Cylinder must hold mid-stroke under load | **5/3 Open-Center** or **Pilot-Operated Check Valves** | Prevents drift |
| High-speed cycling (>60 cycles/min) | **5/2 Pilot-Operated, double pilot** | Fast response, no manual reset |
| Safety-critical (must fail-safe retract) | **5/2 Spring-Return, single pilot** | Spring returns to safe position on air loss |
| Sequential logic with multiple limit switches | **5/2 with roller pilot actuation** | LS directly actuates next step |

## 3.5 The Mapping Logic — Mathematical Representation

The mapping from a **motion set M** to a **cylinder set C** can be expressed as a **bijective function** $f: M \rightarrow C$:

$$f(m_i) = c_j, \quad \forall m_i \in M, \exists c_j \in C$$

**Constraint:** The function must be **injective** (one motion maps to one cylinder) and **collectively exhaustive** (every motion has a cylinder). This is the **Hall's Marriage Theorem** applied to actuator allocation.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, the student must **draw the function as a motion-action table** with arrows, justifying the assignment based on **load, stroke, and speed** requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Mapping Process (Conceptual Flow)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **four-phase methodology** for mapping robotized motion into multi-cylinder actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotized Task Definition] --> B[Phase 1: Motion Audit]
    B --> B1[Decompose into Sub-Motions]
    B1 --> B2[Identify DOF and Direction]
    B2 --> B3[Document Trigger and End Signals]
    B3 --> C[Phase 2: Actuator Allocation]
    C --> C1[Select Cylinder Type SA or DA]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Bore and Stroke]
    C2 --> C3[Map Motion to Cylinder]
    C3 --> D[Phase 3: Sequence Synthesis]
    D --> D1{Control Method?}
    D1 -->|Relay| D2[Cascade Method]
    D1 -->|PLC| D3[Ladder Logic Program]
    D1 -->|Pneumatic| D4[Timer-Based Sequence]
    D2 --> E[Phase 4: Circuit Realization]
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[Draw Power Unit and PRV]
    E1 --> E2[Add DCVs and FCVs]
    E2 --> E3[Integrate Sensors and LS]
    E3 --> F[Final Validated Circuit]
    F --> G[Prototype Testing]
    G --> H{Performance OK?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Deployment]
    H -->|No| B
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — 2-DOF Pick-and-Place Robot with 3 Cylinders

This Mermaid **state diagram** shows the temporal sequence of three cylinders (A: vertical arm, B: gripper, C: horizontal traverse) as the robot executes a pick-and-place cycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Home
    Home: Home Position
    Home --> Descend: Start PB
    Descend: Cyl A Extends
    Descend --> Grip: LS1 Active
    Grip: Cyl B Extends Close
    Grip --> Lift: LS2 Active
    Lift: Cyl A Retracts
    Lift --> Traverse: LS3 Active
    Traverse: Cyl C Extends Right
    Traverse --> Lower: LS4 Active
    Lower: Cyl A Extends Down
    Lower --> Release: LS5 Active
    Release: Cyl B Retracts Open
    Release --> Ascend: LS6 Active
    Ascend: Cyl A Retracts Up
    Ascend --> Return: LS3 Active
    Return: Cyl C Retracts Left
    Return --> Home: LS1 Active
    Home --> [*]: Cycle Complete
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Cylinder Actuation System

This Mermaid block diagram maps the **subsystems** of the complete multi-cylinder actuation setup, showing signal and power flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerUnit[Power Unit Subsystem]
        P1[Electric Motor]
        P2[Hydraulic Pump or Compressor]
        P3[Reservoir or Tank]
        P4[Pressure Relief Valve PRV]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3
        P3 --> P4
    end

    subgraph Conditioning[Conditioning Subsystem]
        F1[Filter]
        F2[Regulator]
        F3[Lubricator]
        F4[FRL Unit]
    end

    subgraph Distribution[Distribution Subsystem]
        D1[Manifold Block]
        D2[Flow Control Valves]
        D3[5/2 DCV for Cyl A]
        D4[5/2 DCV for Cyl B]
        D5[5/2 DCV for Cyl C]
    end

    subgraph Actuators[Actuator Subsystem]
        A1[Cyl A Vertical Arm]
        A2[Cyl B Gripper]
        A3[Cyl C Horizontal Traverse]
    end

    subgraph Control[Control Subsystem]
        C1[Limit Switches LS1 to LS6]
        C2[Relay Panel or PLC]
        C3[Push Button Station]
    end

    P4 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    F4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3 --> A1
    D2 --> D4 --> A2
    D2 --> D5 --> A3
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C2
    C2 --> D3
    C2 --> D4
    C2 --> D5
```

## 4.4 Mapping Matrix — Motion-to-Cylinder Allocation Table (Visual)

This Mermaid diagram visualizes the **bijective mapping** between the 8 sub-motions and the 3 cylinders, including the **limit switch feedback** for each transition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Step 1 Arm Descend] -->|Cyl A Extend| LS1
    M2[Step 2 Grip Close] -->|Cyl B Extend| LS2
    M3[Step 3 Arm Ascend] -->|Cyl A Retract| LS3
    M4[Step 4 Traverse Right] -->|Cyl C Extend| LS4
    M5[Step 5 Descend] -->|Cyl A Extend| LS5
    M6[Step 6 Release] -->|Cyl B Retract| LS6
    M7[Step 7 Ascend] -->|Cyl A Retract| LS3
    M8[Step 8 Return] -->|Cyl C Retract| LS1

    LS1 -->|Trigger| M2
    LS2 -->|Trigger| M3
    LS3 -->|Trigger| M4
    LS4 -->|Trigger| M5
    LS5 -->|Trigger| M6
    LS6 -->|Trigger| M7
    LS3b[LS3 Repeat] -->|Trigger| M8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each `M` block is a **motion step**, each `LS` block is the **limit switch feedback**, and the arrows show the **causal sequence**. Notice how `Cyl A` is reused across **four steps** (1, 3, 5, 7) — a key feature of well-designed multi-cylinder sequencing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Robotised Motion Identification" in the context of industrial automation. List the four canonical phases of mapping robotized motion to multiple cylinder actuation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per key point + 1 mark for listing):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Robotised Motion Identification is the systematic engineering process of decomposing a complex robotic task into its fundamental kinematic sub-motions (linear, rotary, gripping), and mapping each sub-motion to a dedicated fluid-power actuator for synchronized execution.

**Four Phases (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Motion Audit** — Decompose the robotized task into atomic kinematic events (extension, retraction, rotation, grip).
2. **Actuator Allocation** — Map each sub-motion to a specific cylinder (DA, SA, rotary, or tandem) based on force, stroke, and speed.
3. **Sequence Synthesis** — Design the temporal control logic using cascade (relay), PLC (ladder logic), or pneumatic timers.
4. **Circuit Realization** — Draft the complete electro-hydraulic / electro-pneumatic circuit with DCVs, FCVs, PRVs, and limit switches.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 40 mm. The supply pressure is 100 bar. Calculate the extension and retraction thrusts. Take friction force = 5% of the extension thrust.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Piston area (extension side):**

$$A_e = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Annular area (retraction side):**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.08)^2 - (0.04)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[0.0064 - 0.0016\right] = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Extension thrust:**

$$F_e = P \times A_e = (100 \times 10^5) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 50265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Friction force (5 % of extension):**

$$F_{fric} = 0.05 \times 50265 = 2513 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Retraction thrust (net):**

$$F_r = (P \times A_r) - F_{fric} = (100 \times 10^5 \times 3.770 \times 10^{-3}) - 2513$$
$$F_r = 37699 - 2513 = 35186 \text{ N} \approx 35.19 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Extension thrust ≈ **50.27 kN**, Retraction thrust ≈ **35.19 kN**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### KTU 2024 ESE — Internal Choice Pattern (Module 4)

> **INSTRUCTION (Standard KTU Pattern):** Answer **any ONE** full question from the choice. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

A pick-and-place robot is required to perform the following sequence:
1. **Cylinder A** (vertical arm) extends downward to pick the workpiece.
2. **Cylinder B** (gripper) extends to grip.
3. **Cylinder A** retracts upward.
4. **Cylinder C** (horizontal slide) extends rightward.
5. **Cylinder A** extends downward to place.
6. **Cylinder B** retracts to release.
7. **Cylinder A** retracts upward.
8. **Cylinder C** retracts leftward to home.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Construct the complete Motion-Action Table, identifying the cylinder, direction, triggering signal, and end limit switch for each step. Briefly explain the cascade sequencing method.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Motion-Action Table (5 marks):**

| Step | Cylinder | Direction | Trigger | End Signal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | Extend ↓ | Start PB | LS1 |
| 2 | B | Extend → (grip) | LS1 | LS2 |
| 3 | A | Retract ↑ | LS2 | LS3 |
| 4 | C | Extend → | LS3 | LS4 |
| 5 | A | Extend ↓ | LS4 | LS5 |
| 6 | B | Retract ← (release) | LS5 | LS6 |
| 7 | A | Retract ↑ | LS6 | LS3 |
| 8 | C | Retract ← | LS3 | LS1 (Home) |

**Cascade Method Explanation (2 marks):**
- The cascade (or step-by-step) method uses a chain of **relay logic** where the **last limit switch signal of step *n* becomes the trigger for step *n+1***.
- Each step's completion is detected by a limit switch, which energizes a relay coil. This relay's contact then powers the solenoid of the next step's directional control valve.
- **Advantage:** Simplicity and reliability — no PLC required.
- **Disadvantage:** Difficult to modify and debug for complex sequences.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Draw the complete pneumatic circuit diagram (using standard ISO 1219 symbols) for the above sequence. Label all DCVs, FCVs, FRL unit, PRV, and limit switches. Justify the choice of 5/2 double-pilot DCVs.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**
- **Power Unit:** Compressor → FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) → Main line at **6 bar**.
- **PRV:** Set at **6.6 bar** (= 1.1 × 6 bar) for safety.
- **3 × 5/2 Double-Pilot DCVs** (one per cylinder: A, B, C).
- **3 × Flow Control Valves (FCVs)**: Meter-out type to prevent shock loads during extension/retraction.
- **6 × Limit Switches**: LS1 to LS6, mechanically actuated by cylinder rod-end and cap-end positions.
- **3 × Double-Acting Cylinders (A, B, C)** with cushioning at both ends.

**Justification for 5/2 Double-Pilot DCV (2 marks):**
- The robot performs **continuous cycling** (>30 cycles/min), requiring **fast and reliable switching**.
- A **spring-return** DCV would require continuous pilot pressure to hold, wasting compressed air.
- A **double-pilot** DCV is **bi-stable** — once actuated, it holds position until the opposite pilot is pulsed, allowing the cylinder to remain in extended/retracted state with **zero holding energy**.
- Critical for **energy efficiency** in industrial pneumatic systems.

**Circuit Layout (2 marks):** Student must show the complete schematic with:
- FRL upstream of all DCVs.
- Each cylinder with FCV on both lines.
- Pilot lines from limit switches to DCV solenoids.
- Power supply line (red, +24 V DC) and return (blue, 0 V).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the FRL unit** upstream of the circuit (−1 mark).
> 2. **Not setting the PRV** at 1.1 × working pressure (−1 mark).
> 3. **Single-pilot spring-return DCVs** for continuously cycling cylinders (unjustified energy waste) (−1 mark).
> 4. **Missing meter-out FCVs** (causes jerky motion and seal damage in exam valuation) (−1 mark).
> 5. **Pilot lines drawn from wrong port** (left vs right pilot confusion) (−1 mark).
> 6. **No limit switch feedback loop** shown in ladder/relay logic (−1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

A 3-cylinder hydraulic circuit is to be used for a palletizing robot. **Cylinder X** (bore 100 mm) lifts a 1500 kg load vertically. **Cylinder Y** (bore 60 mm) extends horizontally to push pallets. **Cylinder Z** (bore 80 mm) clamps the base. Working pressure = 120 bar.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the extension thrust, retraction thrust, and effective load capacity of Cylinder X. Account for a friction loss of 8% and back-pressure equivalent to 5 bar on the rod side. Also compute the flow requirement for a piston velocity of 0.15 m/s.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Cylinder X geometric data:**
- Bore $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Assume rod diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ (standard 1:2 ratio)
- $P = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_b = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\mu \times W = 0.08 \times F_{ext,raw}$

**Step 2 — Piston area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Annular area:**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.1)^2 - (0.05)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0075 = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Raw extension thrust:**

$$F_{ext,raw} = 120 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 94248 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Friction loss:**

$$F_{fric} = 0.08 \times 94248 = 7539.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Effective extension thrust:**

$$F_{ext,eff} = 94248 - 7539.8 = 86708 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Retraction thrust (with back-pressure loss):**

$$F_{ret,raw} = 120 \times 10^5 \times 5.890 \times 10^{-3} = 70686 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{back} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 5.890 \times 10^{-3} = 2945 \text{ N}$$
$$F_{ret,eff} = 70686 - 7539.8 - 2945 = 60201 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Load capacity check:**
Weight of 1500 kg load = $1500 \times 9.81 = 14715$ N.
Since $F_{ext,eff} = 86708 \text{ N} \gg 14715 \text{ N}$, the cylinder has a **safety factor of ~5.9** — more than adequate.

**Step 9 — Flow requirement for $v = 0.15$ m/s:**

$$Q = v \times A = 0.15 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$Q = 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \times 60000 = 70.69 \text{ L/min}$$

> **Final Result:** Cylinder X delivers **86.7 kN** extension, **60.2 kN** retraction, and requires **70.7 L/min** flow for 0.15 m/s piston speed.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design a PLC-based sequencing program (in Ladder Logic) for the palletizing robot sequence: Clamp (Z) → Lift (X extend) → Push (Y extend) → Lower (X retract) → Unclamp (Z retract). List the input/output address assignments and explain each rung.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**I/O Address Table (2 marks):**

| Address | Type | Device | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | Input | Start PB | Cycle start |
| I0.1 | Input | LS_Z_Extend | Z cylinder clamped |
| I0.2 | Input | LS_X_Extend | X cylinder lifted |
| I0.3 | Input | LS_Y_Extend | Y cylinder pushed |
| I0.4 | Input | LS_X_Retract | X cylinder lowered |
| I0.5 | Input | LS_Z_Retract | Z cylinder unclamped |
| Q0.0 | Output | Sol_Z_Extend | Energize Z extend |
| Q0.1 | Output | Sol_X_Extend | Energize X extend |
| Q0.2 | Output | Sol_Y_Extend | Energize Y extend |
| Q0.3 | Output | Sol_X_Retract | Energize X retract |
| Q0.4 | Output | Sol_Z_Retract | Energize Z retract |

**Ladder Logic Description (5 marks — 1 per rung):**

| Rung | Logic | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `I0.0 AND I0.1` → `Q0.0` (latch) | When Start PB pressed AND clamp confirmed → energize Z extend (already latched). |
| 2 | `I0.1` → `Q0.1` (latch) | When Z is clamped (LS_Z_Extend active) → energize X extend. |
| 3 | `I0.2` → `Q0.2` (latch) | When X has lifted (LS_X_Extend active) → energize Y extend. |
| 4 | `I0.3 AND Timer T1 (2 s)` → `Q0.3` (latch) | When Y has pushed for 2 s dwell time → energize X retract. |
| 5 | `I0.4` → `Q0.4` (latch) | When X is lowered (LS_X_Retract active) → energize Z retract. |
| 6 | `I0.5` → Reset all latches | When Z is unclamped, unlatch all outputs to complete cycle. |

**Interlocking Safety (additional 1 mark):**
- **Anti-coil logic:** Ensure `Q0.0` and `Q0.4` (Z extend and Z retract) cannot be energized simultaneously.
- **Emergency Stop (I0.6):** NC contact in series with all rungs to halt the cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on Ladder Logic Questions:**
> 1. **Missing latching contacts** — students draw momentary logic without holding the output (−1 mark).
> 2. **No interlock between opposing cylinders** (extend and retract of same cylinder) — causes short-circuit risk in exam valuation (−1 mark).
> 3. **Forgetting the emergency stop (E-Stop)** rung (−1 mark).
> 4. **Wrong I/O address format** (e.g., using `I1` instead of standard `I0.0` Siemens notation) (−0.5 mark).
> 5. **Not specifying timer values** for dwell/pause operations (−0.5 mark).
> 6. **No cycle-reset logic** at the end of the sequence (−0.5 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin It! 🚀**

- [ ] **Definition:** Robotised motion identification = decomposition of robot task into atomic kinematic events + mapping to cylinders.
- [ ] **Four Phases:** Motion Audit → Actuator Allocation → Sequence Synthesis → Circuit Realization.
- [ ] **Motion-Action Table:** Always draw this **first** — it is the backbone of any sequencing answer.
- [ ] **Cylinder Type:** Use **Double-Acting (DA)** for 90 % of robotized applications; avoid SA except in simple clamping.
- [ ] **DCV Selection:** **5/2 double-pilot** for high-cycle applications; **5/3 closed-center** for mid-stroke holding; **5/2 spring-return** for fail-safe retract.
- [ ] **PRV Setting:** Always **1.1 × P_max** for safety.
- [ ] **FRL Unit:** Mandatory upstream of every pneumatic circuit.
- [ ] **Flow Control:** Use **meter-out FCVs** to prevent shock loads and seal damage.
- [ ] **Cascade vs PLC:** Cascade = simple + low cost; PLC = flexible + scalable.
- [ ] **Force Formula:** $F = P \times A = P \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- [ ] **Retract Formula:** $F_r = P \times A_r = P \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
- [ ] **Velocity Formula:** $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$; **Time:** $t = \dfrac{L \times A}{Q}$
- [ ] **Synchronization:** $\sum Q_i \leq 0.85 \times Q_{pump}$ for simultaneous cylinders.
- [ ] **Pilot Lines:** Always connect limit switches to the **opposite-side** pilot to enable bi-stable operation.
- [ ] **Interlocks:** **Mandatory** between extend and retract solenoids of the same cylinder.
- [ ] **E-Stop:** Always include an Emergency Stop rung in PLC ladder logic.
- [ ] **Symbols:** Use **ISO 1219** standard symbols — examiners deduct for hand-drawn or non-standard symbols.
- [ ] **Mapping Function:** Treat the motion-to-cylinder mapping as a **bijective function**; justify each assignment with force/stroke/speed data.
- [ ] **Ladder Logic:** Always include latching, timer delays, and reset rungs.
- [ ] **Practical Tip:** In lab, verify cycle time using stopwatch + counter; KTU often asks for **cycle time optimization** in viva.

> **🎯 Final KTU Mantra:** *"Audit → Allocate → Sequence → Realize. Always draw the table first, then the circuit. FRL + PRV + FCV + LS = Full marks."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Topic: Design of Hydraulic or Pneumatic Circuits for Actuation of Multiple Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Topic: Design of Hydraulic or Pneumatic Circuits for Actuation of Multiple Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuit Design for Multi-Cylinder Actuation** is the systematic engineering process of configuring, sizing, and interconnecting fluid-power components — including prime movers, valves, actuators (cylinders/motors), reservoirs, and conditioning units — to deliver coordinated, sequential, or simultaneous motion to **two or more linear actuators** in a controlled industrial application.

In the KTU 2024 project framework, the design activity is expected to demonstrate mastery of:

- **ISO 1219-1:2012** fluid-power graphical symbol conventions.
- **Energy continuity** between pump/compressor source and the work port of each cylinder.
- **Sequencing logic** (cascading, step-counter, relay, PLC) ensuring collision-free, deadlock-free motion of multiple cylinders.
- **Force–velocity–pressure** relationships governed by **Pascal's Law** and **Continuity Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Project Outcome (PO-12):** The student must deliver a working simulation (Automation Studio / FluidSim / Festo Fluidsim) **plus** a design report containing component selection tables, circuit schematic, and cycle-time calculation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **water supply system for a multi-floor building** with independent taps. The pump (prime mover) pressurises water (hydraulic oil / compressed air). A network of valves directs water to whichever tap is open. The **cylinder** is the tap's *user* — open valve → water enters → piston pushes forward; close valve → water exits → piston returns.

Now scale this: instead of one tap, there are **four cylinders** that must be turned ON in a specific order (A → B → A-retract → C → D). The design problem is to choose and wire the valves so that:

- Only one cylinder moves at a time (or synchronised, per the brief).
- The previous cylinder is *fully* extended/retracted before the next begins.
- No two cylinders fight each other (mechanical deadlock).

**In short:** You are designing the *traffic-signal logic* for cylinders using fluid power, where the valves are the signals and the fluid is the messenger.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Pascal's Law (1653):** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. This is the foundational principle for hydraulic force multiplication.
> - **Boyle's Law (1662):** For pneumatic systems, $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ at constant temperature governs compressible flow behaviour.
> - Standard supply pressures: **Hydraulic = 70–210 bar (typical 140 bar)**, **Pneumatic = 4–8 bar (typical 6 bar)**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in B.Tech Projects

| Industry Sector | Typical Multi-Cylinder Application |
|---|---|
| Automotive Manufacturing | Clamping + Drilling + Welding stations |
| Packaging | Filling + Sealing + Labelling + Cutting |
| Machine Tools | Tool change + Clamp + Indexing + Spindle feed |
| Plastics / Injection Moulding | Ejector + Core pull + Clamp + Nozzle touch |

A well-designed circuit reduces **cycle time**, **energy consumption (kWh)**, and **downtime due to valve mis-firing**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Governing Equations

The behaviour of any multi-cylinder circuit is governed by **four core laws**. Each law produces one or more design equations that the examiner will test.

### 2.1.1 Force Equation (Cylinder Output)

The theoretical force a hydraulic cylinder delivers equals pressure multiplied by effective piston area:

$$F_{th} = P \times A$$

The actual (effective) force, after subtracting friction and back-pressure losses, is:

$$F_{eff} = \eta_m \times (P \times A) - F_{friction} - P_{back} \times A_{opp}$$

Where $A$ switches between the **head (cap) end** area $A_c$ and the **rod end** annular area $A_r$ depending on which side is pressurised.

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \quad \text{(cap-end full area)}$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) \quad \text{(rod-end annular area)}$$

### 2.1.2 Velocity Equation (Continuity)

The volumetric flow rate entering a cylinder port must equal the rate of volume displacement:

$$Q = A \times v$$

Where:
- $Q$ = flow rate in **m³/s** (practical: L/min)
- $A$ = effective piston area in **m²**
- $v$ = piston linear velocity in **m/s**

Therefore:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q \times 4}{\pi D^2}$$

### 2.1.3 Power Equation

Hydraulic power delivered to a cylinder:

$$P_{hyd} = F \times v = P \times Q$$

In standard SI units:

$$P_{hyd} \, [\text{kW}] = \frac{P \, [\text{bar}] \times Q \, [\text{L/min}]}{600}$$

### 2.1.4 Pneumatic-Specific Equation (Compressibility)

For pneumatics, the effective force is **not** simply $P \times A$ because the counter-pressure on the opposite side changes as the piston moves. The isothermal cylinder force is:

$$F = (P_1 A_1) - (P_2 A_2)$$

With ideal gas law applied to chambers:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \quad \text{(isothermal)}$$

$$P_1 \left( L A_1 + V_{dead,1} \right) = P_2 \left( (L - x) A_2 + V_{dead,2} \right)$$

This non-linear behaviour is **why pneumatic sequencing is harder** than hydraulic sequencing.

## 2.2 Sequencing Methods for Multi-Cylinder Circuits

When multiple cylinders must move in a defined order, four canonical methods exist:

| # | Method | Principle | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cascade Method** | End-position of one cylinder is the start-signal for the next | ≤ 4 cylinders, hard-wired |
| 2 | **Step Counter (Shift Register)** | Each completed step shifts a bit, enabling the next stage | Long sequences, PLC-based |
| 3 | **Relay / Latching Method** | Electromechanical relays latch signal state | Discrete logic, modular |
| 4 | **PLC / Microcontroller** | Software ladder logic drives solenoid coils | Modern industry, scalable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Project Tip:** Most evaluators award highest marks for the **Cascade Method** because it is graphical, intuitive, and demonstrates physical understanding. PLC-based designs are accepted but must include the full ladder diagram.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation you must memorise for this project topic. **Do not insert vertical pipe characters inside table cells**; absolute values use `\vert`.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cap-end area $A_c$ | $A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | Full piston area |
| 2 | Rod-end area $A_r$ | $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ | m² | Annular area |
| 3 | Theoretical force $F_{th}$ | $F_{th} = P \times A$ | N | Pressure × area |
| 4 | Effective force $F_{eff}$ | $F_{eff} = \eta_m \, P \, A - F_f$ | N | After mech. losses |
| 5 | Piston velocity $v$ | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | Flow / area |
| 6 | Cylinder stroke time $t$ | $t = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{A \, L}{Q}$ | s | Stroke / velocity |
| 7 | Hydraulic power $P_{hyd}$ | $P_{hyd} = F \times v$ | W | Force × velocity |
| 8 | Hydraulic power (alt) | $P_{hyd} = P \times Q$ | W | Pressure × flow |
| 9 | Power in kW | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{P[\text{bar}] \times Q[\text{L/min}]}{600}$ | kW | Engineering form |
| 10 | Pump input torque | $T = \dfrac{P \times D_p}{2 \eta_m}$ | N·m | Pump shaft torque |
| 11 | Pump displacement (vol/rev) | $V_d = \dfrac{Q}{n}$ | m³/rev | Flow / speed |
| 12 | Pump flow rate | $Q = V_d \times n$ | m³/s | Displacement × RPM |
| 13 | Reynolds number (pipe) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | – | Flow regime check |
| 14 | Pressure drop (laminar) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^4}$ | Pa | Hagen-Poiseuille |
| 15 | Pneumatic isothermal | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Pa·m³ | Boyle's law |
| 16 | Force ratio (cylinder) | $k = \dfrac{A_c}{A_r} = \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$ | – | Extension/retraction ratio |
| 17 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{F_{eff} L}{P \, A \, L}$ | – | Energy retained |
| 18 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theory}}$ | – | Pump leakage factor |
| 19 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v$ | – | Pump combined |
| 20 | Tank sizing rule | $V_{tank} \geq 2 \times Q_{pump} \times t_{cycle}$ | m³ | Reservoir capacity |

## 2.4 Component Selection Logic Flow

A **hydraulic / pneumatic circuit** is only as good as the weakest component. The design sequence is:

1. **Define load:** Required force $F$, stroke $L$, speed $v$, duty cycle.
2. **Choose system pressure $P$:** Higher pressure → smaller cylinder bore, but thicker walls and seals.
3. **Compute bore $D$** from force requirement.
4. **Compute flow $Q$** from speed requirement.
5. **Size pump / compressor** = $\eta$-adjusted.
6. **Select valves** rated for $P$ and $Q$.
7. **Select reservoir / receiver tank.**
8. **Verify pressure drop** in lines ≤ 5 % of supply pressure.
9. **Lay out circuit** (ISO 1219 symbols).
10. **Simulate and iterate.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Bore from Required Force

**Problem Setup:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder must push a load of $F_L = 25{,}000$ N during extension. System pressure is $P = 140$ bar. Mechanical efficiency of cylinder $\eta_m = 0.90$. Find the required piston bore $D$. Assume rod diameter $d = 0.5 D$ (a typical design ratio).

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to SI units:**

$$P = 140 \text{ bar} \times 10^5 \text{ Pa/bar} = 14 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 14 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Effective force equation during extension:**

During extension, pressure acts on the cap end ($A_c$):

$$F_{eff} = \eta_m \times P \times A_c - F_{friction}$$

Neglecting friction for first iteration:

$$F_L = \eta_m \times P \times A_c$$

**Step 3 — Solve for area $A_c$:**

$$A_c = \frac{F_L}{\eta_m \times P} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.90 \times 14 \times 10^6}$$

$$A_c = \frac{25{,}000}{12.6 \times 10^6} = 1.984 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Express area in terms of bore:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

$$D^2 = \frac{4 A_c}{\pi} = \frac{4 \times 1.984 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi} = 2.526 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$D = \sqrt{2.526 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.05026 \text{ m} \approx 50.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Standardise to nearest ISO bore size:**

The next standard ISO 3320 bore is **50 mm**. Re-compute effective area:

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Recompute force capability:**

$$F_{eff} = \eta_m \times P \times A_c = 0.90 \times 14 \times 10^6 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{eff} = 24{,}750 \text{ N} = 24.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8 — Verdict on load capability:**

Since $F_{eff} = 24{,}750 \text{ N} < F_L = 25{,}000 \text{ N}$ by 1 %, the design is borderline. **Recommendation:** upgrade to the next standard bore, **63 mm**.

**Step 9 — Repeat for 63 mm bore:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.063)^2 = 3.117 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{eff} = 0.90 \times 14 \times 10^6 \times 3.117 \times 10^{-3} = 39{,}276 \text{ N} \approx 39.3 \text{ kN}$$

This gives a **57 % safety margin** — accepted for industrial design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycle-Time Computation for a Two-Cylinder Cascade

**Problem Setup:** Two cylinders A and B must operate in sequence: A⁺ → A⁻ → B⁺ → B⁻. Cylinder data:

- Cylinder A: bore $D_A = 50$ mm, rod $d_A = 30$ mm, stroke $L_A = 200$ mm.
- Cylinder B: bore $D_B = 63$ mm, rod $d_B = 40$ mm, stroke $L_B = 300$ mm.
- Pump flow $Q = 20$ L/min (constant, single pump, sequential via DCVs).

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**

$$A_{cA} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 19.635 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{rA} = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.050)^2 - (0.030)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0025 - 0.0009) = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 12.566 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{cB} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.063)^2 = 3.117 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 31.17 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{rB} = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.063)^2 - (0.040)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.003969 - 0.001600) = 1.863 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 18.63 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert flow to SI:**

$$Q = 20 \text{ L/min} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Time for A⁺ (extension, cap-end pressurised):**

$$t_{A+} = \frac{A_{cA} \times L_A}{Q} = \frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.200}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.178 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Time for A⁻ (retraction, rod-end pressurised):**

$$t_{A-} = \frac{A_{rA} \times L_A}{Q} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.200}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.754 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Time for B⁺:**

$$t_{B+} = \frac{A_{cB} \times L_B}{Q} = \frac{3.117 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.300}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.805 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Time for B⁻:**

$$t_{B-} = \frac{A_{rB} \times L_B}{Q} = \frac{1.863 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.300}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.677 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7 — Total cycle time (excluding dwell and valve-shift lag):**

$$T_{cycle} = t_{A+} + t_{A-} + t_{B+} + t_{B-} = 1.178 + 0.754 + 2.805 + 1.677 = 6.414 \text{ s}$$

**Step 8 — Add 10 % allowance for valve switching + dead time:**

$$T_{cycle,\text{actual}} = 1.10 \times 6.414 = 7.055 \text{ s} \approx 7.06 \text{ s}$$

**Step 9 — Parts-per-hour throughput (assuming automatic restart):**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{3600 \text{ s/hr}}{7.06 \text{ s/cycle}} = 510 \text{ cycles/hr}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pneumatic Force Curve (Compressibility)

**Problem Setup:** A single-acting pneumatic cylinder (spring-return) is supplied at $P_s = 6$ bar gauge. Bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 100$ mm. Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar. Both ends have a **dead volume** of $V_{d1} = V_{d2} = 20$ cm³. Find the **initial force** when $x = 0$ and the **final force** when $x = L$.

**Step 1 — Convert all pressures to absolute (Pa):**

$$P_s = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \text{ bar} = 7.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute area (cap end only, since single-acting):**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Initial state (x = 0):**

The supply pressure acts on the cap end, and atmospheric pressure acts on the rod side (vented).

$$F_0 = P_s \times A_c - P_{atm} \times A_r$$

Assume $A_r \approx A_c$ (small rod):

$$F_0 = (P_s - P_{atm}) \times A_c = (7.013 - 1.013) \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_0 = 6.0 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 3{,}016 \text{ N} \approx 3.02 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Final state (x = L = 100 mm):**

The trapped air on the rod side is compressed from $V_{d2} = 20$ cm³ to $V_{d2}' = V_{d2} - A_c \times L$ … but actually the rod side **increases** in volume (the rod retracts, leaving more space). Apply Boyle's Law:

$$P_2' = P_{atm} \times \frac{V_{d2} + A_c \times L}{V_{d2}}$$

Wait — the supply continues feeding the cap end, so cap-end stays at $P_s$. The rod-side back-pressure drops. Effective force increases slightly.

$$P_2' = 1.013 \times 10^5 \times \frac{20 \times 10^{-6} + 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.100}{20 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P_2' = 1.013 \times 10^5 \times \frac{20 \times 10^{-6} + 502.7 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \times 26.13$$

$$P_2' = 26.47 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 26.47 \text{ bar (absolute)}$$

**Step 5 — Force at end of stroke:**

$$F_L = P_s \times A_c - P_2' \times A_r \approx (7.013 - 26.47) \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$$

The result is **negative** — this means the back-pressure exceeded supply pressure. In reality, the rod-side is vented to atmosphere, so the back-pressure is at most $P_{atm}$ if vented. This is a textbook simplification. **The key learning is that pneumatic force is non-constant along the stroke**, unlike hydraulics.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sequencing Methods

| Criterion | Cascade | Step Counter | Relay | PLC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinders supported | ≤ 4 | 5–16 | 3–8 | Unlimited |
| Wiring complexity | High (many limit switches) | Medium | High | Low (software) |
| Cost (low volume) | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| Cost (high volume) | High | High | High | Low |
| Fault diagnosis | Easy (visual) | Medium | Hard | Easy (HMIs) |
| Modification difficulty | High | Medium | High | Trivial (software) |
| KTU examiner preference | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |

---

**Reading the diagram:** The electric motor drives a gear pump that draws oil from the reservoir through a strainer. The pressure relief valve caps the system at 140 bar. The 4×3 DCV (4 ports, 3 positions) routes pressurised oil — via individual flow-control valves — to any of four cylinders. Each cylinder reports its end-position back to a **sequence controller** (cascade logic in this case), which decides which DCV coil to energise next.
