| **Annual OPEX (₹)** | 80,000 | 35,000 | **IoT (–56.3%)** |
| **5-yr TCO (₹)** | 7,53,263 | 4,07,678 | **IoT (–45.9%)** |
| **NPV (₹)** | — | +3,45,585 | **IoT** |
| **Payback Period** | — | < 1 year | **IoT** |
| **5-yr ROI (%)** | — | 145.45% | **IoT** |
| **Remote Monitoring** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Mobile App) | **IoT** |
| **Scalability** | Low (re-wiring) | High (plug & play) | **IoT** |
| **Data Analytics** | ❌ Manual logs | ✅ Cloud dashboards | **IoT** |
| **Initial Skill Required** | Electrician | Embedded + Cloud | Conventional |
| **Failure Diagnosis** | Difficult (wired) | Easy (telemetry) | **IoT** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Automation System — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Environment\nTemperature, Humidity,\nLight, Motion, Gas] --> B[Sensor Layer\nDHT22, LDR, PIR, MQ-135]
    B --> C[Microcontroller Layer\nESP32 with Wi-Fi BLE]
    C --> D[Communication Layer\nMQTT over TCP/IP]
    D --> E[Cloud Platform\nThingSpeak / Firebase]
    E --> F[Application Layer\nMobile App / Web Dashboard]
    F --> G[User Decision\nThreshold Alerts]
    G --> C
    C --> H[Actuator Layer\n5V Relay, SSR, Servo]
    H --> I[Physical Action\nLamp, Motor, Valve, Alarm]
    I --> A
    J[Conventional System\nWired Relay + Timer] -.-> K[Cost Analysis Module\nTCO, NPV, ROI]
    E -.-> K
    K --> L[Project Report\nComparison Conclusion]
```

## 4.2 Cost Comparison Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Identify Use-Case] --> A2[Bill of Materials\nConventional]
    A1 --> A3[Bill of Materials\nIoT]
    A2 --> A4[CAPEX + OPEX\nConventional]
    A3 --> A5[CAPEX + OPEX\nIoT]
    A4 --> A6[Apply TCO Formula]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Compute NPV and ROI]
    A7 --> A8{Is IoT\nEconomically\nViable?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Recommend IoT\nDeployment]
    A8 -- No --> A10[Recommend Hybrid\nIoT + Conventional]
```

## 4.3 MQTT Communication Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SensorNode as ESP32 Sensor Node
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker
    participant Cloud as Cloud Dashboard
    participant User as End User
    participant Actuator as Relay Actuator

    SensorNode->>Broker: PUBLISH sensor/data
    Broker->>Cloud: Forward telemetry
    Cloud->>User: Display + Push Alert
    User->>Cloud: Set threshold / override
    Cloud->>Broker: PUBLISH control/cmd
    Broker->>Actuator: Forward command
    Actuator->>SensorNode: Physical state changes
    SensorNode->>Broker: PUBLISH new state
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Choose IoT over Conventional

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Need for Automation?] --> Q1{Number of\nNodes > 10?}
    Q1 -- No --> Conv1[Use Conventional\nRelay Logic]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need Remote\nMonitoring?}
    Q2 -- No --> Conv2[Use Conventional\nwith PLC]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Wireless\nDeployment\nFeasible?}
    Q3 -- No --> Hybrid[Hybrid Wired-IoT\nSolution]
    Q3 -- Yes --> IoT[Adopt IoT\nAutomation]
    IoT --> Cost[Validate TCO,\nNPV > 0]
    Cost --> Deploy[Proceed to\nPrototype Build]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define IoT-based automation. List any four components used in a typical IoT automation system.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Model Answer:**
IoT-based automation is the integration of sensors, microcontrollers, communication networks, and cloud platforms to monitor and control physical processes remotely with minimal human intervention.

Four components:
1. **Sensors** (e.g., DHT22, PIR, LDR) — Perception layer
2. **Microcontroller** (e.g., ESP32) — Processing layer
3. **Communication Module** (Wi-Fi, MQTT) — Network layer
4. **Cloud Platform** (e.g., ThingSpeak) — Application layer

---

### Q2. What is Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)? Write its formula.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**Model Answer:**
TCO is the comprehensive cost of acquiring, operating, and maintaining a system over its entire useful life, expressed in present value.

$$
\text{TCO} = \text{CAPEX} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{\text{OPEX}_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

Where $r$ = discount rate, $N$ = project life in years, OPEX$_t$ = operating cost in year $t$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to discount OPEX** — Students often sum raw OPEX without applying $(1+r)^t$. This inflates TCO and leads to wrong ROI/NPV. **Always show the discount factor explicitly.**
> 2. **Mixing CAPEX and OPEX in NPV** — NPV must include *all* cash flows (positive savings and negative costs) for each year.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — Mention ₹, kWh, years explicitly; KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguous units.
> 4. **No sensitivity analysis** — Toppers vary $r$ between 8–12% and re-compute NPV. This earns **extra 1–2 marks** for demonstrating analytical depth.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Design Approach

#### (a) Design an IoT-based smart irrigation system for a 1-acre KTU campus test farm. Specify the complete architecture, component list, and communication protocol. Justify your design choices. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3-tier):**
1. **Perception:** Soil moisture sensor (capacitive v1.2), DHT22 (temp/humidity), rain-detector module.
2. **Processing:** ESP32 (₹350) with on-board Wi-Fi, programmed via Arduino IDE.
3. **Application:** Blynk IoT mobile app (free tier) + ThingSpeak for analytics.

**Component List with Costs:**

| Component | Qty | Unit Cost (₹) | Total (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESP32 DevKit V1 | 1 | 350 | 350 |
| Soil Moisture Sensor | 4 | 130 | 520 |
| DHT22 | 1 | 250 | 250 |
| 12V Solenoid Valve | 1 | 850 | 850 |
| 5V Relay Module | 1 | 60 | 60 |
| 12V 2A Adapter | 1 | 220 | 220 |
| Waterproof Enclosure | 1 | 350 | 350 |
| Wiring + PVC Conduit | — | — | 400 |
| **Total CAPEX** | | | **3,000** |

**Communication:** **MQTT over Wi-Fi** (publisher: ESP32; broker: HiveMQ public; subscriber: Blynk app).

**Justification:**
- ESP32 chosen over Arduino for native Wi-Fi, reducing BOM cost by ₹250 and PCB complexity.
- Capacitive soil sensor (vs. resistive) avoids corrosion, reducing maintenance OPEX by ~40% annually.
- MQTT reduces data payload by 60% vs. HTTP, extending battery life if solar-powered.

#### (b) Compute the 5-year TCO of this IoT system and compare with a conventional timer-based irrigation system. Assume CAPEX_conv = ₹6,500, OPEX_conv = ₹1,800/year, OPEX_IoT = ₹600/year, r = 10%. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute TCO of Conventional System**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{Conv}} &= 6{,}500 + \frac{1{,}800}{1.10} + \frac{1{,}800}{1.21} + \frac{1{,}800}{1.331} + \frac{1{,}800}{1.4641} + \frac{1{,}800}{1.61051} \\
&= 6{,}500 + 1{,}636.36 + 1{,}487.60 + 1{,}352.37 + 1{,}229.42 + 1{,}117.66 \\
&= 6{,}500 + 6{,}823.41 = \mathbf{13{,}323.41 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute TCO of IoT System**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{IoT}} &= 3{,}000 + \frac{600}{1.10} + \frac{600}{1.21} + \frac{600}{1.331} + \frac{600}{1.4641} + \frac{600}{1.61051} \\
&= 3{,}000 + 545.45 + 495.87 + 450.79 + 409.81 + 372.55 \\
&= 3{,}000 + 2{,}274.47 = \mathbf{5{,}274.47 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: NPV of Savings**

Annual Savings = ₹1,800 − ₹600 = ₹1,200

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{savings}} &= \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{1{,}200}{(1.10)^t} \\
&= 1{,}090.91 + 991.74 + 901.58 + 819.62 + 745.11 \\
&= \mathbf{4{,}548.96 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net Economic Advantage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= (\text{CAPEX}_{\text{Conv}} - \text{CAPEX}_{\text{IoT}}) + \text{NPV}_{\text{savings}} \\
&= (6{,}500 - 3{,}000) + 4{,}548.96 \\
&= 3{,}500 + 4{,}548.96 = \mathbf{8{,}048.96 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: ROI**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{4{,}548.96 + 3{,}500}{3{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{268.30\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** The IoT system achieves a 5-year TCO saving of ₹8,048.94 (60.4% reduction) with an ROI of 268.30% over the conventional timer-based system.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Compare conventional SCADA-based motor control with IoT-based motor monitoring. Draw a block diagram of both architectures and list four advantages of the IoT approach. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conventional SCADA | IoT-Based Motor Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | PLC + HMI + RTU + Master Station | Sensor + ESP32 + Cloud + Mobile App |
| **Wiring** | Extensive shielded cables | Wireless (Wi-Fi/LoRa) |
| **Data Logging** | Local historian server | Cloud (AWS, ThingSpeak) |
| **Remote Access** | VPN only | Anywhere via 4G/Wi-Fi |
| **Cost for 10 motors** | ₹3,50,000 | ₹90,000 |
| **Scalability** | Limited by PLC I/O | Unlimited (cloud-native) |

**Block Diagram of IoT Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[3-Phase Motor 1] --> CS1[Current Sensor\nACS712]
    M2[Motor 2] --> CS2[Current Sensor]
    CS1 --> ESP[ESP32 Gateway]
    CS2 --> ESP
    ESP --> WIFI[Wi-Fi Router]
    WIFI --> CLOUD[Cloud Dashboard\nGrafana + InfluxDB]
    CLOUD --> MOBILE[Operator Mobile App]
    CLOUD --> ALERT[Email / SMS Alert]
    ESP --> RELAY[Contactor via Relay]
    RELAY --> M1
    RELAY --> M2
```

**Four Advantages of IoT:**
1. **Reduced cabling cost** (up to 60% saving on copper cabling).
2. **Predictive maintenance** via vibration + current analytics (downtime reduction).
3. **Energy optimization** through real-time load profiling.
4. **Cloud-based data retention** for 5+ years, supporting compliance audits.

#### (b) A factory has 20 motors. The conventional protection system costs ₹25,000/motor with ₹1,200/year maintenance per motor. The IoT alternative costs ₹9,500/motor with ₹300/year maintenance per motor and additionally generates ₹1,800/motor/year in energy savings. Compute 5-year TCO, NPV, and ROI at r = 10%. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Total CAPEX:**
- Conventional: 20 × 25,000 = ₹5,00,000
- IoT: 20 × 9,500 = ₹1,90,000

**Total Annual OPEX (excluding energy savings for conventional):**
- Conventional: 20 × 1,200 = ₹24,000/year
- IoT: 20 × 300 = ₹6,000/year

**Annual Cash Inflow from IoT (savings):**
- OPEX Savings: 24,000 − 6,000 = ₹18,000
- Energy Savings: 20 × 1,800 = ₹36,000
- **Total Annual Savings = ₹54,000**

**Step 1: TCO Conventional**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{Conv}} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{24{,}000}{(1.10)^t} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 21{,}818.18 + 19{,}834.71 + 18{,}031.56 + 16{,}392.32 + 14{,}902.11 \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}978.88 = \mathbf{5{,}90{,}978.88 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: TCO IoT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO}_{\text{IoT}} &= 1{,}90{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{6{,}000}{(1.10)^t} \\
&= 1{,}90{,}000 + 5{,}454.55 + 4{,}958.68 + 4{,}507.89 + 4{,}098.08 + 3{,}725.53 \\
&= 1{,}90{,}000 + 22{,}744.73 = \mathbf{2{,}12{,}744.73 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: NPV of Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_{\text{savings}} &= \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{54{,}000}{(1.10)^t} \\
&= 49{,}090.91 + 44{,}628.10 + 40{,}571.00 + 36{,}882.73 + 33{,}529.75 \\
&= \mathbf{2{,}04{,}702.49 \text{ INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net Economic Benefit**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = (5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}90{,}000) + 2{,}04{,}702.49 = 3{,}10{,}000 + 2{,}04{,}702.49 = \mathbf{5{,}14{,}702.49 \text{ INR}}
$$

**Step 5: ROI**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{2{,}04{,}702.49 + 3{,}10{,}000}{1{,}90{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{270.37\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** IoT system yields a 5-year economic advantage of ₹5,14,702.49 (TCO reduction of ~64%) and an exceptional ROI of 270.37%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **IoT = Sensors + Microcontroller + Communication + Cloud** (4-layer architecture)
- ✅ **TCO** = CAPEX + Discounted OPEX over project lifetime $N$
- ✅ **Default parameters** in KTU project reports: $r = 10\%$, $N = 5$ years
- ✅ **MQTT** is preferred over HTTP for low-power IoT nodes
- ✅ **ESP32** is the KTU-favored MCU over Arduino Uno for Wi-Fi capability
- ✅ **CAPEX savings** often arise from reduced wiring/conduit even before operational savings
- ✅ **OPEX savings** come from auto-off, predictive maintenance, and energy optimization
- ✅ **NPV > 0** → IoT economically justified
- ✅ **Payback Period** of 0 years occurs when IoT CAPEX < Conventional CAPEX
- ✅ **Always present cost in INR** with vendor references (Robu, Amazon, Quartz)
- ✅ **Include sensitivity analysis** for $r$ varying 8–12% to earn topper marks
- ✅ **Document firmware, BOM, circuit diagram, and cloud screenshots** in the project report appendix
- ✅ **Conventional SCADA/Relay** still wins for ultra-low-node (< 5) and high-EMI environments
- ✅ **Hybrid systems** (wired backbone + IoT monitoring) are often the practical KTU-approved optimum
- ✅ **Final verdict in project conclusion** must be quantitative: state TCO difference, NPV, and ROI in a single summary paragraph

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 — IoT Automation & Cost Analysis Notes**
> *For KTU project viva, prepare a 3-minute pitch covering: use-case, BOM table, TCO comparison table, NPV/ROI result, and a working prototype photograph.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Topic: IT Enabled Automation of Machine Operation Leading to Unmanned Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET604 - PBPET604
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Topic: IT Enabled Automation of Machine Operation Leading to Unmanned Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**IT-Enabled Automation of Machine Operation** is defined as the integration of Information Technology (IT) infrastructure — including computer hardware, embedded controllers, communication networks, sensors, actuators, and intelligent software algorithms — with conventional mechanical and electromechanical machinery to achieve a state of **Unmanned Operation** (also termed "Lights-Out Manufacturing" or "Dark Factory Operation").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: *Unmanned Operations* refers to a production/manufacturing paradigm in which machines execute their assigned tasks, monitor their own status, diagnose faults, and self-recover (or safely shut down) without continuous human presence on the shop floor. Human intervention is reduced to supervisory, planning, and exception-handling roles executed remotely.

The four pillars of IT-enabled unmanned operation are:
1. **Sense** — Distributed sensor network acquiring real-time data.
2. **Decide** — Edge/cloud computing with AI/rule-based decision engines.
3. **Act** — Closed-loop actuation through PLCs, drives, and robotics.
4. **Communicate** — Industrial IoT (IIoT) protocols (MQTT, OPC-UA, Modbus TCP).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Self-Driving Factory"

Imagine a fully automated **driverless car** navigating city streets:
- **Sensors** (cameras, LiDAR) act as the machine's "eyes."
- **Onboard computer** acts as the "brain" processing data.
- **Steering, brakes, accelerator** act as "actuators."
- **Cloud connection** gives real-time traffic updates.

Now scale this concept to a factory floor:
- The **machine tool** is the vehicle.
- The **PLCs and SCADA** are the onboard computer.
- **Robots, conveyors, CNC axes** are the actuators.
- **IIoT gateway** sends telemetry to a remote operator's mobile/laptop.

Just as you don't sit inside a Waymo taxi, an unmanned factory does not require an operator standing at the machine console. The **operator becomes a remote supervisor**, watching dashboards from a control room — or even from home.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Term — "Lights-Out Manufacturing"**: Coined from the practice of switching off factory lights during unmanned night shifts because no human workers need visibility. First realized by FANUC in Japan, where robots built other robots for weeks without human presence.

## 1.3 Core Metrics That Define Unmanned Performance

The effectiveness of unmanned operations is evaluated using standardized industrial KPIs:

| KPI | Full Form | Definition | Target Value |
|-----|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **OEE** | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | Availability × Performance × Quality | **≥ 85 %** (World-Class) |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Total Uptime ÷ Number of Failures | **High (↑)** |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Total Downtime ÷ Number of Repairs | **Low (↓)** |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | For non-repairable components | **High (↑)** |
| **Uptime %** | Operational Availability | (MTBF ÷ (MTBF + MTTR)) × 100 | **≥ 95 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark (Siemens Amberg Electronics Plant, Germany)**: Achieves **75 % unmanned operation** with **99.99885 % quality rate** and **OEE ≈ 85 %** — a global gold standard for IT-enabled unmanned manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Automation Hierarchy

Unmanned operations follow the well-established **ISA-95 / IEC 62264 Automation Pyramid** (also called the Purdue Model). IT enablement maps directly onto each layer:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Level 4 — Enterprise (ERP: SAP/Oracle)│  ← Business decisions
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Level 3 — Manufacturing (MES)         │  ← Production tracking
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Level 2 — Supervisory (SCADA/HMI)     │  ← Operator dashboard
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Level 1 — Control (PLC/DCS)          │  ← Closed-loop logic
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Level 0 — Physical (Sensors/Machines) │  ← Real-world I/O
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

For **unmanned operation**, the critical transformation is the **vertical integration** of these layers using IT protocols, eliminating the human-in-the-loop at Level 2 and below.

## 2.2 Core Functional Subsystems

### A. Sensing Layer (Level 0)
- **Vibration sensors** (accelerometers, IEPE type) for bearing health.
- **Temperature sensors** (RTD PT100, thermocouples) for thermal monitoring.
- **Current/Voltage transducers** for motor load profiling.
- **Vision systems** (Cognex, Keyence) for part inspection.
- **Proximity / Photoelectric sensors** for position feedback.

### B. Control Layer (Level 1)
- **PLC** (Programmable Logic Controller) — Ladder Logic / Structured Text per **IEC 61131-3**.
- **DCS** (Distributed Control System) for process industries.
- **Motion controllers** for CNC and robotic axes.
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for motor speed control.

### C. Supervisory Layer (Level 2)
- **SCADA** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) — Wonderware, Ignition, WinCC.
- **HMI** (Human-Machine Interface) — Touchscreen panels.
- **Historian databases** for time-series storage (PI System, Wonderware InSQL).

### D. IT Integration Layer (Levels 3–4)
- **MES** (Manufacturing Execution System) for work-order management.
- **ERP** for resource planning.
- **IIoT gateways** (Kepware, AWS IoT Greengrass, Azure IoT Edge).
- **Cloud analytics** (AWS, Azure, GCP) with ML-based predictive models.

### E. Communication Protocols
| Protocol | Layer | Use Case |
|----------|-------|----------|
| **Modbus TCP / RTU** | Field | PLC ↔ VFD, sensor networks |
| **OPC-UA** | Field → Cloud | Secure industrial data exchange |
| **Profinet / EtherCAT** | Real-time | High-speed motion control |
| **MQTT** | Cloud | Lightweight IIoT telemetry |
| **HTTP/REST API** | Cloud | ERP-MES integration |

## 2.3 The Closed-Loop Self-Regulation Theory

Unmanned operations rely on **negative feedback control** to maintain process variables without human tuning. The classical control loop:

**Setpoint (SP)** → **Controller (C)** → **Actuator** → **Process** → **Sensor** → **Feedback** → Compared with SP

For unmanned stability, the controller typically implements:
- **PID Control**: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** for multi-variable processes.
- **Adaptive/Fuzzy Control** for nonlinear machine dynamics.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the mathematical backbone for unmanned operation performance evaluation. Memorize for Part A and Part B derivations.**

### Reliability & Availability Formulas

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

Where $A$ = Availability (fraction), MTBF in hours, MTTR in hours.

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

Where $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance rate, $Q$ = Quality rate.

### Exponentially Distributed Failure Probability

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

Where $R(t)$ = Reliability at time $t$, $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ = failure rate.

### Sensor Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}
$$

Where $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $f_{\max}$ = highest signal frequency component.

### PID Controller (Continuous Form)

$$
u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

Where $K_p$ = Proportional gain, $T_i$ = Integral time, $T_d$ = Derivative time.

### Cycle Time Reduction from Automation

$$
\text{Savings (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{T_{\text{auto}}}{T_{\text{manual}}}\right) \times 100
$$

### Energy Efficiency Metric

$$
\eta_{\text{unmanned}} = \frac{W_{\text{useful output}}}{W_{\text{total input}}} \times 100\%
$$

### Communication Latency Bound for Real-Time Control

$$
t_{\text{latency}} < t_{\text{deadline}} - t_{\text{sense}} - t_{\text{actuate}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters**: For unmanned CNC machining, latency must be **< 1 ms** to maintain closed-loop stability. This is why Profinet/EtherCAT dominate over MQTT in motion-critical applications.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace Manufacturing**: Boeing's robotic drilling systems achieve unmanned wing assembly with positional accuracy of **± 0.05 mm**.
2. **Pharmaceuticals**: Closed aseptic filling lines run unmanned with Grade-A cleanroom integrity.
3. **Semiconductors**: TSMC fabs operate **24×7 unmanned** with OEE > 90 %.
4. **Automotive**: Tesla Gigafactories use **500+ robots per production line** with single-shift supervisory staffing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Availability from MTBF and MTTR

**Statement to Prove:**
$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Given:**
- MTBF = 240 hours
- MTTR = 10 hours

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= 240 \text{ h} \\
\text{MTTR} &= 10 \text{ h} \\
A &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
&= \frac{240}{240 + 10} \\
&= \frac{240}{250} \\
&= 0.96 \\
&= 96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The machine is operationally available **96 % of total calendar time** — meeting the world-class threshold for unmanned eligibility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — OEE Computation for an Unmanned CNC Cell

**Statement:** Compute OEE given the following 30-day data from an unmanned CNC cell.

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Planned Production Time | 720 h (30 days × 24 h) |
| Downtime (failures + setups) | 50 h |
| Ideal Cycle Time | 1.2 min/part |
| Actual Parts Produced | 32 000 |
| Defective Parts | 320 |

**Step 1 — Availability ($A$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Time} &= \text{Planned Time} - \text{Downtime} = 720 - 50 = 670 \text{ h} \\
A &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Time}} = \frac{670}{720} = 0.9306 = 93.06\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Performance ($P$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Minimum Time} &= 32\,000 \times 1.2 \text{ min} = 38\,400 \text{ min} = 640 \text{ h} \\
P &= \frac{\text{Theoretical Minimum Time}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \frac{640}{670} = 0.9552 = 95.52\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Quality ($Q$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Good Parts} &= 32\,000 - 320 = 31\,680 \\
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts}} = \frac{31\,680}{32\,000} = 0.9900 = 99.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — OEE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
&= 0.9306 \times 0.9552 \times 0.9900 \\
&= 0.8801 = 88.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** OEE = **88.01 %** → **World-class** category (above 85 % benchmark). The unmanned CNC cell qualifies for full lights-out operation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nyquist Sampling Rate for Vibration-Based Unmanned Monitoring

**Scenario:** A spindle bearing produces vibration signatures up to $f_{\max} = 500$ Hz. The ADC must sample without aliasing.

**Application of Nyquist Theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &\geq 2 \times f_{\max} \\
f_s &\geq 2 \times 500 \\
f_s &\geq 1000 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, engineers oversample by factor 5–10× to preserve harmonics:

$$
f_{s,\text{design}} = 10 \times 500 = 5000 \text{ Hz} = 5 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Sampling at 5 kHz allows FFT analysis to detect bearing race frequencies (BPFO, BPFI) for predictive maintenance — enabling true unmanned fault prediction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reliability Decay Over a 30-Day Unattended Run

A CNC machine has MTBF = 500 h. Compute probability of failure-free operation for 30 days (720 h continuous unmanned run).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ per hour} \\
R(720) &= e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.002 \times 720} \\
&= e^{-1.44} \\
&= 0.2369 = 23.69\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **23.7 %** chance of surviving 30 days without a single failure. **Conclusion:** For true unmanned operation beyond ~10 days (R ≈ 86 %), redundant machines or predictive maintenance algorithms are mandatory.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When is Unmanned Operation Justified?

| Criterion | Threshold for Unmanned | Justification Method |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| OEE (current) | ≥ 85 % | Indicates mature process |
| Failure Cost vs. MTBF | MTTR < 5 % of MTBF | Fast recovery |
| Process Safety Class | SIL-2 or higher (per IEC 61508) | Safe-failure design |
| Sensor Coverage | ≥ 95 % critical parameters monitored | Diagnostic completeness |
| Connectivity | OPC-UA + MQTT dual-stack | Reliable data flow |
| Cyber-security | IEC 62443 compliant | Unmanned = higher attack surface |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Conversion Logic — Why MTBF Matters More in Unmanned

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Manned Scenario: MTTR}_{\text{ops}} &\approx \text{MTTR}_{\text{engineer}} \\
\text{Unmanned Scenario: MTTR}_{\text{remote}} &\leq \text{MTTR}_{\text{ops}} \text{ (if good IT)} \\
\text{But: Detection time} \uparrow &\Rightarrow \text{Downtime} \uparrow
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore:** Unmanned systems must invest in **faster fault detection** (AI/ML diagnostics) more than in **faster repair**, since the on-site repair still requires human dispatch.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define "Unmanned Operation" in the context of IT-enabled manufacturing. List any FOUR enabling technologies.**

**Model Answer:**
Unmanned Operation is a manufacturing paradigm where machines execute production cycles, monitor their own health, and respond to anomalies without continuous human presence on the shop floor. The operator role shifts from active machine-tending to remote supervision.

**Four enabling technologies:**
1. **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — real-time sensor data acquisition.
2. **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** with IEC 61131-3 logic.
3. **SCADA systems** — centralized visualization.
4. **Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning** — predictive maintenance and adaptive control.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for technologies + ½ mark for neat presentation)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** (Module 4 Choice Option A)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an IT-enabled unmanned CNC machining cell**. Identify the role of each subsystem and the communication protocols used between layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC turning center operates unmanned for two shifts. Given data: MTBF = 480 h, MTTR = 6 h, ideal cycle time = 0.8 min/part, actual parts produced = 45 000 in 720 h, defective parts = 450. Calculate **Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE**. Comment on the suitability of the cell for fully unmanned operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The architecture follows the ISA-95 Purdue Model with five layers:

1. **Level 0 — Physical Machine**: CNC lathe with servo motors, spindle, tool turret, and work-holding chuck.
2. **Level 1 — Control**: Siemens SINUMERIK 840D controller executing the NC program; PLC (S7-1500) handles auxiliary logic (coolant, door interlock, chip conveyor).
3. **Sensors**: Renishaw OMP60 probe for tool offset, Kistler dynamometer for cutting force, Keyence vision for finished-part inspection, vibration sensor on spindle.
4. **Level 2 — Supervisory**: SCADA (Ignition by Inductive Automation) on a local server; HMI panel at cell entry.
5. **Level 3–4 — IT/Cloud**: IIoT gateway (Kepware) translates OPC-UA → MQTT → AWS IoT Core; cloud hosts ML model for tool-wear prediction; SAP ERP feeds job orders.

**Communication Protocols:**
- Sensor → PLC: **Analog 4–20 mA / IO-Link**
- PLC ↔ Drive: **Profinet IRT** (cycle time 1 ms)
- PLC ↔ SCADA: **OPC-UA** (encrypted)
- SCADA ↔ Cloud: **MQTT over TLS**
- Cloud ↔ ERP: **REST API / OData**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{480}{480 + 6} = \frac{480}{486} = 0.9877 = 98.77\% \\
\text{Operating Time} &= 720 \text{ h (assume no planned downtime)} \\
\text{Theoretical Min Time} &= 45\,000 \times 0.8 \text{ min} = 36\,000 \text{ min} = 600 \text{ h} \\
P &= \frac{600}{720} = 0.8333 = 83.33\% \\
Q &= \frac{45\,000 - 450}{45\,000} = \frac{44\,550}{45\,000} = 0.9900 = 99.00\% \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.9877 \times 0.8333 \times 0.9900 = 0.8148 = 81.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** OEE of **81.48 %** is **good** but below the world-class 85 % benchmark. The **Performance** component (83.33 %) is the limiting factor — likely due to reduced cutting parameters during unattended conservative operation. Suitable for unmanned operation, but with **scope for optimization** of spindle utilization to push OEE above 85 %.

---

### **Question B-B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** (Module 4 Choice Option B)

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Lights-Out Manufacturing** with a real-world case study. Explain how IT integration makes it feasible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An unmanned assembly line uses a vibration sensor to monitor a critical bearing. The bearing's defect frequency is 250 Hz. Compute the **minimum ADC sampling frequency** required. If the chosen PLC operates at a **scan cycle of 10 ms**, determine whether a closed-loop control action can be executed within the **deadline of 50 ms** given a sensing latency of 8 ms and actuation delay of 12 ms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Lights-Out Manufacturing (LOM)** refers to fully automated production that runs without on-site human operators — so named because factory lights can literally be switched off.

**Case Study — FANUC Robot Factory (Oshino, Japan):**
- Operates **30 days** continuously without human presence.
- Produces **~100 robots per day** using robotic assembly.
- Achieves **OEE > 95 %** due to extreme process stability.
- Uses **vision-guided robots** for self-calibration.

**How IT Integration Enables LOM:**
1. **Sensor fusion** (vision + force + position) provides closed-loop part placement without human judgment.
2. **Edge AI** detects defects in real-time, replacing human inspection.
3. **Predictive maintenance algorithms** replace scheduled human checks.
4. **MES/ERP integration** auto-replenishes raw material via AGVs.
5. **Remote diagnostics** allow engineers to troubleshoot from anywhere.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Nyquist Sampling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &\geq 2 \times f_{\max} = 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ Hz} \\
T_s &= \frac{1}{500} = 2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Latency Budget Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{total}} &= t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{PLC scan}} + t_{\text{actuate}} \\
&= 8 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} + 12 \text{ ms} \\
&= 30 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{\text{deadline}} = 50 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{\text{total}} = 30 \text{ ms} < 50 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** The closed-loop action **meets the deadline** with a **20 ms safety margin (40 % slack)**, confirming that the architecture is suitable for real-time unmanned control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing SCADA with MES** — SCADA is *Level 2* (process), MES is *Level 3* (operations). Marks deducted if hierarchy is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Quality" component in OEE** — Students often compute only Availability × Performance. Quality is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 3. **Using wrong Nyquist direction** — Always sample **faster** than $2f_{\max}$, not slower. Writing $f_s \leq 2f_{\max}$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating units** — MTBF in hours, sampling rate in Hz — examiners mark these explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering comment** — In OEE problems, the final 1-mark interpretation ("world-class or not?") is often missed by students.
> 6. **Mixing protocols** — Writing "MQTT is used for real-time motion control" is **incorrect**; MQTT is for cloud telemetry. Motion control uses **Profinet / EtherCAT**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

### ✅ Key Definitions
- **Unmanned Operation**: Production without continuous human presence.
- **Lights-Out Manufacturing**: Same concept; emphasizes zero lighting/needs.
- **IIoT**: Industrial Internet of Things — sensor-to-cloud connectivity.
- **OEE**: Availability × Performance × Quality (world-class ≥ 85 %).

### ✅ Core Equations (Must Memorize)
1. $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
2. $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$
3. $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
4. $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist)
5. PID: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$

### ✅ Architecture Layers (Purdue / ISA-95)
- Level 0: Physical (Sensors + Machines)
- Level 1: Control (PLC / DCS / Drives)
- Level 2: Supervisory (SCADA / HMI)
- Level 3: Operations (MES)
- Level 4: Enterprise (ERP)

### ✅ Protocol Mapping
- Sensor field → **Modbus / IO-Link**
- Motion control → **Profinet / EtherCAT**
- SCADA ↔ PLC → **OPC-UA**
- SCADA ↔ Cloud → **MQTT / HTTP**
- Cloud ↔ ERP → **REST / OData**

### ✅ Critical KPIs for Unmanned Approval
- OEE ≥ 85 %
- MTTR < 5 % of MTBF
- Sensor coverage ≥ 95 %
- Cyber-security compliant (IEC 62443)
- Functional safety compliant (IEC 61508 / SIL rating)

### ✅ Famous Industrial References
- **FANUC (Japan)** — 30-day unmanned robot factory.
- **Siemens Amberg (Germany)** — 75 % unmanned, 99.99885 % quality.
- **Tesla Gigafactory (Berlin/Shanghai)** — 500+ robots per line.
- **TSMC Fabs (Taiwan)** — 24×7 unmanned semiconductor lines.

### ✅ Common 14-Mark Question Types
1. *Architecture block diagram + OEE calculation* (most frequent).
2. *Lights-Out case study + communication protocol justification*.
3. *Sampling theorem + latency feasibility for closed-loop control*.
4. *Predictive maintenance algorithm + MTBF/MTTR analysis*.
